## 2026-07-19 (batch 497 — pages 4961-4970)

Ten days, 18 March – 1 April 2008, closing out the "Cost of Build - Paradox" question left open at batch 496's close, then running a full "paradox plans" FAQ recurrence to resolution. On cost of build: the designer's own plans specify exterior-grade plywood, in the hope the better material pays off later in longevity and lower maintenance; a firsthand caution reinforces the marine-grade side of the same tradeoff — fiberglass sheathing never fully excludes water over the long term, and rot can set in unnoticed, making the price gap too small to be worth the worry on a boat this size; a finished boat's own experience splits the difference, built in exterior grade sheathed in fiberglass, with epoxy (not the plywood grade) judged the real distinguishing factor of a modern fiberglass boat over the polyester of old, which does not spider-crack the way aged poly does. On paradox plans: a long-time reader's first post reports no reply from the plans distributor, echoing the archive's oldest recurring pattern; a nearly simultaneous order from another newcomer shows the contact address is unchanged, with the likely explanation being the distributor is simply away for a spell; a corollary turnaround-time question is answered consistently at no more than a couple of weeks, borne out by a quick New Zealand order; international delivery to Romania took fifteen days. No new pages created. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` (the special multi-block, continuously-numbered plywood file per WATCHLIST) extended "A 2008 recurrence: the cost gap between a 'top job' and an economy build" — citations [105]-[107] (file-wide numeric-max 104→107; the small pre-existing count-vs-max gap noted at batch 496, unrelated to this batch, remains unchanged); `references/building-plans.md` gained a new "A second March 2008 recurrence, and delivery-time data points" section — citations [176]-[182] (count/max 175→182); `people/alastair-law.md` gained a new bullet in "Design philosophy and community role" — citations [405]-[407] (count/max 404→407); `people/jack-gardiner.md` gained a new closing paragraph — citation [220] (count 219, numeric-max 220 — a pre-existing 1-number gap at citation 216, unrelated to this batch, unchanged). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 496 — pages 4951-4960)

One day, 18 March 2008, closing out two threads left open at batch 495's close ("Sheeting arrangements" and "Bending ply") and the "Paradox plans" correspondence with a new prospective builder, plus a fresh, unanswered materials-cost question. On sheeting/motor fouling: a mainsheet-motor-vane-gear photo link is corrected to point to the group's own files section; agreement is restated that the boom-end attachment, not the sheet's entry point, is the real fouling hazard; whether a self-steering vane gear — bulkier than any motor — is also at risk is answered directly (the part most likely to snag sits aft of the boom's own end, clear of the sheet); a pushpit that lifts and guides the sheet clear of a mounted motor is floated as a further easy fix; encasing the sheet's tail in plastic piping where it crosses the transom, a fix that worked well on another owner's Paradox ("Enuf"), is adopted as a planned experiment, alongside a first thorough test of a newly-fitted, initially-doubted Honda 2.3 outboard before an upcoming cruise. On the stubborn foredeck curve: having left none of the panel's own "wings" for leverage, one build's stem sits a few millimetres too high, giving a "Turkish slipper" profile that calls for more grinding, with steaming (as already used on the chine logs) weighed as the next step over a damp-lawn/sun approach that can't be counted on for sun; the thread closes on a joke about bells and tassels. A different approach entirely sidesteps the tight-curve problem: a lighter, thinner-ply foredeck built in two halves around a kingplank, using a fair batten and pre-beveled sheer clamps. On "Paradox plans": an invitation to visit a fellow builder further south is extended and gratefully deferred; the correspondent reports buying a construction book and reading the archive to avoid already-solved problems, then opens a fresh, unanswered cost-of-build and exterior-vs-marine-plywood question — the same evergreen question the archive has answered at length before, restated without new information. No new pages created; the correspondent remains below the person-page bar (plans ordered, no build/boat yet) and was updated in place in `WATCHLIST.md` section C. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `components/rig/index.md` gained a new "A pushpit as a further fouling remedy, and self-steering vane-gear clearance confirmed" subsection — citations [277]-[279] (count 281 incl. 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes, numeric-max 279, up from count 278/numeric-max 276); `construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md` extended "Fitting the forward deck skin to a tight curve" — citations [47]-[50] (count 58 incl. 8 pre-existing letter-suffixes, numeric-max 50, up from count 54/numeric-max 46); `boats/johanna.md` extended "A mainsheet question relayed to Glen Maxwell, and the foredeck's stubborn curve" — citations [108]-[110] (count/max 110, up from 107/107); `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new closing cross-link paragraph — citation [57] (count/max 57, up from 56/56); `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "A mainsheet-fouling fix adapted from another builder, and a planned outboard test" section — citation [104] (count/max 104, up from 103/103); `people/alastair-law.md` gained a new bullet in "Rig, electrics, and furling" — citation [404] (count/max 404, up from 403/403); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "NOLI's foredeck built in two halves, avoiding the tight-curve problem" section — citation [127] (count 128 incl. 1 pre-existing letter-suffix, numeric-max 127, up from count 127/numeric-max 126); `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md` extended "Carrying company aboard, and the practical cost of going bigger" — citations [139]-[140] (count/max 140, up from 138/138); `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` (the special multi-block, continuously-numbered plywood file) gained a new "A 2008 recurrence: the cost gap between a 'top job' and an economy build" section — citation [104] (file-wide numeric-max 104, up from 103; a small pre-existing gap at citation numbers 5-6, unrelated to this batch, was newly noticed and reported but left untouched). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 495 — pages 4941-4950)

Five days, 13-18 March 2008, resolving the "Hello :o) In new here." mainsheet/boom-bridle sub-thread left open at batch 494's close, opening a related foredeck-bending exchange, and closing with a new correspondent's plans order. On the mainsheet/boom question: Alastair answers the rear-deck-entry proposal directly — he had weighed a Little-Cruiser-style deck-entry sheet himself but judged its effect on the sail's set too uncertain without a kicking strap fitted, and the real fix is not where the sheet enters the hull but that it's made fast at the boom's very end, for which a loose-riding claw fitting (needing the proposed bridle wire to position it) is one option, though the question became moot once he found he had no real need for a motor at all; Alan works the bridle-load geometry further (a 600 mm forward shift on a 3,350 mm boom changes little, and the sheet load splits into two lesser vectors along the bridle); Larry Geib supplies the general rule (an included bridle angle under 60° reduces attachment load, over 60° increases it, at 60° the two are equal); Alastair closes the sub-thread with an unplanned-gybe account — the loose sheet wrapping around a cleat or the yuloh pin, easily freed by hand but far harder to clear from a mounted motor. In parallel, Pete Martin reports Glen Maxwell has rerouted his own mainsheet (a turning block under the deck, draining to the baffle and out the tiller-hole) to clear his self-steering gear, cutting motor fouling too, and asks Glen to comment; the same message reports a stubborn foredeck curve on JoHanna, floating a shallow circular-saw centerline groove to ease the bend. Bill Serjeant answers with a caution from his own "Faith" build: leverage from the panel's shaped "wings" can still open a small ~9 in crack up the centerline, and a saw-groove cut there tends to leave a visible ridge once the panel takes its shape — harmless once glassed, but less pleasing than the design's continuous curve. Alan opens a short related thread on bending solid timber (heat/wet/steam, and a damp-lawn/sun differential-shrinkage trick that straightened a batch of crooked studs). The batch closes with a new correspondent, "Greg" (Bridlington, East Yorkshire), who — having asked about scaling up for a companion aboard in batch 494 — orders the plans, expects a long-term build given limited funds, plans a scale RC model to stay motivated, and gives his location in reply to a welcome. No new pages created; Greg remains below the person-page bar (plans ordered, no build/boat yet) and was added to `WATCHLIST.md` section C. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 494 — pages 4931-4940)

Four days, 10-13 March 2008 (10th, then 12th-13th), closing out the "Hello :o) In new here." newcomer thread's design-comparison sub-debate and opening a fresh mainsheet/boom-bridle rig discussion within the same thread, plus a standalone one-off post. On the design-comparison sub-debate: Alastair argues Paradox's real problem for two-up sailing may be being too seaworthy rather than not seaworthy enough — a hull safe and comfortable in rough weather can tempt a solo sailor into conditions he would not risk with a companion aboard — illustrated by a storm-force Solent crossing from Poole that beat two 40 ft yachts struggling across from Lymington into Cowes, one of which turned back, a crossing he judges he would not have made carrying anyone else; a further, non-Paradox design (a beachable dinghy able to dry out on its own edge) is pointed to as possibly meeting the shoal-draft criterion. "Jeff UK" restates his long-running larger-Paradox-for-two idea as a 17 ft "pipedream," lighter than a straightforward 16 ft scale-up by taking the Enigma/NOLI lightweight-displacement approach further still, with drawings posted to his existing NOLI photo folder. A one-line pointer to a small powerboat's enclosed, vented engine-compartment fuel storage (mokai.com) adds a further precedent to the already-documented petrol-safety exchange, the rest of that message being previously-cited quoted material. On the mainsheet/boom-bridle sub-thread: a wire bridle spanning the boom end-to-end, sheeting from a fixed point above the rear deck, is proposed to keep the sheet clear of a transom-mounted motor and rudder; Alastair works through the load physics (roughly an 11x load multiplication at a shallow bridle angle, and bearing wear at the furling drum and clew), noting a shared problem with the boom-end tang bending under sheet load alone; Bill Serjeant adds that the sheet's own forward thrust is what keeps the boom seated on its tack bearing even without a retaining screw, from firsthand experience; a scaled-back rear-deck-entry variant is floated in response, left open. A standalone one-off: Nels imagines a mainstream yacht-industry design shrunk to Paradox size with a roller-reefing balanced lug rig, reacting to a trade-press quote. No new pages created; all correspondents are established identities except two thin one-offs (George, alaskamokaiman) folded citation-only. ## 2026-07-19 (source ingestion — Messing About In Boats magazine)

Ingested the Paradox/Matt-Layden content from 53 scanned issues of *Messing About In Boats* (MAIB), mirrored locally under `source-publications/Messing about in boats magazine/` (PDF + OCR Markdown per issue, ~1991–2025). Content was mapped by a read-only fan-out of 14 Sonnet recon agents (each faithfully transcribing its issues' Paradox/Layden items to scratchpad); the orchestrator then curated the results into the bundle as a single writer. Created two reference pages: [Messing About In Boats magazine](/references/messing-about-in-boats.md) — the source/hub node, with a 1991–2017 coverage timeline (the earliest Layden mention, a 1991 Bolduc rally letter; the 1993 "Cruising in Little Cruiser" cover feature and its reader-response editorial; Paradox's July 1995 print debut in "Cheating Winter"; Don Elliott's 1996–97 construction series; the 1997–98 reception; 2003 booklet classifieds and Glen Maxwell's published "Paradox Sea Trials" of "Zoë"; the 2001 GJAC-for-sale and "Capsize" series; Cedar Key meet reports 2004–2017 with Layden sightings, the 2007 Everglades Challenge "Sand Flea" run under the WaterTribe name "Wizard," and a documented Layden–Sven Yrvind meeting at Cedar Key 2007; the "Layden Lug"/chine-runner adoption by other builders 2011–13; and Michael Beebe's 2016 two-Paradox columns) — and [The "Building Paradox" MAIB series](/references/building-paradox-series.md) — the full part-by-part construction content of Don Elliott's sixteen-installment series (Parts 1–15 plus a Conclusion, vol. 14 nos. 9–24), including the published design-particulars box (LOA 4.2 m, sail 100 ft², disp. 1,410 lb, prismatic 0.58, D/L 270, plans $35/14 pp from the Bolducs), the whole build sequence (bulkheads → scarphed side panels → strongback-free hull assembly → NASA-airfoil kick-up rudder → "Spherical Filleting" → potable water tanks → Lexan windows → the "submarine" hatch → hollow Douglas-fir mast → finishing), the WEST SYSTEM engineer James R. Watson's public critique of its epoxy/trademark claims and Elliott's "In Defense" rebuttal, the reader Q&A in which Dave Bolduc validates the chine runners (Paradox vs. the centreboard Little Cruiser, "not enough difference in windward performance... to suggest altering Paradox"), and the "Layden Sightings" letter firsthand-dating Terrapin to 1983–84. Corrected the long-standing "five-part (issues 9-13)" record — which came from a single partial mailing-list citation — to the actual sixteen-installment run, in [Building the Paradox articles](/references/building-the-paradox-articles.md) (frontmatter, the "Original publication" section and its now-16-row bibliographic table, and the two derived-count parentheticals) and in [Don Elliott](/people/don-elliott.md)'s body prose (citation block untouched; [1b] still faithfully records the poster's five-part claim). Added both new pages to the [references index](/references/index.md). Anchor-checker (`anchorcheck.js`) clean, 0 broken across 4,365 anchors; a full file-level `.md`-link existence sweep across the tree found 0 missing targets attributable to this work (the 18 pre-existing danglers all live in historical `log.md` entries pointing at pages pruned in earlier reviews). Net tree: +2 `.md`. Deep per-page folding of the magazine's facts into the thematic tree (little-cruiser, biography, sand-flea, zoe, and the construction/epoxy pages — notably the Watson vinegar-on-skin correction) is deferred to the next holistic review; see `WATCHLIST.md`. **Same-day follow-up:** the one previously-missing installment, **Part 11** (vol. 14 no. 19, February 15, 1997), was supplied as a PDF, mirrored (PDF + OCR twin `messingaboutinbo1419hick.md`, bringing the mirror to 54 issues), and its content folded into `references/building-paradox-series.md` (new citation [16], the "missing"-caveats removed and the at-a-glance table row filled) — covering the chine-runner template ("vital to the success of making a good chine runner"), the custom-moulded lower pintle, scarphing the 3/4″ bottom panel, installing sheers/chines, and the "don't float the cloth" general-fibreglassing method; the Part 11 rows in `references/building-the-paradox-articles.md` and the issue count in `references/messing-about-in-boats.md` (53→54) were updated to match. All sixteen installments are now in the archive; anchor checker still clean.

## 2026-07-19 (batch 493 — pages 4921-4930)

Four days, 6-9 March 2008, closing out the "Hello :o) In new here." newcomer thread's outboard/fuel/steering sub-debate and then a fresh alternative-design/Layden-lineage exchange. On outboard mounting and fuel stowage: Alastair explains the tiller sweeps under most of the aft deck, leaving room for a fuel compartment only where already proposed, and that an accessible anchor well was tried at length and abandoned as infeasible — "a boat is a compromise, don't destroy the things you like about a design to add some 'improvement.'" Larry Geib suggests yoke or crank steering to reclaim some of that room; Alastair declines, having judged an aft-facing tiller too complicated to repair remotely, too long to clear the mainsheet, and too shallow-angled on the steering rope. Nels reframes the underlying choice: Matt Layden's original design didn't include a motor at all, so a sailor who judges one essential may be better served by a design with a motor already built in, ideally with a free-draining, vented motor well. Jack Gardiner (Jill) gives his own working setup — a folding-mount 6 hp Johnson, offset, with the gas can stowed between the two rear cleats and bungee-tied — and, in a follow-up, a sturdier ratchet-strap alternative plus a floated small integrated rear-deck tank; Alastair counters with his own side-mounted electric emergency motor and a caution, from repeated green water aboard, against deck-stowed fuel and transom-mounted motors — "don't get blinded by the hype." Bill Serjeant, still deciding on an engine for "Faith," asks Jack whether his mount matches David Beard's Honda 2.3 on Whisper's port-side transom, weighs a starboard mount instead, and invites David to add photos and confirm how it performs — left unanswered on record, motivated by needing engine power to hold station off tidal, fluky-winded harbours like Dover and Ramsgate. The thread then turns to alternative designs: Jeff Michals-Brown doubts Jim Michalak's "Caroline" matches Paradox's seaworthiness and handiness in windy conditions, and asks whether any design at all offers Paradox's combination of inside steering, self-righting, and shoal-draft cruising; Greg answers with the Layden lineage itself — Little Cruiser (built 1988, 4.62 m, a centreboarder still sailed by her owners after 10+ years), a prototype Enigma 460, and "Jeff['s]" own 4.24 m Enigma-style build (likely NOLI) — all sharing Matt's chine runners as "a developmental progression." ## 2026-07-19 (batch 492 — pages 4911-4920)

Three days, 3-6 March 2008, running the "Hello :o) In new here." newcomer thread to a close (room/motor/fuel questions, an alternative-design pointer, and a petrol-safety exchange), plus a standalone "A few questions" continuation and a fresh, one-off "Looking for boldav38" plans request. On "Hello": Greg, having accepted the offset-mast reasoning from a prior batch, still wants a little more room (~15 ft) and would live without a motor if pressed; Alastair answers the room/motor question directly — stock Paradox suits one person comfortably, two only for day sailing (though others, Matt Layden included, have lived aboard two-up), the transom-mounted mainsheet is a hazard to any motor mounted there, and his own solution on "Little Jim" is an emergency-only electric outboard with a 6-mile range; Nels points Greg to Jim Michalak's "Philsboat" as an off-the-shelf, easier-to-modify alternative; Pete Martin, continuing the same thread, mocks up sealed, vented, fiberglassed fuel compartments in both Johanna's stern quarters for 5-litre containers (photographed), recalling safely carrying petrol in steel oil cans on a prior microcruiser; Alastair endorses the idea in principle, restates his own "build to plans first, retrofit later" philosophy for "Little Jim" (in practice none of his pre-build ideas were ever implemented), and reveals his own planned mod — cutting the forward-stateroom access down to floor level to store a folding bike; Greg, drawing on professional experience surveying BP petrol stations (a mandatory 3-day safety course), gives a detailed petrol-vapour safety caution; a new one-off correspondent, Bud, agrees and adds his own wet-towel technique plus an independent version of the aft-deck fuel-compartment idea. On "A few questions": Pete Martin closes the loop on a fellow UK builder's small-gear thread (camping mats, an LED night light, a solar charger, rechargeable batteries), settling on battery-powered lighting over wired cabin electrics and answering the open masthead-solar-light search with a stainless-solar-light suggestion. A first-time poster, Warren, reports the recurring "no reply from Dave [Bolduc]" plans-request pattern, unanswered. No new pages created; Greg (whose full name, "greg bulmer," surfaces in a `From:` header this batch), Bud, and Warren are thin one-offs below the person-page bar, folded citation-only, no WATCHLIST entries warranted. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 491 — pages 4901-4910)

Four days, 28 February - 3 March 2008 (plus one held/late-arriving reply dated 4 March NZ time), closing out the "A few questions" deck-beam/corner-post/interior-paint thread left open at batch 490's close, then running a full "Hello :o) In new here." newcomer thread on scaling up and centering the mast. On "A few questions": the low deck-beam-at-bulkhead-1 problem is confirmed common across builds, with two independent fixes offered (a build-photo comparison, and leaving deck-beam cleats proud to shape once the deck goes on); the sheet-10 corner-post's puzzling 30-degree/14-degree angles are explained in full for the first time — a section through the cabin's uprights, cross-referenced to the sheet's side-frame layout, setting how the front and rear windows seat once wrapped around the post; and interior paint draws four separate answers (a clear primer sealer, plain household paint, a primer formulated to stick to epoxy with a cold-weather caution, and a cheaper Dulux "Super Grip" alternative), while cabin-sole non-slip and kneeling comfort get their own new page section (deck paint on the sole, waterproof kneeling cushions, a foam sleeping mat forward of the feet, a gardener's kneeling mat, and a commercial foam floor-tile product bought for NOLI's own cabin sole). Pete Martin thanks the group, expects the corner posts to become self-evident once cut, and goes looking for cheap camping mats to line JoHanna's cabin sides. "Jeff UK" separately narrates a run of small NOLI gear purchases — Screwfix camping mats for cabin lining, a cheap LED press light, a Maplin AA-battery solar charger, and an open search for a solar mast-top light that draws two suggestions (an LED garden-umbrella-pole light, and a complete solar shed light) from a new one-off New Zealand correspondent, Jim Baltaxe. On "Hello :o) In new here.": a new correspondent, having read Matt Layden's and Sven Yrvind's voyage accounts, dislikes the offset mast on symmetry grounds and asks about scaling the plans up slightly with the mast moved to the centerline; Matt Layden, asked directly, is reported to have approved a 10% scale-up but no more, reasoning a bigger step means designing a new boat, and as of March 2008 no one had actually built one; centering the mast outright (rather than only shifting the sail to starboard, already documented) draws a more decisive answer still — it would cost the furled sail its side-deck self-stow and likely need a hole cut through the rear baffle for the sheet — and the group's "Larger Paradox" files-section document is pointed to again, with a fresh caution about the displacement increase a 5-metre boat carries against available car and trailer capacity, and a recommendation to look at a different design entirely for coastal cruising two-up with a motor. No new pages created; the newcomer and Jim Baltaxe are thin one-offs below the person-page bar, folded citation-only, no WATCHLIST entries warranted. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md` extended "Shimming deck beam #1" and "Mounting deck beams to the sheer clamp," and gained a new "Corner post angles on sheet 10" section — citations [42]-[43] (count 51 incl. the 8 pre-existing letter-suffixed citations [39a]-[40d], numeric-max 43, up from count 49/numeric-max 41); `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` extended "Interior finish" — citations [108]-[111] (count 112 incl. the pre-existing [29a] letter-suffix, numeric-max 111, up from count 108/numeric-max 107); `components/interior.md` gained a new "Cabin-sole non-slip and kneeling comfort" section — citations [47]-[49] (count/max 49, up from 46/46); `components/rig/index.md` extended "Sail bearing on the mast, and mast offset" — citations [267]-[268] (count 270 incl. 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes, numeric-max 268, up from count 268/numeric-max 266); `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md` gained a new "Carrying company aboard, and the practical cost of going bigger" section — citations [131]-[133] (count/max 133, up from 130/130); `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Deck-beam fairing, corner posts, and interior paint answered" section — citations [102]-[105] (count/max 105, up from 101/101); `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new closing section — citation [54] (count/max 54, up from 53/53); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "Paint advice for a fellow UK builder, and cabin-sole matting and small gear for NOLI" section — citations [121]-[124] (count 125 incl. the pre-existing [39a] letter-suffix, numeric-max 124, up from count 121/numeric-max 120); `people/alastair-law.md` gained two new closing bullets — citations [398]-[399] (count/max 399, up from 397/397). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 490 — pages 4891-4900)

Three-and-a-half weeks, 3-27 February 2008, closing out the "New guy question about interior finish" thread, then a brief "Paradox in Taiwan" launch congratulation, a new "Tools for Nicopress?" tangent, and a closing "A few questions" construction-progress message. On interior finish: a new one-off UK correspondent (`keyhavenpotterer`/Brian, building a Keyhaven Scow and an Oughtred Gannet, not a Paradox) reports two-pack varnish over well-sanded epoxy holding up "not a mark anywhere" on marine-ply parts, and Cetol alone keeping hardwood fittings "still perfect" after two seasons, while single-pack paint over epoxy chips off regardless of boat. Alastair traces his own paint failure to an application-temperature mistake — an epoxy-bonding primer applied below its 6°C minimum — and argues Paradox needs even less interior paint than most boats, since the sun only reaches inside while under sail and only past whatever plugs the hatch; Nels answers with a twelve-year finish comparison from his own boat "Micro" (Cetol brightwork crazing, epoxy-varnished spars needing refinishing, house paint and porch paint holding up fine elsewhere) and endorses tinted Lexan for UV protection, then recommends Minwax PolyShades as a cheaper Cetol alternative proven over ten years. Stephen Yahn closes the thread describing his own practice: inexpensive Rustoleum oil-base enamel straight onto bare wood, no fillets except the vent trunk and baffle. Pete Martin then congratulates Jimmy Lu on launching his Taiwan-built Paradox on 18 February 2008 (still unnamed), a new one-off correspondent Pat Smith asks about Nicopress swaging tools for stainless wire (five tool options with prices), Alastair notes Paradox has little use for wire rigging, and Pete closes the batch with three open construction questions on JoHanna — a deck-beam-1 shim (a fourth confirmed case of the recurring beam-height shortfall), an unclear sheet-10 corner-post detail, and a UK-terms interior-paint/cabin-sole-non-skid question, none answered on record yet. No new pages created; Brian and Pat Smith are thin one-offs below the person-page bar, folded citation-only. `.okf-progress/WATCHLIST.md` updated in place: Jimmy Lu's section-C entry now records the launch (still no boat name, trigger not yet formally met, flagged for re-evaluation at ~5000 regardless). Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` extended "Interior finish: bare wood, epoxy, varnish, cetol, or paint" — citations [102]-[107] (count 108 incl. the pre-existing [29a] letter-suffix, numeric-max 107, up from count 102/numeric-max 101); `people/alastair-law.md` gained two new bullets — citations [396]-[397] (count/max 397, up from 395/395); `people/nels.md` gained two new bullets — citations [198]-[199] (count/max 199, up from 197/197); `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a new "An oil-based enamel for interior finish" section — citation [66] (count/max 66, up from 65/65); `boats/johanna.md` gained two new sections, "Congratulating Jimmy Lu on his Paradox's launch" and "A deck-beam shim, a corner-post plans question, and an interior-paint question" — citations [100]-[101] (count/max 101, up from 99/99); `construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md` extended "Shimming deck beam #1" with JoHanna's confirming case — citation [41] (a clean new sequential number in a file that already carries pre-existing letter-suffixed citations [39]-[40d]); `references/materials-sourcing-links.md` gained a new "Tools for swaging Nicopress sleeves onto stainless-steel wire" section — citations [27]-[28] (count/max 28, up from 26/26). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 489 — pages 4881-4890)

One day, 1-2 February 2008, closing out the "Enigma 460" beam-width exchange and running an entire new "New guy question about interior finish" thread. On Enigma 460: Nels clarifies that a prior beam-width remark was not about seat/butt width but shoulder room; a first-time, one-off UK correspondent (`keyhavenpotterer`) admires the prototype's CNC-milled rudder, linking `e460.blogspot.com`. A new correspondent, John Lehman (Milwaukee, WI, sailing Lake Michigan), just starting to cut bulkheads for a Paradox, opens a fresh thread on interior-finish options — bare wood, unthickened epoxy, varnish, or paint. Alastair (Little Jim) reports epoxy-coating all his own interior woodwork held up with no obvious damage after four years of UK sun, while painting the sun-exposed bits (on the belief unprotected epoxy needed shielding) made "a terrible job" that peeled steadily — suspecting the original UV warning was calibrated to Florida sun rather than UK sun — and separately asks how well varnish adheres to epoxy and whether cetol can go over epoxy at all. Nels suggests spar varnish or cetol as UV-protective alternatives (cetol less transparent but more durable, a "woodsy" look), then answers the adhesion questions: pre-sand epoxy for tooth and thin the first varnish coat; cetol likely adheres best to bare wood, like house paint; sees no point epoxying a closed interior at all unless she's an open boat. A one-off New Zealand correspondent (`stevensouthsea`) adds that NZ's strong, ozone-hole-affected sun destroys epoxy and plastics quickly, recommending a thinned epoxy wood-preservative pre-primer, an undercoat, and a two-pack polyurethane topcoat. John Lehman thanks the group, confirms cetol's own application guide calls for bare wood, and plans to try it on some interior surfaces. John Gilbert (`gilberj55`) closes the thread suggesting a paint formulated to bond to epoxy, or etching/scoring a failed epoxy coating before repainting, plus a UV-inhibited topcoat regardless. No new pages created; John Lehman added to `WATCHLIST.md` section C as a new prospective builder (construction just started, no boat name yet). Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` gained a new "Interior finish: bare wood, epoxy, varnish, cetol, or paint" section — citations [94]-[101] (count 101/max 101, up from 93/93, plus the pre-existing [29a] letter-suffix); `matt-layden/enigma.md` extended the Enigma-460 sections with the shoulder-room clarification and the CNC-milled-rudder detail — citations [62]-[63] (count 63/max 63, up from 61/61); `people/nels.md` gained three new bullets closing "Other contributions" — citations [195]-[197] (count 197/max 197, up from 194/194); `people/alastair-law.md` gained two new closing paragraphs — citations [394]-[395] (count 395/max 395, up from 393/393). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 488 — pages 4871-4880)

Three days, 29 January - 1 February 2008, the "Cabin Sole" thread's tail (NOLI's name origin, a beaching/rudder digression, and a new "Rudder blade stop" sub-thread) followed by a fresh round of "Enigma 460" sizing. A newcomer's one-off question draws "Jeff UK"'s answer that NOLI was the pet name of his late mother Nora, from her school days. Bill Serjeant explains why he beaches "Faith" stern-first — angling the sole so his head lies higher than his feet for a good night's sleep, since bow-first on a steep beach puts the head near the vent box — while cautioning that stern-first beaching on a pebbly shore risks thumping the rudder's base, a lesson learned the hard way; Al confirms the rudder's own fittings tolerate the contact regardless, since an upward force on the blade just pushes it up against the hull rather than loading the pintles. Stephen Yahn, puzzled what stops a lowered rudder blade from swinging forward into the transom, gets a direct answer from Al: it does clonk the corner, then moves clear enough for full tiller swing, an angle the haul-up rope can further constrain. On Enigma sizing, "Jeff UK" works out the Enigma 360's 12'3" hull length independently from Matt Layden's own *Small Craft Advisor* panel-layout description, then proposes widening her stock 3'3" beam to 3'6" and her draft to 7 in for a larger crew (drawing a joke from Larry Geib); Nels restates the Enigma 460's particulars on-list, adding a not-previously-recorded ~1,000 lb maximum-payload figure; "Jeff UK" closes by arguing cruising clutter, visible in Matt's own Everglades Challenge footage, would make a scaled-up Enigma more restrictive for a larger sailor than raw dimensions suggest. No new pages created; all correspondents are established identities. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `components/rudder.md` gained two new sections, "Beaching stern-first: a sleeping-comfort technique, and why it doesn't damage the rudder" and "Rudder blade stop: clonking the transom corner, and the haul-up rope as a check" — citations [75]-[78] (count 78/max 78, up from 74/74); `matt-layden/enigma.md` extended "Enigma 360" and "Enigma 460" with the panel-layout hull-length derivation, the beam-widening proposal, the restated/enriched 460 table, and the clutter counter-argument — citations [58]-[61] (count 61/max 61, up from 57/57); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "The origin of NOLI's name" section — citations [119]-[120] (count 121, numeric-max 120 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [39a], up from count 119/numeric-max 118); `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "Beaching stern-first for a comfortable sleeping angle" section — citation [100] (count 100/max 100, up from 99/99); `people/alastair-law.md` gained two new closing paragraphs — citations [392]-[393] (count 393/max 393, up from 391/391); `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a new "A rudder-blade-stop question" section — citation [65] (count 65/max 65, up from 64/64); `people/nels.md` gained a new bullet closing "Other contributions" — citation [194] (count 194/max 194, up from 193/193). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 487 — pages 4861-4870)

Two days, 28-29 January 2008, the entire batch a single "Cabin Sole" sub-thread weighing Paradox's heavy, over-built bottom against a lighter hull. James agrees Paradox is the pre-eminent micro-cruiser but notes many builders value the interior sailing position, simplicity, and self-righting over any specific offshore requirement, since crossing the North Sea in a force nine "is not our cup of tea" for most; days later he questions the logic of hauling a fixed heavy bottom around on the trailer, in light winds, or up a beach, when the same righting effect could come from water ballast instead, concluding Paradox still wins for all-weather use but Enigma suits some builders better. "Jeff UK" adds NOLI's glassing schedule (three layers of cloth to bulkhead 3, tapering to two at the transom, following a conversation establishing that damage on a comparable hull always happens forward of Little Cruiser's lowest point) and, replying to Al, floats a counter-argument that NOLI's lighter, 500-lb-less displacement might make her less, not more, susceptible to hull damage. Al frames Paradox's advantage over both NOLI and Enigma as the "comfort of confidence" of never needing to survey a drying-out spot for rocks, recalling Faith once dried out squarely on a submerged concrete mooring sinker with no damage found, and separately recalls two other, less heavily built boats damaged badly enough to be taking on water in a single season (one from a stone, one from a submerged post) plus a cruise abandoned after a separate serious grounding. Nels broadens the case with some 20-plus other flat-bottomed, self-righting designs sharing the same heavy-bottom philosophy (many field-tested, testers consistently surprised how well they perform), and separately warns that old piers, bridge pilings, and rip-rap are worse hazards than rocks, especially since a shallow-draft boat like Paradox can tempt exploration of places boats have never been before. John Gilbert (`gilberj55`) closes the thread with a general light-vs-robust construction essay: light weight means a less robust structure, and while modern composite technique can build a strong light structure it is more critical to get right, so genuinely light weight suits a car-topper, an easy tow, or a racer more than a cruiser. No new pages created; all correspondents are established identities. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `design/hull-scantlings.md` gained a new "The case for over-construction: field evidence, hazards, and a counter-argument" section — citations [15]-[20] (count 20/max 20, up from 14/14); `boats/faith.md` gained a new "Drying out on a sunken mooring block during the West Country cruise" section — citation [229] (count 231, numeric-max 229 complete plus the two pre-existing letter-suffixes [133a]/[191a], up from count 230/numeric-max 228); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "NOLI's glassing schedule, and a lighter-hull counter-argument" section — citations [117]-[118] (count 119, numeric-max 118 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [39a], up from count 117/numeric-max 116); `people/james-bradford.md` extended "Other contributions" with two new bullets — citations [7]-[8] (count 8/max 8, up from 6/6); `design/weight-and-ballast.md` extended "Ballast" with a water-ballast-versus-fixed-bottom bullet — citation [164] (count 167, numeric-max 164 complete plus three pre-existing letter-suffixes, up from count 166/numeric-max 163); `people/nels.md` extended "Design and build ideas" with two new bullets — citations [192]-[193] (count 193/max 193, up from 191/191); `people/alastair-law.md` gained two new closing paragraphs — citations [390]-[391] (count 391/max 391, up from 389/389). `.okf-progress/WATCHLIST.md` updated in place: John Gilbert's section-A entry (`design/hull-scantlings.md` added to the consolidation list, still no boat), and a new read-only note flagging a second citation-numbering phantom gap discovered in `design/blue-water-suitability.md` ([1]-[57] then jumping to [121]-[127]), deferred to the ~5000 renumbering sweep alongside the two already-flagged files. Anchorcheck and anchorcheck2 both 0 broken across 4,224 anchors, up from 4,195 at batch 486's close.

## 2026-07-19 (batch 486 — pages 4851-4860)

Two days, 26-28 January 2008, the "Cabin Sole" thread's tail folding car-topping weight math and the Enigma-family LOA question to a close. Replying to Al's catamaran anti-vortex-panel distinction, "Jeff UK" gives Enigma's sail area as 56 sq ft. John Gilbert (`gilberj55`, real name now given directly in the `From:` header) estimates a stripped Paradox — no ballast, no water — at 550-650 lb, too heavy for a car roof, and puts a practical car-topping limit around 75-80 lb, reasoning by analogy from car-topping a pair of kayaks. Ray recalls that Matt Layden used a system of extensible rack beams so that only one end of Enigma needed lifting at a time. Nels adds a concrete roof-rack-loading technique from his own canoe experience (a 17 ft, 72 lb royalex canoe with a portage yoke, versus a 16 ft, 45 lb kevlar canoe that sees far more actual use for being so much easier to load) and, separately, gives the design rationale for Paradox's own heavy 3/4 in-plus-three-layers-of-24 oz-glass bottom — ballast, stiffness against oil-canning, and safety — as a contrast to the lighter car-topping builds under discussion. James works out his own build's car-topping weight (about 220 lb for the bare hull) and restates its 2 ft hull depth as close to Enigma's, aspiring to combine Paradox's comfort with a Goat Island Skiff's performance and light weight. Dave Raftery restates the Enigma-360's 12' 3" LOA figure, sourced from the EnigmaBuilders group's own home page, without an independent measurement. "Jeff UK" then resolves the open question from batch 485 directly: Enigma's length is 12' 3" **plus** the rudder. Separately, replying to James's own weight sums, "Jeff UK" gives NOLI's own construction weights — 1/4 in Gaboon ply sides (16 lb a sheet), 3/8 in bottom, liftable by one hand before glassing and two after, and a VW Golf's 220 lb roof-rack rating — while confirming he will stick with a trailer regardless. No new pages created; all correspondents are established identities. Files touched, all count==numeric-max: `matt-layden/enigma.md` gained citations [54]-[57], resolving the 12'3"-plus-rudder question and adding the extensible-rack-beam detail (count 57/max 57, up from 53/53); `design/variants/car-topping-conversion-idea.md` gained a new "A stripped Paradox's own car-topping weight, and roof-rack loading technique" section — citations [11]-[12] (count 12/max 12, up from 10/10); `design/hull-scantlings.md` gained a new "Why the bottom is built heavy" section — citation [14] (count 14/max 14, up from 13/13); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "NOLI's ply weights, a before/after lift test, and a roof-rack capacity figure" section — citation [116] (count 116, numeric-max 116 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [39a], up from count 115/numeric-max 115); `people/james-bradford.md` extended "Building a car-topping, Enigma-style Paradox" with the weight sums and hull-depth restatement — citations [5]-[6] (count 6/max 6, up from 4/4); `people/nels.md` gained two new bullets closing "Other contributions" — citations [190]-[191] (count 191/max 191, up from 189/189). `.okf-progress/WATCHLIST.md` updated in place: John Gilbert's section-A entry (car-topping estimate, `design/variants/car-topping-conversion-idea.md` added to the consolidation list) and Dave Raftery's section-C entry (still intent-only, no plans, no boat). Anchorcheck and anchorcheck2 both 0 broken across 4,195 anchors, up from 4,179 at batch 485's close.

## 2026-07-19 (batch 485 — pages 4841-4850)

One day, 26-27 January 2008 (plus one held-for-moderation message actually written 25 January), continuing and closing out the "Cabin Sole" thread's drift into chine runners, car-topping practicality, and the Enigma family. Alastair distinguishes Paradox's chine runners from catamaran "anti-vortex panels" (a link posted by James) — the panels sit on a hull that sails flat, whereas Paradox's own leeway reduces specifically as she heels. Nels agrees, then makes the fullest practical case on record against car-topping a stock Paradox — all her gear must come out of the boat to load her, an average launching ramp is awkward without a dolly, and a trailer's slide-on/slide-off convenience wins outright — while noting the chine runners themselves need the hull loaded down to work, unlike Enigma's lighter design. Jeff Michals-Brown corroborates from his own experience car-topping a Michalak "Piccup Pram," finding car-topping "overrated." Separately, Ray recalls Matt Layden once car-topping Enigma on a compact car, and Nels points to a photo at the EnigmaBuilders-as-Designed-by-Matt_Layden group's home page confirming her 180 lb weight, then relays the group's Enigma 460 news (plans expected if the prototype works; "360"/"460" denote each boat's length in cm). A new correspondent, Larry Geib — the archive's long-tracked "Larry"/`LJGeib`/`lorenzojose2000` (posting since 2005), whose real name is confirmed here for the first time — questions a length inconsistency in the Enigma-family reference wiki (12'3" for the "360" is over 370 cm, not ~360); Alastair asks whether the figure includes the rudder; neither is answered on record. Jeff UK, replying to James's car-topping build announcement, asks his plan and location (unanswered), and in a separate held-for-moderation message (written 25 January, entering the archive out of date order at msgnum 4888) recalls once owning a Hunter 19 named "Willing Griffin" and restates NOLI's own washboard as a single vee-shaped piece with a small window, given its ~10 in opening height. **Identity fix:** citation [27] on `matt-layden/enigma.md` had mislabeled the "Larry"/`LJGeib` correspondent's real name as "Larry Whited" (a genuinely distinct, separately tracked identity); corrected to "Larry Geib" per this batch's own From-header evidence, and cross-referenced. Files touched, all count==numeric-max: `chine-runners/how-they-work.md` — citation [65] (65/65, up from 64/64); `design/variants/car-topping-conversion-idea.md` — citations [9]-[10] (10/10, up from 8/8); `people/james-bradford.md` — citation [4] (4/4, up from 3/3); `matt-layden/enigma.md` — citations [49]-[53] plus the [27] identity fix (53/53, up from 48/48); `people/jeff-doryman.md` — new "A previous Hunter 19, 'Willing Griffin,' and NOLI's washboard restated" section, citation [115] (115/115, up from 114/114); `people/alastair-law.md` — citation [389] (389/389, up from 388/388); `people/nels.md` — citations [187]-[189] (189/189, up from 186/186). No new pages; `.okf-progress/WATCHLIST.md` updated so Jeff Michals-Brown's entry now tracks four distinct threads. Anchorcheck and anchorcheck2 both 0 broken across 4,179 anchors, up from 4,165 at batch 484's close.

## 2026-07-19 (batch 484 — pages 4831-4840)

One day, 25-26 January 2008, closing the "Drop board options" and "Cabin Sole" threads and promoting a long-tracked UK correspondent to his own page. "Drop board options" closes: Nels adds a "Wileyport" — a screened, bracketed Lexan window usable vertical or angled open — to the group's photo folder, drawn from his own Bolger Micro, and separately posts a standalone "Solar Vent" pointer to a self-contained solar-powered vent fan; Alastair replies that the three-position drop-board idea, while neat, has never actually been needed given Paradox's own already-larger drop-board opening. "Cabin Sole" resumes and closes: "Jeff UK" describes NOLI's washboard-bolt fastening and her foredeck solar vent (replacing the stock vent trunk), joking solar vents do little by moonlight; a UK correspondent, cutting plywood that week for his own car-topping, Enigma-style Paradox, announces the start of construction, asks a boom-trim/spinnaker rig question, and proposes a UK builders' meet at Poole Harbour; Alastair answers with Paradox-vs-Enigma sail-area figures and boom/mast centre-of-effort physics, and reveals a firm West Country cruise planned with Bill Serjeant for most of May 2008, taking in the Transat's Plymouth start (11th) and the Jester Challenge (31st), hoping to reach the Scillies between the two. The car-topping builder — James, `howgegoboat`, Bradford, Yorkshire — clears the person-page bar on this batch's actual construction start plus his already-substantial, multi-file record (the originating car-topping/Enigma-hybrid proposal, an Enigma beam/chine-wing question, a windvane pointer, a barbecue-heater conversion, scaled-up-Paradox naming and beam-widening ideas, and a "what makes a Paradox a Paradox" question) and is promoted to `people/james-bradford.md`, cross-linking rather than re-narrating his prior scattered contributions. `components/vent-box.md`, a previously-flagged content gap, gained its first real content and citations. No boat page created (James's build has no name yet). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 483 — pages 4821-4830)

Two days, 23-25 January 2008, closing the Peep Hen tangent of "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" and running the "Cabin Sole" thread through washboard design into a new "Drop board options" thread. On the Peep Hen tangent: Glen Maxwell answers both of the batch-482 open questions — pointing to a Hensnest Yahoo-group photo folder and to `peephens.org` rather than posting a photo of a Peep Hen under sail directly (still promising more on his own site), and confirming designer Rubin Train remains active in powerboat design, believed to be overseeing production of one of his own designs; whether the Peep Hen bears any relationship to the Bolger Micro or remains in production stay open. A one-line joke coda closes the "mad/sane" bit from batch 482: an established but below-page-bar correspondent, John Button, agrees being mad and sane at once is itself the paradox, and that a Paradox owner needs to be "a self-centred eccentric." The "Cabin Sole" thread resumes with Pete Martin confirming he will leave out the built-in water tanks for lifting/hinged sole plates, still undecided on washboard shape, and floating two sealed aft-quarter fuel-can lockers that double as reserve buoyancy if never used for fuel; "Jeff UK" explains NOLI's vee-shaped washboard was chosen mainly to use up a 15-year-old plywood offcut; Bill Serjeant describes a divided upper/lower washboard technique used on his own other boats (a Hunter 19's viewing window); Nels asks about tapered washboards, citing his own tapered, delrin-slider companionway; a long-running non-Paradox commentator, Ray, contributes a screw-in deck-plate technique, the case for an angled washboard ("a feature, not a bug"), and a cleated-line heavy-weather technique. Nels then opens "Drop board options" with a three-photo folder of a compromise drop-board design and a lighter bug-screened summer board, inviting further ideas; Pete closes the batch weighing a Lexan board like Little Cruiser's but judging a double washboard not worth it given Paradox's 260 mm board height. No new pages created — all correspondents (Glen Maxwell, John Button, Pete Martin, "Jeff UK", Bill Serjeant, Nels, Ray) are established identities folded into existing pages. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 482 — pages 4811-4820)

Two days, 22-23 January 2008, closing out the "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" megathread's height/weight and Peep Hen tangents, closing the "Cabin Sole" thread, and opening a new "Seating Position in Paradox" thread. On the megathread: Nels replies to William Nix's Micro purchase, revealing his own shifted plan to build Matt Layden's smaller Enigma instead of Paradox for solo sailing — easier to handle ashore, lighter to tow, "comes with training wheels" — likening it to an Austin-Healey Sprite convertible, and pointing to an Enigma prototype under construction; Glen Maxwell replies about his own second boat, a 14 ft Peep Hen, revealing the design's origin (Rubin Train) and that his extended family now owns four of them, including his brother Greg's, the first he ever sailed; Bill Serjeant and Alastair each give reference height/weight figures (5'8"/149 lb and 5'6"/180 lb respectively) continuing the thread's running survey; Nels follows up asking whether the Peep Hen remains in production, its relationship (if any) to the Bolger Micro, and whether designer Rubin Train is still active, recalling Small Boat Journal ads — unanswered; "Jeff UK" separately asks Glen for a photo of a Peep Hen actually under sail — also unanswered. On "Cabin Sole": Bill Serjeant gives Pete Martin's water-tank/washboard question a fuller technical answer than Alastair's prior reply — an aluminium-angle-mounted, lift-out bin-and-floor system in place of the plans' hinges, a firm opinion that he would skip built-in water tanks altogether if building again (loose, clasped floors instead), and, after a season's sailing, a change of heart endorsing a drop-in rear-cabin-trunk washboard provided its sides are cut vertical (citing the Fastnet Race's washboard-loss lesson); the thread closes on a one-line joke from Nels. A new "Seating Position in Paradox" thread opens: Nels flags a reclined interior seating position (visible in photos of Glen Maxwell's "Zoë") as a likely source of back pain, floating raised hatch sides, a forward window, and a more upright "mini pilothouse" idea; Alastair replies with a firsthand headroom figure (35.5 in sitting bolt upright at 5'6"), recommends lowering the sole over raising the roof, notes the raised-hatch-sides idea was already explored and not adopted on "Enuf," and floats armrests and an inflatable pool chair as further comfort ideas alongside his already-documented flip-up-backrest mod. No new pages created — all six correspondents (Nels, Glen Maxwell, Bill Serjeant, Alastair, "Jeff UK", William Nix quoted only) are established identities folded into existing pages. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 481 — pages 4801-4810)

One day, 21-22 January 2008, closing the "Cabin Sole" thread opened at batch 480's end, continuing the "There are a lot of dingos about" VE-SCULL sub-thread, and running the "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" megathread into a new, richly detailed scaling sub-topic. Alastair answers Pete Martin's water-tank/hinge/washboard question with firsthand experience: built the tanks to plan rather than converting them to storage since hatch covers can always be cut in later, has since replaced leaked water ballast with lead while keeping the tank nearly sealed for flotation reserve, would return to water ballast with genuinely watertight (preferably metal) inspection hatches, reports the brass sole-plate hinge collects grit (a webbing hinge is the planned fix), and endorses a drop-in washboard provided it doesn't compromise the cabin's structure. On sculling: Ray, no better at conventional sculling himself, offers only the same "even a monkey can use it" reassurance about the VE-SCULL oar; Alastair adds a historical correction — the slalom stroke is the conventional Western technique, the falling-leaf stroke historically the oriental one. The scaling sub-thread is the batch's centerpiece: "Jeff UK" relays a pre-worked 20% Paradox scale-up already sitting in the group's Files section (full dimensions, sail area, displacement, plus a smaller 1.16-factor alternative), prefaced by the document's own claim that Matt Layden has blessed a scaled-up version since no new plans are needed; Nels independently corroborates Matt's approval and notes roughly forty members have floated the same idea over the years without anyone actually building and reporting one back, encouraging William Nix to be the pioneer; Bill Serjeant thanks Jeff, speculates (unconfirmed) that Matt is too busy building and sea-trialing his own next boat for what he calls the "Around in Ten Race" to draw and market plans, and proposes "ParadoxPlus"/"ParadoxToo"/"ParadoxForTwo" as names; James (howgegoboat) prefers "Paradox Max," corrects the worked length for plywood curvature (~15'9" rather than 16'6"), floats a 4 ft-chine/5 ft-deck beam-widening alternative, works out pop-bottle ballast weight, judges Enigma 460 too narrow in the beam, and adds personal color about the ferro-cement "sea tramp" that first got him into sailing; William Nix compares the 10% and 20% steps to Little Cruiser's own size and asks other owners' height and weight, giving his own as 6'4"/270 lb; Nels answers with Glen Maxwell's own figures (5'9", 185 lb, solo sailor) and explains his own choice of a Bolger Micro over Paradox for sailing with crew; William Nix closes the batch weighing a secondhand Micro purchase, planning to keep a Paradox for himself and use the Micro to take others along. No new pages created (Ray remains an established below-threshold non-Paradox commentator; James and William Nix remain below the person-page bar, tracked via WATCHLIST). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 480 — pages 4791-4800)

One day, 21 January 2008, continuing both threads left open at batch 479's end: "There are a lot of dingos about" (the yuloh/VE-SCULL technical sub-thread) and "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" (the design-identity megathread), plus a new "Cabin Sole" thread opened at the batch's close. On the yuloh side: Alastair questions whether a Japanese "VE-SCULL" sculling-oar design is really anything new, or just an oar built for the slalom stroke rather than the falling-leaf stroke a yuloh uses; "Jeff UK" replies that the design avoids stalling the leading edge on the return stroke ("they taught a monkey to use it") and floats a tip-vortex-reducing fence for the blade, alongside further NOLI storage details (under-shelf hooks, sole-lacing eye hooks), a bins-removal suggestion for larger sailors, and an open question about fitting his Brompton folding bicycle forward; Alastair's fuller technical reply holds that stalling is a function of foil section, angle of attack, and stroke speed, recounts varying "Little Jim"'s yuloh-shaft angle by maneuver, suggests the fence would need to be adjustable, and credits the slalom blade with one genuine advantage — usable as a rudder when freewheeling, for quicker turns. On the identity debate: Ray (rhaldridge) presses the "who is allowed to change a design and keep its name" question via the West Wight Potter, and sharply disputes Don Elliott's absolutist anti-modification stance as reflecting a refusal to accept differing views; Perry Phillips answers with Potter manufacturing history — Herb Stewart's uncredited construction/rig changes to the US-built Potter, and his wholly separate Potter 19 design, neither of which "bothered" the original designer; "Jeff UK" adds a second, partly divergent account of the same Potter/Stan-Smith history (an HM Marine mould exchange, royalties unpaid, a Stan-Smith-designed 18 ft derivative). Separately, William Nix ("Bill" — the same `wyliewesty` identity established under "Wayne," confirming the archive's standing "multiple Bills" caution) asks what would be wrong with a Paradox built 10-20% larger while keeping every internal detail, having held plans for years but been put off by past negative reaction to modification; Perry Phillips reassures him that fit-driven scaling is a different matter from customizing the interior or rig, and that a builder who wants to live and sail as Matt Layden did should still follow the plans closely otherwise. The batch closes with Pete Martin opening "Cabin Sole": weighing the built-in water tanks (having collected enough 2-litre pop bottles for over a week's supply) against using the space for storage, favoring a single rigid forward-sole panel, asking whether Faith's own removable side-plates depart from the plans' continuous brass hinge, and still tempted by a Little-Cruiser/NOLI-style drop-in washboard at the rear of the cabin trunk — unanswered as the batch ends. No new pages created; all correspondents are either established page-having identities or de-attributed per the topic-page convention. Files touched, all count==numeric-max unless a pre-existing anomaly is noted: `paradox.md` gained a new Overview paragraph extending the modification-identity debate with the Potter precedent — citations [12]-[14] (count 14/max 14, up from 11/11); `design/variants/enlarging-for-a-large-sailor.md` gained a new "A tall sailor's hesitation, and reassurance that fit-driven scaling is welcome" section — citations [18]-[19] (count 19/max 19, up from 17/17); `operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md` gained a new "Slalom-stroke sculling oars, and whether they improve on the yuloh's falling-leaf stroke" section — citations [37]-[39] (count 39/max 39, up from 36/36); `people/jeff-doryman.md` gained a new "A yuloh fence idea, and further NOLI storage and headroom details" section — citation [110] (count 111, numeric-max 110 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [39a], up from count 110/numeric-max 109); `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Water tanks reconsidered, and a rear-sole-plate question" section — citation [87] (count 87/max 87, up from 86/86); `construction/water-tank-lining.md` gained a new sentence extending "Not using the built-in tanks at all: individual containers instead" — citation [43] (count 43/max 43, up from 42/42); `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new cross-link section, "A water-tank and rear-sole-plate question" — citation [51] (count 51/max 51, up from 50/50). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 479 — pages 4781-4790)

Two days, 20-21 January 2008, continuing directly from batch 478's two threads: a further round of the "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" design-philosophy megathread, and a further round of "Charcoal Stove" (plus its offshoot, "Brick Heater"). The dingos thread works through a concrete test case — does a boat that changes its rig cease to be the boat it was built as? Perry Phillips argues no, drawing on his own West Wight Potter, twice converted to different rigs and still a Potter both times, and that personalizing a design does not show a lack of appreciation for the original; John Pertalion disputes the analogy — an undisclosed rig conversion would mislead a buyer, and most people who set out to heavily modify a boat learn it would have been cheaper to buy or build the boat they actually needed; Bill Serjeant replies point by point, restating that a rig change is a real design change even where it suits its owner better, and that his view Paradox doesn't suit a large sailor is personal opinion, not an obligation on large sailors; Perry closes the thread noting Matt Layden has himself redesigned the sail plan for at least one owner without that boat ceasing to be a Paradox, and citing David Beard's distinctive drop board on "Whisper" as a further example of a personalized boat that remains a Paradox — concluding that modifications which don't destroy a boat's balance shouldn't be derided, with the real caution reserved for uninformed newcomers. Separately, "Jeff UK" answers Alastair's standing "does Noli have a launch date?" question — NOLI is weather-stalled but still hoped for a June launch — and adds a Japanese "VE-SCULL" laminar-flow sculling-oar alternative under study, restates his own lower-sole/no-roof-foam headroom technique with a new roof-lamination detail, gives NOLI's water-storage arrangement (32 litres in 2-litre bottles under the sole), and asks Paradox owners generally how much of the design's storage space they actually use. On cabin heat: Stephen Yahn floats a heated-soapstone/brick heat-retainer box; Jeff Michals-Brown gives a thermodynamic case against candle heaters and recommends a hot-water-bottle-in-a-sleeping-bag technique credited to Charles Stock, plus his own planned kerosene wick stove; Nels endorses the hot-water-bottle idea and adds a thermos-of-hot-beverage technique; Neil Barratt proposes insulation and draught exclusion as an alternative to any heater, citing a Bris Sofia's 18-20°C cabin on body heat alone; Alastair closes the thread confirming his own towel-in-the-vent trick and that he has never felt the need for a heater at all. No new pages created; all seven correspondents are established identities folded into existing pages. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 478 — pages 4771-4780)

One day, 19-20 January 2008, running two threads: a nine-message design-philosophy megathread ("There are a lot of dingos about" / "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox") debating whether and how far to modify the stock design, and a one-message continuation of the "Charcoal Stove" thread. Alastair opens by pointing to a Duckworks article on modifying a design and recounting his own restraint, glad in hindsight every change he once considered would have made his boat worse; Perry Phillips counters that established designers routinely offer variations on one hull and that never considering alternatives risks "an almost cult following," citing Matt Layden's own migration toward Enigma as evidence the designer keeps experimenting; Jeff Michals-Brown stakes a middle ground, wanting to cruise two aboard without much added displacement and being too tall for the stock cabin, but planning to keep any modification small and non-structural; Nels recommends Jim Michalak's "Caroline" — a design aimed at off-shore coastal cruising — as an off-the-shelf alternative to modifying Paradox, having ordered her plans himself; Alastair clarifies he has no argument with *informed* modification, approving of a fellow builder's "Noli" as an example, and asks (unanswered) whether Noli has a launch date yet. Bill Serjeant then opens "Considering Dingos and Modifying Paradox" with an essay arguing Paradox fits a sailor of Matt Layden's own lithe build "like a comfortable slipper" but a taller, fatter, or less agile sailor "like a bunion in an iron shoe" and is better served by a different design (again pointing to Michalak's Caroline) than by modifying this one; he separately judges the lack of a designed-in engine Paradox's one real shortcoming for confined-water maneuvering, recalling Honda 2.3 outboards mounted on the port quarter (prone to snagging the sheet) and a port-side electric outboard that is workable but not instantaneous to set up. James (howgegoboat) asks about oars and argues that if Matt Layden's own move from Paradox to Enigma counts as improvement, some modification should be allowed; Alastair answers with an untried concept — standing, forward-facing rowing through oarlock brackets fitted to the open hatch — and recalls deliberately fitting no motor to "Little Jim" in her first season to force himself to learn to sail and scull, later adding a small trolling motor used only once sail and yuloh fail; Bill offers an alternative oar-port/rowlock siting and, in a fuller Paradox-versus-Enigma comparison, agrees with Don Elliott that a sufficiently altered boat is properly a "Paradox derivative," not a Paradox. Separately, Dave Raftery continues "Charcoal Stove" with two published candle-heater designs (stacked clay flower pots; tin cans) and a magazine-article link. No new pages created (Perry Phillips and Jeff Michals-Brown both remain below the person-page bar, citation-only). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 477 — pages 4761-4770)

Two days, 17 and 19 January 2008, continuing and closing the "Charcoal Stove" thread opened in batch 476. On 17 January: Jeri Peters, a Southeast Alaska liveaboard of twenty-five years, describes a wood-burning cabin stove her husband built from welded six-inch steel pipe with a hinged firebox door cut with a drill and a metal-cutting jigsaw blade, and warns off charcoal for its smell, fumes, and dust; Alastair (openboat) warns not to slice off a chimney when opening the hatch, then reports his own coldest night aboard (about -2°C) was comfortable in a sleeping bag alone, with a boiled mug of coffee warming the cabin enough to dress in, and judges the boat's ventilation ample for a single burner stove even hatch-shut; James "Jim" Hall (flexwing462) suggests a small kerosene lamp is enough and jokes that burning charcoal leaves grey dust "ideal for the boat proud owner"; Stephen Yahn floats fattening and hollowing one of the four cabin posts into an insulated chimney. On 19 January: James (howgegoboat) proposes converting a pot-bellied cast-iron barbecue into a cabin heater across three messages (dimensions, a narrower-flued two-door variant, and a specific Landmann-branded product link), and separately asks what the Paradox design's main constituent elements are, offering his own five-point list; Nels asks the barbecue's diameter and offers a candle-lamp heat alternative; Alastair closes the thread answering James's design-elements question by reframing it as "why," not "what" — the design solves a specific set of problems, so the lug rig (mechanically simple but conceptually inseparable from the rest) would likely produce "a pig's ear" fitted to a different hull, and restates the design's real purpose as carrying the crew in comfort and safety, in shallow water, in relatively poor weather, without needing to do anything energetic, and just as capably in deep water and good weather. No new pages created. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 476 — pages 4751-4760)

Thirteen days, 4-17 January 2008, closing the "Plans for Paradox" FAQ recurrence left open at batch 475's end, continuing and closing the "trailer weight" thread with a more detailed follow-up exchange, then running a new "Charcoal Stove" thread start to finish. Alastair answers waypoint115's plans-FAQ question directly — plans from Dave Bolduc, about $40 the previous year, via microcruising.com — closing the recurrence. The "trailer weight" thread continues: Jeff Michals-Brown, unsatisfied with a trailer-only figure, asks a sharper question — what a Paradox weighs rigged and loaded to the design waterline; John Gilbert ("JG") answers in detail — 1410 lb design displacement, about 1210 lb towed fully loaded except for the skipper, about 1060 lb with the water tanks also emptied, and recalls Matt Layden swapping ballast for stores on a long cruise; Alastair follows up, endorsing John Gilbert's rundown and restating in sailing-displacement terms — roughly 1400 lb sailing, of which about 400 lb is typically lead ballast, with little sensitivity to the exact figure in practice. A new "Charcoal Stove" thread then runs in full: Stephen Yahn asks after a one-gallon-can charcoal stove design with an airtight chimney; Paul Delory points to Triloboat's "Lenihan Charcoal Cabin Heater"; James "Jim" Hall recalls a commercial charcoal stove called "the Pansy"; George links a ybw.com forum thread's photo and discussion of a small heater; Stephen Yahn thanks everyone and settles on a kerosene/paraffin lamp-based heater instead — a metal can as heat dissipater and chimney, with a lantern's burner/wick assembly in its base; Jeff Michals-Brown closes the thread weighing cabin heat generally — the usual lazarette cook-stove/transom-vent setup, the poisonous-emissions risk, and the soot-free-flame/chimney-balance requirements of a kerosene heater. No new pages created. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 475 — pages 4741-4750)

Seventeen days, 17 December 2007 - 3 January 2008, closing the "Basic interior dimensions?" thread with a thank-you reply, then running four short new threads: "Sail Cleaning," "Pictures from Taiwan," "trailer weight," and a fresh one-off "Plans for Paradox" FAQ recurrence. Dennis Mingear closes the interior-dimensions thread, quoting "Jeff UK"'s NOLI-plans-policy reversal in full and thanking him as "a recent Paradox plans owner," calling future NOLI plans a welcome New Year's gift whenever they arrive — "Jeff UK" sent no new message of his own this batch, this being only a repost of his already-documented msgnum-4778 reply. David Beard opens "Sail Cleaning" asking how to clean a white Dacron sail; Perry Phillips, a non-Paradox-owning West Wight Potter sailor from Monroeville, Alabama, answers with Clorox Cleanup for stains and a soft-brush-and-soapy-water routine otherwise. "Pictures from Taiwan" has gilberj55 ("JG") praise Jimmy Lu's build photos; Pete Martin replies that Jimmy's build is "coming on great," posts a link to Jimmy's own build blog, and reports his own JoHanna build has "ground to a standstill" for the winter, cold and dark, hoping to resume in spring; Bill Serjeant congratulates both nearly-finished builders as "2 more water babies in the making to be born in 2008"; Jimmy Lu thanks the group and hopes to finish "within these two months." A new "trailer weight" thread has Jeff Michals-Brown, alarmed that Paradox's stated 1,410 lb displacement is at first glance twice his own 20-footer's weight, ask whether his 1,500 lb trailer could handle a loaded Paradox; Alastair answers with a practical calculation — starting from the ~1,400 lb loaded/ballasted figure and subtracting the skipper, water ballast, and other easily-removed gear brings the real trailer load down to around 1,000 lb, the figure his own trailer was built around. The batch closes with a fresh, unanswered recurrence of the plans FAQ: a new correspondent, "waypoint115," asks directly where to buy Paradox plans and their cost. No new pages created. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 474 — pages 4731-4740)

Sixteen days, 1-17 December 2007, closing out the "Autopilot setup" thread and running a new "Basic interior dimensions?" thread start to finish. The autopilot thread closes with one further exchange: Stephen Yahn asks whether a Tillermaster could connect directly to the tiller instead of the steering lines; Glen Maxwell answers no — not enough athwartship room on "Zoë," and it would be inconvenient to reach. The "Basic interior dimensions?" thread then runs in full: a newcomer, Jeff Michals-Brown, considering a Paradox for his next boat, asks for the stock cabin sole's length and width, whether removing the side bins would meaningfully widen it, headroom, and the trade-offs of a 20% enlargement, wanting to cruise occasionally with his son; Alan points him to the group's "Study Plans" files; "Jeff UK" answers with NOLI's own dimensions (1900 mm sleeping length, 920 mm between the bins at frame 3, matching stock) and a frame-2/frame-3 modification technique for extra sleeping room, judging two aboard better solved by camping ashore than by both sleeping in the cabin — in contrast to Matt Layden, whose own slight build let him and a partner sleep aboard together; Stephen Yahn reports his own already-documented widened-bulkhead-3 modification on "Ironsides" now yields a concrete 41 in cabin sole width; Jeff Michals-Brown thanks all three and, impressed by NOLI (a Paradox/Enigma hybrid), asks to learn more about her; Alastair answers the headroom question (930 mm floor-to-closed-hatch on "Little Jim") and separately doubts many builders actually sit at a removable rear window while sailing, preferring the seat and its backrest, recommending dabbling bare feet in the water over the transom instead. The batch closes with "Jeff UK"'s fullest NOLI-plans-policy statement on record: reversing an earlier decision not to publish, he will release his own drawings free once NOLI is launched and performing well, though builders would still need to separately buy Matt Layden's own Paradox drawings from Dave and Mindy; he credits Matt's years of model/prototype research with bringing his latest design to the drawing board together with "Sven." A short, unanswered "Sail" thread has Stephen Yahn ask whether a Paradox burgee or sail insignia exists. No new pages created. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 473 — pages 4721-4730)

Five days, 17-21 November 2007, closing out the "Vane gear" thread and running a new "Autopilot setup" thread to a close, plus a short car-topping and Enigma-460 recurrence in between. Glen Maxwell promises photographs of his Autohelm-to-tiller-line setup; Bill Serjeant thanks him; Glen posts them to a new "Zoë's Autopilot Setup" files folder. The photos draw praise as a clever, simple system worth duplicating on a steering-line rudder generally (a non-Paradox correspondent building his own catamaran floats duplicating it and using the same rolling hitches as a tiller lock); Alastair spots a third working tiller-lock design in the photos — a single knob that clamps the rope when screwed down and releases with a yank; Bill closes by noting he'll need to mount the pilot to port rather than starboard, since his compass already occupies the starboard side, and Glen agrees port might if anything be the easier side. In between, "James" (howgegoboat) returns to the car-topping thread with concrete refined dimensions (five 1/4 in ply sheets, four 20-litre ballast containers, a cloth top, 2 ft hull depth, 15 ft length, 5 ft beam, a cut-down dinghy mainsail as a standing lug on an 11 ft x 3 in softwood mast, dropping the inside rudder/furling/snorkel tube to save weight) — quoting Jeff UK's already-documented "PARADOX sized ENIGMA" reply in full alongside it. A separate short Enigma-460 recurrence has Dave Raftery (author of the external Enigma wiki already cited throughout `matt-layden/enigma.md`, making his first archive-list appearance) restate the 260 lb/110 lb ballast comparison against the original 180 lb Enigma on-list, and mention hoping to build one himself someday with a small utility trailer; James asks, unanswered, why the 4.6 m Enigma wasn't given a wider 4 ft beam at the chine for speed/planing, and notes her higher-aspect chine wings and working spray dodger. No new pages created.
