## 2026-07-19 (batch 472 — pages 4711-4720)

Nineteen days, 28 October - 16 November 2007, closing the "Car topping?" thread with one further reply, then running a new "Vane gear" thread — a second wave of windvane self-steering discussion — start to finish. "James" (howgegoboat) replies to the car-topping thread with his own variant: thick flexible polythene cabin windows so the whole cabin top could pop up for car-top carrying, sized to a Vauxhall Zafira MPV, built of 1/4 in ply with plastic water containers (80 L) for ballast and reusing his own boat "Summer Breeze"'s gaff rig — floated for trial on "Summer Breeze" first, before adapting it to a Paradox/Enigma hybrid, comparing it to "Day Dreamer," a 15 ft "summer breeze" type with higher freeboard, decked over. The "Vane gear" thread then reopens the windvane question after a summer's quiet: Bill Serjeant asks whether Glen is around and satisfied with his own gear, and what electronic gear he uses; Alastair wonders whether a vane could drive a small, independent auxiliary rudder, possibly bow-mounted given the open foredeck space; Bill judges a bow mount risks sail interference, citing Robin Knox-Johnston's twin bow vanes on "Suhaili" as a complicating precedent; Glen replies that his wife's worsening illness now needs full-time care, cutting into his building and sailing time, that renewed light-air vane testing went poorly (blamed on an undersized blade, to be enlarged), and describes his Autohelm 800's tiller-line coupling in full — a washer-and-rolling-hitch adapter, cradle-mounted on the aft locker, good for short trips but not more than about three days; James passes along a link to a simple horizontal-axis vane design; Stephen Yahn distinguishes vertical- from horizontal-axis vanes' boom-collision behavior and recommends flexible stunt-kite fiberglass rods, citing two published references; Glen thanks him briefly; Bill recalls a similar commercial vane, the QME (Quantock Marine Enterprises, Highbridge, West Country), once fitted to his own Hunter 19, cheap but reliable only on an exceptionally well-balanced boat, liable to be snagged by Paradox's sail and sheet unless mounted well astern; Bill closes by judging himself edging toward an electronic system for its simplicity, given he plans no long ocean passages, though a perfected vane would still tempt him to copy it. No new pages created. Files touched, all count==numeric-max: `operations/self-steering.md` gained five new sections ("An independent, vane-driven auxiliary rudder, and a bow-mounted alternative," "Renewed testing: an undersized vane blade, and a rolling-hitch tillerpilot adapter," "Boom interference, vane-blade materials, and a commercial precedent: the QME," "Weighing an electronic system's simplicity against a vane, revisited") — citations [70]-[78] (count 78/78==max, up from 69/69); `design/variants/car-topping-conversion-idea.md` gained a new "A flexible-window pop-up roof, tested on an existing boat first" section — citation [7] (count 7/7==max, up from 6/6); `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "A November 2007 windvane catch-up" section — citations [85]-[88] (count 88/88==max, up from 84/84); `people/alastair-law.md` gained a new bullet in "Rig, electrics, and furling" — citation [376] (count 376/376==max, up from 375/375); `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a new "Windvane-boom interference, and flexible vane materials" section — citation [58] (count 58/58==max, up from 57/57); `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` gained a new "Continued vane refinement, and a family health setback" section — citation [273] (count 273/273==max, up from 272/272). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 471 — pages 4701-4710)

Two days, 26-28 October 2007, continuing directly from batch 470's "ballast placement" thread and closing it, then running a short "Paradox cruising" thread to a close. The ballast thread's new turns: Jim Hall, replying to Peter (`myss1400`, Auckland NZ), floats replacing internal lead pigs with a large sheet of galvanised steel fixed to the entire underside of the bottom plywood — freeing interior space, adding abrasion protection, and, with lead prices high, potentially saving both material cost and epoxy — and asks about calculating the needed thickness or whether the lower placement earns a righting-moment "discount"; Alastair rebuts from lived "Little Jim" experience — ballast has to be shifted fore-and-aft for trim and removed as gear (an anchor, a battery) is added, flexibility a fixed sheet would not allow; Jim Hall concedes the practical point but still sees merit if some ballast is left free to trim, and asks a new, unanswered question about extra inertia near the bow and stern; John Gilbert ("JG") gives a physics explanation distinguishing fore-and-aft ballast spread (reduces pitch) from athwartship spread (reduces roll, as in Paradox), floating a hypothesis that ballast placement — not just the chine runners — may contribute to Paradox's low-roll reputation, illustrated with a figure-skater/radius-of-gyration analogy; Peter thanks the thread and raises a new, unanswered compass-interference question; Bill Serjeant closes the thread with the authoritative note that the plans' longitudinal ballast centering is correct as drawn for a sea-kindly boat, and that Faith's ballast (including centred water ballast) has needed no adjustment. The "Paradox cruising" thread closes a loop opened in batch 470's era and narrated in full on `boats/faith.md`: Alastair announces the joint Falmouth-to-Scilly-Isles trip report, written for the Dinghy Cruising Association's bulletin, is now published and posted as "Trip 5" on his own website; Bill Serjeant compliments the account and its colour photographs and hopes for another attempt at the Scillies next year; "Jeff UK" thanks Al, praising both Al's and Bill's cruise stories alongside Dave and Mindy (Bolduc), Matt (Layden), and Glen (Maxwell); Jim Hall thanks both authors, noting that reading the two accounts alongside a map or chart "brings it to life." No new pages created. `design/weight-and-ballast.md` gained a new paragraph extending "Ballast placement: outboard vs. centered" and a new closing paragraph on "A steel hull bottom as combined ballast and impact protection (speculative)" — citations [158]-[163] (count 166, numeric-max 163 complete plus three pre-existing letter-suffixes [53a][53b][152a], up from count 160/numeric-max 157). `chine-runners/how-they-work.md` gained a one-line cross-link on "Reduced rolling" (no citation change). `boats/faith.md` gained a new "The full cruise account published in the Dinghy Cruising Association bulletin" section — citations [222]-[225] (count 227, numeric-max 225 complete plus two pre-existing letter-suffixes [133a][191a], up from count 223/numeric-max 221). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "Ballast placement, and the published cruise account" section — citations [83]-[84] (count 84/84==max, up from 82/82). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 470 — pages 4691-4700)

Three weeks, 5-26 October 2007, closing the "paradoxical" chain-e-mail thread with a thank-you reply, then running the "Car topping paradox?" thread start to finish and closing with a fresh "ballast placement" recurrence. Bill Serjeant opens with a two-line thank-you for the prior batch's forwarded "Paradoxical Commandments" chain e-mail — off-topic, correctly left uncited, closing that thread. The "Car topping paradox?" thread then runs the full week: a newcomer, "James," proposes a lighter Paradox/Enigma hybrid whose cabin sides and roof bolt off the hull and invert inside it for stowage, car-topable on a small car; a one-line joke reply (an "Atlas"/armoured-personnel-carrier crack) is correctly left uncited; a new correspondent, "aeroboar," points to Matt Layden's brother's website for a photograph of Enigma already lashed to a Ford Focus's roof — a page that, read in full, turns out to carry **Matt Layden's own first-person account** of Enigma's Cedar Key maiden trials and her first-ever car-topping trip (130 lb empty against the car's 220 lb rated roof load, single-handed loading with a temporary roof-bar extension, 300 highway miles with no handling trouble), newly added to the tree; "Jeff UK" (the established `people/jeff-doryman.md` correspondent) answers with a removable-hardtop concept of his own for a car-topped Enigma-type boat, and James follows up with fastening/glazing refinements (thin ply, bolts-and-wingnuts-and-sealant, or hinged perspex-and-canvas); "Jeff UK" then restates his own build, NOLI, as effectively "a PARADOX sized ENIGMA" built to Enigma's own scantlings, her draught cut to 7 in and computer-tweaked to shave a third of stock PARADOX's weight, possibly car-topable herself; a new correspondent, "DT" in Spokane WA (the same `ddddtodd` account as batch 469's "Don"), argues for a small trailer over car-topping given highway windage, floating the idea of sleeping in the boat itself en route. The batch closes with a fresh "ballast placement" question from a new correspondent, Peter in Auckland NZ, puzzled why lead ballast sits outboard rather than on the centreline — answered by Alastair with the same static/dynamic (moment-of-inertia) explanation already on record elsewhere in the tree, restated with a two-lead-bricks illustration in place of the existing dowel one. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 469 — pages 4681-4690)

Three weeks, 14 September - 5 October 2007, closing the long-running "plans FAQ" and "Chine runners" threads with a scatter of short exchanges, then two off-topic asides. D.R. Bowers thanks Alan for the earlier FAQ-folder pointer, closing that recurrence. Jack Gardiner, replying to Pete Martin within "Chine runners," gives his fullest technical case for 12 oz biaxial cloth over the plans' woven roving — recalling (by memory, not certain) that biax is roughly twelve times stronger for the weight, reasoning a triangle-oriented weave beats a plain box weave, repeating his standing 19 ft power-boat durability account, and adding two new facts: biax is believed not to have been available yet when Matt Layden built the original Paradox, and Paradox's light weight and low speed mean a grounding on a rock tends to let the hull simply move rather than hole or take heavy damage, with real danger instead coming from being struck by something itself carrying speed and mass. Cary asks whether the "Hogamus" and "Jubilee" designs use chine runners or a keel/centerboard and whether they relate to Paradox — unanswered in this batch, and Jubilee's own chine-runner design was already documented via a prior Bill Serjeant question, so no new fact resulted. Bill Serjeant separately thanks David Beard for close-up photographs of his Honda outboard (given in this message as a 2.3 hp model, against the "2 hp" figure established everywhere else in the archive for the same boat) sent to help Bill decide whether to fit the same setup to "Faith," published on the smallsailboats.co.uk Editorial page. A new correspondent, "Don" in Spokane, WA (6 ft/225 lb), reopens the plans FAQ together with a sizing question — whether Paradox has been stretched longer and wider for a bigger sailor; Bill Serjeant points him to his own site and the group's Files/discussion archive, suggesting he practice Paradox's building methods first; a second reply points him to Matt Layden's Enigma instead, already built as a 15 ft prototype by another builder, with no new facts beyond what the tree already documents about that build. The batch closes with two fully off-topic messages, correctly left uncited per standing pruning practice: Pete Martin's tabloid story about Yorkshire "paradox"-tagged stone sculptures, with Bill Serjeant's reply on Chi Rho/Pax Cross etymology, and a forwarded "Paradoxical Commandments" chain e-mail from a new correspondent, Aaron Edelman, whose only Paradox connection is the title's wordplay. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 468 — pages 4671-4680)

Eight days, 6-14 September 2007, closing out the "Chine runners" thread and running three short new ones. Alastair confirms, replying to Jim Baltaxe, that the plans already call for the bottom sheet to be wider than the sides — the "shelf" a builder might otherwise think is a modification is standard practice; George points to a composite build's own "ParaPlastic" photos in the files section for a worked example of a foam-fibre chine runner, having lost track of more recent links. Pete Martin thanks the group and Jack Gardiner in particular for the block-built method, plans to use his own scrap timber offcuts the same way, and asks whether a single layer of 12 oz biax on the bottom (as Jack used) is enough against his own planned 600 gsm biax, noting Matt Layden's plans specify three layers of 24 oz woven roving; the same day he reports joining JoHanna's bottom panels by scarfing them outdoors for lack of space, breaking two power-planer blades and wearing down his hand planes on the Far Eastern marine ply — nicknamed "the Beast from the East" — and expects to fit the bottom the following weekend. Bill Serjeant's published appraisal of "Faith" draws a reply from Pete Martin wondering whether flying the sail from the starboard side of the offset mast, rather than port, would be more symmetrical and reduce missed stays tacking from starboard to port; Alastair answers that the stock port-side arrangement isn't arbitrary — moving the sail further from the cabin edge would need a longer tack strut to support the boom when reefed, raising the boom and requiring either a taller mast (too tall to stow inside) or a smaller sail — and adds that his own GPS shows him consistently faster on the port tack, though which tack points closer to the wind is harder to judge. A short "Comfortable seating at last" thread has Stephen Yahn compliment Alastair's seat-to-backrest solution as "simplicity and genius"; Alastair explains it came about almost by luck, after a couple of years of trying, when a cushion got caught between the seat and the bulkhead — one of many small details he credits to Matt Layden's design. Answering Jeri Payton's "Cost of a Used Paradox?" question from a prior batch, Bill Serjeant adds a UK-specific figure: factoring in labour, Paradox is a genuinely pricey boat to buy, with UK materials alone running to about £4,000 ($8,000) and a trailer adding roughly £1,500 ($3,000) new or £600 ($1,200) secondhand. The batch closes with the recurring "plans FAQ": a new correspondent, D.R. Bowers, asks where to get a set of Paradox plans, and Alan (the established New Zealand watch-list identity) points him to the group's Files section FAQ folder. No new pages created. `chine-runners/construction.md` gained two new sentences extending "A closed-cell foam alternative, and a 2007 durability exchange" — citations [63]-[64] (count 65, numeric-max 64 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [49a], up from count 63/numeric-max 62). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Scarfing the bottom panels" section — citation [81] (count 81/81==max, up from 80/80). `components/rig/index.md` gained a new "Why the sail isn't flown from the starboard side instead" section — citations [265]-[266] (count 268, numeric-max 266 complete plus 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes [7a],[7b], up from count 266/numeric-max 264). `boats/faith.md` gained a sentence extending "A published appraisal of Faith" — citation [221] (count 223, numeric-max 221 complete plus 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes [133a],[191a], up from count 222/numeric-max 220). `components/interior.md` gained a new paragraph extending "A flip-up backrest at bulkhead 4" — citations [43]-[44] (count 44/44==max, up from 42/42). `operations/secondhand-market.md` gained a new sentence extending "Valuing a used Paradox in the abstract" — citation [48] (count 48/48==max, up from 47/47). `references/building-plans.md` gained a new "A September 2007 recurrence, and a pointer to the Files/FAQ folder" section — citations [167]-[168] (count 168/168==max, up from 166/166). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 467 — pages 4661-4670)

Six days, 1-6 September 2007, closing out the "scaling and modifying Paradox"/Mukti thread and running a dense new "Chine runners" thread start to finish. Neil Barratt closes the Mukti exchange with two short asides: wishing Mukti Mitchell well but noting he competes against many similar secondhand boats sold for a tenth the price, predicting a wave of used boats onto the UK market after a busy summer, and separately flagging a Bolger "Birdwatcher" for sale on eBay UK, its owner reportedly another Dinghy Cruising Association member. Bill Serjeant explains a gap in his own cruise-blogging: a week with the Dinghy Cruising Association at Cobnor went unlogged in real time over the cost of a mobile data card, with five of the daily logs posted afterward from home at no extra expense. The batch's real center of gravity is "Chine runners": Pete Martin proposes fitting the runner blank to the hull sides before the bottom panel goes on rather than after; Alastair agrees, adds that steaming the blank helps, and reconfirms cutting full-length pieces into foot-long sections on his own build; Jack Gardiner describes his own method — cardboard patterns tested station by station, then small blocks glued up like courses in a wall, hand-planed and sheathed in 2 oz glass, reasoning that scuffed moisture then only reaches one block rather than the whole run; new correspondent Jim Baltaxe (Wellington, NZ) proposes closed-cell foam in place of solid timber, with the bottom panel cut proud of the hull sides to form a fair-curved "shelf" for the runner's base; John Gilbert raises a grounding-durability concern about lighter construction; Jim Baltaxe ties his shelf idea directly to that concern; and Jack Gardiner rebuts from his own build's record of repeated groundings on beaches, dock pilings, and in surf with no damage, using a heavier 12 oz biaxial weave than the plans' specified roving. No new pages created — Jim Baltaxe is a thin, below-threshold new correspondent (no boat, two messages), and the batch's other correspondents are all previously established identities. `chine-runners/construction.md` gained three new sections ("Method 5 — Cardboard patterns and a block-built 'wall,'" "Fitting order: blank before or after the bottom panel," "A closed-cell foam alternative, and a 2007 durability exchange") — citations [56]-[62] (count 63, numeric-max 62 complete plus the one pre-existing letter-suffix [49a], up from count 56/numeric-max 55). `design/blue-water-suitability.md` gained a closing paragraph to "A round-Britain question, and whether Paradox is a dinghy or a micro-yacht" — citations [126]-[127] (count 64, numeric-max 127 with the standing pre-existing 58-120 gap unchanged, up from count 62/numeric-max 125). `boats/faith.md` gained a new "Cruising logs paused during a Dinghy Cruising Association week at Cobnor" section — citation [220] (count 222, numeric-max 220 complete plus 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes [133a],[191a], up from count 221/numeric-max 219). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Chine-runner fitting order" cross-link section — citation [80] (count 80/80==max, up from 79/79). `people/jack-gardiner.md` gained a new closing paragraph cross-linking his September 2007 chine-runner contributions — citations [218]-[219] (count 218, numeric-max 219 with the standing pre-existing single gap unchanged, up from count 216/numeric-max 217). `people/alastair-law.md` gained a new closing paragraph — citation [375] (count 375/375==max, up from 374/374). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 466 — pages 4651-4660)

One day, 29-30 August 2007, closing out the "Scaling and modifying paradox" thread and opening two short new ones. Bill Serjeant gives a firsthand man-overboard account: caught in the water off the foredeck without a buoyancy aid, waterlogged in two sweaters and an anorak, he found the rudder's built-in boarding step hard but usable to reboard, and suggests a rope loop dropped below the step to ease the climb — Dennis Mingear thanks him, calling it a great fit with the Paradox design philosophy. Bill separately announces an independent "Appraisal of a Paradox Sailboat," linked from the editorial page of `www.smallsailboats.co.uk`, covering his own "Faith." The scaling thread itself continues: "james" (howgegoboat) adds concrete figures to his stretched-Paradox proposal (100 kg ballast, 1/4 in minimum side thickness, a roughly £100 materials estimate, a cut-down secondhand dinghy mainsail) and asks in passing about a small explorer boat then sailing around the UK; Alastair tells him plainly to get the plans first. That explorer-boat aside grows into its own short exchange: James asks how the boat's accommodation compares with Paradox's; Alastair reports it secondhand as minute and cramped, unlikely to sell well at roughly £17,000, and gives the archive's clearest one-line characterization of how a Paradox actually sails — "not really the boat for those who enjoy sailing on the edge," since most of the time she sails herself with the skipper along for the ride; Bill identifies the sailor as Mukti Mitchell (Dinghy Cruising Association, `lowcarbonlifestyle.org`) and muses that Paradox, while no racer, should be capable of a round-Britain passage, wondering whether she is properly a dinghy at all or a micro-yacht. A separate one-off thread has Alastair describe a flip-up backrest cut into the seat support just forward of bulkhead 4, adding comfort to the "protected" sailing position without affecting its normal use; Dennis Mingear approves. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 465 — pages 4641-4650)

Completed after an interrupted prior attempt, which had already written two pieces of this batch's work before stopping: `operations/ground-tackle.md` citation [45] was extended with a `page_4644` cross-reference for Glen Maxwell's anchor answer being quoted in a follow-up (left untouched this pass), and `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md` gained citations [121]-[126] for the "Scaling and modifying paradox" thread's Alastair/Dennis Mingear exchange over pages 4641, 4642, 4643, 4646, 4648, 4650 (verified complete at count 126/max 126, left untouched this pass). The remaining, previously-unwritten content in this ten-page span falls into two threads. First, Pete Martin's "Bottom Fitting questions" (page_4644, answered on page_4647 by Alastair): with JoHanna's chine logs installed and bottom panels scarfed, Pete asked whether it's worth elevating the hull on trestles high enough to crawl underneath for pre-coating, clamp-pressure checks, and squeeze-out cleanup; whether chine runners fit more easily hull-up or hull-down; and how many layers of 600 gsm biax the bottom needs. Alastair answered all three from his own "Little Jim" build experience — yes to trestles (a car inspection trolley helps slide under), right-side-up for the runners since holding the compound bend in both planes proved impossible (cut into foot-long sections and jigsawed together instead), and that the layer count barely matters given four years of flinty-beach groundings around Poole and the Solent with no damage beyond scratched paint near bulkhead 3. Second, a one-off newcomer's blunt storm-conditions question (page_4645) drew Alastair's own tide-race account (page_4649): no deliberate storm-testing, but a navigational error once put him in a wind-against-tide race with solid water regularly breaking over the boat — an open boat would have swamped, he was seasick throughout, but the Paradox kept sailing and not one drop got inside, corroborating the wind-against-tide account already on record. `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md` gained a new "Fitting the chine runners hull-up or hull-down" subsection and extended "Elevating the hull to work underneath" — citations [61]-[63] (count 63/63==max, up from 60/60). `construction/hull-planking.md` gained a new paragraph extending "Woven roving versus biaxial cloth" — citations [40]-[41] (count 41/41==max, up from 39/39). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Bottom fitting questions" section — citations [78]-[79] (count 79/79==max, up from 77/77). `people/pete-martin.md` gained a matching cross-link "Bottom-fitting questions" section — citation [46] (count 46/46==max, up from 45/45). `operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md` gained a new "A navigational error into a tide race: swamping avoided" section — citations [49]-[50] (count 50/50==max, up from 48/48). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 464 — pages 4631-4640)

Two threads run 14-28 August 2007, opening directly from batch 463's close. The "Skin irritation" thread continues: Bill Serjeant suggests UK Epoxy Resins, the brand used throughout building "Faith" with no adverse reaction, and asks Ian Lang to send his details for the UK builders' registry page; Pete Martin reports no trouble with MAS epoxy on his own build and wonders whether a latex-glove allergy, not the epoxy, is to blame, separately noting JoHanna's sheer strakes installed that day with the hull due to be turned over within days; Jeri Payton (new correspondent) warns that epoxy sensitization is a real, sometimes permanent affliction and recommends barrier cream plus doubled-up surgical gloves; Stephen Yahn recommends Gorilla Glue as a faster-curing, differently-based alternative; Ian Lang follows up, having read his own latex-glove box's allergy warning, and switches to vinyl gloves to isolate the cause; Jim Baltaxe, a new New Zealand correspondent, recommends vinylester resin and describes a Derek Kelsall composite/vacuum-infusion workshop, floating the idea of a foam/glass Paradox for the challenge. A second, short thread has Stephen Yahn ask about acrylic-latex paint's durability on a boat deck, drawing a cautionary firsthand report from Perry Phillips of a friend's acrylic-latex deck paint bubbling and peeling off a sunfish in sheets. The batch closes with the archive's "Scaling and modifying paradox" thread: a new correspondent, "james" (howgegoboat), proposes his own widened/lengthened Paradox variant for coastal sailing with his son (5 ft beam, 15'6" length, water ballast in jugs, a widened cabin); Dennis Mingear (the WATCHLIST-tracked prospective builder) replies with a competing, proportional length-only stretch to about 18 ft using a 1.3x bulkhead-spacing factor, still with no boat name or construction of his own reported. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 463 — pages 4621-4630)

Nine days, 9-14 August 2007, across four intertwined threads. Stephen Yahn adds a wheat-flour/epoxy fill technique to his own grooved-block vent trunk drain from batch 462, closing out that thread. Robert Biegler, a new Norway-based owner, introduces himself: he bought Derek Clark's old Paradox "Enuf," shipped her to Trondheimsfjord, re-fixed her ballast, and built two yulohs (the first stolen) — closing the "new owner of Enuf" registry gap the UK registry had carried open since January 2007. He asks three questions at once — reefing friction despite two added blocks, lee helm/leeway on her first two short outings (140 kg ballast, no cruising stores), and where to stow an anchor with no bow roller — drawing three separate replies over the following days: Stephen Yahn (Matt Layden's simultaneous halyard-slip/reef-line-haul technique, not yet mastered), Bill Serjeant (reefing improves with practice and a properly-timed topping lift; lee helm cured by trimming with more ballast to sink the stem, treating the sail like a junk sail when sheeting; Faith's own bower/kedge anchor stowage forward in the cabin and lashed to the foredeck; a request to update "Enuf"'s registry entry), and Andrew Graham (a simple topping-lift arrangement; Zero's own 200 kg ballast/stores figure; a flush-recessed 3 kg sand anchor routed through a homemade hawse pipe). Separately, Pete Martin closes out his own vent-trunk-drain question from batch 462: drilled a 22 mm hole successfully (a heart-stopping moment losing then recovering the drill bit), and completed JoHanna's wet hull assembly, moving on to the sheer strakes. A third thread, "Cost of a Used Paradox?," runs in parallel: Jeri Payton (new, one-off) asks what a used Paradox is worth; Perry Phillips answers in the abstract (materials ~$2,500, price should reflect scarcity and rarity, ultimately worth whatever a buyer will pay); David Beard confirms he is still not selling "Whisper" — briefly weighed trading her for a Peep Hen, decided against it since he is building a second Paradox. The batch closes with Ian Lang reporting a painful skin rash on his arms, hands, and face from West System epoxy while laminating Irene May's curved deck beams, despite full PPE precautions — asking whether he needs a different brand or a proper mask. New page: `people/robert-biegler.md` (2 citations, count 2/2==max) — clears the person-page bar on an international relocation, concrete ballast/yuloh work, and three substantive technical threads; ## 2026-07-19 (batch 462 — pages 4611-4620)

Two intertwined threads run 6-8 August 2007, continuing directly from batch 461's close. The tinted-windows thread wraps up: Stephen Yahn confirms, once corrected, that Glen Maxwell was who he meant to ask, glad to hear the tint report and planning to do the same; Al Garrett Jr independently answers the same question with his own build's firsthand choice — tinted acrylic port lights and tinted safety glass for the hard-dodger side windows, with the windshield left clear, a combination he would not change if building again. A new "Vent Trunk Drain" thread opens: Pete Martin, sizing UK metric PVC tube against Matt Layden's 1 in nominal drain-pipe specification, worries his leftover 22 mm bathroom tube may prove too small if the vent trunk ever ships a load of green water, and asks whether the harder-to-drill 32 mm size is worth the trouble. Stephen Yahn explains the difference between US pipe sizing (a nominal 1 in pipe actually measures 1.315 in / 33.4 mm OD, varying by Schedule) and tube sizing; Larry Geib adds a fuller technical and historical explanation of pipe Schedule ratings, inside-versus-outside diameter conventions, and a Wikipedia reference chart. Bill Serjeant settles the practical question with firsthand experience: he used 22 mm on "Faith" himself, reasoning the vent trunk's small 120 mm deck opening limits how much water can enter at once, and reports the drain has coped fine through brief periods of force 6 with no waves breaking on deck. Stephen Yahn opens a related "Drilling vent box Drain" thread describing his own vent-box construction — glued wooden blocks with a groove cut for the pipe liner, the same copper tubing doing double duty as his crystal-radio ground. Pete Martin thanks everyone, confirms he will use his 22 mm tube (having already glued his blocks before hearing of the grooved-block method, though a router turned up unprompted the next day too late to use), and reports the hull glue-up is close at hand. Andrew Graham offers a third alternative for anyone in Pete's position — an already-glued vent trunk can be cut in half, routed for the drain passage, and glued back together, avoiding an expensive drill bit, the difficulty of drilling straight, and the risk of voids. A thin, one-off "Bill" (`ankle_deep2`, distinct from Bill Serjeant) also comments on Jimmy Lu's Taiwan-build photos, noting most display full-size when clicked but a few recent ones stay thumbnail-sized — a display quirk, folded into the existing Taiwan-build thread. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 461 — pages 4601-4610)

Three intertwined threads run 4-6 August 2007, continuing directly from batch 460's close. Jimmy Lu, the new Taiwan builder, asks Glen Maxwell what weight of anchor suits a Paradox (his reply-chain email trailing an off-topic quoted advertisement, pruned); Glen answers with his own practice on Zoë — two 7 lb Bruce-type anchors, each on 6 ft of chain. Jimmy thanks him and asks a follow-up: is foam flotation needed on the side wall and top deck? Glen explains he left "Zoë" unfoamed there specifically to keep the hull visible for moisture inspection, later worked out a door-skin removable-panel method that gives both foam and inspectability, and separately mentions being unable to relocate an article he remembers writing about painting the hull. Bill Serjeant reassures Pete Martin (whose "Another construction question" closed batch 460) that his dry-fit chine waviness will pull fair once the chine logs go on — fitting them on a hot day helps, and laminating each log from two thinner parts makes the bending considerably easier. Paul Maynard, a new one-off correspondent, asks Bill how "Faith" compares with his earlier 14 ft Bermudan-sloop dinghy "Harriott"; Bill answers with a detailed statistics-and-verdict comparison — length, beam, displacement, ballast, draught, sail area, and heel angle, Paradox's shelter/stability/stowage advantages against Roamer/Harriott's windward speed and two-up-sailing advantages, and his settled view of Paradox as "a single-handed miniature yacht rather than a dinghy." The batch closes with Stephen Yahn misaddressing a tinted-window question to "Gary" instead of Glen, correcting himself minutes later; Glen answers that Zoë's tint is barely noticeable from inside even at night, with visibility differing mainly when the sun is directly on the glazing. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 460 — pages 4591-4600)

Three intertwined threads run 24 July - 3 August 2007, continuing directly from batch 459's close. Bill Serjeant adds a new correspondent, **Jimmy Lu** (already flagged by Pete Martin at the end of batch 459), to the UK owners/builders registry at `smallsailboats.co.uk/paradox/names.htm`, and separately announces the site's been updated with new Faith photos and video from the West Country cruise, credited to Al Law. Pete Martin replies praising the video ("Brilliant!") and asking the windspeed shown; Bill estimates Force 2-3 Beaufort from the state of the water and explains why Paradox tracks and carries her way so well — light wetted area, low drag, a narrow beam, and a near-neutral rudder when the sail is properly balanced. Bill separately posts four new photos of Little Jim from the same cruise to that boat's gallery page. Alastair (Little Jim's owner) adds a further observation from the same footage: no bow wave or wake behind either boat under sail. Bill then relaunches Faith on the River Crouch, heading toward Harwich and the Walton Backwaters as wind and tide allow, promising to keep the blog/log updated. The Taiwan-Paradox thread that opened at the very end of batch 459 continues: Glen Maxwell reviews Jimmy Lu's build photos directly and praises his workmanship and rapid progress; Jimmy Lu thanks him and repeats his pleasure at sharing build photos with the group. The batch closes with Pete Martin's own construction question: during dry-fit disassembly ahead of JoHanna's wet hull assembly, he found the chine not fair — a "3"-shaped wave between the stem, bulkhead 1, and bulkhead 2 — though clamping an external fir strip to the chine visibly improved the line, and he asks whether the full-size chine log will pull the panels fair on its own once glued up. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 459 — pages 4581-4590)

Two intertwined threads run 22-24 July 2007, both direct continuations of batch 458's closing "Faith & Little Jim" post. Bill Serjeant replies that his blog log petered out from a flat laptop battery, promising a fuller write-up, and crediting Little Jim's owner with teaching him hands-on boat-handling — trimming the sail by yuloh and deploying the anchor; Glen Maxwell asks how Bill's simpler topping-lift setup avoids jamming the furling drum; Nels (correctly the established `arvent`/`recree8` identity, not Niels) muses that "Faith and Little Jim" would make a good book title and asks about reactions from the dinghy-cruising crowd the pair have been meeting; Bill answers both — his topping lift just needs to stay taut while the sail rolls, and a standing lugsail can be sheeted like a junk sail (clew held over the leeward quarter) to cure a windward stalling problem. A short standalone "Plymouth to Exmouth" post announces the next cruise leg, logged at `bills-log.blogspot.com`. Pete Martin replies with logistics questions (trailer recovery, yuloh reliance, ever wishing for a motor) and his own JoHanna progress report, cut short by a Gloucestershire flood that cut off his water supply — then separately flags a new correspondent, Jimmy Lu, posting rapid, professional-looking build photos of a Paradox under construction in **Taiwan**; Jimmy Lu replies briefly, thanking Pete, with no further build detail on record. The batch closes with Bill's longest reply of the ten: sympathy for Pete's floods, a yuloh pivot-socket modification (made to swivel fully side-to-side, matching Little Jim's own) prompted by watching Little Jim's owner scull confidently through the whole cruise without ever needing his electric outboard, a detailed account of the cruise's liveliest sailing — a rough force 5 with a big, cliff-bounced swell off Bolt Head and Bolt Tail working into Salcombe — a firsthand judgment that Paradox would ride out a force 8 lying a-hull (uncomfortable but safe), a restated tacking-difficulty fix (reverse the rudder to pay off, ease the sheet, shift crew weight to starboard), and a closing verdict: "absolutely brilliant — probably the best boat I've ever owned." No new pages created. `boats/faith.md` gained four new sections ("The log pauses, and a lesson learned from Little Jim's owner," "Sheeting like a junk sail to windward, and a second topping-lift fix," "Plymouth to Exmouth, then 140 miles on to Essex," "Two days of force 5 off Bolt Head, and the tacking fix confirmed again") — citations [204]-[209] (count 211, numeric-max 209 complete plus 2 pre-existing letter-suffixes [133a],[191a], up from count 205/numeric-max 203). `boats/johanna.md` gained two new sections ("A flooded Gloucestershire, and a Plymouth-to-Exmouth report," "A new correspondent's rapid Taiwan build") — citations [61]-[63] (count 63/63==max, up from 60/60). `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new "Weather woes, and flagging a new Taiwan builder" cross-link section — citations [40]-[42] (count 42/42==max, up from 39/39). `operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md` gained a new paragraph extending "A British Isles sailor's case for lying a-hull, and Paradox's flat bottom" with Bill Serjeant's corroborating force-8 judgment — citation [48] (count 48/48==max, up from 47/47). `components/yuloh.md` gained a new "A swiveling pivot socket modification, matched to Little Jim's" section — citation [70] (count 70/70==max, up from 69/69). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 458 — pages 4571-4580)

Three threads run 19-22 July 2007. The "Vane Gear" thread continues directly from batch 457's close: Ray (`rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`), building a non-Paradox catamaran, replies to Glen Maxwell's trim-tab-over-wind-vane advice with a tongue-in-cheek SA/D-ratio argument (needing a hurricane to hit 5 kt at his boat's 120 sq ft/1,100 lb figures, versus a fully loaded Paradox's own 12.8 ratio), conceding the point but praising the vane gear as "another beautiful job of engineering." A new "great paint for boats" thread is unsolicited commercial spam: Peter Harrett of Top Secret Coatings pitches a one-part epoxy marine paint twice, drawing a skeptical durability question from Glen Maxwell (epoxy paints chalk within 6-18 months against ten years for urethanes like Awlgrip) and a one-line identification from Nels that "this guy and another have been spamming all the boating groups" — no purchase or trial followed. The "Dinghy Cruising Meet at Keyhaven next weekend" thread brings the payoff of the long-running West Country/Scilly Isles cruise plan tracked across many prior batches: Brian Pearson (`keyhavenpotterer`), a new one-off correspondent, invites any nearby Paradoxes to a Dinghy Cruising Association meet; Alastair replies that Bill Serjeant is at that moment sailing from Exmouth to Essex and won't make it, and that his own cruise fell short of the Scillies due to weather; Bill confirms directly — the Scillies were not reached, but Faith and Little Jim sailed 140 miles together eastward from Falmouth, with Alastair getting excellent photos while Bill's own camera failed; Glen Maxwell points the list to Bill's `smallsailboats.co.uk` log. ## 2026-07-19 (batch 457 — pages 4561-4570)

One continuous thread runs 10-19 July 2007, a direct continuation of batch 456's closing "Safety your rudder or else" post. "Jeff UK" asks whether the shorter rudder area (lost to the up-haul lapse) affected "Zoë"'s self-steering; Glen Maxwell confirms very good luck this time and promises a fuller writeup with video, which Ray (`rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`) then chases twice before Glen posts it. The full "Vane Gear" report describes the 8 July 2007 dedicated sea trial: minor teething problems (the oar tiller jumping its crank, the vane blade blown out of alignment in gusts, vibration-loosened screws) sorted out underway, followed by a steady 180° heading held after a short tuning period in 16-19 mph wind — the fullest course-holding demonstration on record and the clearest statement yet of true self-steering's advantage over a simple tiller tamer (freeing the sailor to leave the helm entirely, not just hold a course while stationary). An attempted mpeg upload over dial-up was abandoned at 16%. The report draws three replies: Ray calls the gear "smallscale elegant engineering at its best"; Dennis Mingear (recurring thin correspondent) asks for more detail on the drive mechanism; and a one-off "Bill" (`ankle_deep2`, Palm Bay FL, distinct from Bill Serjeant, confirmed via account/profile) asks three technical questions about steering-rope friction and vane engagement, adding a firsthand account of being shown "Zoë" in person at Cedar Key. Glen's reply explains the full drive linkage (vane to bell crank to push rod to a second bell crank to a U-slotted oar tiller) and confirms the tiller's own U-shaped notch is what lets the steering ropes be slackened to free the rudder for the vane gear. Dennis closes the batch thanking Glen, asking about his painting method, and reporting plans sent for payment the week before. No new pages created. `operations/self-steering.md` gained two new sections ("The 8 July 2007 sea trial: a full course-holding test," "The vane's drive mechanism, explained") — citations [59]-[68] (count 68/68==max, up from 58/58). `boats/zoe.md` gained a new "A full sea trial and course-holding test" section — citations [43]-[50] (count 50/50==max, up from 42/42). `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` gained a new "A full sea trial and course-holding test for the vane gear" section — citations [259]-[266] (count 266/266==max, up from 258/258). ## 2026-07-19 (batch 456 — pages 4551-4560)

Two threads run 5-10 July 2007. The "Hull assembly process" thread from batch 455 gets a closing reply: Bill Serjeant answers Pete Martin's epoxy-coating-sequence question — bulkheads and side panels can just as easily be coated after the wet assembly as before, provided the deck is not yet fitted, giving a cleaner bonding surface for the epoxy joints, chine logs, and sheer strakes — then answers a standing foam-lining question by explaining he skipped it for the extra side-locker stowage space it leaves, judging condensation unlikely and full flooding an unrealistic risk short of Fastnet-Race-type extreme conditions, and closes with a weather update: the Azores high was advancing north, offering a glimmer of hope for the long-delayed West Country cruise after the wettest June on record. Glen Maxwell replies to Bill's yuloh video from batch 455 with a comparison from his own decades of sculling experience — using a yuloh eventually becomes automatic, and old sculling habits can tempt an unneeded twist the yuloh works better without. A separate, longer "Hello, beginner logistics question and rowing thoughts" thread continues from batch 455: Andy Barnhart names a concrete $30 solar trickle-charger product and its 1.26 W/105 mA spec; Al (Alastair) confirms the math (a 300:1 charge-to-discharge ratio) and reports he already owns an electric outboard but prefers the yuloh around the dock; Nels weighs a back-up generator's own fuel-and-storage burden against simply carrying a small outboard; Andy concedes the human-powered-generator idea outright once he understands trickle chargers only maintain an already-charged battery rather than power a load; a new/returning correspondent, Steve (`g4mjw`, previously seen once in batch ~420 sourcing UK plywood), describes an independent DIY project converting a secondhand exercise bike into a 60-watt pedal generator with a home-built permanent-magnet generator; Nels suggests the pedal generator would find better use driving a watermaker than a trolling motor, given how much water matters to survival at sea; Steve reports slow progress, having only started winding the generator's coils. The batch closes with a standalone message: Glen Maxwell reports a rudder up-haul safety lapse aboard "Zoë" — the up-haul line came loose on a 50-mile round trip to the boat ramp, costing about 15% of the rudder's area, coated with Marine Goop and sailed anyway with little noticeable helm difference in 16-18 mph wind. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 455 — pages 4541-4550)

Two threads run 1-5 July 2007, the first a direct continuation of batch 454's close. In "Yuloh's first use is recorded on video.," "Jeff UK" replies to Bill Serjeant's video with a coincidental paint-colour match — his own rowing skiff, already finished in a yellow "Mayonnaise" shade with a light grey rub-rail stripe, matches the colour going onto Faith, and he is weighing the same shade for NOLI's whole hull — while five weeks of rain have cost him against finishing NOLI's glasswork. Bill replies that the same wet, windy spell is exactly wrong for external sheathing, which needs more space than his garage allows; Ian Lang sends a one-line compliment ("Do more!"); Bill closes waiting for calm weather to practise the yuloh technique. The second, larger thread, "Hull assembly process," is new: Pete Martin asks the list whether a Paradox hull must be glued up in a single sitting or can be done safely in stages without losing symmetry. Two independent, complementary answers come in: Bill Serjeant's one-session method (dry-fit and square first, then glue and nail outward from bulkhead 3 to bulkhead 2, 4, 1, the transom, and the stem last, done in about two hours with no adjustment needed since symmetry was already fixed at the dry-fit stage); and Alastair's staged method from Little Jim's original build, using ordinary wire nails pushed into ring-nail pilot holes as temporary locating pins, letting the hull be disassembled and reassembled in exactly the same position and glued up bulkhead by bulkhead without losing alignment, since a nailed joint cannot move regardless of cure state. Dennis Mingear, a one-off correspondent with no boat, quotes the wire-nail technique approvingly ("I'm putting this one away for the future"); Ray (`rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`), building a non-Paradox catamaran, corroborates it with a cautionary tale of his own — locating a panel with screws instead of nails let it bite into the stringers slightly out of true, discovered only after the glue had set. Pete thanks both correspondents, confirms he is already using nails as locators, and reports the vent trunk and an epoxy coat still to finish before the wet build, with a glued hull expected by late July. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 454 — pages 4531-4540)

Two threads run 28 June - 1 July 2007, both direct continuations of batch 453's close. Bill Serjeant, answering Neil Barratt's question about the long-postponed Scilly Isles cruise, explains that wild summer seas near Newquay were exactly why the West Country cruise start was delayed — with rain and high-wind forecasts still unsettled, he is waiting on the Azores "high" to shift north and east, and hopes to meet Alastair at the Dinghy Cruising Association's holiday meeting at St Just in Roseland (7-14 July). In a longer-running "Hello, beginner logistics question and rowing thoughts" thread, new correspondent Andy Barnhart (Raleigh, NC) gets answers to his rowing question: Bill Serjeant explains standing-and-facing-forward rowing is blocked by the cabin top, though the yuloh works as a short-duration paddle; Andy links an "Aussie Paradox" photo suggesting standing rowing looks doable and confirms a Paradox will fit his covered storage space; Stephen Yahn floats a "north woods" pinned rowlock with a downward-curved oar that would stow like the yuloh and could eliminate the plans' deck stowage loops. The thread pivots to auxiliary power: Andy floats a hand-crank 12V generator driving a small trolling motor as a quiet alternative to a gas outboard; Al (Alastair) counters with the physics of stacked conversion losses ("nothing is simpler than a lever"); Andy clarifies he only wants it as backup to a solar trickle charger for tight-space maneuvering; Al closes the thread with hard numbers — a Minn Kota's 30-amp draw would need 300 hours of trickle charging per hour of use — reaffirming the yuloh for close quarters and recommending a proper motor (several builders favor the Honda 2 hp) if a motor is wanted at all. Separately, in the "Building Started on Irene May" thread, Ian Lang thanks Al for his build-order advice, admits he needs a half-finished hull to stay motivated to complete the smaller parts, and reports a bulkhead-2 measuring correction found in the amended drawing. The batch closes with Bill Serjeant posting a video of Faith's yuloh's first use. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 453 — pages 4521-4530)

Three threads run 24-27 June 2007. The "Sheer and Chine strakes question" thread from batch 452 gets a further reply: Andrew Graham confirms that when the sheer and chine pieces are installed per the plans, the chine and deck lines are already perfectly defined along their full length, leaving only a small amount of timber to plane from the external corners inward. The main event is a new "Building Started on Irene May" thread: North London correspondent Ian Lang's plywood and Douglas Fir arrive unexpectedly (two sheets corner-damaged but workable), marking the effective start of construction on a boat he names "Irene May" after his late mother — satisfying the WATCHLIST section-C promotion trigger (build started + boat named). The announcement draws congratulations from Bill Serjeant (who had launched "Faith" himself two weeks earlier), Pete Martin (sympathizing over a shrinking workspace while shaping his own chine and sheer strakes), and Stephen Yahn (a reflection on a project's earliest days, recalling his own childhood intent to name a boat "Martha Louise" after his mother); Al (Alastair) then advises building all the small parts — mast, rudder, yuloh — before finishing the hull, so the boat is ready to launch the moment the hull is done rather than sitting unusable while smaller parts are still made. Separately, Bill Serjeant posts a "Web site update" with new photos of Faith at her first launching, drawing a compliment and a question from Neil Barratt about the long-standing Scilly Isles cruise plan with Alastair, prompted by an unusually wild summer sea observed near Newquay. A new correspondent, Andy Barnhart (Raleigh, NC), introduces himself with a beginner's workspace-logistics question (a shared garage bay, outside storage on a dolly later doubling as a trailer-loader) and an open question on whether Paradox can be rowed with conventional oars, sketching a barstool-seat-and-oar-pin idea from his own rafting/rowing background — left unanswered on record. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 452 — pages 4511-4520)

Three threads run 14-24 June 2007, all direct continuations of batch 451's close. In the "Photos of 'Faith' afloat" thread, Bill Serjeant posts photos of Faith afloat at Burnham Marina, due out of the water again ahead of a West Country cruise; Glen Maxwell and Stephen Yahn both congratulate him, Stephen calling it "a crowning achievement... part of a circle of a very small number of people who build their own boats"; Bill then gives a full two-day sea-trial report — Day 1 calm, sailing flawlessly and working out yuloh technique (a figure-eight stroke doesn't work, a horizontal shaft-twist does); Day 2 brought Force 3-4 gusting 5 and a first tacking failure resolved by freeing the sheet to feather the sail, deliberate 30-degree heeling to test stability with no anxiety, oilskins made unnecessary by the closed hatch, and a walk-through of the cabin layout (under-floor storage, a three-minute cup of tea, the chart table, the gimballed compass); Al (Alastair) closes the thread warning Bill to get used to curious spectators' questions at every launch/recovery. Separately, Stephen Yahn posts "Ironsides" progress — aluminum rudder gudgeons, brazed steel deck keepers, and a first attempt at powder coating, with Al Law's suggested lower, less rope-snagging deck-keeper shape. In a "Sheer and Chine strakes question" thread, Pete Martin asks whether the sheer and chine strakes are easier to bevel after fitting them into the boat rather than before; Stephen Yahn confirms beveling should always follow installation, since the correct angle varies along the strake's length; Pete separately replies to Ian Lang's "The skip is just leaving" post, asking whether his materials have arrived yet and encouraging him to keep the group updated. `boats/faith.md` gained a new "Faith afloat at Burnham Marina, and a first sea trial" section plus an updated frontmatter description — citations [181]-[186] (count 187, numeric-max 186, i.e. numeric [1]-[186] complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 181/numeric-max 180). `operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md` gained a new "A figure-eight stroke does not work; a horizontal twist does" section — citation [29] (count 29/29==max, up from 28/28). `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a new "Rudder gudgeons and deck keepers, and a first attempt at powder coating" section plus an updated frontmatter timestamp — citation [46] (count 46/46==max, up from 45/45). `components/sheer-strakes.md` gained a new "Bevel the sheer strake and chine log after fitting, not before" section — citations [3]-[4] (count 4/4==max, up from 2/2). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "A sheer and chine strake beveling question" section plus a closing sentence to "Welcoming a new North London builder," and an updated frontmatter timestamp — citations [51]-[53] (count 53/53==max, up from 50/50). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 451 — pages 4501-4510)

Two threads run 5-13 June 2007, both direct continuations of batch 450's close. Alastair asks where Bill Serjeant would launch at Falmouth for a possible DCA North West Holiday Meeting rendezvous at St Just; Bill answers that St Just's launch beach is good but reached by a very steep, winding, narrow road untested against his car, with a fallback slip at Loe-ven on the river's other side. Days later Bill announces "Faith" was launched, starting a cruise/build blog at `bills-log.blogspot.com` — drawing congratulations from Nels ("time for a bit of a yodel... Yuloh lay he he!"), Glen Maxwell, and Alastair, who adds a yuloh-handling tip from his own first cross-wind launch (rudder left down, green paint left on every finger pontoon) confirming it's easier to steer by yuloh with the rudder raised. Separately, in the "Another UK Paradox!" thread, new North London correspondent Ian Lang thanks Pete Martin for his sourcing recap and reports two further UK plywood quotes (Goldbergs of Uxbridge, Enfield Timber — the latter "too good to be true"), then asks about Balcotan polyurethane adhesive as an epoxy alternative for interior gluing; Nels answers Ian's earlier plywood-quality question (layer count, uniform thickness, edge voids, weight as quality indicators); Ian closes the batch clearing his garage into an 8 cubic-metre skip and ordering plywood/Douglas Fir from Robbins, with beam-lamination jigs the next job. `boats/faith.md` gained three new sections ("Choosing a launch site for the Scilly Isles cruise," "'Faith' was launched," "A second North London builder's plywood and timber sourcing") plus an updated frontmatter description/intro reflecting the actual launch — citations [173]-[180] (count 181, numeric-max 180 complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 173/numeric-max 172). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "Faith launched" cross-link section — citation [73] (count 73/73==max, up from 72/72). `components/yuloh.md` gained a new "Steering by yuloh is easier with the rudder raised" section — citation [67] (count 67/67==max, up from 66/66). `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` gained two new paragraphs extending "A 2007 UK shopping-list recurrence" — citations [100]-[102] in that sub-block (file total count 100 anchors across 9 `# Citations` sub-headings, numeric-max 102 with the pre-existing gap at [5]-[6] unchanged, up from count 97/numeric-max 99). `construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md` gained a new "Balcotan polyurethane adhesive as a possible interior alternative to epoxy" section — citation [22] (count 22/22==max, up from 21/21). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 450 — pages 4491-4500)

Two threads run 3-4 June 2007, both direct continuations of batch 449's close. Bill Serjeant announces "Faith" has "been born" — the finished hull has left the garage for the front lawn, awaiting her first water launch a few days later at Burnham-on-Crouch — drawing congratulations from Perry Phillips, Pete Martin, Stephen Yahn, Glen Maxwell, and gilberj55 ("JG"/John Gilbert), plus a longer reply from Alastair joking he may need to repaint "Little Jim" so it doesn't look "the poor relation" and cautioning Bill to protect the paintwork once afloat; Bill replies that the light-coloured deck will inevitably scratch and scar and that a darker colour would have hidden it better, and floats meeting the Dinghy Cruising Association's North West Holiday Meeting at St Just in Roseland (7-14 July) during a planned Scilly Isles cruise. Separately, in the "Another UK Paradox!" thread, Alastair welcomes new North London correspondent Ian Lang to the group, and Pete Martin gives Ian a detailed newcomer-facing recap of his own build sourcing — Far Eastern marine plywood from Clarks Wood, Uxbridge (£333.62), Douglas Fir from Robbins of Bristol (£237 so far), MAS Flag epoxy, 20 kg of scrounged scrap lead ballast, his building blog — and confesses his "first monumental cock-up," misreading the stem's bracketed plan figures as full widths rather than half-widths at every station, hidden invisibly with epoxy filler. `boats/faith.md` gained a new closing section ("'Faith' is born: off the trailer and onto dry land") plus an updated frontmatter description/intro reflecting the hull's completion — citations [166]-[172] (count 173, numeric-max 172 complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 166/numeric-max 165). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "'Faith' is born" cross-link section — citation [72] (count 72/72==max, up from 71/71). `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new "Welcoming a new North London builder, and a stem-cutting mistake confessed" section — citations [29]-[30] (count 30/30==max, up from 28/28). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Welcoming a new North London builder" cross-link section — citation [50] (count 50/50==max, up from 49/49). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 449 — pages 4481-4490)

Four threads run 31 May - 3 June 2007, all direct continuations of batch 448. Bill Serjeant reports "Faith"'s launch cannot be far off, waiting on a warm day (above 18°C) to apply the antifouling, which then needs a week to harden; the finished-gadget photos are updated on his website. The "Bronze Lower Gudgeon" thread gets a further reply: Jack Gardiner endorses bronze or stainless steel for the lower rudder fitting wherever a builder's skill and budget allow, framing the plans' wood-and-epoxy gudgeon as a deliberate cost-effective compromise rather than a lesser choice, consistent with Paradox's design brief of common, affordable materials. A new "Another UK Paradox!" thread has a new North London correspondent, Ian Lang, introduce himself as a prospective builder with plans already in hand and plywood quotes being gathered; Bill Serjeant welcomes him as the nearest existing UK builder, naming Friday 8 June as Faith's possible launch date and extending an open invitation to visit; Ian thanks him and wishes him luck with the launch. A "Vent Trunk Query" thread has Pete Martin, with his bulkheads largely finished, ask how the vent trunk's top attaches to bulkhead 2, where the 18 mm front piece's plywood comes from, whether a port-cleat offset visible in Bill's photos is a design change or mistake, and how the PVC drain pipe is installed; Bill answers in full — butt-and-glue construction with the trunk top built from laminated wood roughly 1 in thick, a removable screwed-and-sealed starboard side for later access, 40-grit-roughened PVC pipe bonded with epoxy and filler powder installed after the bottom is fitted — corrects a photo-caption mixup in a follow-up, and reports Faith now on her launching trailer awaiting the rudder and a first sail; Pete thanks him and reports his own stem construction progressing well. `boats/faith.md` gained three new sections ("Waiting on warm weather for the antifouling, and a new UK builder welcomed," "The vent trunk top and drain pipe, and the launching trailer," plus the underlying citations) — citations [157]-[165] (count 166, numeric-max 165 complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 157/numeric-max 156). `construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md` gained a closing paragraph to "Casting a glass lower rudder gudgeon: technique" — citations [32] (count 32/32==max, up from 31/31). `components/rig/mast.md` gained a new "Attaching the vent trunk top to bulkhead 2" section — citations [15]-[19] (count 19/19==max, up from 14/14). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "The vent trunk top and drain pipe" section — citations [45]-[49] (count 49/49==max, up from 44/44). `people/pete-martin.md` gained a cross-link sentence — no new citations (count 28/28==max, unchanged). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "Waiting on the weather, a new UK builder, and onto the launching trailer" section — citations [64]-[71] (count 71/71==max, up from 63/63). `people/jack-gardiner.md` gained a cross-link sentence to the gudgeon reply — citation [217] (count 216 anchors, numeric-max 217, pre-existing gap at [53] unchanged, up from count 215/numeric-max 216). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 448 — pages 4471-4480)

Three short threads run 26-31 May 2007. A new "Home Made Epoxy Pumps" thread opens: John Jamieson asks about making home-made epoxy pumps or improving factory ones; Alastair asks what's wrong with the factory pumps; Bill Serjeant says he prefers his own bought pumps (from Epoxy Resins UK, three boats' worth of use from the same set) or calibrated cups over building custom ones, provided the nozzles stay clean; Bruce Hallman (new correspondent) reports building his own from PVC pipe and fittings when he couldn't find factory pumps to fit his 5-gallon epoxy pails, linking a similar bilge-pump build as a visual reference. The "Peelply" thread gets one further round: Gene T. asks whether peel ply is reusable, and Jeff UK answers no — cured epoxy residue sticks to it, and its fine weave (the same property that stops resin soaking through) also stops it being cleaned for reuse, with using MAS epoxy largely eliminating the amine-blush-clogging problem peel ply otherwise avoids. A new "Bronze Lower Gudgeon" thread has John Jamieson ask for clearer photos than Don Elliott's of building the lower rudder gudgeon and whether anyone has cast one in bronze instead of glass; Glen Maxwell and Alastair both endorse trying bronze if the means exist (Alastair having built his own from a block of Tufnol); Bill Serjeant gives a full molding technique for the glass version — an ice-cream-carton-and-wood mold sealed with cellotape, 5 layers of GRP tape layup lining the curved side, bolt holes drilled in situ against the transom, and an open question on whether water immersion tightens or loosens the bronze pintle's fit. `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` gained a new "Calibrated cups versus dispensing pumps, and a home-made PVC pump" section — citations [90]-[93] (count 94, numeric-max 93, i.e. numeric [1]-[93] complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [29a], up from count 93/numeric-max 89). `references/materials-sourcing-links.md` gained a closing paragraph to "Peel ply: what it is, and US availability" — citations [25]-[26] (count 26/26==max, up from 24/24). `construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md` gained two new paragraphs extending "Casting a glass lower rudder gudgeon: technique" — citations [28]-[31] (count 31/31==max, up from 27/27). No new pages created. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 447 — pages 4461-4470)

Two short threads run 21-26 May 2007. The "Peelply" thread gets a further round: John Button (Poole, England) asks who Jeff UK's local supplier is and whether peel ply works on polyester gelcoat; Jeff UK answers that his source is a local small boat builder who sells to him only as a favor, judges peel ply unsuitable for gelcoat since it would still leave a visible imprint of its own weave, and explains more fully what peel ply actually does — compacting the bulk of resin and cloth left after heavy rolling for a smooth finish with only a faint cloth imprint; John Button thanks him. ## 2026-07-18 (batch 446 — pages 4451-4460)

Three short threads run 20-21 May 2007, all direct continuations of threads batch 445 opened or closed. The "Peelply" thread gets two more replies: "Jeff UK" confirms Alastair's answer (roll it out with a small roller, available in the US, sources it locally from a boat builder cheaper than retail), and Glen Maxwell gives the fuller technical answer — peel ply is basically Dacron fabric, buying it labeled "Peel Ply" carries a premium, and a cheaper source is home-built-airplane suppliers (Wicks, Aircraft Spruce & Specialty) sold as covering fabric, with Dacron dress-lining material from an ordinary fabric store also working, since nothing bonds to Dacron, not even paint. A side branch has Perry Phillips ask whether Glen used peel ply for "Zoë"'s admired finish; Glen answers no — the finish was elbow grease plus a roller/foam-brush Awlgrip application, already documented in full elsewhere. The self-steering/vane-gear thread continues: Glen answers Bill Serjeant's original-sailplan mounting question (the mount would fit, extended back farther) and reports uploading photos to a "Zoë's Vane Gear" files folder; a fuller public "Vane gear" thread follows with more detail (oar blade still needs glassing, up/down-haul lines pending, a trial and video planned, and a note that the vane mechanism could drive other output devices such as a trim tab or servo-pendulum oar); Bill thanks Glen twice for the photos. A third, unrelated thread has a newcomer ask whether Matt Layden has ever discussed scaling the plans for a larger Paradox, answered by the archive's tracked "maxi-Paradox" builder (joe_mapango/Chris Curtis, WATCHLIST section A) — the standard plans already include a 20%-enlargement document, and Chris relays that a direct e-mail exchange with Matt about Matt's own "ultimate cruiser" (a 21 ft Paradox) got an initial reply then went unanswered after a request for engineering help, closing with Chris's personal view that a 33-50% enlargement is feasible. `references/materials-sourcing-links.md` gained new "Peel ply" prose plus a new paragraph on Dacron sourcing — citations [20]-[21] (count 21/21==max, up from 19/19). `construction/topside-painting.md` gained a closing sentence to "Glen Maxwell's full Awlgrip roll-and-tip recipe, and why not to spray it" — citations [52]-[53] (count 53/53==max, up from 51/51). `operations/self-steering.md` gained a sentence closing "A windvane built for the Mark II sailplan, and whether it suits the original rig" plus a new "Photos posted, and a fuller public writeup of the vane gear" section — citations [55]-[58] (count 58/58==max, up from 54/54). `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md` gained a new "An 'ultimate cruiser' recurrence, and a personal case for 33-50% enlargement" section — citations [117]-[118] (count 118/118==max, up from 116/116). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 445 — pages 4441-4450)

A single "Faith's Progress." thread runs 18-20 May 2007, directly continuing batch 444's close. Bill Serjeant's launch announcement (hull down to paint, first launch planned at Burnham-on-Crouch, sea trials to the Walton Backwaters) draws four congratulations in turn — Perry Phillips, "Jeff UK" (with a Charles Stock/"Shoal Waters" spotting tip), Alastair ("I'll have to stop telling people that Little Jim is the only one in the country"), and Hugh Miller (NZ) — before Bill closes the build narrative with a heartfelt thank-you to the community, naming Alastair's generosity in lending "Little Jim" for a sail as the reason the build ever started, and Matt Layden by name as "the genius" behind the design. Bill then turns to a fresh technical question for Glen Maxwell: whether Glen's windvane self-steering gear, built and proven on "Zoë"'s version #2 sailplan with its shorter boom, would suit a boat carrying the plans' original, longer-boom sailplan instead — left unanswered on record. A separate one-off thread has new correspondent John Jamieson ask what "peelply" is and whether it's available in the US, answered plainly by Alastair (a fabric resin won't bond to, used to compact and smooth a layup, peeled off once cured). `boats/faith.md` gained a new closing section extending "The hull painted, and a launch planned at Burnham-on-Crouch" plus a new "A self-steering question for a boat on the original sailplan" section — citations [151]-[156] (count 157 / numeric-max 156, i.e. numeric [1]-[156] complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 151 / numeric-max 150). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a new "Faith down to hull paint, and a thank-you to the community" section — citations [61]-[63] (count 63/63==max, up from 60/60). `operations/self-steering.md` gained a new "A windvane built for the Mark II sailplan, and whether it suits the original rig" section — citation [54] (count 54/54==max, up from 53/53). `references/materials-sourcing-links.md` gained a new "Peel ply: what it is, and US availability" section — citations [18]-[19] (count 19/19==max, up from 17/17). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 444 — pages 4431-4440)

A single continuous 18 May 2007 exchange, running from the ballast/hatch-leak thread batch 443 opened straight through to a UK-registry note and a Faith progress update. "Little Jim"'s Alastair fields a kayak-building "press-in hatches" suggestion from James Hagan, diagnoses the underlying screw-in-hatch design mismatch (built to keep water out, not in), then swaps into a Chichester Harbour cruising-ground exchange with Pete Martin — a rough F5-6 weekend kept "Little Jim" inside the harbour while the lifeboat rescued three boats aground within it, and Pete's Milford-Haven-versus-Poole maiden-voyage question gets both harbours endorsed. In a separate thread, Steve Blair's already-cited Portsmouth Yardstick question draws Alastair's considered decline — competitive sailing runs against most of his reasons for sailing at all — capped with the Water Rat's "messing about in boats" speech from *The Wind in the Willows* quoted in full. Bill Serjeant closes the batch with two short registry/progress notes: "JoHanna" added to the UK Owners/Builders page, and "Faith" down to just hull and bottom paint before a hoped-for early-June launch at Burnham-on-Crouch, ahead of sea trials to the Walton Backwaters. `boats/little-jim.md` gained a paragraph extending "Abandoning water ballast for lead" plus a new "Chichester Harbour, a shallow-water lifeboat callout, and choosing a UK cruising ground" section — citations [144]-[148] (count 148/148==max, up from 143/143). `people/pete-martin.md` gained a new "A settled ballast plan, and a Milford Haven maiden voyage" section — citations [25]-[28] (count 28/28==max, up from 24/24). `boats/johanna.md` gained a new "Added to the UK Owners/Builders registry" section — citation [44] (count 44/44==max, up from 43/43). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained a cross-linking sentence on the registry update — citation [60] (count 60/60==max, up from 59/59). `boats/faith.md` gained a new "The hull painted, and a launch planned at Burnham-on-Crouch" section — citation [150] (count 151 / numeric-max 150, i.e. numeric [1]-[150] complete plus the pre-existing letter-suffix [133a], up from count 150 / numeric-max 149). `people/alastair-law.md` gained a closing sentence to "Design philosophy and community role" — citation [370] (count 370/370==max, up from 369/369). `operations/sailing-characteristics.md` gained a closing sentence to "No Portsmouth Yardstick rating on record" — citation [51] (count 51/51==max, up from 50/50). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 443 — pages 4421-4430)

Three short threads run 13-18 May 2007. The self-steering-gear thread closes out: Glen Maxwell tells Stephen Yahn he locks his steering line rather than relying on any pendulum weight; John Gilbert ("JG," `gilberj55`, real name newly revealed in this batch's `From:` header) asks how a "tillermaster" autohelm photographed aboard "Zoë" worked out, and Glen answers that it drained its battery too fast for anything beyond a short cruise, with the windvane itself still awaiting epoxy and a pair of new bow fairleads fixing the earlier mainsheet-fouling problem; Stephen Yahn closes the thread admitting he still hasn't fitted his own rudder's lead weight. The same thread then drifts subject lines: John Jamieson (new one-off correspondent) asks about adapting self-steering gear to a Paradox under the leftover "Paradox Ballast" subject, Glen answers (not that he knows of) with a plea to keep subject lines accurate for the archive's sake, and Steve Blair jokes about the mixup. Separately, "Little Jim"'s Alastair reports a leaking water-ballast tank inspection cover after a rough F5-6 sail and abandons water ballast for permanent lead, drawing a refined dowel-and-1x2 tool from Glen Maxwell for seating stubborn tank plates; and Steve Blair asks, unanswered, whether the Paradox will ever get a Portsmouth Yardstick handicap rating. `operations/self-steering.md` gained a tillermaster/vane-repair paragraph extending "Sea trial and damage at the 2007 Cedar Key gathering," a closing paragraph on "A pendulum-weight self-steering idea, and its limits," and a new closing paragraph on the subject-line drift, the batch's centerpiece — citations [47]-[53] (count 53/53==max, up from 46/46). `boats/little-jim.md` gained a new "Abandoning water ballast for lead, after a leaking tank inspection cover (May 2007)" section — citations [142]-[143] (count 143/143==max, up from 141/141). `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a closing sentence to "A self-steering weight idea" — citation [45] (count 45/45==max, up from 44/44). `people/alastair-law.md` gained a new closing paragraph on the ballast-tank leak and lead conversion — citation [369] (count 369/369==max, up from 368/368). `operations/sailing-characteristics.md` gained a new "No Portsmouth Yardstick rating on record" section — citation [50] (count 50/50==max, up from 49/49). `construction/transom-deck-plate.md` gained a cross-link to the refined dowel-tool fix on `boats/little-jim.md`, no new citation (count 58/58==max, unchanged). `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` gained two cross-linking sentences (tillermaster/vane status, refined dowel-tool), no new citation (count 256/256==max, unchanged). ## 2026-07-18 (batch 442 — pages 4411-4420)

The Cedar Key windvane thread closes out (Glen Maxwell explains his fascination with wind vanes is Paradox's own longitudinal stability, needing very little steering even on an overnighter; Bill Serjeant thanks him, having previously only had secondhand reports of that course-keeping to go on), and Jerry Payton's previously-unanswered "Cedar Key Pictures" question gets a reply two days later from a new one-off correspondent, Dennis, pointing to the same West Coast Trailer Sailers photo page already cited for earlier years. The batch's centerpiece is a fresh, independent self-steering-gear thread: Stephen Yahn proposes a pendulum-weight rudderhead idea (a slider weight that shifts to leeward as the hull heels, correcting a windward round-up tendency, tying into the plans' own lead-weighted rudder blade), which draws gilberj55 ("JG")'s two model-boat precedents (a weighted-shaft Tahiti Ketch and a sheet-to-tiller cruising model), Alan's (`logicaidnz`) trial-and-error assessment plus two book recommendations, Bill Serjeant's windward-only/steering-line-lock caveat and a recalled 1974 Hasler SP90 windvane with an eBay link, Stephen's own casting-pool anecdote and a rudder-strap question, and Jerry Payton's answer identifying those straps as plank-reinforcement hardware, not self-steering gear. `operations/self-steering.md` gained a new closing paragraph to "Glen Maxwell's horizontal-axis wind-vane concept" plus a new "A pendulum-weight self-steering idea, and its limits" section, the batch's centerpiece — citations [39]-[46] (count 46/46==max, up from 38/38). `operations/group-cruise-reports.md` gained an updated citation and a new one closing out the previously-unanswered Cedar Key photo request — citations [41]-[42] (count 42/42==max, up from 41/41). `people/stephen-yahn.md` gained a new "A self-steering weight idea" section — citations [42]-[44] (count 44/44==max, up from 41/41). `people/bill-serjeant.md` gained two new paragraphs extending the windvane exchange and covering the pendulum-weight thread — citations [57]-[59] (count 59/59==max, up from 56/56).
