## 2026-07-05 (batch 286)

* **Update (batch page_2851-page_2860, msgnum 2882-2883-2884-2885-2886-2887-2888-2889-2890-2891,
  continuing directly from batch 285's last msgnum 2881; no new gap in this
  range)**: 27-28 June 2005 (all ten messages within about a 34-hour window).
  0 files created, 1 extended (people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **The "paradox mpeg movie" thread reaches its conclusion, with the
  archive's fullest firsthand account of Jill under sail.** This entire
  batch is the direct continuation of the video-sharing thread recorded at
  the end of batch 285 (cites [139]-[148]) — again a single, self-contained
  thread with no other topic present in any of the ten messages. Paul
  loaded a newer, larger revision of his own copy of the file without yet
  testing it; a second Yahoo file area ("Paradoxbuilders2") proved
  invisible to Jack's own account, so Paul supplied a BitComet download
  link and confirmed the working torrents were also in the main group's
  regular file-sharing folder. That evening Paul confirmed he had finally
  watched the video ("Out in a hurricane again! Looking good"), prompting
  Jack's own detailed reply: two days sailing Jill in a steady 20-35 mph
  south wind along a lee shore, at times carrying too much sail; a 6 hp
  outboard idling on a folding mount in the footage; the boat loaded with
  two adults, tool ballast, and food with her water tanks left unfilled;
  she took freighter wakes from every angle with no fuss; and a bungee cord
  clipped from the outboard's top to the cleat solved a persistent
  sheet-snagging problem. Jack created a dedicated torrent-file folder for
  the footage and a system notification recorded the upload. A newcomer to
  BitTorrent downloaded the file but could not find how to play it; Jack
  walked him through BitComet's default download path and confirmed
  playback in Windows Media Player or Winamp. Asked what Mac users could
  view the file on, Paul reported success in QuickTime on his own Windows
  98 machine and expected QuickTime or Microsoft's Mac Media Player to work
  equally well on a Mac. No further report of the video's final reach among
  viewers is on record — the thread simply ends there.

  Extended [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites
  [149]-[158], 158 total; sequence 1-158 verified gapless except the known
  pre-existing [53] gap, documented in HANDOVER.md, which remains
  unaffected and was not newly introduced). No new heading was added — the
  new prose extends the existing "Launch: 'Jill'" section's closing
  paragraph (the same paragraph batch 285 extended), respecting the
  heading-freeze rule.

  **No new person or boat pages.** Adolf "Alf" Sawatzky (`alfswatzky`)
  reappeared asking a BitComet playback question — already a standing,
  below-threshold correspondent since his February 2004 introduction (see
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md) and
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md)); still no

## 2026-07-05 (batch 285)

* **Update (batch page_2841-page_2850, msgnum 2872-2873-2874-2875-2876-2877-2878-2879-2880-2881,
  continuing directly from batch 284's last msgnum 2871; no new gap in this
  range)**: 26-27 June 2005 (all ten messages within about a 30-hour window).
  0 files created, 1 extended (people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **The "paradox mpeg movie" thread runs its full course.** This entire
  batch is the direct continuation of Jack Gardiner's video-sharing offer
  recorded at the end of batch 284 (cite [138]) — a single, self-contained
  thread with no other topic present in any of the ten messages. Jack's
  30 MB MPEG video of his own Paradox under sail drew several interested
  viewers: a previously-cited correspondent (last seen asking about
  condensation under Zoë's hatch skylight) asked to receive the file
  directly given his own cable modem; another offered to host it on his own
  website if Jack e-mailed it to a Gmail account; and a longtime Oslo-based
  correspondent, well established elsewhere in the tree for his Sven
  Yrvind/BRIS reporting, simply asked for a copy. Jack confirmed the file
  exceeded his e-mail provider's 10 MB outgoing limit, ruling out sending it
  directly by e-mail — a period-accurate illustration of 2005-era file-size
  constraints. Paul, a frequent commenter on Jack's build throughout this
  file, proposed a BitTorrent transfer instead using the BitComet client;
  after his first tracker-tutorial link proved unworkable he posted a
  working replacement tracker and instructions. Jack's own attempt to
  download BitComet itself failed twice with a refused connection, so he
  asked Paul to e-mail the file directly; Paul obliged, uploading both the
  video file and its torrent to the group's file-sharing folder for testing.
  No further report of the video actually reaching viewers is on record.
  Extended [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites
  [139]-[148], 148 total; sequence 1-148 verified gapless except the known
  pre-existing [53] gap, documented in HANDOVER.md, which remains
  unaffected and was not newly introduced). No new heading was added — the
  new prose extends the existing "Launch: 'Jill'" section's closing
  paragraph, respecting the heading-freeze rule. No new person or boat

  Harbo, and Paul/kayaker37) are already-standing below-threshold or
  established correspondents with no boat/build of their own on record, so

## 2026-07-05 (batch 284)

* **Update (batch page_2831-page_2840, msgnum 2862-2863-2864-2865-2866-2867-2868-2869-2870-2871,
  continuing directly from batch 283's last msgnum 2861; no new gap in this
  range)**: 15-26 June 2005 (nine messages on 15-18 June, one outlier on 26
  June). 0 files created, 4 extended (references/off-topic-cross-posts.md,
  construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md, operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md).

  **(1) The "Derek's Rob Roy" secondhand-canoe thread closed out.**
  Continuing directly from batch 283's opening of this thread, three more
  messages settled it: portability against a two-person canoe already on
  hand (needing two people to lift, versus the Wee Rob Roy's one-handed
  portability, plus a same-vendor outrigger option for fishing stability),
  confirmation that fitting buoyancy bags fore and aft would add a safety
  margin, and a paintball-gun/aerator joke about a "private bubbling spa" if
  swamped. Extended off-topic cross-posts — Derek Clark's secondhand Wee
  Rob Roy canoe,
  cites [4]-[6] (this section's own per-section numbering; 6 total in the
  section, gapless).

  **(2) A new "Front Deck" thread: fitting the curved forward deck skin.**
  A builder struggling to bend the stock forward deck panel into its curve —
  despite webbing straps and windlasses, and short of cutting the panel into
  three pieces or grooving it — drew a full construction answer: re-check
  plans measurements first; failing that, a thinner sheet (half to a third
  of the specified thickness) laid up dry, glued, then doubled with a second
  layer once cured; if still resistant, heat and moisture (heat gun, quartz
  lamps, or a nearby heat source, kept moist) will bend it further, though
  this risks delayed damage to plywood not laminated with waterproof glue; a
  fastening tip (start at the center screw, work outward to each side); and,
  for a beam-crown mismatch specifically, either an isolated flat panel from
  stem to bulkhead one with the adjacent bulkhead shaved, or the panel split
  lengthwise down the centerline and the two halves fitted with the gap
  filled — the latter fix, first floated as a half-joking suggestion,
  accepted and embellished with a contrasting-wood or white-caulk reveal
  along the join. New subsection at [deck and cabin beam lamination —
  fitting the forward deck skin to a tight
  curve](/construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md#fitting-the-forward-deck-skin-to-a-tight-curve),
  cites [32]-[36] (36 total, sequence 1-36 verified gapless).

  **(3) The "Batteries as ballast?" human-power tangent gets a real-world
  data point, closing the thread out.** Answering the open WAG-on-continuous-
  human-hp question from batch 283, a real (non-Paradox) precedent was
  offered: a human-powered craft, under pedal and oar power in the right
  boat with the right gear, has covered Alberta to Nova Scotia via rivers and
  the coast at 4 knots, no batteries involved — with two Gartside Boats
  pedal-drive design links. Extended [auxiliary outboard power — how much
  continuous power a human body can actually
  generate](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#how-much-continuous-power-a-human-body-can-actually-generate),
  cite [38] (38 total, sequence 1-38 verified gapless).

  **(4) Jack Gardiner offers a video of his own Paradox sailing.** A short,
  standalone item: an offer to share a roughly 30 MB MPEG video of his boat
  under sail with any interested list member over AIM. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md), cite [138] (138 total;
  the pre-existing [53] gap, documented in HANDOVER.md, remains the only
  gap — no new gaps introduced).

  **Standing-watch checks.** David Beard/David LeBlanc/Smith/Derek Clark

## 2026-07-05 (batch 282)

* **Update (batch page_2811–page_2820, msgnum 2842-2843-2844-2845-2846-2847-2848-2849-2850-2851,
  continuing directly from batch 281's last msgnum 2841; no new gap in this range)**:
  14 June 2005 (a single busy day). 0 files created, 5 extended
  (operations/radio-and-electronics.md, operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md,
  design/weight-and-ballast.md, references/beachcruising-book.md, design/rig.md),
  plus a narrate-once-cross-link addition to people/nels.md.

  **(1) A firsthand Air-X wind-generator reliability report, closing the
  loop on batch 281's tail-end mention.** Glen Maxwell, replying to Brent
  Geery's standing reliability question, reported the units work fine in
  his own experience, with genuine "horrible racket" noise above 20 kts as
  the blades unload — tolerable only if tethered — and a partial electric
  brake that reverses the generator into a motor so the wind itself resists
  turning it. Brent Geery followed up quoting Southwest Windpower's own
  marketing explanation for the newer Air-X's quieter behavior versus the
  older AIR 403's flutter-prone "aero-elastic" blades. Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extends "A wind generator and radar: a scale-mockup exercise, and how
  little power a Paradox actually needs", cites [20]-[21], 21 total).

  **(2) "Batteries as ballast?" — a new correspondent revives the
  2004 electric-outboard/battery-ballast idea with worked numbers.** A
  below-threshold newcomer (signing "Blll," posting as BllFs6/bllfsh666 —
  confirmed via headers as a distinct handle from 2004's earlier "Bill"/
  wam_53, not the same identity) asked whether small daysailer designs
  could use battery ballast to power an electric trolling motor instead of
  an outboard, working the case in detail against Phil Bolger's "Old Shoe"
  (200 lb ballast ≈ 2-3 deep-cycle batteries, good for about 1/2 hp
  continuous over 5 hours). Derek Clark, having independently reached the
  same conclusion after seeing Minn Kota electric outboards, agreed the
  logic holds up but flagged recharging — staying independent of marinas
  with no room aboard for a generator — as his real hesitation. Brent
  Geery closed with a wry aside that the implied rowing could double as
  exercise. Folded into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md)
  (extends "An electric outboard, and gel-cell batteries doing double duty
  as ballast", cites [31]-[33], 33 total).

  **(3) A displacement-versus-load-capacity tangent, settled by Archimedes'
  principle.** Derek Clark, in the same message thanking Nels for a book
  link (see item 4), asked what relationship exists between a hull's
  displacement and its load-carrying capacity, prompted by a canoe
  advertised at a displacement he first misremembered as 130 lb against his
  own ~300 lb of body-plus-kit — corrected in a same-day follow-up to
  180 lb. Chris Ostlind settled the physics plainly: displacement means the
  complete weight a hull can support at its design waterline, including the
  boat's own weight plus anything carried aboard, so 180 lb would only suit
  a canoe roughly six feet long and nine inches wide. This directly extends
  the archive's standing weight-vs-displacement debate (Archimedes'-principle
  side). Folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) (extends
  "Weight versus displacement", cites [103]-[105], 105 total).

  **(4) A reading-list addition.** Nels passed Derek Clark a link to an
  online small-boat-voyage e-book hosted at ibiblio.org. Folded into
  [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  (extends "A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cite [8], 8 total), with
  a narrate-once-cross-link bullet on [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md)
  (cite [132], 132 total).

  **(5) The halyard-fixing-point question (closed in batch 281) got a late,
  concrete coda.** Brent Geery, reading the plans' page-1 sail drawing
  directly, supplied precise measurements for the sail-gap/halyard fixing
  point — 664.35 mm from the front, 1935.65 mm from the back — advising
  the reader still double-check against their own plan set. Folded into
  [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md) (extends "Halyard fixing point on the
  spar: an open question", cite [184], 184 total).

  No new person or boat pages. **Bill/BllFs6** (`bllfsh666`) is a genuinely

* **Update (batch page_2821-page_2830, msgnum 2852-2853-2854-2855-2856-2857-2858-2859-2860-2861,
  continuing directly from batch 282's last msgnum 2851; no new gap in this
  range)**: 14-15 June 2005. 0 files created, 4 extended
  (operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md, matt-layden/mini-paradox.md,
  matt-layden/biography.md, references/off-topic-cross-posts.md).

  **(1) The "Batteries as ballast?" thread's human-power tangent closed
  out, plus a real-world design confirming the concept.** Continuing
  directly from batch 282's opening of this thread, Bill/BllFs6 doubted
  occasional weekend rowing keeps the relevant muscles conditioned for
  when they are actually needed, and corrected his own earlier battery-weight
  estimate (a 100 amp-hour 12V deep-cycle battery weighs closer to 50 lb
  than 100 lb). Larry Whited (LJGeib/lorenzojose2000 -- the already-
  established, separate Larry, not the below-threshold UK newcomer of the
  same first name) supplied a published trained-cyclist figure (200 W for
  20 min, about 0.27 hp, with Lance Armstrong roughly doubling that), and
  Bill used it to peg an in-shape cyclist at roughly 1/6 hp continuous leg
  power (less for arms alone), closing with a joking pedal-drive/folding-
  bicycle hybrid and a defense of "that beer gut" as flotation-cum-
  insulation. Nels then closed the thread for real with a genuine built
  example: Phil Bolger's Bird Watcher II already uses batteries for
  ballast and stability, carries enough solar panels to stay charged
  except under extended heavy use, is easier to trailer/launch than it
  looks despite being visually larger than "Old Shoe," and can also be
  rowed, sculled with a yuloh, or driven by a 25 lb 2 hp four-stroke in a
  waterproof nacelle. Folded into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md)
  as two new sections ("How much continuous power a human body can
  actually generate" and "A real design using battery ballast: the Bird
  Watcher II"), extending "An electric outboard, and gel-cell batteries
  doing double duty as ballast" (cites [34]-[37], 37 total).

  **(2) "What software is Matt using?" -- the mini-Paradox renderings
  identified as Rhino 3D.** Larry Whited, looking at Matt Layden's
  brother's mini-Paradox photo site, asked what 3D software produced the
  JPEGs; Chris Ostlind identified it as Rhino 3D (the same program he uses
  himself), noting it handles chine or smooth-hulled boats and takes a
  RhinoMarine hydrodynamic-analysis plugin from Proteus Engineering (also
  makers of FastShip), though the computer that produced these particular
  screen grabs was not running RhinoMarine. Folded into
  [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md) as a new
  section "The 3D modeling software behind the published renderings"
  (cites [15]-[16], 16 total).

  **(3) Derek Clark's secondhand Wee Rob Roy canoe purchase question, and
  a confirmed Matt Layden fact about his own Everglades-Challenge
  canoes.** Off-topic, non-Paradox: Derek Clark described a used Ian
  Oughtred "Wee Rob Roy" canoe (12'6", 28 in beam, 30 lb) he was
  considering buying, with the seller's claimed 180 lb displacement
  (continuing the exact canoe already flagged in batch 282's item 3, now
  with dimensions and a design name attached). Nels reacted enthusiastically,
  guessed she could handle over 200 lb with reduced freeboard, and -- in
  passing -- stated that Matt Layden loves the Rob Roy design and drew his
  direct inspiration from it for the canoes he used in his last two
  Everglades Challenge entries, a new, citable confirmation of the design
  lineage behind the canoe already documented on
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md#the-14-ft-rob-roy-based-canoe-and-the-2005-everglades-challenge).
  Chris Ostlind closed with his own similar-design carrying-capacity data
  (220 lb at 4 in draft, 9 in midship depth, +85 lb per additional inch).
  Folded into
  references/off-topic-cross-posts.md
  as a new section "Derek Clark's secondhand Wee Rob Roy canoe" (3
  section-local citations, matching this file's existing per-section
  numbering convention), with the Matt Layden fact folded into
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md) (extends "The 14 ft
  Rob Roy-based canoe, and the 2005 Everglades Challenge", cite [56], 56
  total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 281)

* **Update (batch page_2801–page_2810, msgnum 2832-2833-2834-2835-2836-2837-2838-2839-2840-2841,
  continuing directly from batch 280's last msgnum 2831; no new gap in this range)**:
  13–14 June 2005. 0 files created, 7 extended (design/rig.md,
  construction/sailmaking.md, references/building-plans.md,
  operations/radio-and-electronics.md, people/alastair-law.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md, people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md).

  **(1) The halyard-fixing-point question (opened batch 280) answered.**
  Left open at the end of batch 280, three builders gave independent
  answers for where the halyard's fixing point belongs on the top spar:
  **Alastair** ties the halyard round the spar through a hoop screwed to
  its underside near the sail's own gap in the cloth, movable if needed;
  **Jack Gardiner** uses the center of the yard's tapers, roughly 700 mm in,
  tied off rather than hard-fixed, though never confirmed correct; **Glen
  Maxwell** could not recall the dimension from memory but confirmed it is
  given on the plans. **Derek Clark**, the original asker, was not sure his
  own sail even has a gap in the cloth and planned to check before cutting
  one. Folded into [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md) (extends "Halyard fixing
  point on the spar: an open question", cites [180]-[183], 183 total).
  Narrate-once-cross-link additions to [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md)
  (cite [252], 254 total), [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md)
  (cite [137], 137 total — pre-existing [53] gap unaffected), and
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [48], 48 total).

  **(2) A light coda to the closed Mk2-sail luff-tape thread.** Jack
  Gardiner thanked Derek Clark for his Jekylls tape-size data (closed in
  batch 280) and added a passing remark that the enclosed tape likely
  carries some stretch of its own. Folded into
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md) (extends "A
  suspected cause of creasing in the newer sail plan, and the case for a
  professional loft", cite [24], 24 total). Cross-link addition to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [136], folded
  into the same paragraph as item 1's cross-link).

  **(3) The "More Paradox Plans Questions" backlog (opened batch 280)
  extended with precise cleat figures and a plans-error note.** Continuing
  Brent Geery's plans-reading backlog, **Alastair** gave his own exact
  bulkhead 1 bin-side cleat placement (120 mm to port, 220 mm to starboard
  of the centerline, corrected in a follow-up from an initial "12 mm to
  port" typo), confirmed the bulkhead 2 cleat at 390 mm to port belongs to
  the aft bin, described his own tank-vent pipe routing, and advised fitting
  outboard bulkhead cleats loose/wide and leaving bevels unfinished until
  trial-fitting the sides. Brent Geery, in reply, flagged a suspected
  drafting error on the bulkhead 2 sheet (a cleat-arrow seemingly
  misplaced, implying a port bin that other sheets show absent), reasoned
  that the bin sides' semi-structural role on an already over-designed boat
  makes a few cut corners low-risk, and shared his own ~1,000-movie DVD-R
  storage solution as an aside. Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (extends
  "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of questions", cites
  [116]-[119], 119 total). Cross-link additions to
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cites [253]-[254], 254 total).

  **(4) The wind-generator/heavy-weather thread closed with an Air-X
  reliability note.** Replying to Alastair's wind-against-tide account
  (closed in batch 280), Brent Geery joked about a submarine being more
  apt, clarified he would not rely on excess electricity for safety
  functions, and noted the Air-X wind generator's main reported weakness is
  radio-frequency interference rather than any mechanical shortcoming — the
  units are sold as marinized, though how successfully is unconfirmed on
  record. Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extends "A wind generator and radar: a scale-mockup exercise, and how
  little power a Paradox actually needs", cite [19], 19 total). Brent
  Geery has no person page of his own (standing below-threshold
  correspondent), so this citation lives only in
  operations/radio-and-electronics.md, not on a person page.

  No new person or boat pages. Brent Geery (`fasttimes`/`brentgeery`)
  remains a standing below-threshold correspondent (no boat/build of his
  own on record; a prolific plans-questions asker since batch 270) — not

  once this batch (the original halyard question, answered above) and as a
  quoted correspondent throughout — reconfirmed **STRENGTHENED but NOT

## 2026-07-05 (batch 280)

* **Update (batch page_2791–page_2800, msgnum 2822-2823-2824-2825-2826-2827-2828-2829-2830-2831,
  continuing directly from batch 279's last msgnum 2821; no new gap in this range)**:
  11–13 June 2005 (the archive's densest single-day cluster tails off into a
  second and third day). 0 files created, 7 extended
  (operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md, construction/sailmaking.md,
  matt-layden/mini-paradox.md, design/rig.md, references/building-plans.md,
  people/alastair-law.md, people/jack-gardiner.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md — 8 files total).

  **(1) A wind-against-tide encounter closed out: dry inside despite lost
  deck gear, credited to the design.** Continuing batch 279's storm account,
  **Alastair** reported that a PVC-tape sheer line applied for earlier launch
  photographs was washed almost entirely off the hull during the same trip
  and ended up wrapped around the mast and cabin — physical evidence of how
  much green water came aboard — yet not a drop of water got inside the
  boat. Asked whether that result reflected the design or his own
  workmanship, Alastair credited the **design** (singling out the cabin
  hatch seal as "so simple and elegant"), rating his own workmanship a
  distant second. Folded into
  [operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md)
  (extends "A wind-against-tide encounter, and the case against foredeck
  storage", cites [35]-[37], 37 total). Narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cites [249]-[251], 251 total).

  **(2) Mk2 sail luff tape: the sizing question finally closed with two
  independent data points.** Closing out the creasing/luff-tape thread
  running since batch 276, **Glen Maxwell** measured his own Dabbler
  Sails-built sail: luff tape **1 in or 2 in, folded in half over the
  luff**— thanked by **Jack Gardiner** ("Thanks Glen!"). Separately, **Derek
  Clark** checked his own Jeckylls-built sail: tape **25 mm wide, enclosed
  inside a folded hem**, matching in width/thickness on luff and leech
  though sewn with different stitch patterns (parallel zigzag vs. straight).
  Folded into
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md) (extends "A
  suspected cause of creasing in the newer sail plan, and the case for a
  professional loft", cites [21]-[23], 23 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite
  [135], 135 total — jack-gardiner.md's pre-existing gap at [53] predates
  this batch, unaffected) and
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [47], 47 total).

  **(3) Mini Paradox's chine runners settled as a keel extension for
  center-of-lateral-plane purposes.** Answering an open question held over
  from batch 279, **Glen Maxwell** confirmed a chine-runner boat's runner
  area should indeed be treated as a keel extension when computing center of
  lateral plane — adding the runners' profile to the bottom of the chine
  curve and computing lateral resistance from the combined profile "would be
  real close" to the true figure. Folded into
  [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md) (extends
  "Runner placement compared to a low-aspect-ratio keel, and a
  center-of-lateral-plane question", cite [14], 14 total).

  **(4) A new open question: the halyard's fixing point on the top spar.**
  **Derek Clark** asked where on the top spar (yard) the halyard's own
  fixing point should sit — a distinct question from the masthead-hole/
  quarter-turn-twist question already on record — left unanswered on
  record. Folded into [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md) as a new section
  "Halyard fixing point on the spar: an open question" (cite [179], 179
  total).

  **(5) The "reading the plans" backlog grows: hull rocker resolves an
  apparent bulkhead-level mismatch, and cleat dimensions are not critical.**
  A newcomer working through the plans page by page flagged an apparent
  ~20 mm mismatch between bulkhead 1's and bulkhead 2's stateroom-sole
  support levels, plus inconsistent bin-side cleat callouts and an
  ambiguous outboard-cleat bevel dimension. **Derek Clark** resolved the
  level question — the hull's own rocker, not a plans error, explains the
  apparent mismatch (bulkhead 1 sits considerably higher than bulkhead 2) —
  and confirmed cleat dimensions are not critical, offering scrap-stock
  substitution and a "let the force guide you" philosophy for the plans'
  minor ambiguities; he also detailed his own decision to skip the
  plans' bin-side and tank-vent/mast-ground fittings entirely, for
  reasons already on record (no internal water tanks, no wooden-mast
  lightning grounding needed in his location). Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (extends
  "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of questions", cites
  [114]-[115], 115 total).

  **Standing-watch checks.** David Beard, David LeBlanc, and Smith (all three
  distinct identities) did not appear this batch — no confusion possible.
  Derek Clark (`dclark@`/`dclark52001`) appeared as author three times
  (halyard question, sail-tape data, plans answer), confirmed via headers
  each time, correctly not conflated with Smith and correctly given no link to
  people/david-c-smith.md per standing instruction (STRENGTHENED promote-later

  standing below-threshold "Questions regarding Paradox plans" role — no new

  Roberts, and two distinct Rons watches were not triggered (no traffic from

  Citation-collision check clean across all eight touched files via full

  jack-gardiner.md's single pre-existing gap at [53] predates this batch,

## 2026-07-05 (batch 279)

* **Update (batch page_2781–page_2790, msgnum 2812-2813-2814-2815-2816-2817-2818-2819-2820-2821,
  continuing directly from batch 278's last msgnum 2811; no new gap in this range)**:
  10–11 June 2005 (same day/next day as batch 278 — the archive's densest
  single-day cluster continues). 0 files created, 9 extended
  (matt-layden/mini-paradox.md, chine-runners/how-they-work.md,
  operations/radio-and-electronics.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md, construction/sailmaking.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md, operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md,
  people/alastair-law.md, people/nels.md).

  **(1) Mini Paradox — speed attributed to skill and light weight, a
  keel-extension question, and the plans-release policy.** Closing out the
  "Mini Paradox" thread from batches 277-278, **Glen Maxwell** answered
  **Ray**'s open question on the boat's tortured-in bow V: not sure, but
  more likely her speed comes from being sailed by someone who knows small
  sharpies well and from her being very light, guessing her sail-area-to-
  displacement ratio beats Paradox's own (he had hoped to race Matt directly
  to find out, but the two were never in the same place at once). Ray then
  estimated Mini Paradox's all-up displacement (130 lb boat + 250 lb crew ≈
  400 lb) at under a third of Paradox's own, observed the runners are placed
  like a low-aspect-ratio keel (ending abruptly aft, faired in forward), and
  asked whether runner area should count as a keel extension when computing
  center of lateral plane (left open on record) — separately reporting he
  had finally ordered the Paradox plans. Answering a private query from
  **Dan Matyja** (cc'd to the list, a new below-threshold correspondent),
  Glen confirmed **Matt Layden** will not release plans for a boat he has
  not personally built and sailed enough to trust, though strong interest
  and his own satisfaction with the boat keep the door open, and pointed to
  his brother's website's full build-process documentation. Folded into
  [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md) (cites
  [10]-[13], 13 total, two new sections: "Runner placement compared to a
  low-aspect-ratio keel…" and "Whether Matt Layden will release plans for
  Mini Paradox"). Narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [46], within the [44]-[46] range below).

  **(2) A 2005 primary-source designer's quote on chine runners.** **Glen
  Maxwell** posted, without inviting fresh debate, a quote from **Matt
  Layden** himself describing the chine runners/winglets as small
  horizontally protruding fins that let the hull act as a "lifting body" to
  resist leeway; Layden states a centerboard would work better but he has
  "a mental allergy to moving parts and complication," and the runners are
  easy to build, protect rather than weaken the hull, and take no interior
  space. This 2005 statement predates and independently corroborates the
  fuller 2007 *Small Craft Advisor* interview already on file. Folded into
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) as a new
  section "An earlier designer's quote (2005), posted directly to the list"
  immediately after the 2007 account (cite [54], 54 total).

  **(3) The mast-weight-for-roll report, resolved.** **Brent Geery** located
  the report Glen Maxwell had been unable to relocate in batch 278: a
  January 2004 mast-construction exchange in which **Smith** said a heavier
  carbon-fiber/wood composite mast "would provide a more comfortable
  motion," to which Glen had replied a composite mast would run "about half
  the weight and triple the strength" — a case for a lighter, stronger mast
  rather than a heavier one added on purpose for roll comfort. Glen closed
  the thread: "Keeping weight aloft down is ALWAYS a good thing." Folded
  into [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extension to "A wind generator and radar", cites [17]-[18], 18 total);
  the "unrecovered archive report" section on
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  was updated to reflect the resolution (cites [44]-[45], 46 total with [46]
  above).

  **(4) Mk2 sail — luff tape confirmed universal, size left open.** **Jack
  Gardiner** (`tidybowlmann`) closed his own creasing thread with a
  one-liner ("Knowing the size would help getting some on my sail"),
  quoting Glen Maxwell's luff-tape confirmation from batch 278 in full; the
  size question was never answered on record. Folded into
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md) (cites
  [19]-[20], 20 total, closing "A suspected cause of creasing…" with the
  "no sail is known to have been built without luff tape" restatement).
  Narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [134], 134
  total — a pre-existing gap at [53] predates this batch and is unrelated to
  this addition; not re-numbered, per the no-renumbering rule).

  **(5) A wind-against-tide firsthand account, and a case against foredeck
  storage.** **Alastair** (Yeovil, England) recounted a tactical error on an
  early coastal trip that put him close-hauled in a fast tide running
  against the wind: the boat handled the conditions with no fuss (he fared
  slightly less well, often seasick the first day out), and a large
  quantity of green water passed over the cabin throughout — settling a
  practical case against loose foredeck storage of fuel or an outboard, and
  prompting a recommendation to test any safety-critical electrical
  installation "with a fire hose before setting off," since such conditions
  are common on the south coast of the UK. **Nels** seconded the account in
  one line ("Words of deep wisdom Al"). Folded into
  [operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md)
  as a new section "A wind-against-tide encounter, and the case against
  foredeck storage" (cites [33]-[34], 34 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  additions to [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cite [248], 248
  total) and [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cite [131], 131 total).

  **Standing-watch checks.** David Beard, David LeBlanc, Smith, and Derek Clark
  (all four distinct identities) did not appear this batch — no confusion
  possible. Jack Gardiner ("John Gardiner"/`tidybowlmann`) appeared and was
  correctly kept distinct from John Hall (`jdmhall`, Tasmania), who did not
  appear this batch. The two distinct UK Jeffs, three distinct Larrys, two
  distinct Roberts, and two distinct Rons watches were not triggered (no
  traffic from any of those identities). Alastair's "Al, Yeovil, England"
  signature on page_2786/2792 was confirmed to be the same established

  Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split

  Ray (`rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`), John Hall, and Dan Matyja remain

## 2026-07-05 (batch 278)

* **Update (batch page_2771–page_2780, msgnum 2802-2803-2804-2805-2806-2807-2808-2809-2810-2811,
  continuing directly from batch 277's last msgnum 2801; no new gap in this range)**:
  10–11 June 2005. 0 files created, 6 extended
  (matt-layden/mini-paradox.md, operations/radio-and-electronics.md,
  construction/sailmaking.md, references/building-plans.md,
  people/alastair-law.md, people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md).

  **(1) Mini Paradox — the runner-placement and chine-curvature question
  resolved, and a catamaran hypothetical.** **Glen Maxwell** answered
  **Ray**'s open question from batch 277 directly: Mini Paradox's chine
  runners start farther aft and end a little farther forward than on a
  stock Paradox, an effect he attributed to **end-plate effect** rather
  than the chine's curvature. Ray, on reflection, judged end-plate effect
  the likelier mechanism, since pitching would constantly change a
  curvature's orientation relative to the boat's motion, and pointed to the
  Bolducs' `microcruising.com` site's own Mini Paradox construction page.
  Ray also asked, hypothetically, whether Glen would fit chine runners to
  both sides of both hulls of a catamaran; Glen answered only the outboard
  side of each, and **Chris Ostlind** gave a fuller technical answer arguing
  against inner-hull runners on a catamaran (surface turbulence, the other
  hull's bow wake, an uneven heel angle between hulls) in favor of larger
  outboard runners, drawing a parallel to molded keel-form appendages on
  beach catamaran hulls. A new correspondent, **John Hall** (`jdmhall`,
  Tasmania — a standing below-threshold identity), pointed to the Bolducs'
  construction photographs directly, describing the runners as following
  the hull's curve ("sea of peas") and linking a second page showing the
  boat car-topped; Ray agreed some curvature is visible but remained unsure
  of its function, and separately asked Glen, unanswered on record, whether
  Mini Paradox's speed owed anything to a slight tortured-in V at the bow.
  Folded into [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md)
  (cites [4]-[9], 9 total). Narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cites [42]-[43], 43 total).

  **(2) Wind generator/radar mockup — a corrected drawing, and an
  unrecovered mast-weight-for-roll report.** **Brent Geery** posted a
  corrected version of his scale mockup (both units re-scaled to within
  about 3mm of accurate size, plus a corrected keep-clear-zone marking).
  **Glen Maxwell**, independently, recalled from having read the entire
  list archive a past report of a builder considering adding mast weight
  to damp a Paradox's roll — the same report Brent had separately mentioned
  being unable to relocate the batch before — and noted he personally had
  never experienced roll problems himself. Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extension to "A wind generator and radar", cites [15]-[16], 16 total).
  Narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [40], within the [40]-[43] range above).

  **(3) Mk2 sail creasing — Glen Maxwell's luff-tape confirmation.**
  Answering Jack Gardiner's luff-tape/creasing theory from batch 277, Glen
  Maxwell stated he had never seen a Paradox sail, or any other sail, built
  without luff tape — treating it as standard sailmaking practice rather
  than an optional detail. Folded into
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md) (extension to
  "A suspected cause of creasing in the newer sail plan, and the case for a
  professional loft", cite [19], 19 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [41], within the [40]-[43] range above).

  **(4) Plans backlog — a worked 18 mm plywood cutting-diagram answer.**
  **Alastair** answered Brent Geery's request for a cutting diagram of the
  costlier 18 mm sheet, working through the vent trunk sides/front, breast
  hook, and rudder pieces' exact layout (flipping the bottom rudder piece
  to fit inside bulkhead 3, which incidentally also solves the rudder
  "nose" falling off the sheet's edge), and suggested taping up newspaper
  templates for anyone without CAD software to test a layout by hand.
  Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (extension
  to "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of questions",
  cite [113], 113 total). Narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cite [247], 247 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 277)

* **Update (batch page_2761–page_2770, msgnum 2792-2793-2794-2795-2796-2797-2798-2799-2800-2801,
  continuing directly from batch 276's last msgnum 2791; no new gap in this range)**:
  9–10 June 2005. 0 files created, 4 extended
  (references/building-plans.md, operations/radio-and-electronics.md,
  people/alastair-law.md, matt-layden/mini-paradox.md).

  **(1) "Questions regarding Paradox plans" thread, closed out further.**
  **Alastair** corrected **Derek Clark**'s misreading of the page-5
  metal-angle fitting — the strap folds across its width, not down its
  length, forming a small bracket that stops the bin face's middle flapping
  loose and holds it to the floor panel — which Derek accepted without
  further pushback. **Brent Geery** then worked through five further
  follow-ups: asked whether a circulated new-sail drawing's 8.5 in
  boom-trim and 4–4.5 in mast-trim figures were accurate (closely matching
  Glen Maxwell's own 8 in estimate from the prior batch); reported he still
  could not fully follow Alastair's Drawing #12 correction, being only up to
  page 7 of his own build; replied to Derek Clark confirming his stock-first,
  trim-later plan and reporting the Paradoxboatbuilders group's files/photo
  sections nearly empty when he checked for Don Elliott's colored mast-plan
  redrawing; reasoned through why the metal-angle-and-bin-face arrangement
  forward leaves roughly 4 in of the sole unsupported at bulkhead 3, unlike
  the hinge-supported aft sections, concluding he might be over-thinking a
  detail with no reported practical problems; and worked through Alastair's
  full plywood-sourcing answer part by part, asking for a cutting diagram of
  the costlier 18 mm sheet and accepting he may need extra 6 mm ply given
  his local stock runs slightly larger than Alastair's metric sheets. Folded
  into [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md)
  (extension to "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of
  questions", cites [106]-[112], 112 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cite [246], 246
  total).

  **(2) Wind generator/radar mockup — closed out with the builder's own
  replies.** **Brent Geery** answered both open questions from batch 276:
  to **Chris Ostlind**'s righting-moment/radar-reflector question, proposed
  offsetting a masthead radar's weight with a lighter mast material (Sitka
  spruce or carbon fiber) and no lightning rod, subject to a proper
  calculation, recalled an unlocated archive report of a builder adding
  mast weight on purpose to damp roll, judged active radar preferable to a
  passive reflector whenever affordable, and passed on the foil-stuffed
  hollow-mast trick for radar return as a fallback; to **Alastair**'s
  NiCad-battery power-budget counterpoint, explained his own full-timing
  liveaboard power needs (laptop/internet, entertainment, digital
  photography, 12V heaters to stay warm without burning propane, a small
  watermaker run off surplus generation), while agreeing a wind generator
  would be wasted on a weekend sailor. Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extension to "A wind generator and radar", cites [13]-[14], 14 total).

  **(3) Mini Paradox — an open question on runner placement.** **Ray**
  asked **Glen Maxwell** whether Mini Paradox's chine runners start further
  aft or end short of the transom compared to a stock Paradox, and whether
  either detail bears on the theory that the runners work because of the
  chine's curvature — left unanswered on record. Folded into
  [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md) (new section
  "An open question: runner placement and the chine-curvature theory", cite
  [3], 3 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 276)

* **Update (batch page_2751–page_2760, msgnum 2782-2783-2784-2785-2786-2787-2788-2789-2790-2791,
  continuing directly from batch 275's last msgnum 2781; no new gap in this range)**:

  (matt-layden/mini-paradox.md), 7 extended
  (references/building-plans.md, construction/sailmaking.md,
  operations/radio-and-electronics.md, people/jack-gardiner.md,
  people/alastair-law.md, people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md,
  people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md — 8 files touched in total),
  plus matt-layden/index.md updated to list the new design.

  **(1) "Questions regarding Paradox plans" thread, closed out further.**
  Continuing directly from batch 275, five more replies filled in remaining
  detail: **Derek Clark** described cutting his spar blanks to the plans'
  stock lengths pending final trim against the finished sail, pointed to
  **Don Elliott**'s hand-colored mast-plan redrawing and **Al Law**'s own
  plan-reading write-up, gave a fuller reading of the page-5 metal-angle
  detail (bent from 2 mm x 25 mm strap, fitted under the water-tank top),
  and described his own alternative — no internal water tanks, floor run to
  within 1/4 in of the hull sides, bin sides resting directly on the floor;
  **Glen Maxwell** confirmed spar sizes are unchanged between the original
  and newer sail plans (only the boom needs roughly 8 in trimmed, and only
  once the new sail is in hand), and that he has sailed both sail plans on
  "Zoë," judging the original flat cut good enough that he might still use
  it were it not for the newer sail's added draft; he separately gave a
  fuller recap answer to Brent Geery's outstanding questions (glass yardage,
  shelf mounting, the metal angle) duplicating detail already captured
  piecemeal earlier in the thread; and **Alastair** added that anything
  built into the forward stateroom must fit back out through its opening,
  installed before the deck goes on, and that he kept his own boat's stock
  central partition while reducing its height. Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (extension
  to "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of questions", cites
  [101]-[105], 105 total). Narrate-once-cross-link additions to
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cite [239], 239 total) and
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cite [245], 245 total).

  **(2) "Mk2 sail" — a suspected cause of creasing in the newer sail plan.**
  **Jack Gardiner** floated missing luff tape as the likely cause of
  creasing reported in Matt Layden's taller Mk2 sail plan, reasoning the
  luff would otherwise shrink in light air and stretch again once the sail
  filled; **Derek Clark** agreed it was "a good question" he had never
  thought to check on his own professionally-made sail (from Jekylls of
  Wroxham), and undertook to inspect and report back. Folded into
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md) (new section "A
  suspected cause of creasing in the newer sail plan, and the case for a
  professional loft", cites [17]-[18], 18 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  addition to [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite
  [133], 133 total — file retains its pre-existing, unrelated numbering gap
  at [53] predating this batch).

  **(3) Wind generator/radar mockup — a righting-moment and radar-reflector
  follow-up.** New below-threshold correspondent **Chris Ostlind**
  ("wedgesail") followed up on Brent Geery's scale wind-generator/radar
  mockup (from batch 275), asking whether the roughly 9 lb masthead unit
  would need a righting-moment correction and pressing the case for pairing
  any radar fit with a proper radar reflector, given how low a Paradox sits
  in the water — illustrated with his own account of capsizing a racing
  catamaran at night in a shipping channel outside Los Angeles Harbor.
  Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (extension to "A wind generator and radar", cite [12], 12 total).

  **(4) New page: "Mini Paradox" — an experimental Matt Layden variant.**
  A correspondent asked whether chine runners were absent from photographs
  of a narrower "Mini Paradox" design; **Glen Maxwell**, who has sailed
  aboard her with Matt Layden and his wife Karen, confirmed the runners are
  present (just hard to see in photos), that the sail is shorter but wider
  as Matt explores a higher aspect ratio, and that she "sails like a witch"
  and is very roomy for her size. New file
  [matt-layden/mini-paradox.md](/matt-layden/mini-paradox.md) (cites
  [1]-[2], 2 total); [matt-layden/index.md](/matt-layden/index.md) updated
  to list the new design; narrate-once-cross-link addition to
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (cite [39], 39 total).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 275)

* **Update (batch page_2741–page_2750, msgnum 2772-2773-2774-2775-2776-2777-2778-2779-2780-2781,
  continuing directly from batch 274's 2771; no new gap in this range)**:
  8–9 June 2005. No new files, 5 extended
  (references/building-plans.md, operations/radio-and-electronics.md,
  people/jack-gardiner.md, people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md,
  people/alastair-law.md — 5 files touched in total).

  **(1) "Questions regarding Paradox plans" backlog thread, answered in
  full.** Continuing directly from batch 274, **Brent Geery**'s plans-reading
  backlog (glass yardage, shelf mounting, the page-5 "metal angle," and a
  follow-up on mast wood/hinges) drew answers from four correspondents:
  **Jack Gardiner** explained his choice of 12 oz biax over woven roving
  (no mat backing needed, less epoxy, no wear-through on a similarly-built
  19 ft powerboat after years of diving use) and clarified his mast's
  finished length; **Glen Maxwell** explained Drawing #12's mixed full-size/
  scaled spar-dimensioning convention; **Derek Clark** described his own
  webbing-and-copper-tack hinges and a single centerline-hinged floor built
  in place of the stock stateroom divider; and **Alastair** gave a full
  point-by-point answer (glass on 50 in rolls for join-free panels, shelf
  epoxy-fillet mounting, a bent-stainless metal angle, and sourcing for each
  "missing" plywood part) plus spotted a probable **Drawing #12 drafting
  error** (stray dimension-arrow marks) affecting the sheet's readability.
  Folded into [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md)
  (extension to "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of
  questions", cites [93]-[100], 100 total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullets
  added to [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [132],
  132 total — file retains a pre-existing, unrelated numbering gap at [53]
  predating this batch), [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cite [238], 238 total), and [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md)
  (cites [243]-[244], 244 total).

  **(2) A wind generator and radar scale mockup, and how little power a
  Paradox actually needs.** **Brent Geery** built and posted a scale mockup
  testing whether a small wind generator (Southwest Windpower's Air-X
  Marine, 13 lb) and radar (JRC's Radar 1000 Mk II, under 9 lb) could
  plausibly fit a Paradox. **Alastair** answered with a real-world
  power-budget counterpoint: a single 1977-salvaged 12V wet NiCad battery,
  charged once each spring, runs his GPS, echo sounder, running lights, and
  water-ballast/bilge pump all season unattended — illustrating how modest
  the boat's baseline load is without radar or a wind generator, though he
  allowed a radar installation specifically would justify the added
  generating capacity. Folded into
  [operations/radio-and-electronics.md](/operations/radio-and-electronics.md)
  (new section "A wind generator and radar: a scale-mockup exercise, and how
  little power a Paradox actually needs", cites [10]-[11], 11 total).
  Narrate-once-cross-link bullet added to
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (covered within the same
  cites [243]-[244] extension above plus a dedicated electrics bullet).

## 2026-07-05 (batch 274)

* **Update (batch page_2731–page_2740, msgnum 2762-2763-2764-2765-2766-2767-2768-2769-2770-2771,
  continuing directly from batch 273's 2761; no new gap in this range)**:
  7–8 June 2005. No new files, 6 extended
  (design/variants/stretched-paradox.md, chine-runners/how-they-work.md,
  operations/crew-size-and-fit.md, references/building-plans.md,

  construction/hull-planking.md — 6 files touched in total).

  **(1) "Sleep two in a Paradox?" thread closed out.** Continuing directly
  from batch 273's exchange, **Alastair** gave the as-designed flat floor's
  precise dimensions — 1.9 m by 0.94 m, tapering to 0.78 m at the narrow end
  for the mast step — and how much more space can be gained by sacrificing
  side-bin storage (~100 mm) or flotation as well (~200 mm). Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md)
  (extension to "Sleeping two aboard a Paradox-family cabin", cite [90],
  90 total — file retains the pre-existing, unrelated numbering gap between
  [53] and [88] predating this batch).

  **(2) "chine runners": is a flat bottom essential?** **Ray**
  (`rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`) asked whether the chine runners' success
  depends on Paradox's flat-bottomed hull specifically, citing Wharram
  catamaran builders' unsuccessful "vortex generator" experiment (a
  narrow-V hull, unlike Paradox) and Nathaniel Herreshoff's flat-bottomed
  catamaran Amaryllis — fast enough under gaff rig, in her day, that
  multihulls were subsequently banned from racing monohulls — as a
  historical precedent that a flat bottom need not be a performance
  liability. Unanswered on record. Folded into
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md) (new
  section "Whether a flat bottom is essential to the mechanism", cite [53],
  53 total).

  **(3) "scaled-up Paradox": has anyone actually built one?** Ray also asked
  whether anyone had gone ahead and built one of the long-discussed
  enlarged/two-up Paradox variants. No further completed build beyond Pete
  Hodges's "Paradox 1.1" is on record in answer. Folded into
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md)
  (new section "A recurring question: has anyone actually built a scaled-up
  Paradox?", cite [65], 65 total).

  **(4) "Questions regarding Paradox plans" — a first-time builder's
  backlog.** **Brent Geery**, working through the plans page by page,
  compiled a representative backlog of plans-reading questions: what "P&S"
  means (port and starboard), glass yardage estimates, whether mast/boom/
  yard lengths are given (they are, on page 12), hatch-hinge type for the
  stateroom sole hatch (piano hinge), and which plywood parts are genuinely
  missing from the plans' "10 sheets" cutting diagram (several interior
  parts, left to builder discretion). **Jack Gardiner** answered point by
  point from his own finished build. A follow-up sub-thread on woven roving
  versus biaxial cloth drew a full technical explanation from new
  correspondent **James Hanahan** (weave structure, resin uptake, why
  chopped strand mat is incompatible with epoxy). Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (two new
  sections, "Reading the plans: a first-time builder's backlog of
  questions" and "The 12 oz biaxial-versus-woven-roving question,
  continued", cites [86]-[92], 92 total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullet
  added to [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [131],
  131 total — file retains a pre-existing, unrelated numbering gap between
  [52] and [54] predating this batch); cross-link-only pointer added to
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md) (no new
  citation, 33 total unchanged) from the existing woven-roving-vs-biax
  section to James Hanahan's fuller technical answer.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 273)

* **Update (batch page_2721–page_2730, msgnum 2751-2752-2754-2755-2756-2757-2758-2759-2760-2761,
  continuing directly from batch 272's 2750; msgnum 2753 is a newly
  identified gap, not previously flagged)**: 3-6 June 2005. No new files,
  6 extended (references/beachcruising-book.md,
  construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md, design/rig.md,
  operations/crew-size-and-fit.md — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to
  people/nels.md, people/david-beard.md, and
  people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md — 7 files touched in total).

  **(1) Reading-list thread continued.** **Nels** followed up his own
  "Books" recommendations with a pointer to the Pardeys' own website,
  landlpardey.com, for further living-aboard tips. Folded into
  [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cite [7], 7 total).

  **(2) "Finish" thread closed out.** Continuing directly from batch 272's
  weave-filling discussion, **Derek Clark** agreed filling the weave and
  finishing properly makes sense, and reported a viral infection had set
  his decking back a further week, doubting "Enuf" would reach the water
  this year at all. **Nels** answered with practical finishing guidance:
  a warning about **amine blush** (a water-soluble epoxy cure byproduct in
  moist air, removable with water/vinegar or diluted ammonia) and a
  time-pressed builder's sequence — fill the weave, apply two thinned
  (~10%) paint coats, then go sailing, finishing fully (bright work
  included) the following season if motivated, sanding the sheen off
  between coats with 80-100 grit before moving to finer grits or wet
  sanding much later. Derek closed the thread reporting himself recovered,
  his hull near complete (decks, chine-runner trimming, full glassing, the
  cabin top, and the mast/spars still outstanding), his plan to trust
  Paradox's get-under-cover "Stock" cruising style rather than pack
  traditional foul-weather gear, and a borrowed folding bicycle stowed
  forward for shore trips. Folded into
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  (extension to "Filling the fiberglass weave before painting...", cites
  [71]-[73], 73 total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullets extended on
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [129]-[130], 130 total).

  **(3) "Heart Pine & Epoxy": a new mast-wood data point.** **David Beard**,
  finishing his second Paradox and starting the mast, asked whether epoxy
  would bond well to old, rock-hard heart pine salvaged from a 1920s house
  remodel. **Alf Sawatzky** confirmed the wood was sound and usable, if the
  builder was prepared to dull blades cutting it; David accepted the
  trade-off. **Larry** (`lorenzojose2000`) gave the fuller technical answer:
  old-growth heart pine's tight grain and lack of knots likely make it
  stronger than pine cut today, with the one real risk being wood that
  overdried in a hot attic rather than a remodeled house — a failure mode
  also seen in old-growth Douglas fir, causing splits when fasteners are
  driven without a pre-drilled pilot hole; recommended testing a piece
  first, with no issue expected using epoxy on it. Folded into
  [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md) ("Wood species for a scratch-built wooden
  mast", new paragraph, cites [175]-[178], 178 total). Narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet added to [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cites
  [240]-[241], 241 total).

  **(4) "Sleep two in a Paradox?" answered with hard dimensions.** A
  prospective builder (**Ray**, `rhaldridge`/`knobmaker`) asked how skinny
  two people would need to be to share a Paradox cabin overnight, and how
  large the air-mattress space actually is. **Glen Maxwell** answered with
  exact figures — the cabin's clear sleeping space is 930 mm (37.2 in) wide
  by 1900 mm (76 in) long, workable for two 170 lb people "if you love each
  other" — but framed the harder problem as day-to-day cohabitation rather
  than fit: with two aboard, simple tasks like cooking in the rain leave
  no room to get out of each other's way. He and his wife's practice-tested
  answer is to pitch a tent ashore as a base camp and return to the boat,
  rather than try to live in the cabin continuously; solo, Glen has spent
  up to five days aboard without going ashore at all. Folded into
  [operations/crew-size-and-fit.md](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md)
  (extension to "Sleeping two aboard a Paradox-family cabin", cites
  [88]-[89], 89 total — file retains a pre-existing, unrelated numbering
  gap between [53] and [88] predating this batch). Narrate-once-cross-link
  bullet added to
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (cites [236]-[237], 237 total).

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link

## 2026-07-05 (batch 272)

* **Update (batch page_2711–page_2720, msgnum 2741-2742-2743-2744-2745-2746-2747-2748-2749-2750,
  continuing directly from batch 271's 2740, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  1-2 June 2005. No new files, 2 extended
  (references/beachcruising-book.md, construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md —
  plus a narrate-once-cross-link touch to people/nels.md — 3 files touched in
  total).

  **(1) "Books" thread closed out.** Continuing directly from batch 271's
  reading-list section, **Derek Clark** replied that "Tilikum" (38 ft) was
  larger and less instructional than he had hoped, naming Shane Acton's
  "Shrimpy" books as closer to what he wanted. **Nels** answered with the
  book's fuller context — Tilikum was a hollow cedar-log Haida Indian dugout
  canoe later decked over and sailed around the world by John Voss, whose
  other boat was only 19 ft; its appendix's storm tactics (trailing warps,
  riding to a sea anchor, staying quartering to the seas, and the older
  practice of streaming oil to blunt breaking seas) still hold value —
  and recommended the Pardeys, Annie Hill's "Voyaging on a Small Income,"
  and Charles Stock's skill at finding storm-sheltering "hidey holes" as
  proof a small boat can be safer than a large one coastal cruising. Folded
  into [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cite [6], 6 total).

  **(2) "Finish": whether filling the fiberglass weave matters beyond
  looks.** **Derek Clark**, finishing "Enuf" for a hard working life rather
  than a showroom look, asked what fairing compound really does and whether
  leaving the weave unfilled costs anything besides cosmetics. **Nels**
  gave the paint-sequence answer (thinned coats sanded between, fairing
  applied only afterward, bottom paint recommended regardless of water
  type); on Derek's narrower follow-up, **Chris Ostlind** and Nels
  converged on **yes** — an unfilled weave adds real hull drag and should be
  filled with clear or microballoon-thickened epoxy even where the hull
  stays unpainted below the waterline, while an open weave separately
  absorbs moisture/dirt/oil, holds paint worse, and leaves bare epoxy
  without its needed UV protection. The thread closed on Nels's broader
  point: water that gains any way in is very hard to keep out afterward, so
  finishing correctly once at the outset — glass and epoxy being
  fundamentally just a matrix keeping water out of the wood — pays back in
  far less annual upkeep than patching a poorly finished hull later. Folded
  into [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md)
  as a new section ("Filling the fiberglass weave before painting: fairing
  compound, and the case against leaving it exposed", cites [64]-[70], 70
  total). Narrate-once-cross-link bullets added to
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (cites [127]-[128], 128 total).

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check: Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf", STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate)

  standing instruction, content genuinely topical rather than build
  narrative. Jason Stancil identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill"

## 2026-07-05 (batch 271)

* **Update (batch page_2701–page_2710, msgnum 2731-2732-2733-2734-2735-2736-2737-2738-2739-2740,
  continuing directly from batch 270's 2730, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  28 May – 1 June 2005. No new files, 8 extended
  (design/rig.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md, operations/running-lights.md,
  design/blue-water-suitability.md, construction/water-tank-lining.md,
  construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md,
  references/beachcruising-book.md — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to
  people/kjell-karlsson.md, people/bolduc/dave.md, people/nels.md, and
  people/jack-gardiner.md — 12 files touched in total).

  **(1) Brent Geery's "Introduction" thread, resolved in full.** Continuing
  directly from batch 270's introduction, **Jack Gardiner** welcomed Brent
  with his own finished-build techniques — exterior ply skinned in two
  layers of 2 oz fabric to stop checking, "blue" extruded-foam insulation
  that does not collect water, a sewn rubber-roofing tiller boot, and
  rubber-clad commercial windows in place of stock lexan — folded into
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) with cross-links to
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md) and
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md).
  **Dave Bolduc** then answered Brent's original four questions and several
  follow-ups in full: **1/4 in lexan** windows are plenty strong across
  three of Matt Layden's own boats, with Little Cruiser's own port windows
  surviving a broadside hit from a 17 ft powerboat at ~15 kts intact but for
  a buffed-out rub mark — folded into a new section on
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  ("Window strength: a decades-long 1/4 in lexan track record, and one
  collision", cites [56]-[57], 57 total), alongside Brent's own
  window-reinforcement ideas (3/8 in lexan, wider hanging knees, a
  reinforcing ring at the bolt holes) as cheap insurance for a boat that is
  also the life raft. Little Cruiser and Swamp Thing's own **tiller-boot**
  and **water-tank** answers, plus Brent's plastic-welder tank idea and a
  raised tank-access objection, were folded into
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md)
  (cites [28]-[29], 29 total). **Insulation** — Celotex (polyisocyanurate,
  identified by Brent) versus white polystyrene versus pink Home Depot
  foam, plus an ants-in-the-Celotex anecdote — and **fir marine plywood**
  sourcing/cost tradeoffs against Okoume were folded into
  [construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md)
  (cites [48]-[53], 53 total). **CCFL versus LED masthead lighting** was
  closed out (LEDs now cheap enough at ~$1 apiece that the Bolducs see no
  reason to switch), alongside Dave's own **$6 solar-garden-light anchor
  light** and its year-round performance, tested a few days later by
  **Alan** (`logicaidnz`) with a seasonal-charging caveat — both folded into
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md) (cites
  [35]-[36], extending the existing CCFL section). No boat/build of Brent
  Geery's own yet, so still no person page — remains on the standing
  below-threshold correspondent list.

  **(2) Kjell Karlsson's "2 pics 2 questions."** Weighed his "ParaPlastic"
  hull (complete except mast/boom/yard/battery, including its 70 kg bronze
  bottom plate) at **326 kg**, folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) ("Independent
  weight and displacement estimates", cite [102], 102 total); also asked,
  unanswered on record, how other builders route a solar panel's cable into
  the cabin (new section on
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md), cite [37],
  37 total) and whether Matt Layden's masthead drawing really does route
  the halyard's deck hole aft of the mast, twisting the halyard a
  quarter-turn (new section on [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md), cite [174],
  174 total).

  **(3) Derek Clark's "Books" thread, closed by Nels.** Derek Clark offered
  his own reading-list starter (Charles Stock's *Sailing Just for Fun*,
  Douglas Alvord's *Beachcruising*, Little and Walsh's *Beachcruising and
  Coastal Camping*, John Mellor's *Pilotage*); **Nels** added a Sven
  Yrvind link and nominated any future writing by the Bolducs or Matt
  Layden as reading-list material in its own right. Folded into a new
  section on
  [references/beachcruising-book.md](/references/beachcruising-book.md)
  ("A wider Paradox-sailor reading list", cites [4]-[5], 5 total).

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings. Narrate-once-cross-link

## 2026-07-05 (batch 270)

* **Update (batch page_2691–page_2700, msgnum 2721-2722-2723-2724-2725-2726-2727-2728-2729-2730,
  continuing directly from batch 269's 2720, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  19–28 May 2005. No new files, 7 extended
  (construction/build-effort.md, design/weight-and-ballast.md,
  construction/non-skid-deck.md, operations/running-lights.md,
  construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md,
  construction/water-tank-lining.md, references/materials-sourcing-links.md
  — plus narrate-once-cross-link touches to people/mat-man22.md,
  people/david-beard.md, and people/alastair-law.md — 10 files touched in total).

  **(1) The "PS" thread — a direct continuation of batch 269's hull-weight
  and moving-methods thread.** **Derek Clark**'s own aside about finding
  scrap lead shaped like pipe discarded in a skip, useful as ready-made
  ballast, was folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) (cite [101],
  101 total). His "PS" grew into a wider exchange: **Adrian Dodd**, feeling
  close to a decision, asked whether Derek's moving crew were big or small
  (since he expected to do all the persuading himself), and weighed a
  precut kit against building from scratch — concluding that completing a
  boat from start to finish is part of Paradox's appeal, and that getting a
  boat in the water matters more than the method. **Alastair** reassured
  him that even a tired-out moving crew can safely leave the hull propped
  on rocks partway through a move, since there is "no danger of putting a
  hole" in the 18mm bottom. **Derek Clark** replied in kind — joking about
  his own moving crew (his 20-year-old son's "weedy" rock-band friends) —
  and closed with the observation that Paradox's real case rests on her
  practical track record, since in his view essentially all the design's
  negative criticism comes from theoreticians rather than actual owners.
  Folded into [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md)'s
  existing "Workspace" section (cites [81]-[83], 84 total).

  **(2) The "Non-skid" thread.** **David Beard** asked about roll-on
  truck-bed liner (Rhino-lining) as a possible deck non-skid. **Alastair**
  answered with a much cheaper find: rubberized stair-tread strip from
  Ikea's childcare section, a bargain at £1.50 a roll. **Mat**
  (`mat_man22`) added that workboat operators use similar spray-applied
  industrial coatings (now available in colors, though not cheap), having
  also seen one sprayed onto an old wooden Chris Craft's hull, and pointed
  to `rec.boats.building` for more. Folded into a new section on
  [construction/non-skid-deck.md](/construction/non-skid-deck.md) ("Truck-bed
  and industrial coatings", cites [6]-[8], 8 total), with narrate-once
  cross-link bullets added to people/alastair-law.md, people/david-beard.md, and
  people/mat-man22.md.

  **(3) A new correspondent's "Introduction," with four build questions.**
  **Brent Geery** (Los Angeles, `fasttimes`/`brentgeery`) introduced
  himself: already owns Don Elliott's construction book, plans just
  arrived, planning a fall build timed to avoid LA's summer heat, building
  as an apartment dweller in the space of a single outdoor parking spot
  (roughly 8 ft x 15 ft). His four questions were each folded into their
  existing topical home: Cold Cathode Fluorescent (CCFL) as a
  higher-efficiency alternative to LED masthead lighting (new section on
  [operations/running-lights.md](/operations/running-lights.md), cite [34],
  34 total); extruded polystyrene (XEPS) over white EPS for insulation,
  since EPS absorbs water
  ([construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md),
  cite [47] in the file's final citation block, which runs 38-47 with no
  gap); a plastic-welded poly-sheet water tank idea
  ([construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md),
  cite [27], 27 total); and marine-plywood sourcing in the Los Angeles area,
  answered by standing correspondent **Chris Ostlind** (`wedgesail`) with a
  supplier recommendation (Allied Plywood/Veneer) — Brent's own follow-up
  found the best-grade stock ran "a little over a grand for 10 sheets."
  Folded into a new section on
  [references/materials-sourcing-links.md](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md)
  ("Marine plywood sourcing in the Los Angeles area", cites [16]-[17], 17
  total). His apartment-build workspace detail was also added to
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md)'s
  "Workspace" section (cite [84]). No boat/build of his own yet, so no
  person page — added to the standing below-threshold correspondent list.

  No new person or boat pages, no prunings. Narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Timeline reached 19–28 May 2005.

## 2026-07-05 (batch 269)

* **Update (batch page_2681–page_2690, msgnum 2711-2712-2713-2714-2715-2716-2717-2718-2719-2720,
  continuing directly from batch 268's 2710, fully consecutive, no new gaps)**:
  15–19 May 2005. No new files, 3 extended
  (operations/head-and-sanitation.md, operations/provisioning-and-water.md,
  construction/build-effort.md — 3 files touched in total).

  This batch closed out all three newcomer questions batch 268 had left
  unanswered on record, each answered in full within a few days by returning
  correspondents.

  **(1) "A Delicate question when cruising" — the toilet/sanitation thread,
  answered in full.** Daved Ferrell's Puget-Sound toilet question drew a
  five-reply round: **Dave Bolduc**'s short porta-potty answer; **Derek
  Clark**'s two further alternatives (a white-water-rafter's ammunition-box
  and cat-litter method, and a "green" composting-toilet design he judged
  could be scaled down to fit a Paradox); **Glen Maxwell**'s pointer to a
  commercial "WAG Bag" disposable kit; a new correspondent, **Paul Reppeto**
  (Olympia, WA), voting for the porta-potty and linking a "sawdust toilet"
  design; and **Dave Bolduc**'s own field report of using exactly that
  sawdust method at home during a plumbing repair, surprised at how well it
  controlled odor. Folded into the existing canonical home
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md)
  (cites [13]-[17], 17 total).

  **(2) Water-container question, answered.** Dave Bolduc gave Daved
  Ferrell his own direct answer: one-quart containers that fold flat,
  similar to clear plastic cubes sold at K-Mart and outdoor stores, topped
  off with two 2.5-gallon cubes; his preferred original small cubes
  (Campmor, Adventure 16) are now discontinued, so he expects to switch to
  space-hungrier round bottles once his stock wears out. Folded into the
  existing canonical home
  [operations/provisioning-and-water.md](/operations/provisioning-and-water.md#storage-individual-water-cubes-in-the-bilge)
  (cite [23], 23 total).

  **(3) "How heavy without ballast?" — answered, plus a build-time
  follow-up.** Adrian Dodd's bare-hull-weight question drew two independent
  guesses converging on the existing ~400 lb design figure — **Dave
  Bolduc**'s 450–500 lb, and **Glen Maxwell**'s 350–400 lb for "Zoë" before
  cabinets and ballast. Relieved, Adrian asked what part of the build tends
  to be most time-consuming; **Derek Clark** answered from his own "Enuf"
  build: no single stage stood out, but scarf joints were consistently
  irritating (he switched to a lapped butt joint for the floor) and chine
  logs were a pain (narrowed to simplify, with a warning against removing
  the vent box before the bulkheads are fitted); he also gave concrete
  hull-lifting data points at two build stages and vouched for hull-builder
  Alec Jordan's work quality on an unrelated commission. Folded into the
  existing canonical home
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#workspace)
  (cites [77]-[80], 80 total).

  **Standing-open-items check:** Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, boat "Enuf",
  STRENGTHENED promote-later candidate) appeared twice this batch as author
  — still NOT promoted per standing instruction; his content this batch is
  genuinely topical (folded into existing topical pages) rather than a
  build narrative, so no boat-page trigger was met. Jason Stancil
  identity-reconcile and Jack Gardiner's "Jill" boat-page-split remain OPEN
  3000-review items, neither surfaced this batch (Jack Gardiner did not
  appear). One genuinely new below-threshold correspondent — **Paul
  Reppeto** (`reppeto@`/`ldjonerik`, Olympia, WA) — added to the standing
  list; Daved Ferrell (`jackefroste`) and Adrian Dodd (`adrianrdodd`) both
  reappeared as already-standing identities from batch 268, correctly not
  re-flagged as new. **Attribution-trap check:** David Beard
  (dlb@/buster38801), David LeBlanc (whisper@/meerkatoz), and Smith
  (kd7ixk@/betelgeuserdude) all did not appear this batch, no confusion
  possible; Derek Clark (dclark@/dclark52001) appeared twice as author,
  confirmed via headers each time, correctly not conflated with Smith; the two
  distinct UK Jeffs, three-distinct-Larrys, two-distinct-Roberts, and
  two-distinct-Rons watches all not triggered (no traffic from any of those
  identities this batch). Glen Maxwell appeared twice this batch (WAG Bag
  pointer, Zoë hull-weight guess), correctly routed to topical pages rather
  than any `people/glen-maxwell/` sub-file, since neither is
  Matt-Layden-eyewitness, list-admin, or Zoë-build/design narrative

  dispatch instructions exactly (2344/1614/1860/2019/5702/1730/2686/4512/3122/4877
  bytes for page_2681 through page_2690 respectively) — no stale-read

  full sorted-dedup pass (zero gaps, zero duplicates, count equals max in
  each): `operations/head-and-sanitation.md` 17,
  `operations/provisioning-and-water.md` 23,
  `construction/build-effort.md` 80 numeric + pre-existing [1a]-[1d]
  letter-suffixed entries unaffected; no letter-suffixed citation numbers
