## 2026-07-08

* **Update (batch page_2271–page_2280, msgnum 2298-2307, continuing directly
  from batch 227's 2297, no new gap)**: 12-13 September 2004. No new files, 2
  extended. The entire batch is a single continuous thread — the direct
  continuation of Jack Gardiner's "Another Paradox hits the water" launch
  announcement (batch 227) — so, per narrate-once-cross-link, all ten
  messages were folded into the existing "Launch: 'Jill'" section on
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill), which has
  always been the canonical home for his whole build/launch narrative. Paul
  (`kayaker37`) got his viewing request answered ("Sure Paul"); Glen Maxwell
  added congratulations and asked for photos; Jack explained his only camera
  was an unreliable phone camera (having already dunked three proper cameras,
  with his wife vetoing a replacement), and that nobody had known in advance
  where he would launch, so no one was on hand with a real camera — a dead
  calm that day left him weighing his 1.5 hp/2.5 hp outboards, an electric
  trolling motor, or simply waiting for forecast wind. **Dave Bolduc gave his
  standing ballasting advice**: don't hesitate to load a Paradox all the way
  up to her full cruising displacement (9 in draft), since the boat "loves
  the weight" and keeping the chine runners submerged as deep as possible
  maximizes windward performance — folded as a new cross-link into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#real-world-under-ballasted-handling)
  (cites [91]-[92], 92 total) alongside Glen Maxwell's and Alastair's existing
  real-world ballast data points, and summarized in one line on
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (no new citation, per
  narrate-once-cross-link). Jack's own maiden-sail report confirmed the
  practical stakes directly: without enough ballast aboard (only a few
  sandbags, daylight running out after eight tacks to windward and a run back
  down), Jill sat on her waterline short of the full 9 in draft and felt a
  little tender, though she held her trim "without fuss for long periods" once
  settled and threw remarkably little wake for her short beam; a feared
  following-sea problem (the rear window not yet installed) proved unfounded,
  and he described a square (rather than the plans' curved) rubber tiller-hole
  boot with a cam cleat mounted on it, awkward to reach in practice. Jack then
  uploaded his first photos of the finished boat to a "Jill Baby" folder,
  including one showing what Paul mistook for "a hand operated outboard" —
  after a full night's sleep on the puzzle, Paul worked out for himself that
  it was a [yuloh](/components/yuloh.md), not a
  mechanical device at all. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [104]-[113], 113 total, one pre-existing gap at [53] predating this batch)
  with the full ten-message continuation of the launch thread. No new person
  pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout (Dave
  Bolduc's advice cross-linked rather than re-narrated). Standing-open-items
  check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3,

## 2026-07-07

* **Update (batch page_2261–page_2270, msgnum 2288-2297, continuing directly
  from batch 226's 2287, no new gap)**: 9-12 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 4 extended. The batch closes out the seven-batch "chinerunners"
  thread (opened batch 224) and its "polyester?" sub-thread, then reports a
  new build milestone. Jack Gardiner answered "Jeff UK"'s pinhole/corrosion
  concern about steel chine runners — coating a weld in epoxy is a known
  backyard steel-hull practice that fills any pinholes before priming and
  painting, with routine maintenance preventing the corrosion Jeff UK feared —
  and separately warned that beating or hammering bronze work-hardens it into
  a brittle state. Kjell Karlsson, working the hydrodynamic-curve question
  from his sheet-metal build, found Paul's kayak-chine analogy plausible but
  raised sharper, unresolved questions of his own about laminar/turbulent flow
  and whether the usual sideways-tripping mechanism even applies to a Paradox
  hull at all. On the practical side, Kjell worked through three fallback
  plans for bending his 2 mm bronze sheet's curve — hand-forming around a
  pattern (cast into doubt by Jack Gardiner's work-hardening warning),
  grinding and bonding on a curved strip ("sounds impossible"), and
  laminating offcut strips (ruled out since the divinycell core cannot take
  welding heat) — before accepting he would have to find out empirically
  whether the curve mattered; his own scrap-bronze tests suggested the metal
  was not brittle, the real obstacle being that 2 mm gauge is simply thick to
  bend by hand, and the sheet was already attached to the boat by the time
  the exchange closed. Separately, Bengt Genell (standing below-threshold
  correspondent) thanked the list for its earlier polyester-vs-epoxy replies
  and gave budget/location detail (central Sweden, ~2 hrs from the coast,
  trailerability priority, exterior-grade plywood); Kjell answered with a
  detailed lamination cautionary tale from his own 240 g/m² polyester cloth —
  "tricky round angles," his first attempt becoming "just a mess" until a
  professional painter rescued it — and practical advice (thinner cloth for
  a first attempt; an extra thin fiberglass layer over sanded polyester,
  which goes "fluffy, like velvet," to save on filler). Finally, Jack Gardiner
  **launched his Paradox on 11 September 2004**, naming her **"Jill"** after
  his wife, at Gaspee Point, Narragansett Bay, R.I. — deliberately chosen for
  the site's Revolutionary War history and the 9/11 date — reporting a calm
  first sail that "took to doing what she is hailed to do, beat to wind!,"
  an easy motion through a ship's wake, and an enthusiastic reception from
  onlookers at the ramp; Paul (`kayaker37`) sent congratulations and asked
  about visiting the boat in Connecticut. Extended
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#bronze-versus-steel-if-a-builder-insists-on-a-shoe)
  (cites [42]-[47], 47 total) with the epoxy-over-welds/bronze-work-hardening
  rebuttal and the three-fallback-plans bronze-bending narrative, both folded
  into existing sections; extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#a-kayak-chine-analogy-for-the-deflector-view)
  (cite [33], 33 total) with Kjell's laminar/turbulent-flow follow-up;
  extended
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#polyester-resin-versus-epoxy)
  (cites [35]-[36], 36 total) with Bengt Genell's and Kjell's polyester
  follow-ups; extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#launch-jill) (cites
  [101]-[103], 103 total) with a new "Launch: 'Jill'" section — his boat's
  name is now on record for the first time; and extended
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cites [40]-[44], 44
  total) with two new cross-link bullets (the bronze-bending saga, and the
  polyester-lamination cautionary tale). No new files created — per
  narrate-once-cross-link, Jack Gardiner's build and launch stayed on his
  existing person page rather than spinning up a new `boats/` page, since his

* **Update (batch page_2251–page_2260, msgnum 2278-2287, continuing directly
  from batch 225's 2277, no new gap)**: 9 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 6 extended. All ten messages continue the single **"chinerunners"**
  thread (opened batch 224, continued batch 225) across its full duration —
  every message in this batch falls on the same single day, 9 September 2004.
  Two strands ran through it: (1) Kjell Karlsson explained why he bonds metal
  with an elastic epoxy rather than fastening it — epoxy will not adhere to
  stainless steel, but a fellow Swedish builder of the related "Sofia" design
  bonds it anyway by heavily grinding the sheet and applying an elastic filler
  within seconds (the filler is markedly thixotropic), a bond that outlasted
  the polyester laminate beneath it when tested years later; bronze poses no
  comparable gluing problem, but he follows the same elastic-bond practice
  regardless. Paul (`kayaker37`) offered a kayak-chine analogy for the
  deflector mechanism (leaning into a curved hard chine steers the boat toward
  that side, similar to Phil Bolger's bottom-plated rudders), speculating a
  Paradox in trip-risk conditions should be sailed upright to present the
  runner's rounded radius rather than its sharper upper edge. (2) A new
  fabrication side-thread opened on whether chine runners could be built in
  **steel** on a steel-hulled Paradox: "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`, a below-
  threshold correspondent distinct from the established Jeff/"doryman_uk" —
  reconfirmed non-merged, see backlog) proposed bolting a hardwood runner onto
  a steel hull instead of fabricating the runner itself in steel; Jack
  Gardiner, drawing on his professional fabrication background and a long
  first-person account of shipyard-scale steel work (submarine steam-loop
  piping, confined-space boiler-tube welding), judged steel chine runners no
  real difficulty at any hull size, crediting modern affordable tools with
  putting the job within a backyard builder's reach; Glen Maxwell suggested
  welding a length of pipe along the runner's edge to form the curved radius,
  which Jack answered with a plasma-cutting alternative; Jeff UK countered
  that steel chine runners need at least two full-length welds, and even a
  skilled welder can leave a pinhole that lets water reach unprotected steel
  inside the runner and start corrosion from within — a failure he had seen
  firsthand in local boatyards with rusted-through steel boats — concluding a
  genuinely skilled welder is worth hiring for that joint specifically.
  Alastair proposed a lamination alternative to bending sheet metal to the
  curve at all: build up the edge's thickness from offcut strips laminated in
  layers (glued, riveted, brazed, or welded) until enough material
  accumulates to grind the final curve — a metal-specific analogue of his own
  plywood block-and-fill method. Separately, on a fully composite/metal hull,
  Glen Maxwell passed on a corrosion-control technique for bonding epoxy to
  **aluminum** (coat with epoxy first, then sand through the still-wet epoxy
  so the freshly abraded metal is never exposed to open air, applicable to
  bronze too) and recalled, from his own "Zoë" build, drilling and
  countersinking holes along a bonded ground-strip's edge so the cured epoxy
  in each hole acts like a rivet against peeling forces. No builder on record
  ever built or reported a steel-hulled Paradox with steel chine runners; the
  fabrication debate remained speculative, mirroring the 2003 "Steel?"
  thread's own outcome. Extended
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#fabricating-chine-runners-in-steel-if-the-whole-hull-is-steel)
  (cites [35]-[41], 41 total) with a new "Fabricating chine runners in steel,
  if the whole hull is steel" section plus the epoxy/stainless bonding
  addition to "Bronze versus steel," and
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#a-kayak-chine-analogy-for-the-deflector-view)
  (cite [32], 32 total) with a new "A kayak-chine analogy for the deflector
  view" section, with cross-link summaries in
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [39], 39 total),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [98]-[100], 100
  total), [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cite [220], 220 total),
  and [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md#building-zoë)
  (cite [225], 225 total). No new person pages (all six participants —
  Kjell Karlsson, "Jeff UK"/`roverticket`, Jack Gardiner, Paul/`kayaker37`,
  Glen Maxwell, Alastair — are already-established identities); no prunings;

## 2026-07-06

* **Update (batch page_2241–page_2250, msgnum 2268-2277, continuing directly
  from batch 224's 2267, no new gap)**: 8-9 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 6 extended. All ten messages continue the single **"chinerunners"**
  thread opened in batch 224, plus one short closing reply on the separate
  "new builder in Oz" thread. Centerpiece: the hydrodynamic-curve question
  widened but stayed unresolved. Paul (`kayaker37`) recalled Matt Layden's own
  *Beachcruising and Coastal Camping*, in which Warren Bailey described 1930s
  Key West sponging dinghies with a 4-inch-radius "knuckle-V" — a "non-trip
  chine" that let a boat surf sideways rather than trip — and noted Matt's own
  next boat after Swamp Thing's sideways-surfing capsize had gently radiused
  chines; unconvinced, Kjell Karlsson sharpened the question to whether the
  drawn "downward convexity" curve (roughly 15 mm radius) might make tripping
  *easier* than the opposite ("upward") curve would, while detailing his own
  sheet-metal build: a 2 mm, 70 kg bronze sheet (chosen over stainless steel
  for lightning-grounding), glued on with an elastic epoxy filler and no
  mechanical fasteners, with the 15 mm bend proving very hard to form in two
  directions even with a neighbor's mechanical-shop help. Alastair, restating
  his own plywood method (bottom ply proud of the sides, chine-runner wedge
  filled with wood blocks and epoxy, shaped with an angle grinder) and
  suggesting Kjell do the same in metal, offered his own guess that the curve
  really exists to protect the fiberglass laminate's edge rather than for any
  hydrodynamic reason; a separate correspondent, Robert (`carolfo`/
  `cheepwings`, standing below-threshold identity), asked the same metal-bottom
  question from the stainless-steel side and about the adhesive, which Kjell
  answered (NM/Nils Malmgren elastic epoxy, no mechanical fasteners) — folded
  into the existing bronze-vs-steel section as a settled bedding-method answer.
  No builder on record ever confirmed a hydrodynamic function for the curve one
  way or the other. Extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#the-chine-runner-curve-cosmetic-fabrication-choice-or-hydrodynamic-feature)
  (cites [23]-[31]),
  [chine-runners/construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#bronze-versus-steel-if-a-builder-insists-on-a-shoe)
  (cites [35]-[36]), with cross-link summaries in
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cites [35]-[38]) and
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (cites [218]-[219]). A minor
  aside in the same message — Kjell mentioned reworking rudder fittings after
  installing the main bronze rod askew, and building his own tiller from
  fiberglass and epoxy rather than wood — was folded into
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-rudder-fitting-rework-and-a-fiberglass-tiller)
  (cite [64]) as a routine build-progress note; a failed round-the-Baltic car
  trip (stopped by Finnish customs at the Tallinn ferry) was judged too thin
  to record. Separately, Glen Maxwell sent a brief closing reply on the "new
  builder in Oz" thread — "Congratulations Jack!!! Let us know how it goes,
  can't wait to see the pictures" — folded into
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md#build-complete-sail-hoisted)
  (cite [97]) as the thread's final beat. No new person pages, no prunings,
  narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout. Standing-open-items check:
  Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3, Derek

  checked, none newly promoted, no genuinely new below-threshold

## 2026-07-05

* **Update (batch page_2231–page_2240, msgnum 2258-2267, continuing directly
  from batch 223's 2257, no new gap)**: 4-7 September 2004. No new files, 0
  created / 7 extended. Two centerpiece items plus a shorter build-progress
  note. (1) A new **"chinerunners" thread**: a Swedish builder laying up his
  bottom in sheet metal (rather than plywood) asked whether the chine
  runner's upward-curving outer edge served a genuine hydrodynamic function,
  since the curve was proving hard to reproduce in metal. Jack Gardiner
  suggested fabrication approaches (a hardwood pattern beaten or heat-formed
  around the sheet metal, or grinding thicker stock directly) and recalled a
  remark attributed to Matt Layden that the runner's outward dimension
  matters more than its exact shape; Alastair, initially confused about the
  bottom's fore-and-aft-only curvature, raised a gap-at-the-chine concern for
  an unbent sheet, which Jack Gardiner clarified was about the chine-runner
  profile's own radius; David Beard reported no upward curve at all on his
  own "Whisper," without a noticed difference; Paul (`kayaker37`) offered an
  uncorroborated guess (a sled effect when beaching, and/or protecting the
  fiberglass roving's edge). No definitive answer emerged. Extended
  [chine-runners/how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md#the-chine-runner-curve-cosmetic-fabrication-choice-or-hydrodynamic-feature)
  (cites [17]-[22]), with brief cross-link summaries added to
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (cite [34]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cites [95]-[96]), and
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cite [233]). (2) Closing
  the **Australian plywood-sourcing sub-thread** opened in batch 223: John
  Hall (standing below-threshold identity since batch 190) gave his
  workaround for the local-vs-imported plywood sheet-size mismatch pinching
  bulkhead #3 — build it in two parts with a scarf on the bottom section,
  since a 2400 mm local sheet cannot supply the full 1214 mm width in one
  piece — and named two Australian timber alternatives to imported Gaboon:
  Hoop Pine (better-made veneers, in his view) or the cheaper but CCA-treated
  Proof Ply; Shane Hersey's sustainable-timber follow-up remained unanswered
  on record. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cite [38]). (3) A **build-progress item**: Jack Gardiner reported his
  Paradox essentially complete and the sail hoisted, passing the news to
  William Serjeant (maintaining the UK build-count list) as another boat "to
  add to the list"; Serjeant thanked him and said he would follow up by
  e-mail for the boat's details. Extended
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [94]) and
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cite [32]). Also closed the "roller reefing lug sail?" thread's true final
  beat: Glen Maxwell's brief good-wish reply to Jason Stancil's winter-boom
  plan, extending
  [design/furling-system.md](/components/rig/furling.md#interest-from-outside-the-paradox-community)
  (cite [55]). No new person pages; no genuinely new correspondents (all
  participants are already-established identities); narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout.

* **Update (batch page_2221–page_2230, msgnum 2248-2257, continuing directly
  from batch 222's 2247, no new gap)**: 31 August - 4 September 2004. Two
  centerpiece items plus two smaller ones, no new files. (1)
  Closing out the **"roller reefing lug sail?" thread** left unanswered in
  `design/furling-system.md` since batch 220: a builder finishing a Bolger
  Micro Navigator with his own 200 sq ft lug sail had asked whether
  Paradox's furling gear was designed to avoid an awkward open-hatch
  reefing routine, whether the approach could be adapted to other lug rigs,
  and how the furling drum and swiveling tack fitting are built. Glen
  Maxwell answered in full over a week later — the fullest first-person
  description on record of the gear's mechanics (a bronze shaft riding in a
  bronze/copper pipe inside the boom end, a drum turned via a furling line
  led below deck, a tack fitting on a swiveling pipe fitting to clear the
  hatch when the rig is stowed) — judged it "about as simple a solution as
  I have come across" once built, declined to photocopy the plan sheets
  themselves out of respect for Matt Layden's authorship, and pointed to
  the photo section and a specific Files-section image (`TL07.jpg`) for
  visual reference. The builder, having had no other list reply, reported
  he had already built his own boom/yard traditionally to get sailing that
  season and planned a beefier tapered drum-and-thimble boom over the
  winter, crediting Matt Layden's "well thought out" design. Extended
  [design/furling-system.md](/components/rig/furling.md#interest-from-outside-the-paradox-community)
  (cites [52]-[54], closing out [49]). (2) An **Australian/Tasmanian
  plywood-sourcing sub-thread**, continuing the "new builder in Oz" family
  of threads: Shane Hersey apologized for assuming the plans weren't
  metric and asked a new Australian builder about ply grade; John Hall
  (Launceston, Tasmania — a standing below-threshold identity since batch
  190's "Sheet Lead Ballast" thread) identified a genuine plywood-sourcing
  problem — Australian locally produced plywood runs 2400 x 1200 mm while
  imported Gaboon (Okoume) runs 2500 x 1220 mm, a closer fit to Matt
  Layden's own sheet layout, leaving bulkhead #3 (drawn 1214 mm wide) a
  tight fit against the local sheet size — and reported a 1:5 scale model
  well advanced toward CNC files for a local cutting service, offering to
  share the DXF files with Dave and Mindy Bolduc for distribution alongside
  the plans; Shane asked about a workaround and a preference for
  sustainably sourced local ply, left unanswered on record. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cites [35]-[37]). Two smaller items: Nels and Jason Stancil both replied
  to batch 222's "New Member" thread confirming DIY scale-up is easy on the
  metric plans (15% by simple arithmetic, or Matt Layden's own "all clear"
  for a 20% enlargement on the same scantlings), extending
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#no-official-larger-plans-exist)
  (cites [61]-[62]), cross-linked from
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#storage-beachability-and-who-buys-the-beer);
  and William Serjeant, replying to Alastair's pointer to his own site,
  estimated "at least 3" more completed Paradoxes could be added to his
  existing list and invited builders to report new boats, extending
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cite [31]); Dave Blow gave a routine building-manual progress update,
  extending [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md)
  (cite [51]). No new person pages, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout.

* **Update (batch page_2211–page_2220, msgnum 2238-2247, continuing directly
  from batch 221's 2237, no new gap)**: 28-31 August 2004. Three items, no
  new files. (1) Closing out the **"polyester?" thread** (opened batch 221):
  Smith agreed with James Greene's verdict but floated a hybrid layup for the
  bottom specifically — epoxy for the first and last laminates, polyester
  for the layers between, provided both resins are properly dewaxed before
  the changeover — reasoning that the bottom glass functions mainly as a
  skidplate and secondary ballast rather than a structural bond. Nels
  pointed to designer Dynamite Payson, who built stitch-and-glue boats like
  Paradox using only polyester resin (ring nails for joinery/seam taping),
  many still afloat some 30 years on, though Payson himself now recommends
  epoxy where affordable; noted polyester cannot go over epoxy (though epoxy
  can go over polyester) and is a poor choice for a boat that stays wet most
  of the time. Alastair closed the thread with a one-line reminder that
  Little Cruiser has proved a Paradox-family hull can go to sea with nothing
  on the outside but paint — no fiberglass sheathing of either resin at all.
  Extended [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#polyester-resin-versus-epoxy)
  (cites [32]-[34]). (2) The **"new builder in Oz" thread's final coda**:
  Shane Hersey asked a new Australian builder how he was reconciling
  Imperial-measure US plans against Australian metric plywood; the builder
  confirmed his own plans arrived already in metric, apparently standard for
  the design, and shared a free unit-conversion utility. Derek Clark,
  reading along, double-checked his own bulkheads (already cut in
  millimetres) and confirmed his plans were metric too, then asked how many
  completed Paradoxes exist worldwide and how many are under construction in
  England — a question with no running total on record; Alastair pointed to
  Bill Serjeant's `smallsailboats.co.uk` site as holding "a fairly complete
  list," and could name only one interested-but-unconfirmed prospective
  Cornwall builder ("Neil") for the UK specifically. Extended
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md#metric-vs-imperial-measuring)
  (cites [32a]-[34]) and [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md#a-uk-and-worldwide-build-count)
  (cites [29]-[30]). (3) A new **"New Member" thread**: Lee Corbridge, DVM
  (Sebring, FL), introduced himself wanting a trailerable boat for Western
  US mountain-lake/coastal camp-cruising with an eventual Bahamas crossing,
  asking whether a bigger-than-14ft plan set exists, the build time and
  cost, and (in jest) who buys the beer — closing the loop first opened by
  the archive's original, long-unanswered newcomer-FAQ snapshot on
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#open-newcomer-questions-cabin-comfort-capacity-and-perceived-safety).
  Alastair answered directly this time: no larger-than-14ft plans exist,
  though one Australian builder had modified his own interior by removing
  the forward lockers to widen the cabin for two-person sleeping; Paradox
  carries far more storage than similarly sized boats; beaching itself is
  easy, but a falling tide strands the boat until it returns, and even with
  three helpers pushing a beached Paradox by hand proved impossible in
  practice; and by custom, whoever gets back last buys the drinks. Extended
  [operations/first-boat-suitability.md](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md#storage-beachability-and-who-buys-the-beer)
  (new section, cites [24]-[25]), cross-linked from
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cites
  [49]-[50]), [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#cost)
  (cites [70]-[71]), and a new cross-link section on
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#no-official-larger-plans-exist).
  Separately, Jack Gardiner, finishing his own build's mast/boom/yard, asked
  what size luff tape he needed and where to source it — left unanswered on
  record within this batch. Extended
  [construction/sailmaking.md](/construction/sailmaking.md#corner-patches-cut-tabling-and-the-luff-tape)
  (cite [16]), cross-linked from
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (cite [93]). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings, narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie
  Fossel, Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/dclark52001 (resurfaced twice —
  metric double-check and the build-count question — strengthened
  promote-later status unchanged, still not promoted per standing hint),
  Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2, kayaker37/Paul, paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V.,
  Steve/Kansas City, Howard Welch, Jason Stancil, John Hall, Todd/ktsrfer,

* **Update (batch page_2201–page_2210, msgnum 2227-2237 with a new gap at
  2229, continuing directly from batch 220's 2226)**: 23-28 August 2004. Four
  items, no new files. (1) Closing out the "new builder in Oz" hardware
  thread: Nels asked Alastair to elaborate on the unfamiliar term "tufnol";
  Alastair explained it as an engineering composite of linen fabric and
  resin, resembling bakelite, easily machined, chemically inert to most
  reagents, sold in blocks and sheets — he used an offcut from work for his
  own rudder fittings rather than building the plans' composite part, purely
  because he had a piece large enough. Extended
  [design/furling-system.md](/components/rig/furling.md)'s "Hardware materials:
  stainless steel and tufnol as bronze/plastic substitutes" section (cites
  [50]-[51]). (2) A coda to the "Fiberglassing question for Glen" thread
  (batch 220): Dave Bolduc thanked Glen Maxwell for the fiberglassing
  information, having briefly worried Hurricane Charley (which struck
  Florida in August 2004) had "washed him and his microcruiser away."
  Extended [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md#construction-techniques)
  (cite [224]), cross-linked from
  [people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (cite [90]). (3) A new
  **"Standing Bevels?" thread**: Derek Clark, marking out plywood for the
  transom and bulkheads, asked what the drawing note "Bottom Bevel Left
  Standing" means and how it affects the stated 668 mm transom-height figure
  and an unexplained outer line on the bulkhead drawings. Paul (`kayaker37`)
  first pointed to an earlier archive answer (message 1267), then gave the
  fuller answer directly: the 668 mm figure is bulkhead 1's total plywood
  height taken at the station line, beveled inward since the panel lies
  forward of that line; a dotted line below it marks the floor, which lies
  aft of the station line and bevels outward; the sides are additionally
  crowned (curved outward), shown by a further offset line to the right of
  centre. Paul also disclosed he has never built a Paradox himself — his
  questions all concerned a CAD model built to understand the design better.
  Extended [construction/boom-end-tack-line-holes.md](/construction/boom-end-tack-line-holes.md#bottom-bevel-left-standing-the-bulkhead-bevel-convention)
  (cites [9]-[12]), the file's existing canonical home for this drawing
  convention since June 2002. (4) A new **"polyester?" thread**: Bengt
  Genell, partway through his own dry-run assembly, asked whether cheaper
  polyester resin could substitute for or work alongside epoxy, for instance
  in the bottom layers. New correspondent James Greene answered definitively:
  polyester is not an adhesive and will never bond as well as epoxy; epoxy
  suits repairs where polyester cannot be used; polyester can be used for
  original construction but the result is neither as strong nor as
  long-lasting. New section on
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#polyester-resin-versus-epoxy)
  (cites [30]-[31]). One independent new-member introduction: Dave Blow
  (`smallshelters`), having researched small boats online and settled on the
  Paradox, ordered Don Elliott's "Building the Paradox" booklet with the full
  plans to follow within weeks — his only prior boatbuilding experience a
  Bolger Brick completed in May 2004, rigged to mimic the Paradox's own
  controls as sailing practice. Folded into
  [references/building-plans.md](/references/building-plans.md) (cite [48]).
  Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/dclark52001 (resurfaced via the Standing

  unchanged, pending its own dedicated consolidation pass), Fredrik, Jake,

  below-threshold — never built a Paradox), paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V.,

  Sawatzky, Shane Hersey, Bengt Genell (resurfaced, see above), Bill/wam_53,
  Craig/crag8, Ron/churo65, Ron/rondune508, Phil/eliallegra(topsail@),
  Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-Larry, Ken Preston/kenpreston46,
  James/jcbanik, Roger Nuffer/rogernuffer, Robert/boat142,
  Robert/carolfo(cheepwings), LEON/melodymann, William Serjeant, Hugh
  Miller(NZ), chadoora, roger chan/light369369, Matt Wagner/trisurfnturf,
  OzBill/bilbilau all checked, none newly promoted; two genuinely new
  below-threshold correspondents added to the standing list going forward
  (Dave Blow/smallshelters, James Greene/james56greene). Attribution-trap
  check: David Beard (dlb@/buster38801) vs. David LeBlanc (whisper@/meerkatoz)
  — neither appeared this batch; Smith (kd7ixk/betelgeuserdude) vs. Derek Clark
  (dclark@/dclark52001) — only Derek Clark appeared, correctly attributed via
  headers; three-distinct-Larrys, two-distinct-Roberts, two-distinct-Rons
  watches not triggered (no traffic from any of those six this batch).
  **New archive gap: msgnum 2229 missing** between page_2202 (msgnum 2228)
  and page_2203 (msgnum 2230) — joins the standing 2122 and 2190 gaps, not
  recoverable within this batch's fixed page range. Byte sizes verified for
  all ten source files (1303/2252/2260/1761/2085/2697/2673/1690/1386/1779)
  against the batch brief before reading, all exact matches, no stale-read
  issues. Citation-collision check run on all six touched files, all
  sequential with no duplicates or gaps (furling-system.md's pre-existing
  [29a] letter-suffix convention preserved, unrelated to this batch's edits).
  Cursor set to 2210.

* **Update (batch page_2191–page_2200, msgnum 2217-2226, continuing directly
  from batch 219's 2216, no new gap)**: 19-23 August 2004. Four items. (1) A
  **"new builder in Oz" thread** answered, point by point, the questions
  OzBill (Bill Anderson, Brisbane) had left on record at the end of batch 219:
  Alastair confirmed galvanized ringnails work if fully epoxy-encapsulated
  (though bronze lasts longer), read the tack-strop spec plainly as a 6 mm
  rope inside a 14 mm OD pipe (12 mm water pipe being standard stock in both
  the UK and Australia), gave an uncertain secondhand recollection that Dave
  Bolduc may have used stones as ballast aboard Little Cruiser, confirmed
  marine-grade aluminium is fine for the lightning rod, and confirmed no
  steel-hulled Paradox has been built "yet" — closing the loop first opened in
  the December 2003 "Steel?" debate. OzBill's follow-up clarified his hardware
  question meant the fabricated hoops/rudder-hardware/boom-reefing bits, not
  the ringnails, drawing Alastair's confirmation that he used tufnol and
  stainless steel for his own rudder fittings and boom axle. Extended
  [design/furling-system.md](/components/rig/furling.md) (new sections
  "Hardware materials: stainless steel and tufnol as bronze/plastic
  substitutes" and "Interest from outside the Paradox community," extended
  "Build-tolerance lesson: the tack-strop pipe," cites [45]-[49]),
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (cite [116], closing note on the steel-Paradox question), and cross-link
  summaries on [construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md](/construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md)
  (cite [27]), [design/lightning-protection.md](/design/lightning-protection.md)
  (cite [4]), and [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md)
  (new "Sandbags as ballast" section, cite [90]). A separate, unrelated
  question in the same window — a Bolger Micro Navigator builder (Jason
  Stancil) asking how Paradox's rolling-boom furling compares to his own
  hand-reefed lug sail — was left unanswered on record and folded into the
  same file (cite [49]). (2) **Jack Gardiner** (the Rhode Island builder,
  `alex29`/`tidybowlmann`) gave a late-build status update — cabin on, mast
  half complete, lower rudder leaded and finished, upper rudder still being
  built — and described using lead sinkers and weight belts recovered while
  diving as his own ballast source; restated his stock-wood-first, steel-maybe
  resolution from the 2003 "Steel?" debate; and answered David Beard's
  dive-gear-storage question, floating a stretched ~19 ft Paradox as better
  suited to tank storage and weighing the tradeoffs of towing a dive-tender
  inflatable. Extended [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md)
  (new section "A late-build status report, and stowing dive gear aboard,"
  cites [91]-[92]), with a 1-2 sentence cross-link summary added to
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (cite [232]). (3) **Glen
  Maxwell** answered Dave Bolduc's batch-219 chine-reinforcement question
  (left unanswered at the time): 7 oz "E" glass overlapped four times at the
  chine runners, a fairing technique using microballoons worked into the
  epoxy and smoothed with a 12 in drywall knife, rolling the hull to let
  gravity help fairing on each face in turn, and applying each epoxy coat
  within 24 hours of the last to avoid sanding between coats — extended
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md#construction-techniques)
  (cite [223], resolving [222]'s open question). (4) A new "Oak Stem" thread:
  the below-threshold correspondent Derek Clark (`dclark52001`) described
  being given a length of green Cornish oak by shipwrights restoring a
  200-year-old fishing boat, intended for his Paradox's stem in place of a
  planned plywood lamination, and asked how to counteract warping as the
  green wood dries — left unanswered on record. Folded into
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md#a-piece-of-cornish-oak-for-the-stem-and-the-warping-question)
  (new section, cite [13]). A fifth message (Robert/`carolfo`/`cheepwings`,
  standing below-threshold identity) was purely off-topic — a one-line
  mention of a Wooden Boat magazine article on a Bolger Birdwatcher sharpie,
  no Paradox content — and was not folded into any topic page. No new person
  pages; no prunings. Attribution traps checked: David Beard (`dlb@...`/
  `buster38801`) vs. David LeBlanc (`whisper@...`/`meerkatoz`) — only David
  Beard appeared, correctly attributed throughout; Smith (`kd7ixk`) vs. Derek
  Clark (`dclark52001`) — only Derek Clark appeared this batch, correctly
  attributed via headers, and NOT promoted to a person page per the
  standing strengthened promote-later hint. Msgnum run 2217-2226, fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 219's last msgnum 2216 — no
  new gap (standing gaps at 2122 and 2190 unaffected). Byte sizes verified
  for all ten source files against the batch brief before reading. Citation
  sequencing verified with no collisions in every touched file. Cursor
  advanced to 2200.

* **Update (batch page_2181–page_2190, msgnum 2207-2216, continuing directly
  from batch 218's 2206, no new gap)**: 6-19 August 2004. Five items. (1) The
  **"Glen's Foam cushions" thread closed out**: David Beard, having leftover
  Lux Foam of his own, offered to mail Dave Bolduc a sample directly rather
  than waiting on Foambymail, describing its firmness as falling in between
  the two reference points Dave had asked about; Dave thanked him but opted
  to try Foambymail first — extended [boats/little-jim.md — cabin-cushion
  solutions](/boats/little-jim.md#cabin-cushion-solutions-glen-maxwells-foam-by-mail-system-june-2003)
  (cites [93]-[94]). (2) A new **"Ground plate attachment" thread**: Dave
  Bolduc asked Glen Maxwell how his lightning-protection ground plate was
  fitted flush and faired, and what sealed the vacuum bag over it; Glen gave
  the fullest construction detail on record — a plate cut from 6 in copper
  flashing (sourced at a roofer's supply), and the bag sealed with mastic (a
  cheaper equivalent of West's vacuum-bag sealer, sold at Lowe's/Home Depot),
  a technique Chris Morejohn recommended to him back in 1992 that has never
  leaked since, and which he prefers to 3M 5200 for its reversibility —
  extended [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md — construction
  techniques](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md#construction-techniques)
  (cites [220]-[222], the last also recording Dave's separate, unanswered
  question on chine-reinforcement glass weight), with a cross-link summary
  added to [design/lightning-protection.md — a grounding plate seen under the
  glass](/design/lightning-protection.md#a-grounding-plate-seen-under-the-glass)
  (no new citation, per narrate-once). (3) Dave Bolduc, preparing to
  fiberglass-sheathe **Little Cruiser** ahead of her eventual 2005 refit
  (already on file from the microcruising.com retrospective), set out his own
  glassing plan on the list a year ahead of time — 6 oz glass on deck/sides,
  biaxial/mat glass on the bottom, bias-cut 2-4 oz glass for the toe rails,
  and a kevlar/glass combination likely reserved for just the rudder and
  centerboard tip — plus his blow-torch technique for stripping 3M-5200-bonded
  deck hardware without damage — extended [matt-layden/little-cruiser.md —
  construction scantlings and the 2005
  refit](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md#construction-scantlings-and-the-2005-epoxyfiberglass-sheathing-refit)
  (cites [21]-[22], the direct 2004 mailing-list precursor to the 2005 refit
  already on file). (4) A new **"New Sail" thread**: David Beard, having a new
  sail made, asked whether Matt Layden might want to revise his posted sail
  dimensions to add more draft; Dave Bolduc recommended sticking with the
  already-tested revision, confirming Little Cruiser's own next sail (5 oz
  cloth, shorter top batten, tighter leech tabling — no added draft) would
  finally realize the "still evolving" plans first flagged in December 2001;
  Glen Maxwell confirmed he remains happy with his own sail, Matt still
  possibly considering a touch more draft — extended [design/rig.md — how
  much draft a Paradox lug sail should
  carry](/components/rig/index.md#how-much-draft-a-paradox-lug-sail-should-carry)
  (cites [150]-[152]) and [matt-layden/little-cruiser.md — sail: larger and
  more complex than Paradox's](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md#sail-larger-and-more-complex-than-paradoxs)
  (cites [23]-[25], closing the loop on the sail's three-year-old "planned
  future changes" note). (5) A new Brisbane, Australia correspondent
  ("OzBill") introduced himself as the mailing list's third-noted Australian
  builder (after Chris Becker's "Walden" and Andrew Graham's "Aussie
  Paradox"/"Zero"), plywood delivery imminent; below threshold (no boat or
  build yet), his durable questions were routed to topic pages rather than
  given a person page: a possible Sheet 9 tack-strop spacer-pipe dimension
  discrepancy (1/4 in/6 mm ID vs. an 8 mm strop) folded into
  [design/furling-system.md — build-tolerance lesson: the tack-strop
  pipe](/components/rig/furling.md#build-tolerance-lesson-the-tack-strop-pipe)
  (cite [45]), and his question on whether a steel-hulled Paradox was ever
  actually built (following the archive's own earlier "heated" steel debate)
  answered from the existing record — none is known to have been completed —
  folded into [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md — a later tangent: the
  Gibbs Aquada, and "spray rails" vs. chine
  runners](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-later-tangent-the-gibbs-aquada-and-spray-rails-vs-chine-runners)
  (cite [115]); his stainless-vs-bronze and sandbag-ballast questions were
  left unanswered on record within this batch, already well precedented
  elsewhere (bronze hardware sourcing, sheet-lead/lead-pig ballast) and not
  independently re-flagged; added to `backlog.md` as a below-threshold
  correspondent to watch. Cross-link bullets added to `people/bolduc/dave.md`
  (cites [85]-[89]) and `people/david-beard.md` (cites [230]-[231]). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. All ten messages distinct and fully consecutive by msgnum
  (2207-2216); byte sizes verified against dispatch exactly; citation
  numbering verified sequential and collision-free in all eight touched files
  (`boats/little-jim.md` to [94], `people/bolduc/dave.md` to [89],
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` to [222], `people/david-beard.md` to
  [231], `matt-layden/little-cruiser.md` to [25], `design/rig.md` to [152],
  `design/furling-system.md` to [45], `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`
  to [115]).

* **Update (batch page_2171–page_2180, msgnum 2197-2206, continuing directly
  from batch 217's 2196, no new gap)**: 1-6 August 2004. Two centerpieces,
  both closing out threads opened in the prior batch. (1) The **"Sail
  Draft"/"Paradox weight" pair closed out**: Glen Maxwell corroborated Smith's
  point that Matt Layden's own sail already carries generous draft, with Matt
  considering adding still more — extended [design/rig.md — how much draft a
  Paradox lug sail should
  carry](/components/rig/index.md#how-much-draft-a-paradox-lug-sail-should-carry)
  (cites [147]-[149], including a brief David-Beard-welcomes-Glen-back
  exchange); separately, Paul (`kayaker37`) asked what "design displacement"
  actually means (waterline weight, or full-swamped volume?), and Glen
  Maxwell answered precisely: displacement is the boat's weight at her
  designed waterline, distinct from "light ship" (empty weight), the
  difference being useful load — extended [design/weight-and-ballast.md —
  independent weight and displacement
  estimates](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#independent-weight-and-displacement-estimates)
  (cites [88]-[89]). (2) A new **"Glen's Foam cushions" thread**, a direct
  year-later revival of the archive's original June-2003 Foam Cushions
  exchange: Dave Bolduc, planning to replace Little Cruiser's own
  bottomed-out cushion foam, asked Glen Maxwell to elaborate on the Lux Foam
  system behind "Zoë"'s cushions; Glen confirmed the foambymail.com source,
  his own lamination workaround (only 1 in stock was available when he
  built his cushions, so he glued two layers to reach 2 in) — noting David
  Beard later found the same foam sold directly in 2 in stock, no
  lamination needed — and described the foam's firmness (soft yet firm
  enough for 2 in to sleep comfortably without bottoming out, though not
  firm enough to sit on directly); David Beard vouched for the foam's
  durability firsthand. Extended [boats/little-jim.md — Cabin-cushion
  solutions: Glen Maxwell's foam-by-mail
  system](/boats/little-jim.md#cabin-cushion-solutions-glen-maxwells-foam-by-mail-system-june-2003)
  (cites [88]-[92], the pre-existing canonical home for this exact topic);
  cross-links added to `people/bolduc/dave.md` (cites [83]-[84]) and
  `people/david-beard.md` (cites [228]-[229]). No new files, no renames, no
  prunings; narrate-once-cross-link maintained throughout. All ten messages
  distinct and fully consecutive by msgnum (2197-2206); byte sizes verified
  against dispatch exactly; citation numbering verified sequential and
  collision-free in all five touched files
  (`design/rig.md` to [149], `design/weight-and-ballast.md` to [89],
  `boats/little-jim.md` to [92], `people/bolduc/dave.md` to [84],
  `people/david-beard.md` to [229]).

* **Update (batch page_2161–page_2170, msgnum 2186-2196, gap at 2190)**:
  21 July - 1 August 2004. Three items. (1) A **continuation of the "Small
  Jib" thread** opened in the prior batch: Dave Bolduc reported having
  already tried the equivalent experiment on one of Matt Layden's own
  chine-runner models, fitting a jib and finding it pointed a few degrees
  higher — worth trying for fun though not obviously worth the added
  complexity — and recalled Matt tinkering with a spinnaker on one of his
  own cruisers, the remains of which Dave found aboard Little Cruiser and
  repurposed into a windscoop; Alastair gave the small-jib idea a second,
  independent rationale from his own experience getting stuck head-to-wind
  in irons repeatedly one weekend in the Salcombe estuary, recoverable only
  with the yuloh, judging a jib would help push the bow through. Extended
  [design/rig.md — a speculative small
  jib](/components/rig/index.md#a-speculative-small-jib) (cites [142]-[143], the
  question now answered rather than left open); cross-link summary added to
  `operations/sailing-characteristics.md` (no new citation); cross-links
  added to `people/bolduc/dave.md` (cite [82]), `people/alastair-law.md` (cite
  [215]), and `people/david-beard.md` (bullet updated, no new citation). (2)
  A new **"Sail Draft" thread**: David Beard asked how much draft a properly
  cut Paradox lug sail should carry; Larry ("LJGeib") recalled Matt Layden's
  own sail as cut flat; Smith qualified that a heavier vessel needs more, not
  less, draft, pointing to Glen Maxwell's own writeup and Matt Layden's "new
  sail" post already on file. New section, [design/rig.md — how much draft a
  Paradox lug sail should
  carry](/components/rig/index.md#how-much-draft-a-paradox-lug-sail-should-carry)
  (cites [144]-[146]); cross-links added to `people/david-beard.md` (cite
  [227]) and `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite [154]). (3) Two new one-off correspondents
  asked generic newcomer questions, both answered by Alastair: **Matt
  Wagner** (`trisurfnturf`) asked how many plywood sheets, yards of glass
  cloth, and gallons of epoxy a Paradox build needs, plus build-hours/cost
  tips, drawing Alastair's itemized bill of materials (2 sheets 18mm, 4
  sheets 12mm, 3 sheets 9mm, 1 sheet 6mm plywood; 6 oz cloth, ~14 yards; 4mm
  woven roving on the bottom; 10 gallons of epoxy) alongside his existing
  £2,500/18-month figures — extended [construction/build-effort.md —
  cost](/construction/build-effort.md#cost) (cites [68]-[69]); and **Paul**
  (`kayaker37`) asked a Paradox-weight question, drawing Alastair's design
  displacement figure (1,410 lb/640 kg full sailing trim, minus crew and 70
  kg water ballast for trailering weight) and Glen Maxwell's admission he
  still had not gotten around to scaling "Zoë" — extended
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md — independent weight and displacement
  estimates](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#independent-weight-and-displacement-estimates)
  (cites [85]-[87]); cross-links added to `people/alastair-law.md` (cites
  [216]-[217]) and `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` (cite [219]). A
  genuine archive gap was confirmed at msgnum 2190 (between page_2164 and
  page_2165), joining the standing 2122 gap; flagged in the backlog, no
  action taken. No new files, no renames, no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Cursor set to 2170.

* **Update (batch page_2151–page_2160, msgnum 2176-2185)**: 6-20 July 2004.
  Four independent items. (1) The **"Has anyone gone sailing lately"**
  thread (opened batch 215) closed: William Serjeant pointed directly to
  Glen Maxwell's "Zoë's Sea Trials" article at its settled address,
  `www.smallsailboats.co.uk/paradox/trials.htm`; Alastair answered with a
  genuinely fresh update — no open-water adventures that year, but several
  weekend outings in sheltered estuaries — and a cautionary real-world
  ballast story: having added a season's gear (notably a second anchor)
  without feeling overweight, he removed 100 lb of lead entirely for his
  next outing, which proved disastrous — considerable weather helm and lost
  tracking with the center of gravity moved forward, promptly reversed by
  restoring the lead. Extended
  [operations/group-cruise-reports.md — where to find a written cruise
  report, absent a fresh
  one](/operations/group-cruise-reports.md#where-to-find-a-written-cruise-report-absent-a-fresh-one)
  (cites [33]-[34]); cross-links added to `references/paradox-uk-website.md`
  (cite [28]), `people/alastair-law.md` (cite [214], new "Sailing performance and
  weight" bullet), and `design/weight-and-ballast.md` (cite [84], new
  paragraph in "Real-world under-ballasted handling"). (2) **Smith offered his
  part-built Paradox "Swash" for sale**, citing his own divorce, with the
  archive's fullest itemized account of an unfinished hull's completion
  state and cost ($1,600+ in receipts, ~$2,000 estimated total material
  cost, $3,000 asking price); Nels inquired on a third party's behalf
  (further photos, remaining cost/time, glass and chine-runner status,
  offering a trailer and cash); Smith confirmed the boat unsold, credited David
  Beard with urging him to finish her himself through the divorce, and
  admitted growing less willing to give her up the longer the process ran.
  New section,
  [operations/secondhand-market.md — an unfinished hull for sale:
  "Swash"](/operations/secondhand-market.md#an-unfinished-hull-for-sale-swash-july-2004)
  (cites [23]-[25]); cross-links added to `people/david-c-smith.md` (cites [151]-[153])
  and `people/nels.md` (cite [93]). (3) **Derek Clark** reported building a
  1/10-scale cardboard mock-up of his own future Paradox to check dimensions
  and refresh his sense of the interior arrangement, confirmed he now has
  plans, a Don Elliott build book, sourced fastenings (Robbins Timber of
  Bristol, UK), lead, a building fund, and enough exterior ply for the
  bulkheads, bought a Land Rover for towing, and named his still-unstarted
  boat **"So-So."** Extended [construction/build-effort.md — staying
  motivated through a long
  build](/construction/build-effort.md#staying-motivated-through-a-long-build)
  (cites [65]-[67]); flagged in the backlog as a strengthened promote-later
  candidate for a dedicated `people/derek-clark.md` consolidation pass, given
  his substantial existing content already scattered across 8+ files. (4)
  Two minor independent items: **David Beard** floated experimentally
  fitting a small jib from a 14 ft Blue Jay dinghy to his own Paradox, purely
  to see what it would do — the only recorded proposal to add a headsail to
  the design, left unanswered — new section,
  [design/rig.md — a speculative small
  jib](/components/rig/index.md#a-speculative-small-jib) (cite [141]), cross-linked
  from `people/david-beard.md` (cite [226]); and **roger chan** (`light369369`,
  a below-threshold correspondent last seen in November 2001) resurfaced
  asking, unanswered, how a Paradox handles large motorboat wakes in shallow
  water and a following sea, quoting the "new sail rev." thread in full —
  folded into `operations/sailing-characteristics.md` (cite [41]) and a new
  closing section,
  [operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md — an open question on motorboat wakes
  and following
  seas](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md#an-open-question-on-motorboat-wakes-and-following-seas)
  (cite [32]). No new files, no renames, no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Timeline reached 6-20 July 2004.

## 2026-07-04

* **Update (batch page_2141–page_2150, msgnum 2166-2175)**: 18 June - 6 July
  2004. Three threads, all continuations of ongoing topics carried over from
  batches 213-214, plus one new minor thread. (1) The **"depth finder"**
  thread reached its close: Alastair gave the fullest practical case for a
  sounder from his own experience — soft mud banks 2-3 ft under the surface
  where grounding isn't obvious until speed is already lost, position-fixing
  by depth contour in fog, and a whole unit picked up new for £25 — and
  explained why he carries no petrol outboard (deck-stowed fuel is
  routinely washed off on his lengthy passages); chadoora clarified his
  original skepticism about needing a sounder at all was not personal, just
  "a value judgement"; **Derek Clark** gracefully closed the thread
  apologizing for over-reacting to chadoora's original post. Extended
  [operations/gps-navigation.md — fitting a depth sounder or fishfinder
  without through-hulling the
  bottom](/operations/gps-navigation.md#fitting-a-depth-sounder-or-fishfinder-without-through-hulling-the-bottom)
  (cites [14]-[17]); cross-links added to `people/alastair-law.md` (cites
  [212]-[213]). (2) The **"Greetings from Horta, Azores"** thread continued:
  Jack Gardiner answered David Beard's "total re-design?" musing about a 25
  ft Paradox with a one-word joke, "Steel!!!"; David Beard separately asked
  what rig "Hogfish Maximus" carries and whether photos exist of her under
  sail, left unanswered in the record. Extended
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md — a report from Horta, and
  chine runners proven on an ocean
  crossing](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md#a-report-from-horta-and-chine-runners-proven-on-an-ocean-crossing)
  (cites [23]-[24]); cross-links added to `people/jack-gardiner.md` (cite
  [90]) and `people/david-beard.md` (cite [224]). (3) The **"sailmakers"**
  thread closed: Kjell Karlsson thanked the list, reported he does not sew
  his own sails (following his sailmaking mentor Sven's own example), and
  relayed Sven's explanation of *why* the yuloh's blade is curved at all —
  to shift its center of rotation off its own longitudinal axis, a new
  rationale distinct from the existing how-to-build-the-curve material;
  William Serjeant offered a list of UK sailmakers (Banks, Crusader,
  Jeckells, Kemp, Lucas), recommending Lucas from firsthand experience.
  Extended [design/rig.md — sail
  pricing](/components/rig/index.md#sail-pricing) (cites [139]-[140]) and
  [design/yuloh-and-stowed-rig-retention.md — building the yuloh
  itself](/components/yuloh.md#building-the-yuloh-itself-blade-curvature-and-the-pivot-boltsocket)
  (cite [30]); cross-links added to `people/kjell-karlsson.md` (cite [33])
  and `references/paradox-uk-website.md` (cite [27]). A fourth, independent
  minor thread: **Todd** — a previously-seen correspondent, not a new
  identity — asked to hear of recent Paradox sailing adventures; David Beard
  pointed him to Glen Maxwell's existing Files-section article and
  Alastair's "Little Jim" web pages in lieu of a fresh trip report. Added as
  a new closing section,
  [operations/group-cruise-reports.md — where to find a written cruise
  report, absent a fresh
  one](/operations/group-cruise-reports.md#where-to-find-a-written-cruise-report-absent-a-fresh-one)
  (cites [31]-[32]); cross-link added to `people/david-beard.md` (cite
  [225]). No new files, no renames, no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link
  maintained throughout. Timeline reached 18 June - 6 July 2004 (the last
  two messages fell nearly two weeks after the rest of the batch).

* **Update (batch page_2131–page_2140, msgnum 2156-2165)**: 17-18 June 2004.
  Two independent threads, both self-contained within this batch. (1) A
  **"depth finder" thread**: Derek Clark asked whether anyone had fitted a
  depth finder to a wooden Paradox hull, and whether it needed to be
  through-hull; Jeff UK suggested a fishfinder instead; Alastair reported
  fitting one to his own transom (having found the transducer could not
  transmit through the hull's wood and epoxy from inside, and preferring to
  avoid a through-hull); a new below-threshold correspondent, "chadoora,"
  questioned whether an electronic depth finder is needed at all on a
  boat drawing about a foot, arguing coastal-navigation competence and the
  boat's own "slowing" against the bottom suffice; **Smith** made the
  Pacific-Northwest case for a depth sounder as cheap insurance and the most
  useful coastal-navigation tool in fog; Derek Clark closed the thread with a
  detailed rebuttal (RYA-certified, fog/position-finding rationale, and his
  own outboard carried for age/back reasons despite the designer's
  sail/yuloh-only preference). Added as a new section,
  [operations/gps-navigation.md — fitting a depth sounder or fishfinder
  without through-hulling the
  bottom](/operations/gps-navigation.md#fitting-a-depth-sounder-or-fishfinder-without-through-hulling-the-bottom)
  (cites [7]-[13]); cross-links added to `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite [149]) and
  `people/alastair-law.md` (cite [211]). (2) **Glen Maxwell's first at-sea report**
  from his Atlantic crossing: writing in from **Horta, Azores**, he thanked Smith
  and Nels for covering the list, congratulated Hugh Miller on launching
  "Petite Lady," and gave a detailed passage account aboard Chris Morejohn's
  **"Hogfish Maximus"** — a slow but uneventful crossing (Bermuda in 14 days/
  1,070 miles, then Flores in 17 days 20 hours/1,890 miles) — with the first
  firsthand verdict on a **chine-runner hull at genuine ocean-crossing scale**:
  the chine runners worked well even at this larger size, with the
  daggerboard needed for under 48 hours of the whole passage. He judged
  "Hogfish Maximus" itself too big for his own taste but thought a **21-25 ft
  Paradox** would suit ocean voyaging well; Smith replied enviously, and David
  Beard wondered what a 25 ft Paradox would take, guessing a total redesign.
  Extended
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md — a report from Horta, and
  chine runners proven on an ocean
  crossing](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md#a-report-from-horta-and-chine-runners-proven-on-an-ocean-crossing)
  (cites [20]-[22], the canonical home, continuing the existing "Gone
  sailing" departure narrative) and
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#glen-maxwells-case-for-blue-water-capability-from-personal-experience)
  (cites [37]-[39], extending Glen's existing blue-water endorsement and
  adding a new "What remains unresolved" bullet on the untested 21-25 ft
  stretched-Paradox idea); cross-links added to `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite [150]),
  `people/david-beard.md` (cite [223]), and `people/chris-morejohn.md` (no
  new citation, prose-only). A separate, incidental sailmaker endorsement from
  David Beard — recommending **Dabbler** on hearing that sailmaker Hugh Horten
  uses Dabbler-built sails on his own sailing canoes — extended
  [design/rig.md — sail pricing](/components/rig/index.md#sail-pricing) (cite [138]),
  with a cross-link added to `people/david-beard.md` (cite [222]). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings; narrate-once-cross-link maintained
  throughout. Timeline reached 17-18 June 2004.

* **Update (batch page_2121–page_2130, msgnum 2146-2155)**: 12-17 June 2004.
  Two threads, both direct continuations of ongoing questions, plus one
  independent report, none newly opened outside existing topics. (1) The
  **"Yuloh" thread** continued past batch 212's resolution with **Smith**
  answering David Beard's wood-species question — a single piece of **5/4
  Douglas Fir**, chosen for straight grain despite running slightly diagonal
  across the board, judging most species/qualities would work fine — and
  **Dave Bolduc** restating his own firsthand preference, having used both
  the new and old yuloh aboard Matt Layden's own boat: the original's extra
  curve sculls more automatically, while the newer, straighter oar is more
  powerful and easier to control for blade pitch at the cost of more wrist
  effort. Extended
  [design/yuloh-and-stowed-rig-retention.md](/components/yuloh.md#wood-for-the-yuloh)
  (cites [28]-[29]) and
  [operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md](/operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md#matt-laydens-own-yuloh-speed-and-the-straight-vs-curved-blade-trade-off)
  (cite [28]); cross-links added to `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite [148]) and
  `people/bolduc/dave.md` (cite [80]). (2) A **New Zealand Paradox update**:
  Dave Bolduc reported the builder's surname for the first time — **Hugh
  Miller** (previously recorded only as "Hugh") — still sailing and enjoying
  his finished "Petite Lady," with further photos posted. Propagated to
  [boats/index.md](/boats/index.md)'s "Other known builds" block and
  `people/bolduc/dave.md`'s existing "Petite Lady" bullet. (3) A new **"Yard
  and Boom" thread**: David Beard asked what effect spar shape has on
  pointing ability; Alastair answered that the yard is shaped mainly for
  strength-to-weight (flat edge up) while the boom's shape mainly governs
  furling, tying together material already on file about the yard's bend
  directly setting the sail's draft. Added as a new section,
  [design/rig.md — why yard and boom shape matter for pointing](/components/rig/index.md#why-yard-and-boom-shape-matter-for-pointing)
  (cites [133]-[134]); cross-links added to `people/david-beard.md` (cite
  [221]) and `people/alastair-law.md` (cite [210]). (4) A **"sailmakers" cost
  thread**: Kjell Karlsson reported a Stockholm sailmaker's **12,000 SEK**
  quote (about **$2,000**, some four times the going US price once Nels
  checked the exchange rate); Dave Bolduc answered by re-quoting Glen
  Maxwell's own msgnum-2020 yard-deflection sail-building advice in full
  (a repost, cited once); Jack Gardiner then gave the record's fullest
  from-scratch home-sewn sail account — 5.5 oz Dacron, Sailrite thread and
  seamstick tape, broadseams sewn using a vinyl-gutter sliding track over a
  PVC-pipe core, all for under $150 US on a donated Sailrite LS-1 Ultrafeed.
  Extended
  [design/rig.md — sail pricing](/components/rig/index.md#sail-pricing)
  and
  [design/rig.md — building a lugsail at home](/components/rig/index.md#building-a-lugsail-at-home-measuring-yard-deflection-before-cutting)
  (cites [135]-[137]); cross-links added to `people/kjell-karlsson.md` (cite
  [32]), `people/nels.md` (cite [92]), and `people/jack-gardiner.md` (cite
  [89]). (5) Separately, **Robert** (`carolfo`/"cheepwings", standing
  below-threshold identity) asked, citing Don Elliott's own cost figures
  ($217 kit, $330 completed sail), whether Don's Paradox project was
  ultimately a success — unanswered on the list, but already established in
  the archive's own record (Don's boat "The Edge" launched years earlier).
  Added to
  [people/don-elliott.md](/people/don-elliott.md#sail-cost-and-a-later-question-about-the-outcome-of-his-own-build)
  (cite [42]). No new files, no renames, no prunings — all content extended
  existing pages per narrate-once-cross-link. Timeline reached 12-17 June
  2004. Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie

* **Update (batch page_2111–page_2120, msgnum 2136-2145)**: 7-11 June 2004.
  Three threads, all continuations of ongoing questions, none newly opened.
  (1) The **"new guy" introduction thread**, carried over from batch 211's
  Robert (`carolfo`/"cheepwings") flush-cabin-sides question, closed with
  **Nels** answering that the stock stepped-in side decks store the yuloh
  and lowered spars when not in use, and **LEON** (`melodymann`, standing
  below-threshold identity) following up with an untested thought — whether
  a sprit rig (no boom/yard to stow) or cabin-roof brackets could remove the
  need for that stowage in a flush-sided design. Added to
  [design/flush-cabin-sides-idea.md](/design/flush-cabin-sides-idea.md#flush-cabin-sides-as-an-unexplored-alternative)
  (cites [9]-[10]); cross-link added to `people/nels.md` (cite [91]). (2) The
  **"Budget Building" cost thread**, carried over from batch 211, saw LEON
  ask for photos of Alastair's boat and Alastair repoint to his "Little Jim"
  website — a minor addendum to the existing itemized UK cost breakdown.
  Extended `construction/build-effort.md` "Cost" section (cites [63]-[64]);
  cross-link added to `people/alastair-law.md` (cite [208]). (3) The centerpiece:
  a **"Yuloh" thread** reopened the plans' ambiguous "30mm longitudinal
  curve concave downward" instruction, four months after the Feb-2004
  "yuloh and pivot questions" thread first tackled it. **David Beard**,
  starting his own yuloh build, asked how to read the figure and where the
  bend should start/stop; **Steve** pointed to a laminating photo in the
  group's Photos album and read the 30mm as deviation from a straight
  end-to-end line; **Smith** clarified it as **deflection from the yuloh's own
  centerline**, not a fixed radius, since the blade is laminated around an
  arbitrary center and shaped afterward with some springback, and recalled
  secondhand from Dave Bolduc that Matt Layden had since built a new,
  differently-curved yuloh; **Alastair** restated his own lamination method
  (two planks over a 30mm block, ends weighted down to the floor until the
  glue cured); **David Beard** then confirmed **Matt Layden's new yuloh is
  straight**, working well but harder on the arm and wrist than a curved
  blade — resolving Smith's own remembered uncertainty and independently
  corroborating Dave Bolduc's April-2003 account on
  [operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md](/operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md#matt-laydens-own-yuloh-speed-and-the-straight-vs-curved-blade-trade-off);
  **Steve** then built his own yuloh from two Douglas fir 2x4s (thinned on a
  radial arm saw, handle narrowed with a saber saw, laminated via Alastair's
  method). Extended
  [design/yuloh-and-stowed-rig-retention.md](/components/yuloh.md#building-the-yuloh-itself-blade-curvature-and-the-pivot-boltsocket)
  (cites [22]-[27]), with a light corroborating addition to
  `operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md` (cite [27]); cross-links added
  to `people/david-beard.md` (cites [219]-[220]), `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite
  [147]), `people/alastair-law.md` (cite [209]), and `people/steve-kc.md` (cites
  [9]-[10]). No new files, no renames, no prunings — all content extended
  existing pages per narrate-once-cross-link. Timeline reached 7-11 June
  2004. Standing-open-items check: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie
  Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/dclark52001,
  Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2, kayaker37/Paul, paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V.,
  Steve/Kansas City, Howard Welch, Jason Stancil, John Hall, Todd, Alf
  Sawatzky, Shane Hersey, Bengt Genell, Bill/wam_53, Craig/crag8,
  Ron/churo65, Phil/eliallegra(topsail@), Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-
  Larry, Ken Preston/kenpreston46, James/jcbanik, Roger Nuffer/rogernuffer,
  Robert/boat142, Robert/carolfo(cheepwings), LEON/melodymann, William
  Serjeant all checked; only LEON/melodymann resurfaced (already-standing
  identity, confirmed via X-Yahoo-Profile `melodymann` u=94140893 on both
  his messages), not newly flagged. No new below-threshold correspondents

  (David Beard) confirmed via From:/X-Yahoo-Profile on all four of his

  2144-2145 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing directly
  from batch 211's last msgnum 2135, no new gap (batch 210's confirmed 2122

  against dispatch instructions exactly (1580/2897/1188/3989/1505/2245/

* **Update (batch page_2101–page_2110, msgnum 2126-2135)**: all 2 June 2004,
  save the last message on 3 June and one on 5 June 2004. Two independent
  threads plus one closed-out below-threshold question and one new
  correspondent. (1) **Kjell Karlsson**, ahead of ordering his sail, revived
  his own earlier (msgnum 1478) question about the lug rig's dynamics — was
  the forward ("jib") portion of the sail too small, and would moving the
  halyard aft help, or is the whole question moot given the lug sail's free
  movement. **Smith** answered with the fullest textbook-style tacking-mechanics
  case in the archive: think in terms of the sail's overall center of effort
  rather than a fore/aft mast split, find balance empirically via halyard
  placement once trimmed, diagnose most tacking trouble as rudder stall
  (pushed to leeward too fast) or over-sheeting, tack a catboat rig more
  slowly with sheets tended throughout, and add telltales at luff/leech/draft
  plus a set aft of the mast; praised the balanced lug's shape and low
  center of effort, and floated supplying Matt Layden's own "new sail" post
  to any loft he might use. **Alastair** answered separately, putting the
  question in hull-form terms instead: compared to a centerboard dinghy
  Paradox is sluggish tacking simply as a heavy-displacement, full-keel-
  equivalent craft — probably behaving just as the numbers for that hull
  type would predict, echoing Dave Bolduc's "stiff tracking" verdict from
  batch 210's neighboring material. Added as a new section, "Center of
  effort, rudder stall, and telltale placement as the real tacking
  variables," on
  [operations/sailing-characteristics.md](/operations/sailing-characteristics.md#center-of-effort-rudder-stall-and-telltale-placement-as-the-real-tacking-variables)
  (cites [38]-[40]); cross-link summaries added to `people/kjell-karlsson.md`
  (cite [31]), `people/david-c-smith.md` (cite [146]), and `people/alastair-law.md` (cite
  [205]). (2) A **newcomer's generic "how long/how much does a Paradox
  cost" question** (Ron, `rondune508`, below-threshold, no further posts)
  drew **Jack Gardiner**'s $2,000-8,000 US cost-range answer (plywood grade,
  finish, and hardware quality as the real drivers — "an item is worth what
  someone will pay for it") and **Alastair**'s own 18-month/£2,500 headline
  figures with a pointer to his "Little Jim" website; days later **LEON**
  (`melodymann`, standing below-threshold identity) asked the same question
  from the other direction in a new "Budget Building" thread (how could such
  a small boat cost £2,500), drawing Alastair's fullest itemized UK cost
  breakdown on record (£600 timber, £375 ply, £630 epoxy/consumables, £30
  insulation foam, £170 Lexan, £350 sail, plus hardware/tools/gear, trailer
  not included) and his note that Matt Layden's own suggested exterior ply,
  and skipping pre-planing timber to exact sizes, would cut costs further.
  Extended `construction/build-effort.md` "Cost" section (cites [58]-[62]);
  cross-link summaries added to `people/jack-gardiner.md` (cite [88]) and
  `people/alastair-law.md` (a restated-breakdown sentence in the "Little Jim"
  intro, cites [206]-[207]). (3) **LEON** separately closed out his own
  unanswered batch-210 "Epoxy" question (WEST System vs. generic, and
  masonry sealant) with a terse one-line follow-up, "I guess thats a
  no..." — folded into the existing "Brand-name epoxy versus cheaper
  equivalents, and masonry sealant" section on
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#brand-name-epoxy-versus-cheaper-equivalents-and-masonry-sealant)
  (cite [29a]). (4) A **new correspondent, "Robert" (`carolfo`/`cheepwings`,
  distinct from the standing below-threshold "Robert"/`boat142`)**,
  introduced himself after resolving a Yahoo login problem, a longtime fan
  who "might" build one someday, and asked an unanswered question about
  building a Paradox with the cabin sides flush to the hull sides for
  interior room and knockdown righting — folded as a new "Flush cabin
  sides, as an unexplored alternative" section on
  [design/flush-cabin-sides-idea.md](/design/flush-cabin-sides-idea.md#flush-cabin-sides-as-an-unexplored-alternative)
  (cite [8]). No new files, no renames, no prunings — all content extended
  existing pages, consistent with narrate-once-cross-link. No archive gap
  this batch: msgnum run 2126-2135 fully consecutive, continuing directly
  from batch 210's last msgnum 2125. Standing-open-items check — Jeff
  UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3,
  Derek Clark/dclark52001, Fredrik, Jake, Lugger #2, kayaker37/Paul,
  paull01/Paul-Seattle, Paul V., Steve/Kansas City, Howard Welch, Jason
  Stancil, John Hall, Todd, Alf Sawatzky, Shane Hersey, Bengt Genell,
  Bill/wam_53, Craig/crag8, Ron/churo65, Phil/eliallegra(topsail@),
  Shorty/shortypen, LJGeib/second-Larry, Ken Preston/kenpreston46,
  James/jcbanik, Roger Nuffer/rogernuffer, Robert/boat142, LEON/melodymann,
  William Serjeant (UK website) all checked — only LEON/melodymann
  resurfaced (already-standing identity, confirmed via X-Yahoo-Profile
  `melodymann` u=94140893 on both his messages, not newly flagged); one
  genuinely new below-threshold correspondent this batch, Ron/rondune508,
  and one new distinct-identity below-threshold correspondent,
  Robert/carolfo ("cheepwings"), both added to the standing list going
  forward. Attribution-trap check — dlb@.../buster38801 (David Beard) and
  whisper@.../meerkatoz (David LeBlanc) both had no traffic this batch;

  one message, no confusion with dclark@.../dclark52001 (Derek Clark, no

  of the three this batch. Byte sizes cross-checked via `wc -c` against

  2992/1309 bytes for page_2101 through page_2110 respectively), no

* **Update (batch page_2091–page_2100, msgnum 2115-2121, 2123-2125)**: 15-16
  May 2004, then 21-29 May 2004. One continued thread plus two independent
  minor items. (1) The "Storm Tactics Handbook"/heaving-to thread carried
  over from batch 209 pivoted into a **second round of "Whisper"'s
  light-air tacking trouble** — the same weathercocking/ballast-trim
  question first debated in July 2002, unresolved then and reopened here.
  **David Beard** reported "Whisper" stopping dead, nose-to-wind, on a tack
  in light air; **Alastair** answered from his own "Little Jim" experience
  (too much weight forward hangs the bow to wind, too much weight aft
  prevents her from coming up at all — a fix he'd applied himself, now
  suspected needing rebalancing after a winter's added gear); **Larry**
  (`LJGeib`, the standing "second Larry," distinct from Larry Whited) gave
  the same weight-aft "weathervaning" fix he'd offered in the 2002 debate,
  plus a sail-forward/mast-rake alternative; **Nels** added a Bolger/sharpie
  momentum theory (lighter displacement weathercocks less) and a
  rudder-stall caution (too much rudder trying to force the bow through
  before way is lost turns the rudder into a brake — recommending minimal
  rudder, falling off to regather speed, or an oar-stroke kick); LJGeib
  pushed back on the sharpie comparison, citing Paradox's ~1,400 lb weight
  and his own sailing canoe's identical behavior, agreeing the rudder would
  stall easily. As in 2002, no resolution specific to "Whisper" was
  reached. Added as a new section, "A second round: weathercocking,
  ballast, and rudder stall," directly following the original 2002 tacking
  debate on
  [operations/sailing-characteristics.md](/operations/sailing-characteristics.md#a-second-round-weathercocking-ballast-and-rudder-stall)
  (cites [31]-[37]); closing cross-link added to

  cross-link summaries added to `boats/whisper.md`, `people/david-beard.md`
  (cites [217]-[218]), `people/alastair-law.md` (cites [203]-[204], plus a new
  "weathercocking/ballast-trim diagnosis" bullet), and `people/nels.md`
  (cite [90], plus a new bullet). (2) **LEON** (`melodymann`, standing
  below-threshold identity), in an unrelated one-off message, asked whether
  WEST System epoxy differs meaningfully from generic epoxy at about half
  the price, and whether anyone had tried masonry sealant on a boat —
