  Matt Layden's evident intent was strength over repairability — new section
  on [chine runner construction](/chine-runners/construction.md#build-order-runners-integral-with-the-bottom-not-a-sacrificial-add-on).
  (3) **Centerpiece:** the "Sea Trial article" saga's true close (William
  Serjeant posts the finished "Zoë's Sea Trials" page) immediately followed
  by the "Double Rudders" thread pivoting into self-steering — Serjeant asks
  what wind-vane gear suits Paradox, William Longyard gives the archive's
  fullest auto-pilot-versus-wind-vane comparison, Serjeant explains that
  Paradox's **folding rudder** blocks the trim-tab wind vane he wants, and
  Longyard suggests a Bowden-cable fix — new sections on
  [self-steering](/operations/self-steering.md#auto-pilot-versus-wind-vane-self-steering-a-practical-comparison),
  extending [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md#press-coverage-two-2003-magazine-articles),
  [Small Sailboats / Paradox-UK websites](/references/paradox-uk-website.md),
  [rudder and chine experiments](/components/rudder.md), and
  [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md). No new files; all
  content extended existing pages.

* **Update (batch page_1851–page_1860, msgnum 1874-1883)**: Three threads
  across 23-24 January 2004, all extending existing pages (no new files).
  (1) The **"Sea Trial article"** saga's true close — William Serjeant
  confirms he has everything needed and only has to build the page before
  reproducing it on paradox-uk.co.uk; extended
  [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md#press-coverage-two-2003-magazine-articles) and
  [Small Sailboats / Paradox-UK websites](/references/paradox-uk-website.md).
  (2) A new **"Double Rudders"** thread — William Serjeant floats twin
  rudders to free the transom centerline for an outboard, paired with a
  UK wind-vane self-steering unit; William Longyard judges it not worth the
  added complexity and drag on Paradox's narrow transom; new section on
  [rudder and chine
  experiments](/components/rudder.md#a-twin-rudder-proposal-for-a-centerline-outboard-judged-not-worth-the-complexity).
  (3) **The centerpiece** — Jack Gardiner's "building update" thread turns to
  cabin-height ideas: extending the cabin forward, stepping up the aft hatch,
  or a plexiglass dome, the last mistakenly attributed to "Nels" then
  corrected to **Smith**, who clarifies the dome was never meant for a Paradox
  and supplies the archive's only aircraft-canopy precedent (the F-16
  fighter canopy, adapted for unlimited-hydroplane racing boats); Alastair
  counters with a floor-lowering-vs-roof-raising design tradeoff (boom
  height, mast stowage, sail center of pressure), and Jack settles on a
  stretched-length Paradox as the likelier answer; new section "A raised
  cabin, canopy, and cockpit-height ideas" on [aluminum and steel Paradox
  (speculative)](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-raised-cabin-canopy-and-cockpit-height-ideas),
  extending [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md#construction-begins),
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md), and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md). Eight files
  extended (`boats/zoe.md`, `references/paradox-uk-website.md`,
  `design/rudder.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`, `people/jack-gardiner.md`,
  `people/david-c-smith.md`, `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`,
  `people/alastair-law.md`); no new files, no renames, no prunings. Standing-
  watchlist check: none of Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark/dclark52001, Fredrik, Jake,
  "Lugger #2," kayaker37/Paul, Steve/Kansas City, or Jason Stancil resurfaced
  this batch. Smith (`kd7ixk`/`betelgeuserdude`) appeared but Derek Clark did
  not, so no Smith-vs-Derek-Clark confusion risk arose; no David Beard or David
  LeBlanc traffic this batch; Jack Gardiner's `alex29`/`tidybowlmann`
  identity (u=111173743) confirmed consistent, not split. Msgnum run
  1874-1883 confirmed distinct and consecutive, continuing from batch 185's
  1873; all ten byte sizes matched dispatch instructions exactly, no
  stale-read issue; no citation-number collisions in any of the eight
  touched files.

* **Update (batch page_1841–page_1850, msgnum 1864-1873)**: Two threads across
  21-22 January 2004, both extending existing pages (no new files).
  **Centerpiece:** a new **"enlarged paradox?"** thread — newcomer
  **Jason Stancil**, over 6 ft tall, asks whether a straight 20% scale-up is
  really as simple as multiplying every dimension; **Smith** (6'1", plenty of
  room himself) points to Glen Maxwell's already-circulated larger-Paradox
  files; **Nels** adds a two-up-cruising case for going bigger (a visitor
  left standing in the rain for lack of cabin room; Andrew Graham's own
  humid-weather discomfort aboard "Zero"); **Larry ("LJGeib")** gives the
  archive's fullest breakdown of three named stretch methods — "Big
  Paradox" (the existing larger-Paradox lines), "Stretch Paradox" (lengthen
  only, spreading the stations), and "Pug Paradox" (a non-uniform,
  amidships-concentrated stretch borrowed from kayak design) — all capped
  around a 10% enlargement; extended
  [Stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#enlarging-for-a-tall-sailor-and-three-named-stretch-methods),
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md), and [Nels](/people/nels.md). Second thread: the
  **"Sea Trial article"** saga continues from batch 184 — Glen Maxwell
  offers to zip pictures directly to below-threshold **Derek Clark**, who
  asks to also share them with UK builder **William Serjeant**; Serjeant
  separately asks Glen's permission to reproduce the *MAIB* article on his
  own paradox-uk.co.uk site (granted) and, prompted by the article, asks
  about topping lifts, answered by **Alastair** from his own trial (possible
  without one, much simpler with one); extended
  [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md#press-coverage-two-2003-magazine-articles),
  [furling system](/components/rig/furling.md#a-uk-builders-topping-lift-question),
  [Small Sailboats / Paradox-UK websites](/references/paradox-uk-website.md),
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), and
  [Glen C. Maxwell — build and "Zoë"](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md).
  Eight files extended (`design/variants/stretched-paradox.md`,
  `people/david-c-smith.md`, `people/nels.md`, `boats/zoe.md`,
  `design/furling-system.md`, `references/paradox-uk-website.md`,
  `people/alastair-law.md`, `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`); no new
  files, no renames, no prunings. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold
  **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`) resurfaced three times (continuing the Sea
  Trial article thread); a new below-threshold newcomer, **Jason Stancil**,
  asked the enlargement question that opened this batch's centerpiece
  thread (kept inline, no boat/build); none of Jeff UK/roverticket,
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake,
  "Lugger #2," kayaker37/Paul, or Steve/Kansas City resurfaced. Smith vs. Derek
  Clark attribution trap checked carefully — both appear this batch, but in
  different threads, verified against `From:`/`X-Yahoo-Profile:` headers
  throughout; no David Beard or David LeBlanc traffic this batch; Jack
  Gardiner's identity not implicated. Msgnum run 1864-1873 confirmed
  distinct and consecutive, continuing from batch 184's 1863; all ten byte
  sizes matched dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; no
  citation-number collisions in any of the eight touched files.

* **Update (batch page_1831–page_1840, msgnum 1854-1863)**: Six small threads
  across 19-21 January 2004, all extending existing pages (no new files).
  (1) A reader, **Howard Welch**, having just finished William Longyard's
  book *A Speck on the Sea*, asks how "capsule" boats provision and cook for
  a long voyage; Longyard answers with the archive's fullest provisioning
  account — packing food like a grocery stockboy's boxes, ~30 gallons of
  water for a 75-day passage, Robert Manry's *Tinkerbelle* for interior
  cutaways, primus-on-gimbal cooking, and a warning against alcohol fuel's
  near-invisible flame; extended
  [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md#author-of-a-speck-on-the-sea).
  (2) Once Glen Maxwell's *Messing About In Boats* "Zoë" sea-trial article
  reached the Files section, **David Beard** suggests Glen write a book of
  his own stories, and below-threshold **Derek Clark** thanks Glen and asks
  (unanswered) for the pictures in a separate file, since his Mac would not
  download them embedded; extended
  [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md#press-coverage-two-2003-magazine-articles) and
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md). (3) **Centerpiece:** **Jack
  Gardiner** posts his first construction photographs (deck framing on his
  S&G hull); William Longyard can't find them (sorted to the bottom of the
  section) and Glen Maxwell asks for interior/seaming detail, drawing out
  Jack's fullest technique account — no wire-stitching, joints clamped/pinned
  with sheetrock screws, West epoxy and silica with wood flour to slow runs,
  4 oz tape on inside joints, 12 oz biaxial cloth on the bow glue-ups;
  extended
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md#construction-begins),
  [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md), and
  [Glen C. Maxwell — build and "Zoë"](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md).
  (4) UK builder **Bill Serjeant** announces his Yahoo profile changed from
  `barnacleid` to `barnacletoo`; extended
  [Small Sailboats / Paradox-UK websites](/references/paradox-uk-website.md).
  Six files extended (`people/william-longyard.md`, `boats/zoe.md`,
  `people/david-beard.md`, `people/jack-gardiner.md`,
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`,
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md`); no new files, no renames, no
  prunings. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold **Derek Clark**
  (`dclark52001`) resurfaced once (thanking Glen for the Sea Trial article);
  none of Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2," kayaker37/Paul, or
  Steve/Kansas City resurfaced. David Beard vs. David LeBlanc and Smith vs.
  Derek Clark attribution traps checked against `From:`/`X-Yahoo-Profile:`
  headers throughout (only David Beard's `dlb@`/`buster38801` and Derek
  Clark's `dclark52001` appeared; no David LeBlanc or Smith/`kd7ixk` traffic
  this batch); Jack Gardiner's `alex29`/`tidybowlmann` identity (u=111173743)
  confirmed consistent. Msgnum run 1854-1863 confirmed distinct and
  consecutive, continuing from batch 183's 1853; all ten byte sizes matched
  dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; no citation-number
  collisions in any of the six touched files.

* **Update (batch page_1821–page_1830, msgnum 1844-1853)**: Six small,
  distinct threads across 14-19 January 2004. (1) The "Wiring for masthead
  light" thread closes out with two more builder answers — **Glen Maxwell**
  routes his own wiring outside the mast at the top (photographed under
  "topping lift" in the Files section) and **David Beard** instead routes
  his flush in a shallow groove cut into the mast — new in
  [running lights](/operations/running-lights.md#routing-masthead-light-wiring-outside-the-mast-below-the-sheave).
  (2) **Jack Gardiner** reaches his "keel laying ceremony" hull milestone —
  fully three-dimensional, stitch-and-glue, no nails or screws — drawing
  congratulations from Glen Maxwell and William Longyard, and answering
  below-threshold **Derek Clark**'s question that he is installing both
  chine logs and sheer clamps, laminated from plywood/epoxy with 50 in-wide
  12 oz biaxial cloth on the bottom; extended
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md#construction-begins).
  (3) **Centerpiece:** a new "Lightning rod system" thread — **Steve**
  (Kansas City), weighing whether to fit his mast's lightning-dissipation
  system, asks for firsthand strike evidence, and **Glen Maxwell** gives the
  archive's fullest explanation of the de-ionization (not attraction)
  principle behind Matt Layden's design, illustrated with his own
  catamaran's years strike-free until an accidental epoxy coating insulated
  its grounded skegs — silent electronics failures over the following months
  traced the fault back to the lost ground path; new file
  [Lightning protection](/design/lightning-protection.md). (4) Glen Maxwell
  closes a months-old thread, finally uploading the reduced-size *Messing
  About In Boats* "Zoë" sea-trial article (Aug 15, 2003 issue) to the Files
  section in both full-color and photo-free versions; extended
  [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md#press-coverage-two-2003-magazine-articles). (5) Andrew
  Graham reports a further, longer solo passage — a three-day singlehanded
  cruise across open Moreton Bay to Moreton Island in 15-knot winds, again
  using Zero's shallow draught to shortcut sandbanks — extended
  [Aussie Paradox](/boats/aussie-paradox.md#a-three-day-singlehanded-cruise)
  and [Andrew Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md). Cross-linked from
  [Steve (Kansas City)](/people/steve-kc.md),
  [Glen C. Maxwell — build and "Zoë"](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md),
  and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md). One new file
  (`design/lightning-protection.md`, indexed in `design/index.md`); eight
  files extended; no renames or prunings. Standing-watchlist check:
  below-threshold **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`) resurfaced once (the Jack
  Gardiner chine-log/sheer-clamp question); none of Jeff UK/roverticket,
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake,
  "Lugger #2," kayaker37/Paul, or Steve/Kansas City's original open item
  (now a recurring correspondent) raised new concerns. Smith (`kd7ixk`) did not
  appear this batch, so no Smith/Derek Clark confusion risk arose; Jack
  Gardiner's `alex29`/`tidybowlmann` identity confirmed consistent via
  headers throughout. Msgnum run 1844-1853 confirmed distinct and fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 182's 1843; all ten byte sizes
  matched the dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; verified
  no new citation-number collisions in any of the nine touched files.

* **Update (batch page_1811–page_1820, msgnum 1834-1843)**: The "mast" thread
  continues and closes: **Smith** gives the fullest case that aluminum and wood
  masts of the same diameter do not carry comparable strength, and that a
  secondhand spar should either be sized by yacht-design spar formulas or
  bought new rather than trusted to luck; **Alastair** adds that converting
  an already-finished hull to a different mast would be harder than simply
  building the plans' wooden mast right the first time (which he himself
  found easier than expected); below-threshold **Derek Clark** asks,
  unanswered, whether Matt Layden's own mast was engineering-calculated or
  materials-driven, and Smith closes with a one-line "Spare masts" reply; new
  in [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#aluminum-as-a-mast-material-engineering-fit-not-just-substitution).
  The "Translating Sven's Book" project gets a further Nels update
  (confirming William Longyard's chapter count and photocopy offer, plus a
  Scandinavian-membership aside) and a related **"Swedish members?"**
  thread — a Swedish newcomer ("bgenell") asks, in Swedish, whether other
  Scandinavians are on the list, and Nels answers with the archive's only
  member-count breakdown by country (~15 Sweden, 2 Norway, 5 Denmark, 1+
  Finland) plus a friend's English translation of the original question;
  new in [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-proposed-group-project-to-translate-sven-yrvinds-full-book-into-english).
  **Centerpiece:** a new "Wiring for masthead light" thread — **Steve**
  (Kansas City) asks whether other builders route masthead-light wiring
  fully inside the mast or exit it below the light to run outside, and
  **Alastair** answers from his own build (wires exit below the mast's
  solid top and run up the outside, with a hindsight wish he'd instead run
  copper strips under the glass sheathing near the top); new in
  [running lights](/operations/running-lights.md#routing-masthead-light-wiring-outside-the-mast-below-the-sheave).
  Extended [Nels](/people/nels.md), [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md),
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), and
  [Steve (Kansas City)](/people/steve-kc.md) with cross-links and citations
  for all of the above. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold
  **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`) resurfaced once more (the "mast" thread's
  close); none of Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2",
  kayaker37/Paul resurfaced. **Smith vs. Derek Clark attribution trap**
  carefully checked against `From:`/`X-Yahoo-Profile:` headers, since both
  appear this batch (Smith = `kd7ixk`/`betelgeuserdude`; Derek Clark =
  `dclark52001`) — no confusion. Timeline: 11-13 January 2004. No new
  files, no renames, no prunings — all content extends existing pages.
  Msgnum run 1834-1843 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing
  directly from batch 181's 1833; all ten byte sizes matched dispatch
  instructions exactly, no stale-read issue.

* **Update (batch page_1801–page_1810, msgnum 1824-1833)**: Nels advances the
  "Translating Sven's Book" project with a concrete mechanic — scanning each
  chapter to PDF and circulating them one at a time — and names Swedish,
  Finnish, and Norwegian list members who have translated for the group
  before; new in
  [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-proposed-group-project-to-translate-sven-yrvinds-full-book-into-english).
  The LED-navigation-lights thread gets a coda on charging voltage and
  COLREGS: **Smith** asks whether Alastair's LED figures hold under a
  charging battery, and restates that COLREGS side lights, unlike the
  steaming/stern functions, may never be folded into a combined tricolor
  unit; new in
  [running lights](/operations/running-lights.md#running-leds-off-a-charging-battery-rather-than-a-steady-12v-supply).
  A new one-off "Bow Fairlead" exchange has Smith asking Glen Maxwell whether
  his PVC fairlead horns are drilled or just scuffed for bond, and whether
  they flex; new in
  [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md#glen-maxwells-bow-fairlead-construction).
  **Centerpiece:** a new "mast" thread — **Derek Clark** asks whether a
  secondhand aluminum Skipper 12 dinghy mast can be reused for a Paradox,
  and **William Longyard** gives the archive's fullest answer on reusing a
  ready-made metal mast: match the plans' mast minor-axis width, inspect
  the whole length for signs of prior weakness (circumferential holes, a
  straightened former bend), and — restating his general case for
  aluminum masts — favor them for being lighter, more reliable, and
  maintenance-free, with a cable-tie "Y" trick to stop mast wiring
  "pinging" at night; new in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#reusing-a-secondhand-dinghy-mast).
  Extended [Nels](/people/nels.md), [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md),
  [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md), and
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) with cross-links and
  citations for all of the above. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold
  **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`) resurfaced twice (the "mast" thread);
  none of Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2", kayaker37/Paul, or
  Steve/Kansas City resurfaced. **Smith vs. Derek Clark attribution trap**
  carefully checked against `From:`/`X-Yahoo-Profile:` headers, since both
  appear this batch in adjacent threads (Smith = `kd7ixk`/`betelgeuserdude`
  in the LED/fairlead threads; Derek Clark = `dclark52001` in the mast
  thread) — no confusion. Timeline: 9-10 January 2004. No new files, no
  renames, no prunings — all content extends existing pages. Msgnum run
  1824-1833 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing directly
  from batch 180's 1823; all ten byte sizes matched dispatch instructions
  exactly, no stale-read issue. Full detail in `.okf-progress/backlog.md`
  (batch 181 entry).

* **Update (batch page_1781–page_1790, msgnum 1804-1813)**: William Longyard
  relays Sven Yrvind's own integrated rudder-transom design — a hinged
  false transom doubling as a kick-up guard and sea-anchor jigger, a
  rudder-mounted mast for leverage/self-steering, a figure-eight trim-tab
  pulley giving "power steering" and airplane-flap-like low-speed power,
  and friction sticks for tiller-taming and shoal-angle locking — which
  Yrvind judged suitable for Paradox too; new in
  [rudder and chine experiments](/components/rudder.md#sven-yrvinds-own-integrated-rudder-transom-system).
  The "Main Sheet" thread's magazine source is pinned down precisely in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#the-sailing-magazine-photograph-matts-actual-sheet-routing-confirmed):
  Derek Clark's month query and Larry's confirmation identify the photo as
  running in *Sailing Magazine*'s June 2003 issue, in an article titled
  "To Key Largo, Any Way You Can" covering the WaterTribe Everglades
  Challenge Matt Layden won. In
  [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-revised-translation-and-an-open-question-about-sven-yrvinds-own-design-choices),
  Kjell Karlsson posts a revised "Chapter9rev" translation of Sven
  Yrvind's book chapter after a further conversation with Yrvind, asking
  Glen Maxwell to remove the outdated original; Bert Vercauteren praises
  the chapter and asks why Yrvind chose not to use the chine
  runner/heavy-displacement Paradox concept on his own latest project, a
  question the archive leaves unanswered. Alastair fulfills a
  months-old suggestion (from Derek Clark, batch ~166) with a two-part
  LED navigation-light write-up; part 1's electrical theory — current vs.
  voltage driving, forward-voltage/operating-current figures, resistor
  sizing, and the series-wiring technique that triples light output for
  the same power draw — is new in
  [running lights](/operations/running-lights.md#led-electrical-theory-and-how-to-wire-several-in-series).
  Extended [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md),
  [Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md),
  [Bert Vercauteren](/people/bert-vercauteren.md), and
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) with cross-links and citations for all
  of the above. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold **Derek Clark**
  (`dclark52001`) resurfaced (the magazine-month query, folded into
  `design/rig.md` per the existing below-threshold convention); none of
  Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2", kayaker37/Paul, or
  Steve/Kansas City resurfaced. No new person pages, no renames, no
  prunings — all content extended existing pages. Timeline: 6-8 January
  2004.

* **Update (batch page_1791–page_1800, msgnum 1814-1823)**: Derek Clark's
  follow-up questions on Alastair's LED nav-light write-up (LED count and
  wiring, resistor sizing, UK supplier) draw a full "part 2" construction
  post from Alastair — LED counts per COLREGs sector (3 red, 5 green, 4
  white, 6 anchor), using each LED's own viewing angle rather than opaque
  baffles for sector demarcation, a five-circuit reverse-polarity wiring
  scheme giving two-wire mast operation, and silicone-potted circuit-board
  construction — new in [running lights](/operations/running-lights.md#building-a-sectord-led-masthead-light-led-counts-wiring-and-potting).
  Glen Maxwell raises a COLREGs objection to fitting a tricolour at all:
  showing only the anchor light risks being misread by an overtaking
  vessel as a stern light, ceding a sailing vessel's right of way — the
  same point made earlier in the file from a different angle. Separately,
  in [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-revised-translation-and-an-open-question-about-sven-yrvinds-own-design-choices),
  Kjell Karlsson finally answers Bert Vercauteren's standing question: Sven
  Yrvind's prior "Bris" project predated his knowledge of Paradox, and his
  current design goal — a car-toppable boat, easy to scull and run
  downwind — he judges better served by his own established approach than
  by chine runners, conceding design also comes down to "feeling." This
  prompts a new, fully narrated thread — [a proposed group project to
  translate Sven Yrvind's full book into
  English](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-proposed-group-project-to-translate-sven-yrvinds-full-book-into-english):
  Nels proposes a crowd-translated English edition (pre-order print run or
  PayPal e-book), David Beard volunteers to buy a copy, William Longyard
  supplies the reality check from his own conversations with Yrvind (too
  busy to translate himself) and matches Nels's volunteer translators with
  three of his own, and Nels works out a concrete chapter-by-chapter
  translation pipeline — outcome not recorded in the archive. Extended
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), [Glen
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md), [Kjell
  Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md), [Bert
  Vercauteren](/people/bert-vercauteren.md), [Nels](/people/nels.md),
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md), and [David
  Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with cross-links and citations for all of
  the above. Standing-watchlist check: below-threshold **Derek Clark**
  (`dclark52001`) resurfaced (the LED follow-up question, folded into
  `operations/running-lights.md` per the existing below-threshold
  convention); none of Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2",
  kayaker37/Paul, or Steve/Kansas City resurfaced. No new person pages, no
  renames, no prunings — all content extended existing pages. Timeline:
  9-10 January 2004.

* **Update (batch page_1771–page_1780, msgnum 1794-1803)**: The multi-batch
  "Main Sheet" photo search (opened batch 175) reaches its true close in
  [rig and sail — the Sailing Magazine photograph](/components/rig/index.md#the-sailing-magazine-photograph-matts-actual-sheet-routing-confirmed):
  "Larry" identifies a June 2003 Sailing Magazine photo showing Matt Layden's
  actual single-line mainsheet routing through a transom hole just to port of
  the upper rudder pintle, initially unsure if it was printed mirror-reversed;
  David Beard confirms it is; Derek Clark asks for a Files-section scan;
  Alastair raises a fresh, unanswered design-intent question (why does the
  plans' two-part sheet differ from the single-part rig Matt actually sails
  with?); and Larry re-photographs, corrects, and uploads the image himself,
  confirming it shows Matt's real-world method as distinct from the plans.
  Also closes the "Transom missing dimension" thread (opened batch 177) in
  [panel layout and nesting](/construction/panel-layout.md#the-missing-transom-radius-dimension-resolved):
  Alastair's pencilled-in **57 mm** figure, cross-checked by "Paul"
  (`kayaker37`) against his own digital model, and a general beam-crop
  shortcut from "Jeff UK" applicable to all four bulkheads. **Correction:**
  the questioner in that thread, previously logged as a new below-threshold
  newcomer "Jack" (`tidybowlmann`), is confirmed via matching
  `X-Yahoo-Profile`/Yahoo `u=` headers to be **Jack Gardiner** himself, not a
  distinct person — `construction/panel-layout.md` and
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) corrected accordingly; see
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md` for the full correction note. Also extended
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md)
  with their roles in both threads. No new files, no renames, no prunings.

* **Update (batch page_1761–page_1770, msgnum 1784-1793)**: The "Main Sheet"
  thread (opened batch 175) reaches its actual close in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) — Derek Clark's two candidate photo links for
  Matt Layden's single-line mainsheet turn out not to show the rig after all,
  but Glen Maxwell settles the open question directly: the single-line setup
  is Matt's **everyday rig**, not a Water Tribe Challenge-only modification,
  requiring noticeably more effort to haul in than a blocked sheet; Alastair's
  follow-up on the exact fairlead routing goes unanswered. A second thread
  closes out "Center of Gravity" in
  [heavy-weather tactics](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md#securing-the-crew-during-a-knockdown):
  Glen Maxwell, inspired by Gerry Spiess's *Alone Against the Atlantic*,
  floats (but never builds) shoulder/thigh/shin seat belts; William Longyard
  cautions that cabin injuries more often come from flying objects than a
  sliding body, so any locker built for the belts needs to be secure and its
  corners rounded and padded; Alastair reflects on his own earlier knockdown
  as simply "a learning experience." Also extended
  [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-paraplastic-photo-update-dry-weight-and-the-carbon-mast)
  (Glen Maxwell praises Kjell Karlsson's translated Yrvind chine-runner
  chapter as the best explanation of the mechanism he has read),
  [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md#tools) (Glen
  Maxwell's practical middle ground on the Build Manual's tool list — a
  starting point, not a requirement), and
  [panel layout and nesting](/construction/panel-layout.md) (Glen's dxf-file
  answer to Bert Vercauteren, and a new, unanswered newcomer question about
  the plans' un-dimensioned transom-radius top point from "Jack"
  (`tidybowlmann`) — a different person from the already-established Jack
  Gardiner). Cross-linked from
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md), and
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md). No new files, no renames, no
  prunings — all content extended existing pages. Timeline reaches
  4-5 January 2004.
* **Manual editorial pass (user feedback, not a batch) — promoted chine runners
  into their own top-level branch, folded in Sven Yrvind's website, and gave
  Yrvind his own person-folder.** Acting on user direction that the chine runner
  is the Paradox's single most striking, most-noticed, most-defining feature and
  warrants its own branch and a rewrite. **(1) New top-level
  [/chine-runners/](/chine-runners/index.md) branch** (sibling to `matt-layden/`
  and `design/`), consolidating and rewriting the former
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` and
  `construction/chine-runner-construction.md` into four concepts:
  [how-they-work.md](/chine-runners/how-they-work.md),
  [windward-performance.md](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md),
  [origin-and-evolution.md](/chine-runners/origin-and-evolution.md), and
  [construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md). **(2) Ingested Sven Yrvind's
  website** (`yrvind.com`) — three pages + 101 images mirrored under
  `source-web/yrvind/` on 2026-07-04 — adding his primary-source "deflector, not
  hydrofoil" mechanism hypothesis and seven-reason rationale, his firsthand
  October 1997 month sailing Paradox (an independent windward confirmation), the
  "greatest innovation of the century" verdict, the 1982-concept/1985-first-boat
  dating reconciliation, the Layden-family dimensions, and his composite
  chine-runner build method; recorded as new reference
  [/references/yrvind-website.md](/references/yrvind-website.md). **(3) Gave
  Yrvind his own person-folder** (mirroring the Bolducs/Glen Maxwell), framed as
  an oft-mentioned *external authority who never posted to the list*: hub
  [/people/yrvind.md](/people/yrvind.md) + sub-pages
  [voyages-and-boats.md](/people/yrvind/voyages-and-boats.md) and
  [paradox-and-the-chine-runner.md](/people/yrvind/paradox-and-the-chine-runner.md).
  **(4) Relocated two off-topic riders** that had ridden along in the old chine
  threads: the "enlarged boom / taller sail furling" digression consolidated into
  [/components/rig/index.md](/components/rig/index.md) (Nels's reply + citations moved there), and
  the foil-faced-foam cabin-insulation/GPS-shielding discussion into
  [/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md#foil-faced-foam-and-radiogps-shielding).
  **(5) Old paths kept as redirect stubs** so the ~155 inbound links across ~49
  files still resolve; root [/index.md](/index.md), the
  [trunk](/paradox.md), [/design/index.md](/design/index.md),
  [/construction/index.md](/construction/index.md),
  [/references/index.md](/references/index.md), and
  [/people/index.md](/people/index.md) updated. Deleted the empty
  `design/chine-runners.md` placeholder. **Pending:** a global sweep to repoint
  inbound `/design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md#…` and
  `/construction/chine-runner-construction.md#…` deep-links (and their anchors) at
  the new `/chine-runners/` files; until then the redirect stubs bridge them.
* **Manual editorial pass (user feedback, not a batch) — promoted the Bolducs
  into their own top-level person-branch and corrected an over-stated Glen
  Maxwell "Matt Layden eyewitness" framing.** Acting on user feedback that
  reading the archive from its first post has skewed some early
  characterizations — the whole history is not yet visible — two corrections
  were made. **(1) The Bolducs got their own folder, mirroring Glen Maxwell's.**
  Moved `people/dave-bolduc.md` → [/people/bolduc/dave.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md)
  and `people/mindy-bolduc.md` → [/people/bolduc/mindy.md](/people/bolduc/mindy.md);
  added a hub page [/people/bolduc.md](/people/bolduc.md) and a new
  primary-goal page
  [/people/bolduc/matt-layden-authority.md](/people/bolduc/matt-layden-authority.md)
  synthesising David & Mindy Bolduc's firsthand history with the designer
  (met Matt in 1992, own three of his boats — Little Cruiser, Swamp Thing,
  Enigma — cruised the Bahamas in his company, and relayed his techniques,
  figures, and words to the list; all facts and citations drawn from the
  existing vetted pages). Inbound links updated across **70 files** (`log.md`
  left as the historical record); [/people/index.md](/people/index.md) reworked
  to a single Bolduc hub entry, promoted up beside Glen Maxwell as the two
  firsthand-Layden pillars. **(2) Softened the "Glen is *the* / *most valuable*
  / *single richest* Matt Layden eyewitness" superlatives** in
  [/people/glen-maxwell.md](/people/glen-maxwell.md),
  [/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md),
  [/matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md), and
  [/matt-layden/index.md](/matt-layden/index.md) to precise, defensible claims
  (Glen = the one *builder* to sail his own Paradox alongside Matt's), each now
  cross-linking the Bolducs as the deeper, longer-running firsthand authority.
  Also fixed a pre-existing broken link in
  [/people/bert-vercauteren.md](/people/bert-vercauteren.md)
  (`/people/matt-layden.md` → `/matt-layden/biography.md`). Full-bundle
  absolute-link sweep afterwards: no broken links, no dangling references to the
  removed flat files.
* **Batch 176 (pages 1751–1760, msgnum 1773–1781 and 1783 — 1782 absent
  from the archive, 4 January 2004) — centerpiece: the "Main Sheet"
  thread's full resolution, revealing Matt Layden's own single-line
  mainsheet rig and an unresolved photo search.**
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) answered [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md)'s
  brass-arch mainsheet-fouling proposal (opened batch 175) with a load
  question, then, absent any calculated figure, proposed an empirical
  spring-balance test under sail. **David Beard revealed that Matt Layden
  himself used a simpler single-line mainsheet, with no block on the boom
  end, during the Water Tribe Challenge** — new in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted).
  A below-threshold correspondent, **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`), asked
  for a photo of the setup; David Beard pointed him to a general "Water
  Tribe Challenge" search and, failing that, guessed *Sail* magazine or the
  group's own archives; neither Derek Clark nor
  [Nels](/people/nels.md) could find a stern view, only a beach shot with
  the sail down — the search remains unresolved. **Nels separately
  reported Matt Layden placing second in a Water Tribe race on Lake
  Michigan sailing a 10 ft inflatable**, from a *WaterTribe Magazine*
  feature — a distinct result from the 2003 Everglades Challenge, new in
  [Matt Layden](/matt-layden/biography.md#other-craft-and-events) and
  cross-linked from [Nels](/people/nels.md). A last, light exchange closed
  out the "tools" debate (opened batch 172, resolved batch 175): recurring
  correspondent **Larry** (`LJGeib`) replied to David LeBlanc's Nelson's
  *Victory* line with a dry Roman-water-power joke, folded into
  [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings — all content extended existing pages.
  Checked all ten messages against the standing "threads to watch" list
  (Jeff UK/`roverticket`; botebilder/Ernie Fossel; below-threshold
  Larry/`newspipetr3`; Derek Clark/`dclark52001`; Fredrik; Jake; "Lugger
  #2"; kayaker37/Paul; Steve/Kansas City): **Derek Clark (`dclark52001`)
  resurfaced** (as above, still below-threshold, folded inline per the
  existing convention); none of the other items resurfaced — recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. Applied the David
  Beard (`dlb@`/`buster38801`) vs. David LeBlanc (`whisper@`/`meerkatoz`)
  disambiguation check throughout — all `dlb@`/`buster38801` citations this
  batch verified against their own `From:`/`X-Yahoo-Profile:` headers as
  David Beard; no David LeBlanc traffic this batch. Smith (`kd7ixk`/
  `betelgeuserdude`) vs. Derek Clark (`dclark52001`) also held cleanly — no
  Smith traffic this batch. No Glen Maxwell content this batch requiring the
  `people/glen-maxwell/` sub-file split. Verified no new citation-number
  collisions in any of the six touched files
  (`design/rig.md`, `construction/build-effort.md`, `people/david-beard.md`,
  `people/alastair-law.md`, `people/nels.md`, `matt-layden/biography.md`) — one
  near-collision was caught and corrected: a first attempt at
  `construction/build-effort.md` citation `[46]` collided with an existing
  `[46]`, renumbered to the file's true next-free `[56]`. **Flagged, not
  fixed (pre-existing, out of this batch's scope):** eight files, including
  `design/rig.md` and `construction/build-effort.md`, carry a stray literal
  `</content>` line at end-of-file — an apparent ingestion artifact
  predating this batch; left as-is per the citation/file-identity caution
  and recorded here for a future dedicated cleanup pass. Msgnum run
  1773–1783 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive except for the known
  gap at **1782, genuinely absent from the archive** (no corresponding
  page exists); all ten byte sizes matched the dispatch instructions
  exactly, no stale-read issue.

* **Batch 175 (pages 1741–1750, msgnum 1763–1772, 2–3 January 2004) —
  centerpiece: the tail of the "Build Manual, Preparations" tools debate,
  resolving with Kjell Karlsson's answered ParaPlastic dry-weight/carbon-mast
  question, Alastair's firsthand 90-degree knockdown account, and Jack
  Gardiner's first actual construction progress.**
  The tools debate drew several further contributions: a below-threshold
  correspondent, **Larry**, made the case for accumulating fine antique
  tools cheaply over years rather than buying new, replying to
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md)'s dislike of ornamental "modern outlook" tools; a
  below-threshold newcomer, **Derek Clark** (`dclark52001`), replied with
  his own tradesman-vs-one-boat-builder distinction and a minimal starter
  kit; [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) drew out a "builders vs. boaters"
  distinction and endorsed splitting the list into essential and desirable
  categories, judging a Paradox buildable for well under £50 in tools;
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) added a 6 in orbital sander to the
  list; and [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) forwarded the whole
  debate to New Zealand boat designer and author **John Welsford**, whose
  reply — his own 21 ft boat built with a single power tool, a full
  stationary-tool shop mainly serving production builders — gave the
  minimalist case independent professional weight. All new in
  [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md#the-build-manuals-preparations-installment-build-site-and-a-full-tool-inventory).
  **[Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) answered
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md)'s previously-unanswered "ParaPlastic" question** —
  expecting his composite Paradox to come out slightly lighter than Matt
  Layden's stock design, confirming matching house/deck sandwich
  construction, and confirming a 60mm, 2mm-thick carbon-fiber mast —
  resolving an open item in
  [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-paraplastic-photo-update-dry-weight-and-the-carbon-mast).
  **[Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) gave the archive's first firsthand
  account of a knockdown from the crew's own perspective**: during his own
  90-degree knockdown aboard "Little Jim," he braced a hand against the
  window frame and was back upright within seconds, cushion still in place —
  confirming Bill Serjeant's earlier view that no elaborate crew restraint is
  needed, new in
  [heavy-weather tactics](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md#securing-the-crew-during-a-knockdown).
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) opened a new "Main Sheet" thread on
  the sheet fouling the rudder and rear cleats, worsened by a fitted
  outboard, proposing a brass-pipe arch over the rear deck as a fix — new in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#mainsheet-fouling-on-the-rudder-and-rear-deck-with-an-outboard-fitted),
  cross-linked from the related transom-notch fouling fix in
  [rudder and chine experiments](/components/rudder.md#rudder-stock-height-and-mainsheet-fouling-at-the-transom-notch).
  **[Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) reported his first actual
  construction progress** — plywood purchased, building started — closing
  out weeks of "Steel?"/"Mast" debate with real progress on his stock wood
  Paradox; [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) congratulated
  him. New "Construction begins" section in
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md#construction-begins).
  No new files, no renames, no prunings — all content extended existing
  pages. Msgnum run 1763–1772 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive.

* **Batch 174 (pages 1731–1740, msgnum 1753–1762, 2–3 January 2004) —
  centerpiece: the tail of the "Build Manual, Preparations" tool-list
  debate (David LeBlanc's "workshop or a boat?" pushback and Smith's defense),
  Alastair's cabin-only anchoring routine, and Jack Gardiner's resolution
  of the "Steel?" saga to build stock wood first.**
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md)'s comprehensive Build Manual tool inventory (batch
  173) drew a sharper second round of pushback: [David
  LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) called it "wildly excessive," joking it
  would cost more than the boat, and proposed a tiered
  minimum/nice-to-have/luxury list instead, citing a fiddlier boat design
  whose prototype was built with only an electric drill and planer as power
  tools. Smith defended the list's comprehensiveness — his own stationary power
  tools cost no more than $1,200 new, with a $300 drill press deliberately
  deferred — and reposted the full inventory unable to find consensus on any
  item to trim; LeBlanc then softened his tone with an apology, restating
  his underlying concern about deterring first-time builders. New material
  in [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md#the-build-manuals-preparations-installment-build-site-and-a-full-tool-inventory).
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) continued the "anchor locker" thread with
  his own cabin-only anchoring technique — anchor in the floor locker with a
  permanent bow line and aft snap hook, dropped and dug in under sail, then
  made fast and furled without leaving the cabin — new in
  [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md#a-cabin-only-anchoring-routine-done-entirely-under-sail).
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) settled the long-running
  "Steel?" debate for his own build: a stock, plans-as-drawn Paradox in wood
  first, continuing toward steel only if he liked the boat enough to
  justify it, reasoning the stock hull suits steel less well than a
  stretched hull and that his wooden barn workshop and winter weather ruled
  out steel fabrication for now — new in
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative)](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#jack-gardiners-own-resolution-a-stock-wood-paradox-first-steel-later).
  He also settled on **birdsmouth** mast construction for his own build in
  the "Mast" thread, new in
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md#a-proposed-birdsmouth-building-tutorial-and-existing-published-guides).
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) posted a bare link to Sven
  Yrvind's own website (`www.yrvind.com`), folded into his existing Yrvind
  expertise section. [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) asked
  [Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) about his new "ParaPlastic"
  build photos — dry weight, house/deck construction, carbon mast — left
  unanswered in the archive; new in
  [the "BRIS Paradox"](/boats/bris-paradox.md#a-paraplastic-photo-update-dry-weight-and-the-carbon-mast).
  No new person, boat, or concept pages; no renames or prunings. Standing
  open items checked (Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger
  #2", kayaker37/Paul, Steve/Kansas City) — none resurfaced this batch.
  msgnum run 1753–1762 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive continuing
  from batch 173's 1752; cursor set to 1740.

* **Batch 173 (pages 1721–1730, msgnum 1743–1752, 2 January 2004) —
  centerpiece: Bert Vercauteren's independent software confirmation of Matt
  Layden's stability calculation, plus a new safety debate over flat-panel
  hulls and a new "Center of Gravity" crew-restraint thread.**
  [Bert Vercauteren](/people/bert-vercauteren.md), a Belgian boat designer
  who had modeled a Paradox in his own "Nautilus" boat-design software,
  reported the software confirms Matt Layden's stability calculation is
  **100% correct**, assuming Matt's own Vertical Center of Gravity is as low
  as believed (~120 mm above DWL) — new to
  [weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md)
  ("Independent software confirmation of Matt Layden's stability
  calculation"). In the same message he raised an unresolved safety worry
  from his own traditional European boatbuilding education: Paradox's flat
  bottom and side panels look, to his eye, like anvils inviting the "brute
  hammer" of a breaking wave, unlike a round hull such as Sven Yrvind's —
  new to
  [blue-water suitability](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#flat-panels-and-the-brute-hammer-of-a-breaking-wave),
  left without a direct reply in the archive. Separately,
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) opened a new "Center of Gravity" thread asking how a
  sailor should be secured to the sole during a knockdown (Velcro pants,
  snaphook, seatbelt), reasoning from his own ~225 lb frame and a drawing of
  Sven Yrvind's new boat that appeared to show the crew buckled in; **Bill
  Serjeant** (`barnacleid`) answered that no such restraint is needed, since
  the crew need only shift their own weight for the boat to right herself —
  new to
  [heavy-weather tactics](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md#securing-the-crew-during-a-knockdown).
  The same file also picked up the tail of "Paradox in rough weather":
  [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) added a further
  keel-less-hull precedent — ocean rowers **John Ridgway and Chay Blyth**,
  who crossed the Atlantic in 1966 in their dory-like, keel-less rowboat
  *English Rose*. In "Wooden spars,"
  [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) answered Kjell Karlsson's
  previously-unanswered "what's birdmouth?" question (a spar named for the
  open "V" cut into each stave, dovetailing into its neighbor), resolving an
  open item carried from batch 172 — new to
  [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md). Three smaller items extended existing
  construction pages: [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) cautioned that Smith's
  full Build Manual tool inventory might have discouraged him before he
  started building, since he owns only about a tenth of it (extending
  [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md)); Alastair
  also elaborated on his own already-cited short-sections/angle-grinder
  chine-runner technique, answering a newcomer's "Chinerunners" question
  alongside David Beard (extending
  [chine runner construction](/chine-runners/construction.md));
  and Alastair confirmed Smith's plans-based reading that the rudder pivots on
  the ship's centerline, closing out David Beard's "rudder" question
  (extending [rudder and chine experiments](/components/rudder.md)). No new
  files, no renames, no prunings. Standing open items checked in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md` — none of Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2",
  kayaker37/Paul, or Steve/Kansas City resurfaced this batch; all ten
  messages come from six already-tracked correspondents plus
  already-below-threshold Bill Serjeant. Msgnum run 1743–1752 confirmed
  distinct and fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch 172's 1742;
  all ten byte sizes matched the dispatch instructions exactly, no
  stale-read issue.

* **Batch 172 (pages 1711–1720, msgnum 1733–1742, 1–2 January 2004) —
  centerpiece: a new "Paradox in rough weather" thread weighing lying a-hull
  against Glen Maxwell's sea-anchor doctrine, plus a direct continuation of
  the "parachute anchor" exchange and a short "Wooden spars" thread.**
  [Bill Serjeant](/design/blue-water-suitability.md) (`barnacleid`), a UK
  sailor experienced in British Isles and Bay of Biscay gales, opened
  "Paradox in rough weather" with his own storm doctrine — heave to, then
  strip sail and drift beam-on downwind given sea room — judging Paradox's
  **completely flat bottom** could plausibly heave to Pardey-style (reef
  down, sheet hard amidships, tiller lashed to leeward), while flagging
  possible rudder overloading as a wave throws the boat backward off a
  crest; he judged she could lie a-hull safely up to **force 7**. This sits
  in direct tension with [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md)'s
  already-documented experience that lying a-hull was uncomfortable on every
  boat he tried it on, favoring a sea anchor instead — the archive's
  clearest point-counterpoint on Paradox storm tactics.
  [Nels](/people/nels.md) suggested streaming an anchor off the bow to aid
  heaving to (citing Phil Bolger), with a canoeing parallel — a rock-filled
  bucket dragged on a bow-to-bow bridle to slow a canoe in a blow, the same
  rig used for lining/tracking through rapids.
  [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) added the reason
  Paradox's **keel-less, dory-type hull** suits steep waves: old dory
  fishermen prized the lack of a keel specifically because a **keeled** dory
  slipping sideways down a wave would "trip" over it. [Kjell
  Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) pointed back to the same idea in his
  own translated excerpt of Sven Yrvind's book. All new to
  [heavy-weather tactics](/operations/heavy-weather-tactics.md) (new
  sections "A British Isles sailor's case for lying a-hull, and Paradox's
  flat bottom" and "Why a keel-less, dory-like hull resists tripping in
  steep waves"), cross-linked from `people/william-longyard.md`,
  `people/nels.md`, and `people/kjell-karlsson.md`.
  Separately, [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) replied directly to Glen Maxwell's earlier
  sea-anchor report with rigging notes on surplus military parachutes
  (closer to a cargo-drop drogue than a purpose-built sea anchor unless
  re-rigged with long shrouds and a swivel) and monohull-vs-multihull lead
  angle, warning against ever including chain in a parachute rode; Glen
  replied in turn, doubting a Paradox can easily heave to under her lugsail
  and settling on deploying the sea anchor early instead. [Ron
  Eike](/people/ron-eike.md), in the ongoing "Mast" thread, recalled a
  composite gaff built over a tapered PVC-pipe mandrel wrapped in carbon
  fiber, judging a spruce core (as Smith proposed for a Paradox mast) would add
  real strength the PVC core lacked. A short "Wooden spars" thread asked
  whether Douglas fir is the only alternative to Sitka spruce; Smith ranked
  Douglas fir strongest, with Honduras mahogany and Alaskan yellow cedar
  also usable at some cost in strength, noting a birdmouth/carbon-sleeve
  build loosens the wood choice further; Kjell Karlsson's follow-up
  "what's birdmouth?" went unanswered. New sections on
  [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md) ("Surplus
  military chutes as sea anchors, and lead angle under different hulls")
  and [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) (Ron Eike's composite-gaff aside, and
  "Wood species for a scratch-built wooden mast"), cross-linked from
  `people/david-c-smith.md` and `people/ron-eike.md`. No renames; no pruning; no new
  files. Standing open items checked in `.okf-progress/backlog.md` — none of
  Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry, Derek Clark,
  Fredrik, Jake, "Lugger #2", kayaker37/Paul, or Steve/Kansas City
  resurfaced this batch. Msgnum run 1733–1742 confirmed distinct and fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 171's 1732; all ten byte
  sizes matched the dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue.

* **Batch 171 (pages 1701–1710, msgnum 1723–1732, 1 January 2004) —
  centerpiece: Glen Maxwell's fuller anchoring follow-up (port-side clutter
  and a first sea-anchor report), alongside the "Mast" thread's close on a
  proposed birdsmouth tutorial and a revived composite-mast idea.** The
  "Mast" thread continued directly from batch 170's close (David LeBlanc's
  box-girder/birdsmouth explanation, msgnum 1722): [Jack
  Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) thanked the list, calling taper-jig
  making itself "fun and challenging" with the right tools and agreeing
  irrigation pipe is a workable cheap alternative. [Glen C.
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) then floated writing an illustrated
  birdsmouth-building tutorial — a short, five-foot practice sample shows
  "the mast literally builds itself when cut," with the real skill in
  **making the taper jig** — using a walking stick as the teaching piece;
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) welcomed the offer.
  [Nels](/people/nels.md) supplied two published birdsmouth-tutorial links
  and recalled a WoodenBoat Magazine article on the subject, which Glen
  judged both links **too thin on detail and worked examples** for a
  first-time taperer. [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) revived his August-2003
  birdmouth-spruce-mandrel/braided-carbon-sleeve composite mast proposal,
  judging the weight penalty against the plans' spec mast negligible and the
  real payoff the building experience; Glen estimated the resulting spar
  would come out at roughly **half the weight and triple the strength** of
  the stock mast. "Paul" (`kayaker37`) closed the thread with the precise
  WoodenBoat citation (issue 149, July/August 1999, p. 31, "Bird's Mouth
  Hollow Spars" by Aime Ontario Fraser) and his own 6 ft test section built
  from it. New sections "A proposed birdsmouth-building tutorial, and
  existing published guides" and "A birdmouth-and-carbon composite mast,
  revisited" on [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md), cross-linked from
  `people/jack-gardiner.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`, `people/nels.md`,
  `people/david-c-smith.md`, and `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`.
  Separately, Glen Maxwell gave a direct continuation of the
  "anchor lockers and Anchoring" thread from batches 169–170: why the
  **port side deck** stays cluttered under sail (raised boom sits too low,
  or the furled sail covers the whole port deck and stern cleat) while the
  **yuloh** tosses easily aside to port and never gets in the way; his own
  built-in rode fairlead, and why he avoids hanging the rode on a bare cleat
  (chafe, the boat sailing around at anchor); and the archive's **first
  field report on a sea anchor** — a six-foot chute bought for about $15 via
  an eBay source [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) had posted to the
  `microcruising` group, rigged for cockpit-worked deployment and retrieval
  (bow-eye thimble, fairlead, stern cleat, 100 ft rode, float-controlled
  depth, polypropylene retrieval line) but not yet tested in real wind and
  depth. New section "Glen Maxwell's follow-up: port-side clutter, the
  fairlead, and a first sea-anchor report" on
  [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md).
  **Minor repair:** `design/rig.md` carried a pre-existing defect of two
  `# Citations` headers, with two ordinary body-prose sections ("Rig details
  from microcruising.com" and "A birdmouth carbon-fiber composite spar...")
  misplaced beneath the first, stray header as if they were citations.
  Consolidated into a single `# Citations` block at the end of the file by
  removing the stray header; the two sections now read as ordinary body
  content in place, and the real numbered citation list is unchanged and
  unrenumbered. No new person, boat, or concept pages this batch; no
  renames or prunings. Standing "threads to watch" checked in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md` — none of Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake, or new builder
  Steve/Kansas City resurfaced; "Paul"/`kayaker37` resurfaced once more,
  kept citation-only per his established below-threshold status.

* **Batch 170 (pages 1691–1700, msgnum 1713–1722, 31 December 2003 – 1
  January 2004) — centerpiece: a "Mast" thread closing with box-girder and
  birdsmouth mast-building technique.** [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md)
  opened a New Year's "Mast" thread asking what everyone was using, having
  found none specifically mentioned in the archive (also grousing that the
  list's search always jumps to the latest-posts page). "Paul"
  (`kayaker37`) explained the search workaround and guessed the real
  question was about wood type, since most builders just follow the plans.
  A new prospective builder, **Steve** (`sdhender`/`steveh2992`, Kansas
  City, MO), had introduced himself the same day in a "Getting started"
  post — plans, plywood, and fir 2x4s in hand, epoxy and nails on order, a
  plan to build the "appendages" (mast, yard, boom, rudder, yuloh, beams)
  before the hull, and a retirement-driven multi-year timeline, with
  "Windependent" or "Cette Assez" as candidate boat names — enough of a
  committed-builder signal to warrant a new page,
  [people/steve-kc.md](/people/steve-kc.md); [David
  Beard](/people/david-beard.md) welcomed him. Steve then answered Jack's
  mast question, pointing to page 12 of the plans and crediting
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md)'s earlier "Rosetta Stone" explanation
  (msgnum 1477) of the mast's plan-view drawings. Jack's forward-looking
  follow-up (dimensions, wood type, taper shape, tied to a possible future
  scaled-up hull) resolved itself a message later when he realized he had
  mistaken the plans' yuloh drawing for the mast drawing. [William
  Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) offered irrigation tubing as a
  workable low-effort mast alternative (3 in, used successfully on an
  earlier 17 ft boat), and [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) closed
  the thread with the archive's fullest general wooden-mast-building
  explanation, drawn from Lin and Larry Pardey's "Seraffyn" books —
  box-girder interior buildup at fitting points, 12:1-or-longer scarphs,
  tapering/rounding technique, and a crumpled-aluminum-foil radar-reflector
  trick — plus Bud McIntosh's book as a further source, and a description of
  the **birdsmouth** (staved, hollow) mast-building method as "the pinnacle
  of the mast maker's art." New sections "Mast material choices" and
  "Box-girder mast construction technique, and the birdsmouth alternative"
  on [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md), cross-linked from
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md),
  [people/david-leblanc.md](/people/david-leblanc.md), and
  [people/steve-kc.md](/people/steve-kc.md). Checked all standing open
  backlog items (Jeff UK `roverticket`; botebilder/Ernie Fossel;
  below-threshold Larry `newspipetr3`; Derek Clark `dclark52001`; Fredrik;
  Jake; the "Lugger #2" footnote; kayaker37/Paul): none resurfaced beyond
  Paul's single already-tracked, below-threshold "Mast" reply. One new file
  (`people/steve-kc.md`, added to `people/index.md`); no renames; no
  pruning. Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of the six
  touched/created files. Msgnum run 1713–1722 confirmed distinct and fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 169's 1712; all ten byte
  sizes matched the dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue.

* **Batch 169 (pages 1681–1690, msgnum 1703–1712, 31 December 2003 – 1
  January 2004) — centerpiece: the Gibbs Aquada tangent, chine runners vs.
  "spray rails."** The batch opens by closing the tail of the "Woven roving
  vrs 45X45 biax" thread carried over from batch 168 — [Jack
  Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md)'s one-line agreement (1703) — then
  turns to a new, much lighter New Year's Eve thread, "Steel RE RE
  revisited." Jack posted a link to the real-world **Gibbs Aquada**
  amphibious car/boat prototype, describing it as steel-hulled with
  chine-runner-like features (1704); [Nels](/people/nels.md) questioned the
  steel claim against the manufacturer's own "composite" description (1705);
  "Jeff (UK, `roverticket`)" corrected the terminology — the Aquada's hull
  features are **spray rails, not chine runners**, serving a different
  purpose — and priced the vehicle at roughly £150,000, enough, he joked,
  for a 40–45 ft steel Paradox with a helipad (1707); Jack countered that
  heeled over under sail, spray rails function exactly like chine runners in
  practice, reporting his own project as hull-and-power-only so far, no mast
  yet fitted (1708) — folded as a new closing section into [design/aluminum
  construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md), cross-linked
  from [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md),
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md), and
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md). [William
  Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) contributed only a joke reply
  (1706, "Jack Bond... Agent 007"), and the thread's remainder (1709–1712)
  devolved entirely into New Year's Eve banter between Jack and Nels —
  periscope-mast/"drive it along the bottom" jokes, "whitestick sonar" and
  "onstarfish" gags, and a closing sting-ray/barracuda joke — recorded only
  as terse citation entries with no body prose, per the KEEP/CUT calculus
  (pure conversational residue, no durable fact). "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`)
  resurfacing is a standing backlog identity-check item; no new
  corroborating link to the list's other Jeff (`doryman_uk`) surfaced, so
  the identity remains open. No new files created; no renames; no pruning.
  Msgnum run 1703–1712 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing
  directly from batch 168's 1702.

* **Batch 168 (pages 1671–1680, msgnum 1693–1702, 30–31 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: Glen Maxwell's full anchoring account and Smith's cockpit-worked
  lanyard retrieval system.** The batch opens by closing out the "Another
  book" thread's tail: [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md)
  answered Jack Gardiner's question of whether his next book was "in the
  can" — *A Speck on the Sea* was selling well, with a possible next-edition
  announcement a few months off, the City of Ragusa research story, and
  Paradox's place in the Bill Verity/Gerry Spiess/Serge Testa "capsule boat"
  lineage (1693) — and accepted [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md)'s
  suggestion that Swedish builder "Sebastian" (a Sven Yrvind acolyte)
  deserved fuller coverage in any reprint (1694, 1697); folded into
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md), with
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md)'s prior "unanswered"
  note closed out. The centerpiece is a five-message continuation of the
  "anchor locker"/"anchor lockers and Anchoring" threads: [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md)
  answered Longyard's split-window idea with a Lewmar hatch/portlight
  alternative he'd already weighed (1695); [Glen C.
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) gave the archive's fullest first-person
  anchoring account — declining a below-deck well aboard Zoë for canvas-ice-bag
  stowage, Matt Layden's stern-set cockpit-worked method, and the Cedar Key
  bag-overboard anecdote (1696); Smith answered with an elaborate cockpit-worked
  lanyard-and-roller retrieval system adapted from a parachute-sea-anchor rig,
  conceding "lot to be said for the simplicity" of Glen's method (1699); and
  "Jeff (UK, `roverticket`)" closed with a bow-ring/bridle scheme and a
  flat-afterdeck counterproposal to Glen, both unanswered (1701, 1702) — all
  new in [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md),
  cross-linked from [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md),
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md),
  and [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md). A short "Woven
  roving vrs 45X45 biax" side-thread (1698, 1700) — Jack Gardiner asking
  whether biaxial cloth could substitute for the plans' woven-roving glass
  schedule, Smith answering that the schedule targets abrasion resistance rather
  than raw strength — is new in
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md). "Jeff UK"
  (`roverticket`) resurfacing is a standing backlog identity-check item; no
  new corroborating link to the list's other Jeff (`doryman_uk`) surfaced,
  so the identity remains open. No new files created; no renames; no
  pruning. Msgnum run 1693–1702 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive,
  continuing directly from batch 167's 1692.

* **Batch 167 (pages 1661–1670, msgnum 1683–1692, 30–31 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "anchor locker" thread, and the rudder pivot-point question
  answered.** The batch opens with the true tail of the nesting-software
  thread: [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) confirmed the Hull/DXF
  files were uploaded to the Files section, naming which program opens each
  (1683), then considered uploading a 2D dxf file too after William
  Longyard's suggestion (1684) — closing beats folded into
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md). [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md)
  then answered [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md)'s batch-166 rudder
  pivot-point question (1685) — the plans put the upper rudder half's
  centerline on the vessel centerline, a cosmetic-only concern since the
  lower half and tiller work fine off-center — closing that open item in
  [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md). The centerpiece is a new
  four-message "anchor locker" thread: Smith proposed a flush, self-draining
  afterdeck Danforth well and a PVC-tube scheme for a folding grapnel (1686);
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) countered with a
  conventional bow anchor well for stability, from his experience that
  insufficient forward weight makes small boats fidgety in chop (1687); Smith
  explained his preference for aft, inboard stowage as a big man (6'1", 225
  lb) whose forward movement upsets a small boat's balance (1690); Longyard
  closed with a split forward window for foredeck access without going on
  deck, and a mast-trunk drain with an in-line strainer (1692) — all new in
  [operations/ground-tackle.md](/operations/ground-tackle.md), cross-linked
  from [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) and
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md). A minor
  "Another book" sub-thread (1688, 1689, 1691) — a newcomer (Kellan,
  `dagon_gsl`) hoping for a follow-up to *A Speck on the Sea*, Longyard's
  wistful reply that he once hoped to fit in a Paradox voyage, and [Jack
  Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md)'s unanswered question about the new
  book's status — extends the existing book section of
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md). No new files
  created; no renames; no pruning. Msgnum run 1683–1692 confirmed distinct
  and fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch 166's 1682.

* **Batch 166 (pages 1651–1660, msgnum 1673–1682, 30 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the Hull-program nesting/CAD workflow, and the Build Manual's
  first reception.** [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) thanked
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) for the "Build Manual, Preparations" installment
  (1673), and Smith previewed the manual's next sections — deckbeams, then
  bulkheads (1675) — closing beat folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md). The
  centerpiece is a six-message "nesting" thread in which Glen introduced
  Gregg Carlson's freeware **"Hull"** program — already loaded with the
  Paradox's own offsets, with a nesting function for developed panels (1674)
  — [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) pointed out Hull files
  convert for **AutoCAD** and asked Glen to post the Paradox offsets to the
  Files section (1677); Glen agreed (1678); [Jack
  Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) didn't yet have a copy but would get
  one (1679); Glen then worked the conversion himself — VRML to DXF via a
  program called "Crossroads," imported cleanly into **IntelliCAD 2000**
  (1681) — and Longyard judged the resulting DXF file would be easier for
  most builders to work with than the native Hull file (1682). New section
  in [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md),
  distinct from (and not a resolution of) the still-open stretched-Paradox
  nesting question from batch 165. A one-line closer (1676, Longyard
  thanking Smith for a "well reasoned and written letter") ends the multi-batch
  "Alternative building materials" epilogue in
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md).
  The batch's one new fact: [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) opened a
  fresh "rudder" thread (1680) asking whether the rudder's pivot point
  belongs on the transom's centerline — unanswered in the archive — new
  section in [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md). Extended
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md) (citations
  [43]-[44]), [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md)
  (new section, citations [17]-[22]),
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (citation [97]), [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md) (new section,
  citation [36]), [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) ([94]-[95]),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md) ([60]-[62]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) ([39]),
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) ([184]), and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  ([183]-[186]). No new files created; no renames; no pruning. Verified no
  new citation-number collisions in any of the nine touched files. Msgnum
  run 1673-1682 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing
  directly from batch 165's 1672; all ten byte sizes matched the dispatch
  instructions exactly, no stale-read issue.
* **Batch 165 (pages 1641–1650, msgnum 1663–1672, 29–30 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: Jack Gardiner returns as an ordinary builder, and clenched
  nails explained.** The batch opens with the true tail of the KONSTRUKTOREN
  book-ordering exchange (msgnum 1663) — [Glen C.
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md)'s one-line thanks to [William
  Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) for the tip to e-mail the Swedish
  publisher Prisma directly — folded into
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md), which now runs the "New
  Pictures For Paradox Phans!" thread to its actual close. The centerpiece
  is [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) (`alex29@`/`tidybowlmann`,
  the "Steel?" thread's central figure in batches 163–164) returning to the
  list as an ordinary plans-in-hand builder: he opened "Chinerunners"
  (1664), answered by [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md)'s
  templates/epoxy-fill/hand-plane method ("turned out great," 1665) — new
  section in
  [construction/chine-runner-construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md);
  asked, unanswered in the archive, whether a plywood nesting plan existed
  for a stretched-length Paradox (1668) — new section in
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md); and opened
  "Sad Comentary" questioning why the plans show bent-over cabin nails
  (1667, 1669), which drew the archive's fullest explanation of **clenched**
  (not merely bent) nails as a permanent lapstrake/canoe fastening technique
  — from kayaker37 ("Paul," an established below-threshold handle) citing
  Don Elliott's building guide and linking illustrated instructions (1670),
  and from [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md), who cited his father's clench-nailed
  cedar/canvas Oldtown canoe and two reference books, "Gougeon Brothers on
  Boat Construction" and Robert Steward's "Boatbuilding Manual" (1671) — new
  section in
  [construction/fastening-and-gluing.md](/construction/fastening-and-gluing.md).
  The batch closes with Smith's return-visit reply (1672) to William Longyard's
  "heresy" joke on the "Alternative building materials" thread (which Derek
  Clark had closed in batch 164 with his "will build in wood" post): Smith's
  fullest general statement that "the Paradox design is what it is,"
  surveying the archive's actual build variety (Kjell Karlsson's foam-core
  composite, Jeff UK's stitch-and-glue plywood, the aluminum/steel
  proposals, and named boats Zoë, Little Jim, Whisper, RHXII, and Petite
  Lady) and crediting Matt Layden with treating his own design as a guide
  rather than a manual — folded into
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)'s
  "Epilogue" section as a second paragraph. Extended
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md) (citation [36]),
  [construction/chine-runner-construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md)
  (new section, citations [25]-[27]),
  [construction/fastening-and-gluing.md](/construction/fastening-and-gluing.md)
  (new section, citations [21]-[24]),
  [construction/panel-layout.md](/construction/panel-layout.md) (new
  section, citation [16]),
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (citation [96]), [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md)
  ([34]-[38]), [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) ([183]), and
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) ([92]-[93]). No new files created; no
  renames; no pruning. Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of
  the eight touched files. Msgnum run 1663-1672 confirmed distinct and
  fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch 164's 1662; all ten
  byte sizes matched the dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read
  issue.
* **Batch 164 (pages 1631–1640, msgnum 1653–1662, 29 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: Smith's "Build Manual, Preparations" installment, and the
  "Steel?" thread's true last word.** [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) posted the group
  Build Manual's fullest single tool inventory — build-site guidance (shelter
  against dew/rain/airborne debris; heating/cooling considerations for epoxy
  and paint; a garage as optimal but not required) and a categorized tool
  list spanning basic tools, measuring tools, edge tools, abrasive tools,
  saws, rotary tools, clamps, stationary power tools, safety equipment, and
  consumables, framed throughout as a suggested rather than fixed inventory.
  Separately, once the "Steel?" thread itself had gone quiet, Derek Clark
  reopened the underlying materials question under "Alternative building
  materials" (see
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#epilogue-why-materials-questions-draw-pushback-and-a-belated-apology)),
  arguing Matt Layden built
  from whatever was available to a builder of modest means and that his
  design can reasonably be altered to suit others — a view
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) seconded ("accused of
  heresy on a group dedicated to building an enclosed dinghy!"). [Glen C.
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) then made good on
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md)'s parting complaint from batch
  163 — that Glen's own conciliatory close had itself drifted off-topic into
  epoxy — with a direct apology, which Jack answered with a dry "white
  stick, a white steel stick" instead of a grievance, closing the "Steel?"
  saga on a joke. Also folded in: [Nels](/people/nels.md)'s general
  plans-buying-philosophy note ("Paradox Building Sites" — buy plans matched
  to a preferred material/method rather than modify existing ones, to keep
  the designer's own support available); and a four-message sub-thread in
  which Longyard revealed the title of Sven Yrvind's new book — long known
  to the list only as "Sven's book" — as **KONSTRUKTOREN** (ISBN
  91-518-4154-1), posted its illustrations including one of Yrvind's current
  project drawn by **Matt Layden** himself (the archive's only recorded
  instance of Matt contributing artwork to an outside publication, the
  book's other illustrations credited to Martin Mörck), and fielded Glen
  Maxwell's unanswered-locally ordering question. No steel Paradox was ever
  built by anyone; Jeff UK's plywood build (batch 162) remains the thread's
  one confirmed real construction outcome. Extended
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md) (new
  section, citation [42]),
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (new "Epilogue" section, citations [92]-[95]),
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md) (citations [32]-[35]),
  [references/materials-sourcing-links.md](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md)
  (citation [8]), [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) ([91]),
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) ([73]),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md) ([56]-[59]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) ([33]),
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) ([150]),
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md)
  ([70]-[71]), and
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md) ([45], new "Asides
  and footnotes" paragraph on Matt's KONSTRUKTOREN illustration). No new
  files created; no renames; no pruning. Verified no new citation-number
  collisions in any of the eleven touched files. Msgnum run 1653-1662
  confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch
  163's 1652; all ten byte sizes matched the dispatch instructions exactly,
