  no stale-read issue.

* **Batch 163 (pages 1621–1630, msgnum 1643–1652, 29 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's genuine final close, one batch after
  its coda appeared to have already landed.** Smith and Jeffery Please
  ("Jeff UK") traded a short epoxy-shelf-life exchange — Smith has used epoxy
  "much older" than three years, with the hardener rather than the resin
  the real limiting factor — which drew [Glen C.
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) in with the archive's fullest epoxy-
  revival technique: boiling water to un-crystallize an old can's resin,
  confirmed by West System's own ten-year-old-epoxy testing, plus a
  cautionary story of a 15-second microwave reheat that nearly exothermed
  out of the appliance. Running alongside, [Nels](/people/nels.md) and
  [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) sparred over whether Nels's welded-aluminum-hull/
  inflatable-collar RIB hybrid post and photo-album upload belonged on a
  Paradox-specific list at all, with Smith pointing to Boatdesign/
  Microcruising as better homes for off-topic material and Glen Maxwell's
  moderator say as the final word. William Longyard corrected Smith's
  auto-body steel-gauge comparison (12-gauge is roughly three times
  thicker than the 20-22 gauge actual auto bodies use). The thread then
  closed for real: [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md) answered Smith's
  rebuttal point by point before announcing he was taking his steel-
  Paradox research private; Glen Maxwell offered the list's closing word —
  welcoming a Paradox in "steel, aluminum, foam sandwich, or any other
  material" and inviting Jack to keep reporting progress — which Jack read
  as backhanded (Glen had posted an unrelated epoxy reply inside the same
  thread) before confirming he would proceed privately regardless. No
  steel Paradox was ever built by Jack or anyone else; Jeff UK's plywood
  build (batch 162) remains the thread's one confirmed real construction
  outcome. Extended
  [construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md](/construction/epoxy-brands-and-chemistry.md)
  (new section, citations [8]-[9]),
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (citations [84]-[91]), [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) ([89]-[90]),
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) ([71]-[72]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) ([31]-[32]),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md) ([55]),
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md)
  (new section, citation [69]), and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  ([182]). No new files created; no renames; no pruning. Checked all
  standing "threads to watch" / open backlog items (Jeff UK `roverticket`;
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel; below-threshold Larry `newspipetr3`; Derek
  Clark; Fredrik; Jake; the "Lugger #2" footnote) — none resurfaced this
  batch; recorded in `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. Verified no new
  citation-number collisions or gaps in any of the eight touched files.
  Msgnum run 1643–1652 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive,
  continuing directly from batch 162's 1642; all ten byte sizes matched
  the dispatch instructions exactly.

* **Batch 162 (pages 1611–1620, msgnum 1633–1642, 29 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's true final coda closes out a debate
  running since batch 158.** Jeffery Please ("Jeff UK") and Jack Gardiner
  traded one more round — Jack objecting to the cut-and-paste reply style
  rather than the substance — before pivoting to concrete fabrication ideas:
  Jack's solid-round-stock chine-runner edge over square-tube frames, and
  William Longyard's counter-proposal of frames bent from 0.10 in plate on
  a brake into welded "L"-flange shapes with no water-trapping cavity,
  which Jack confirmed shop access to try. [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) — the
  Pacific Northwest professional boatbuilder behind the archive's
  designer-fidelity credo — then returned after a long absence to deliver
  the thread's fullest technical rebuttal of a steel Paradox: welding
  distortion and stiffening longitudinals eating into stowage, water traps
  at every longitudinal, and a "horrible mistake" verdict weighed against
  Paradox's proven plywood/glass-roving bottom, "darn near bulletproof."
  Jeff UK welcomed the rebuttal and finally laid out his own real,
  plywood build plan — 3/8 in sides, a two-layer 1/2 in + 3/8 in bottom,
  solid-shaped screwed-in runners, egg-box frames — delayed three years by
  family circumstances and about to start. No steel Paradox was ever built
  by Jack or anyone else; Jeff UK's plywood build is the thread's one
  confirmed real construction outcome. Also folded in: Jeff UK's
  cross-reference to William Longyard's new "Stitch 'n Glue" thread;
  "Larry" (`LJGeib`)'s reply on the Bris 5.4 mast/daggerboard placement
  question, citing a Bolger aft-mast/forward-jib precedent and Paradox's
  own large rudder as a lateral-plane contributor; and a small new
  footnote — Larry's unanswered question, prompted by Kjell Karlsson's
  translated Sven Yrvind book chapter, about a boat named "Lugger #2"
  understood to have been a larger Swamp Thing, not previously documented
  anywhere in the design lineage. Extended
  [design/aluminum-construction-idea.md](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md)
  (citations [77]–[83]), [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) ([88]),
  [people/jack-gardiner.md](/people/jack-gardiner.md) ([28]–[30]),
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md) ([54]),
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) ([39]–[39a]–[40]),
  [references/study-plans.md](/references/study-plans.md) ([5]), and
  [matt-layden/biography.md](/matt-layden/biography.md) ([44], with a
  cross-link added from
  [boats/bris-paradox.md](/boats/bris-paradox.md)). No new files created;
  no renames; no pruning. Msgnum run 1633–1642 confirmed distinct and
  fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch 161's 1632.

* **Batch 161 (pages 1601–1610, msgnum 1623–1632, 28–29 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Stitch 'n Glue" thread pivots into concrete cabin/window
  design, "Big Cruiser" resurfaces after ten months, and a new "Bris 5.4"
  thread opens.** Continuing directly from batch 160's opening of "Stitch 'n
  Glue," Jack Gardiner proposed stretching the default cabin top forward and
  asked how to size/place windows for open-water use; William Longyard
  answered from his own Siren 17 refit — windows get stove in regardless of
  thickness (citing Kenichi Horie's story), so several smaller windows beat
  a few large ones, with aluminum angle brackets and a rubber shock buffer
  for any long windows kept — and Jack agreed smaller windows are more
  practical. Nels then floated an unrelated welded-aluminum-hull/wood-
  topsides/inflatable-hypalon-collar hybrid ("Another Option"), before
  resurfacing a long-dormant Open item: Matt Layden's own unfinished "Big
  Cruiser" stretched/enlarged Paradox cartoons, pointing Jack to their exact
  `microcruising` group files-section location — the name's only other
  archive appearance since a one-off February 2003 mention, now finally
  located. Jack judged the concept "would make more sense in steel,"
  looping back to the batch 158–160 steel debate. David LeBlanc then
  relayed Phil Bolger's window/porthole design lore (window plastics
  stronger than steel; the traditional small-window blue-water rationale; a
  storm-repair technique for a stove-in porthole), which Nels expanded with
  further Bolger porthole designs, the "Wiley window," and the "Birdwatcher
  top" ventilation concept (JOCHEMS schooner example). The batch closed with
  a new, unrelated thread: Nels questioning the mast/daggerboard placement
  on Sven Yrvind's "Bris 5.4" study plans (which he had uploaded days
  earlier), unanswered. Folded into [aluminum and steel Paradox
  (speculative) — stretching the cabin top and sizing windows for
  open-water
  use](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#stretching-the-cabin-top-and-sizing-windows-for-open-water-use)
  and [— a welded-aluminum hull with an inflatable
  collar](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-welded-aluminum-hull-with-an-inflatable-collar)
  (citations [68]–[76]), and [Paradox study plans — mast and daggerboard
  placement questioned on the Bris
  5.4](/references/study-plans.md#mast-and-daggerboard-placement-questioned-on-the-bris-54)
  (citation [4], new subsection under the existing "Bris 5.4 Study Plans"
  folder heading, plus a names-disambiguation note distinguishing the BRIS
  composite *method* from the Bris 5.4 hull *design*). Corrected the
  resurfaced "Big Cruiser" item at its source: updated
  `matt-layden/biography.md`'s "Asides and footnotes" citation [41] (which
  had wrongly claimed the name "never resurfaced elsewhere in the
  archive"), added a cross-link section to
  [design/variants/stretched-paradox.md](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#big-cruiser-matt-laydens-own-unfinished-enlargement-cartoons),
  and fixed the same stale claim in `people/harald-lillevang.md`.
  Cross-linked from `people/jack-gardiner.md` ([24]–[27]),
  `people/william-longyard.md` ([53]), `people/nels.md` ([67]–[70]), and
  `people/david-leblanc.md` ([53]). No new files, promotions, or prunings;
  no renames. Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of the nine
  touched files (all sequential; `william-longyard.md` and
  `david-leblanc.md` each carry a pre-existing legacy `[0]` citation
  predating this batch, left untouched). Standing Open items checked (Jeff
  UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake — none resurfaced) plus
  HANDOVER.md's threads-to-watch (Bolducs' Little Cruiser trip report,
  Smith/Andrew Graham/Kjell Karlsson/NZ build, "Sail balance"/lee-helm, "Jeff
  Please"/Paramour, the moderator-approval switch — none resurfaced),
  recorded in backlog.md. Timeline: messages dated 28–29 December 2003.
  msgnum run 1623–1632 confirmed distinct and fully consecutive, continuing
  directly from batch 160's 1622, all ten byte sizes matched dispatch
  instructions exactly, no stale-read issue. Cursor set to 1610.

## 2026-07-03

* **Batch 159 (pages 1581–1590, msgnum 1602–1611, 28 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's design-philosophy objection escalates
  from argument into a personal dispute, then stands down.** Continuing
  directly from batch 158's apparent close (David LeBlanc's "What Jeff
  said!"), the thread reignited: "Jeff UK" (Jeffery Please) sharpened his
  design-philosophy objection into a direct jab at Jack Gardiner's
  seamanship ("take up golf or buy a white stick"); Jack answered in kind
  with a pointed link to a UK charity for the blind ("Thats not nice
  Jeff") while still pressing his technical questions; Jeff escalated
  further before answering Jack's fabrication questions in more detail
  (fog/chart-reading, Bruce Roberts steel designs, "if steel were better
  Matt would have built in it"); Jack held to a stated intent to "stay
  away from the personal insults," countering with his own build plan
  (one Paradox in wood, one/two in steel), fabrication specifics
  (brazing-rod vs. wire-feed filler), and a defense of the thread's
  legitimacy on a Paradox-builders forum. William Longyard tried to defuse
  the exchange with dry humor ("arguing blondes versus brunettes") and a
  Hammerite paint recommendation; Alastair, from outside the personal
  exchange, restated his designer-fidelity objection more sharply
  ("Paradox offers no challenges to anyone who can assemble a decent set
  of bookshelves... The boat is complete") and questioned the whole
  debate's premise ("a silk purse... why does everyone want to turn it
  into a sow's ear?"). Jack misread Alastair's post as telling him to
  leave the list, answered with genuine hurt, then stood down: "Il go
  away." No further "Steel?" posts follow anywhere in the archive — the
  thread ends here, unresolved. Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — the design-philosophy
  objection escalates into a personal
  dispute](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#the-design-philosophy-objection-escalates-into-a-personal-dispute)
  (citations [48]–[57]), cross-linked from `people/jack-gardiner.md`
  ([18]–[20]), `people/jeff-doryman.md` ([36]–[38], Disambiguation
  section), `people/william-longyard.md` ([48]–[49]), and
  `people/alastair-law.md` ([148]–[149]). No new files, promotions, or
  prunings; no renames. Verified no new citation-number collisions in any
  of the five touched files (all sequential, no gaps). Standing Open
  items checked (Jeff UK/roverticket — resurfaced as this batch's
  centerpiece, consistent with his established recurring-but-unconfirmed
  identity, no new corroborating link found; botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik/fredrilu,
  Jake/jacobtm6 — none resurfaced) plus HANDOVER.md's threads-to-watch
  (Bolducs' Little Cruiser trip report, Smith/Andrew Graham/Kjell
  Karlsson/NZ build, "Sail balance"/lee-helm, "Jeff Please"/Paramour, the
  moderator-approval switch — none resurfaced), recorded in backlog.md.
  Timeline: all ten messages dated 28 December 2003. msgnum run
  1602–1611 confirmed distinct and consecutive from batch 158's 1601, all
  ten byte sizes matched dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read
  issue; msgnum order confirmed to match true chronological (UTC) order
  despite mixed US-Eastern/UK timezone headers. Cursor set to 1590.

* **Batch 160 (pages 1591–1600, msgnum 1612, 1614–1622 — 1613 absent from
  the archive, 28 December 2003) — centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's
  closing scale argument, and the pivot to a new "Stitch 'n Glue" thread as
  steel's practical alternative.** Nels closed out the substantive "Steel?"
  debate with a commercial-precedent scale argument — fairmetalboats.com
  sells no steel design under 25 ft/5+ tons against a 10 ft smallest
  plywood design, matching Phil Bolger's published view that 25 ft is the
  practical minimum for serious offshore steel construction, since smaller
  sizes risk welding distortion without expensive jigs and eat into
  storage space needed for flotation and ballast alike — after a brief
  misaddressed Al/Jack correction and Jack Gardiner's curt "I dont need
  insults" closed the personal dispute from batch 159. William Longyard
  then reframed material choice as a non-issue — wood, steel,
  ferro-cement, or animal hides would all work because the underlying
  design is sound, citing Phil Bolger, Paul Fisher, and Jay Benford as
  designers who let builders choose — drawing a correction from Nels (in
  his own direct experience, Bolger gets upset over scantling changes,
  illustrated by Bolger's own steel solo-circumnavigator design "COL H.G.
  HASLER," which Bolger considers a breakthrough but nobody has yet built
  in steel). Longyard then opened a new "Stitch 'n Glue" thread proposing
  stitch-and-glue construction specifically as steel's practical
  alternative; Jack Gardiner welcomed it as viable and modern, mentioning
  monocoque construction; Nels combined it with bilge keels, a Chinese lug
  rig/yawl/soft-wing sail, and a carbon-fiber tabernacled mast; Longyard
  stayed unconvinced on bilge keels but liked a carbon-fiber crow's nest.
  Folded into [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — why practical
  steel boatbuilding starts around 25 feet, not 14
  feet](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#why-practical-steel-boatbuilding-starts-around-25-feet-not-14)
  and [— a stitch-and-glue Paradox as steel's practical
  alternative](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-stitch-and-glue-paradox-as-steels-practical-alternative)
  (citations [58]–[67]), cross-linked from `people/nels.md` ([63]–[66]),
  `people/jack-gardiner.md` ([21]–[23]), and `people/william-longyard.md`
  ([50]–[52]). No new files, promotions, or prunings; no renames. Verified
  no new citation-number collisions or gaps in any of the four touched
  files. Standing Open items checked (Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie
  Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake)
  plus HANDOVER.md's threads-to-watch: none resurfaced, recorded in
  backlog.md. Timeline: all ten messages dated 28 December 2003. msgnum run
  1612, 1614–1622 confirmed distinct, with msgnum 1613 confirmed genuinely
  absent from the archive (a real gap, not a stale-read or missing-file
  issue) — all ten byte sizes matched dispatch instructions exactly, no
  stale-read issue. Cursor set to 1600.

* **Batch 155 (pages 1541–1550, msgnum 1561–1570, 27 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's third wave, settling the core gauge and
  weight debate and closing the batch-152 ballast-placement question.**
  Jack Gardiner and William Longyard converged on a practical steel
  specification: Longyard supplied the square-foot weight figures (12-gauge
  steel at **4.375 lb/sq ft** against ~1 lb/sq ft for 1/4 in plywood, offset
  by needing far less material for equal strength) and a historical parallel
  to 1903–1933 aircraft construction, where steel fuselages displaced wood
  on the same weight-adjusted-strength logic; Jack agreed 3/8 in was much
  too heavy, proposed ~3/16 in for the bottom plate, and described his own
  incremental tack-and-move welding technique for avoiding heat stress. A
  new one-off correspondent ("topsail") asked about composite wood/steel
  hulls, weld stress-relief, and corrosion protection; Longyard answered
  with an 18th-century composite-hull precedent and modern steel-hulled
  warships, explained the English-petrol-tin origin of the term "oil
  canning," and — comparing steel against Sven Yrvind's flexible-composite
  approach to collision protection — judged a steel hull could be fabricated
  in as little as a couple of days. Alan Terry (a known recurring one-off)
  cited a Buehler steel-to-the-waterline precedent. Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — gauge, weight, and the case
  for steel over wood
  generally](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#gauge-weight-and-the-case-for-steel-over-wood-generally)
  and
  [— composite wood/steel hulls, and steel's fabrication
  practicalities](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#composite-woodsteel-hulls-and-steels-fabrication-practicalities)
  (citations [22]–[30]), cross-linked from `people/jack-gardiner.md`
  ([11]–[13]) and `people/william-longyard.md` ([40]–[42]). Separately,
  Alastair closed the open ballast-placement question from batch 152's
  steel-hull-bottom idea: Paradox's 70–180 kg ballast range exists to tune
  trim to whatever stores are loaded for a given trip, not to cover
  build-weight variance, so a steel-bottomed Paradox would still need the
  same ~110 kg (240 lb) of ballast a plywood one does — folded into both
  `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md` (new section "Ballast placement:
  independent of hull material," citation [31]) and
  `design/weight-and-ballast.md` (citation [66]), cross-linked from
  `people/alastair-law.md` ([147]). No new files created; no renames or
  prunings. Standing Open items checked (botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik, Jake): none
  resurfaced this batch, recorded in backlog.md. Timeline: all ten messages
  dated 27 December 2003. msgnum run 1561–1570 confirmed distinct and
  consecutive from batch 154's 1560, all ten byte sizes matched dispatch
  instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; verified no new
  citation-number collisions across all five touched files
  (`design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`, `design/weight-and-ballast.md`,
  `people/jack-gardiner.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`,
  `people/alastair-law.md`); cursor.txt set to 1550.

* **Batch 156 (pages 1551–1560, msgnum 1571–1580, 27 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: a new "Bouyancy" thread opened by below-threshold recurring
  newcomer Derek Clark, giving the archive its clearest statement of basic
  buoyancy physics and Nels's fullest ballast/flotation placement doctrine,
  interwoven with the still-live "Steel?" thread.** Derek Clark's basic
  buoyancy questions (does "buoyant" mean floating deck-level when swamped;
  how much foam does a given material need) drew William Longyard's rule of
  thumb (**55 lb of buoyancy per cubic foot of foam**) and Jack Gardiner's
  positive/negative/neutral explanation; Derek's own follow-up pivoted the
  thread into a steel-Paradox weight question (bottom/sides/stern in steel
  under a wood deck), answered by Longyard with a rough panel-weight
  estimate (**4.3 lb/sq ft**) and a caution against direct wood/steel
  contact. Jack Gardiner clarified that buoyancy foam does not raise the
  working waterline (only affects flooded trim), unlike ballast. Nels then
  gave the fullest ballast-and-flotation placement doctrine in the archive:
  ballast belongs low and amidships (Paradox's bottom curves like a banana,
  so amidships is the low point), flotation belongs high at the ends, for
  self-righting — and a steel hull needs flotation beyond a wood boat's own
  to offset the steel's weight; Longyard corrected Nels's aside on steel
  aircraft-fuselage history (Fokker-era WWI through the 1930s). Folded into
  [plywood grade and foam flotation debate — buoyancy fundamentals: positive,
  negative, and
  neutral](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md#buoyancy-fundamentals-positive-negative-and-neutral),
  [— ballast and buoyancy placement, and why the Paradox's rocker
  matters](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md#ballast-and-buoyancy-placement-and-why-the-paradoxs-rocker-matters),
  and [— wood decks over a steel hull, and material-contact
  cautions](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md#wood-decks-over-a-steel-hull-and-material-contact-cautions)
  (citations [36]–[43]). Separately, the "Steel?" thread continued: Jack
  Gardiner asked whether a steel hull frees up any of the lead ballast
  itself, and Alastair answered with his own real-world ballast data from
  "Little Jim" (weighed to the **640 kg** design displacement; shifting lead
  significantly affected handling; a pound of lead out per pound of stores
  added, mattering most on long trips) — folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — whether a steel hull frees up
  any of the lead ballast at
  all](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#whether-a-steel-hull-frees-up-any-of-the-lead-ballast-at-all)
  (citations [32]–[33]). Cross-linked from `people/william-longyard.md`,
  `people/nels.md`, `people/jack-gardiner.md`, and `people/alastair-law.md`. No
  new files, promotions, or prunings; Derek Clark remains below-threshold
  and citation-only. Standing Open items checked ("Jeff UK"/roverticket,
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3,
  Fredrik/fredrilu, Jake/jacobtm6) and HANDOVER.md's "threads to watch
  next": none resurfaced this batch, recorded in backlog.md. Timeline: all
  ten messages dated 27 December 2003. msgnum run 1571–1580 confirmed
  distinct and consecutive from batch 155's 1570, all ten byte sizes matched
  dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; verified no new
  citation-number collisions across both touched files
  (`construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md`,
  `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`); cursor.txt set to 1560.

* **Batch 157 (pages 1561–1570, msgnum 1581–1590, 27 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Bouyancy" thread's steel-fabrication coda (compound-curved
  chine logs and sheer clamps, and a stitch-and-glue composite cabin-top
  idea), plus a fresh "Coastal vs Blue Water" thread giving the archive's
  fullest Sven-Yrvind-vs-Matt-Layden design-philosophy comparison.**
  [Nels](/people/nels.md), pressing William Longyard's plain-steel
  recommendation (1020/1018 stock, not aircraft-grade chrome/moly alloy) for
  a practical answer, asked how steel could reproduce the chine logs' and
  sheer clamps' two-plane compound curvature using 1 in square tubing while
  still carrying the welded bulkhead framing, and whether wood's beveling
  step disappears in steel. Longyard clarified his "couple of hours" claim
  meant the basic hull only, and confirmed thin-walled tubing bends easily
  with a torch (tack, move, heat-bend, tack, repeat); Jack Gardiner added
  that round thin-wall tubing gives a smoother arc for the same members, and
  Longyard agreed angle iron would work just as well. Jack separately floated
  a companion idea for a metal-hulled Paradox: a stitch-and-glue composite
  cabin top/deck (door-skin plywood over foam board with epoxy, comparable to
  Sven Yrvind's cold-molded method, with rubber window molding). Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — fabricating steel chine logs
  and sheer clamps: two curves at
  once](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#fabricating-steel-chine-logs-and-sheer-clamps-two-curves-at-once)
  and [— a stitch-and-glue composite cabin top as a steel-hull companion
  idea](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-stitch-and-glue-composite-cabin-top-as-a-steel-hull-companion-idea)
  (citations [34]–[40]), cross-linked from `people/nels.md` ([57]–[59]),
  `people/william-longyard.md` ([43]–[45]), and `people/jack-gardiner.md`
  ([14]–[15], plus a new prose paragraph). Separately, David Beard reopened
  the archive's "coastal vs blue water" question from a designer's point of
  view (referring explicitly to Matt Layden's and Sven Yrvind's boats), and
  this time drew two substantive answers: David LeBlanc laid out the
  conventional blue-water-vs-coastal-cruiser checklist and explained why
  neither Paradox nor Yrvind's "Bris" fits it cleanly (no cockpit, one wide
  berth, capacity capped by size), illustrated with Ottavio Testa's *500
  Days* circumnavigation in a sub-12 ft boat; Nels then itemized Yrvind's
  concrete design choices against Layden's — divinycell foam-sandwich
  laminates over plywood, a reluctant return to a centerboard over chine
  runners for big-wave performance, hulls designed to invert-unstable with a
  short mast for fast self-righting, a multi-sail (tops'l gaff + jibs +
  light-air spinnaker) plan versus Layden's single simple rig, and a
  double-ended hull for stern buoyancy. Folded into
  [blue-water suitability — what conventional blue-water features Paradox and
  "Bris" both
  forgo](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#what-conventional-blue-water-features-paradox-and-bris-both-forgo)
  and [— Sven Yrvind's specific design choices, weighed against Matt
  Layden's](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#sven-yrvinds-specific-design-choices-weighed-against-matt-laydens)
  (citations [21]–[23]), cross-linked from `people/david-beard.md` ([182]),
  `people/david-leblanc.md` ([50], plus a new prose bullet), and
  `people/nels.md` ([59]). No new files, promotions, or prunings. Standing
  Open items checked (botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik/fredrilu, Jake/jacobtm6): none
  resurfaced this batch, recorded in backlog.md. Timeline: all ten messages
  dated 27 December 2003. msgnum run 1581–1590 confirmed distinct and
  consecutive from batch 156's 1580, all ten byte sizes matched dispatch
  instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; verified no new citation-number
  collisions or sequence gaps across all six touched files
  (`design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`, `design/blue-water-suitability.md`,
  `people/nels.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`, `people/jack-gardiner.md`,
  `people/david-beard.md`, `people/david-leblanc.md`); cursor.txt set to 1570.

* **Batch 158 (pages 1571–1580, msgnum 1591–1601 with 1598 absent from the
  archive, 27–28 December 2003) — centerpiece: the "Coastal vs Blue Water"
  thread's conclusion, and the "Steel?"/"Steel Dinghy Photo" mega-thread's
  closing act.** David LeBlanc judged Nels's Yrvind-vs-Layden itemization a
  matter of materials rather than design philosophy, and disputed the
  double-ender's stern-buoyancy rationale on hydrostatic grounds (a transom
  box encloses more volume than a canoe-stern triangle; per John Welsford, a
  double-ender carries less overall than an equivalent transom-sterned boat
  of the same LOD/beam/draft). Nels countered that Yrvind's expensive
  divinycell/epoxy choice was genuinely design-driven (North Sea insulation
  needs, largely irrelevant to warmer-water Paradox), and offered two
  possible reasons for the double-ender choice beyond LeBlanc's volume
  argument (less tendency to overload a canoe stern in a following sea; a
  possible Scandinavian cultural preference), closing the thread without
  final reconciliation. Folded into
  [blue-water suitability — Sven Yrvind's specific design choices, weighed
  against Matt
  Layden's](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#sven-yrvinds-specific-design-choices-weighed-against-matt-laydens)
  (citations [24]–[25]), cross-linked from `people/david-leblanc.md` ([51])
  and `people/nels.md` ([60]). Separately, a Yahoo file-upload notice
  ("Bris 5.4 Study Plans/rowing_boat.jpg") was folded into
  [references/study-plans.md](/references/study-plans.md) as a new section
  (citation [2], renumbering the prior microcruising.com citation to [3]),
  cross-linked from `people/nels.md` ([61]). The "Steel?" thread resumed with
  William Longyard's photo of a real 9 ft Dutch steel dinghy (a common
  motor-barge tender on the Rhine/Danube and throughout Holland), prompting
  Jack Gardiner's priming/painting caution (avoid unprimed steel-to-steel
  joints from flat square stock; prefer preprimed steel or round tubing) and
  Nels's fullest case that Paradox's plywood/glass hull may not need steel at
  all — comparing her to whitewater-canoe foam-core/tough-coating
  construction (the same principle behind Sven Yrvind's Bris), judging a
  well-built plywood Paradox probably stronger than most fiberglass boats her
  size, and cataloguing bottom-toughening options (extra cloth, a sacrificial
  hardwood keel strip, or a carbon-powder-reinforced bottom as used on
  rock-running whitewater canoes). The thread then closed with its sharpest
  disagreement: "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`/`apcf14`) argued strongly against
  steel on design-philosophy grounds — Paradox's whole point is buildability
  by anyone with low woodworking skills, and steel adds fabrication problems
  (chine-runner shaping, fairing, a wooden deck over a steel hull) for no
  real gain, when Matt Layden has already sailed the plywood original
  thousands of miles; William Longyard answered that steel poses no more
  challenge than wood and is a matter of personal preference ("we'd all
  still be using coracles" otherwise); David LeBlanc sided fully with Jeff UK
  ("What Jeff said!") and pointed to `www.fairmetalboats.com` for anyone
  seriously considering steel. Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — a real-world precedent: small
  steel dinghies on European
  canals](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-real-world-precedent-small-steel-dinghies-on-european-canals),
  [— why Paradox itself may not need steel: plywood/glass toughness and rock
  protection](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#why-paradox-itself-may-not-need-steel-plywoodglass-toughness-and-rock-protection),
  and [— a builder's case against steel, on design-philosophy
  grounds](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#a-builders-case-against-steel-on-design-philosophy-grounds)
  (citations [41]–[47]), cross-linked from `people/william-longyard.md`
  ([46]–[47]), `people/jack-gardiner.md` ([16]–[17], plus a new prose
  paragraph), `people/nels.md` ([62]), `people/jeff-doryman.md` ([35], plus a
  new Disambiguation paragraph), and `people/david-leblanc.md` ([52]). No new
  person, boat, or concept pages; no renames or prunings. Standing Open items
  checked (botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek
  Clark, Fredrik/fredrilu, Jake/jacobtm6): none resurfaced. "Jeff UK" did
  resurface (msgnum 1599) — consistent with his established recurring,
  still-unconfirmed identity relative to Jeff/`doryman_uk`; no new
  corroborating link found, recorded in backlog.md along with the rest of
  this check. Timeline: nine messages dated 27 December 2003, one (msgnum
  1595–1601, i.e. page_1575 onward) dated 28 December 2003 — the archive's
  first calendar-day advance since batch 154. msgnum run 1591–1601
  confirmed distinct and consecutive from batch 157's 1590, **with msgnum
  1598 confirmed genuinely absent from the archive** (no corresponding page
  exists; not a stale-read or missing-file issue), all ten byte sizes matched
  dispatch instructions exactly, no stale-read issue; verified no new
  citation-number collisions across all eight touched files
  (`design/blue-water-suitability.md`, `references/study-plans.md`,
  `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`, `people/david-leblanc.md`,
  `people/nels.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`, `people/jack-gardiner.md`,
  `people/jeff-doryman.md`); cursor.txt set to 1580.

* **Batch 154 (pages 1531–1540, msgnum 1551–1560, 26–27 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread's second wave, resolving the practical
  fabrication/finishing questions left open by batch 153.** William Longyard
  answered with field-welding detail (a small oxy/acetylene kit heats
  0.10 in steel hot enough to gas-weld away from shore power), a firsthand
  data point on how small steel boats have really been built (steel-hulled,
  multi-chined boats as small as **9 ft** cruising Amsterdam's canals,
  confirming Paradox at 14 ft "unquestionably suitable"), cheap plasma
  cutters for fast plate-cutting (a full hull's plates cut in under an hour,
  or ~$100 at a muffler shop), and ordinary red oxide primer rather than
  epoxy for finishing. Nels, who doubted Paradox suited steel at all,
  corrected his own earlier citation of a Phil Bolger steel
  solo-circumnavigator design — the true figure is **1/8 in** plate, not
  3/16 in, claimed equal in weight to 1.5 in mahogany — and asked
  unanswered follow-up questions about 0.10 in-plate weight and oil-canning.
  Jack Gardiner closed the thread reflecting that his own draw to
  unconventional construction mirrors Matt Layden's, Sven Yrvind's, and Phil
  Bolger's shared rule-breaking spirit. Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — field welding, and
  confirmation from real small steel
  boats](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#field-welding-and-confirmation-from-real-small-steel-boats),
  cross-linked from [Nels](/people/nels.md),
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md), and
  [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md). Separately, **Kjell Karlsson's
  Sven Yrvind book-chapter translation drew brief thanks** from Nels and
  David Beard, folded into [the "BRIS Paradox" — reception of the
  translated book
  chapter](/boats/bris-paradox.md#reception-of-the-translated-book-chapter),
  cross-linked from [Nels](/people/nels.md),
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), and
  [Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md). A small aside — Jack Gardiner
  buying William Longyard's book *A Speck on the Sea* mid-thread, and
  Longyard's thanks — folded into both
  [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md#author-of-a-speck-on-the-sea)
  and [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md). No new files, no
  promotions, no prunings. Standing "threads to watch" identity items
  checked (Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry, Derek
  Clark, Fredrik, Jake): none resurfaced this batch. Msgnum run 1551–1560
  confirmed distinct and consecutive, continuing directly from batch 153's
  1550.

* **Batch 153 (pages 1521–1530, msgnum 1541–1550, 26 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Steel?" thread — whether a whole Paradox hull could be
  built in steel rather than plywood.** Jack Gardiner (the prospective
  Rhode Island builder introduced in batch 152, who had already floated a
  narrower steel-hull-*bottom* idea) widened the question to a full steel
  hull; William Longyard gave the archive's fullest technical answer
  (12-gauge plate over thicker 3/16 in, added stringers/frames, steel-tube
  bulkheads that could open the mast bulkhead, square-tube gunnels, a small
  110V welder sufficing, radar reflectivity unchanged since Paradox already
  rides low), while David LeBlanc pushed back on weight/carrying-capacity
  grounds and suggested Kevlar instead. Folded into
  [aluminum and steel Paradox (speculative) — whole-hull steel construction](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md#whole-hull-steel-construction-gauge-framing-and-damage-tolerance),
  which was retitled from "Aluminum Paradox" to cover both materials, with
  cross-links from [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md) and
  [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md). Jack Gardiner — now a
  sustained, substantive contributor across two batches — was **promoted to
  a full person page**, [Jack Gardiner](/people/jack-gardiner.md), added to
  [people/index.md](/people/index.md). Separately, **Kjell Karlsson
  translated the Matt Layden/Paradox chapter of Sven Yrvind's new
  Swedish-language book** ("chapter9.doc"), answering the standing wish
  voiced twice earlier in the archive (by Nels, secondhand, and by Bjorn
  Harbo directly) that someone bilingual translate it — folded into
  [the "BRIS Paradox" — Sven Yrvind's book, translated: the Matt
  Layden/Paradox chapter](/boats/bris-paradox.md#sven-yrvinds-book-translated-the-matt-laydenparadox-chapter),
  cross-linked from [Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md). No
  renames of existing files (kept `aluminum-construction-idea.md`'s
  filename to avoid breaking cross-links; only its title/description
  widened). No prunings. Standing open items checked (botebilder/Ernie
  Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3, Derek Clark, Fredrik): none
  resurfaced; "Jack of Rhode Island" resurfaced and was promoted, resolving
  that standing item — recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. Timeline: all ten
  messages dated 26 December 2003. Msgnum run 1541–1550 confirmed distinct
  and consecutive from batch 152's 1540; all ten byte sizes matched
  dispatch instructions exactly; no stale-read issue. Verified no new
  citation-number collisions across all six touched/created files. Updated
  [design/index.md](/design/index.md) and
  [people/index.md](/people/index.md). `cursor.txt` set to 1530.

* **Batch 152 (pages 1511–1520, msgnum 1531–1540, 23–26 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: William Longyard's "What Sven Yrvind Said" — the designer's
  own verdict on why a taller, higher-aspect-ratio sail would not suit
  Paradox's single-sail, keel-less hull.** Folded into
  [rig and sail — sail aspect ratio, and why a taller, narrower rig would
  not suit Paradox](/components/rig/index.md#sail-aspect-ratio-and-why-a-taller-narrower-rig-would-not-suit-paradox),
  with a cross-link from [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md).
  A holiday-season **"Help The Troops" Hero Miles appeal** (Longyard,
  Derek Clark's "whose troops?" question, David LeBlanc's answer) and a
  one-line "Good Old Boat" magazine addendum from a one-off correspondent
  (Bill Jaine) added to
  off-topic cross-posts,
  with a cross-link from [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md).
  A new prospective builder ("Jack," Rhode Island) checked in and was
  welcomed by Glen Maxwell with a fresh census figure — **six Paradoxes
  launched, "that we know of"** — extending the running census in
  [Southern California sailing — census: six Paradoxes launched (December
  2003)](/operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md#census-six-paradoxes-launched-december-2003),
  with a cross-link from
  [Glen Maxwell's list-founder-and-moderator page](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md).
  The same newcomer's speculative **steel hull-bottom ballast/impact-protection
  idea** (unbuilt, unanswered) added to
  [weight, ballast, and displacement — a steel hull bottom as combined
  ballast and impact protection](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#a-steel-hull-bottom-as-combined-ballast-and-impact-protection-speculative),
  cross-linked from [the aluminum Paradox idea](/design/aluminum-construction-idea.md).
  Dave Bolduc reported Hugh's New Zealand Paradox **finished**, named
  **"Petite Lady"** — updated on [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) and
  the [boats index](/boats/index.md)'s "other known builds" note. Checked
  the standing open items (Jeff UK/`roverticket` — already resolved;
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel; below-threshold Larry; Derek Clark — a third,
  still-thin appearance, status unchanged; Fredrik; Jake): none resurfaced
  beyond Derek Clark's expected off-topic one-liner, recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. No new person, boat, or design concepts
  created — all content extends existing files. No renames or prunings.
  Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of the nine touched
  files (`references/off-topic-cross-posts.md`, `references/index.md`,
  `people/william-longyard.md`, `design/rig.md`,
  `operations/southern-california-and-catalina-crossing.md`,
  `operations/index.md`, `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`,
  `design/weight-and-ballast.md`, `design/aluminum-construction-idea.md`,
  `boats/index.md`, `people/dave-bolduc.md`). Msgnum run 1531–1540 confirmed
  distinct and consecutive via byte-size and header cross-check (stale-read
  guard passed).

* **Batch 151 (pages 1501–1510, msgnum 1521–1530, 15–22 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Lower Rudder Gudgeon" casting-technique thread.**
  David Beard's question about the plans' 6-layer, 24 oz roving lay-up for
  the lower rudder gudgeon casting, and Glen Maxwell's confirmation and
  bias-cut/mold/dowel-rod technique answer, folded into
  [lower rudder fitting — casting a glass lower rudder gudgeon:
  technique](/construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md#casting-a-glass-lower-rudder-gudgeon-technique),
  with cross-links from [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) and
  [Glen Maxwell's build-and-Zoë page](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md).
  Derek Clark's corroborating report — visiting "Little Jim" and finding
  Alastair had actually built his lower rudder fitting from a block of
  Tufnol (previously only proposed, in 2002) and a masthead light from
  LEDs — folded into the same section, with cross-links added to
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md); David Beard's interest in the LEDs
  recorded as a citation. The batch also closed out the "Zero proves
  herself" thread from batch 150: Alastair's reply (ballast importance;
  sailing without a motor made him a better sailor; shopping for an
  electric outboard, VHF first), Bill Serjeant posting Andrew Graham's
  deck photos to his own Paradox website, and Nels's admiring reply on the
  chine-runner shots — folded into
  [Aussie Paradox](/boats/aussie-paradox.md#zero-proves-herself-an-18-mile-open-water-passage-across-moreton-bay),
  with a cross-link added from [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md). William
  Longyard's UK boating-magazine recommendation (Watercraft and Practical
  Boat Owner over WoodenBoat-style "big American glossies") added as a new
  subsection of
  off-topic cross-posts,
  with a cross-link from [William Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md).
  Checked the standing open items (Jeff UK/`roverticket` — already
  resolved per batch 150; botebilder/Ernie Fossel; below-threshold Larry;
  Derek Clark — now recorded twice as a below-threshold correspondent with
  still no boat/build of his own, unchanged status; Fredrik; Jake): none
  resurfaced beyond Derek Clark's expected reappearance, recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. No new person, boat, or design concepts
  created — all content extends existing files. No renames or prunings.
  Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of the seven touched
  files (`boats/aussie-paradox.md`, `construction/rudder-bearing-materials.md`,
  `people/david-beard.md`, `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`,
  `people/alastair-law.md`, `people/william-longyard.md`,
  `references/off-topic-cross-posts.md`).

* **Batch 150 (pages 1491–1500, msgnum 1511–1520, 11–15 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: Andrew Graham's "Zero proves herself" — the first substantial
  post-launch sail report.** An 18-nautical-mile round trip across Moreton
  Bay (Wellington Point to Dunwich via Peel Island, one tack) with ballast
  and stores increased to about 190 kg confirmed satisfactory stability and
  pointing, folded into
  [Aussie Paradox](/boats/aussie-paradox.md#zero-proves-herself-an-18-mile-open-water-passage-across-moreton-bay),
  with a cross-link added from [Andrew Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md).
  The batch's other thread continued "Paul" (`kayaker37`)'s CAD-model
  upload (introduced in batch 149): [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) praised the
  renderings and asked about the build manual; Paul explained his Rhino 2.0
  workflow (bulkhead-outline lofting, Greg Carlson's Hull design software,
  under-scaled chine runners); [Nels](/people/nels.md) asked about scale
  accuracy and input figures; and [Glen
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md), back from a trip, asked
  to use a rendering on the group's header page — all folded into
  [notching the stem for chine logs and sheer clamps](/construction/stem-notching.md#aside-an-early-catia-3d-model-of-the-paradox).
  In the same thread, Nels's welcome-back question drew Glen's own verdict
  on Zoë's new sail — 90° tacking (100° for more speed) and unqualified
  praise for the chine runners — folded into
  [chine runners and windward performance](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md#pointing-angles-owners-report),
  with cross-links from Nels and Glen's own build-and-Zoë page. A one-off
  topical joke post resolved the "Jeff UK" open item as already-merged
  (confirmed `roverticket` = "Jeffery Please," already disambiguated on
  [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) in an earlier batch);
  the joke itself added as a single citation. Checked the standing open
  items (Jeff UK/`roverticket`, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry, and the batch-149 newcomers Fredrik and Jake): none resurfaced
  this batch beyond the Jeff UK confirmation above; recorded in
  `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. No new person, boat, or design concepts
  created — all content extends existing files. No renames or prunings.
  Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of the eight files
  touched (`construction/stem-notching.md`, `boats/aussie-paradox.md`,
  `people/andrew-graham.md`,
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md`, `people/nels.md`,
  `people/david-c-smith.md`, `people/jeff-doryman.md`,
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`). Cursor advanced to 1500.

* **Batch 149 (pages 1481–1490, msgnum 1501–1510, 3–11 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: closing out the "Zero" launch thread, plus two newcomer
  Q&As.** [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) congratulated Andrew Graham on "Zero,"
  praising her paint scheme and plexiglass tint and asking about interior
  arrangement and galley access; Andrew replied with his Moreton Bay/Brisbane
  sailing grounds, crediting Alastair's "Little Jim" launch photos with
  driving his own commitment to build, and noting his partner "Senorita"
  grew up on a Fijian farm; [Nels](/people/nels.md) followed up with a
  Moreton Bay link and a "Crocodile Hunter" joke — all folded into
  [Aussie Paradox](/boats/aussie-paradox.md), with cross-links added from
  [Andrew Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md) and [Nels](/people/nels.md).
  Separately, two new members asked the archive's two most common
  newcomer questions within a day of each other: **Jake** (`jacobtm6`)
  asked how well the chine runners let Paradox point to windward, answered
  by [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) (the group's FAQ file, a Small
  Craft Advisor article, a firsthand Seapearl-pointing comparison — "The
  chine runners work !!") and by Nels (weight-on-heel, sail cut, and lug-rig
  handling skill, plus a pointer to the Bolducs' own `microcruising` group),
  folded into
  [chine runners and windward performance](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md#pointing-angles-owners-report);
  and **Fredrik** (`fredrilu`, Norway) asked about a 20% scale-up and
  build time/cost, answered by Nels (the "Larger Paradox" files folder,
  Matt Layden's own endorsement of the enlargement, a Scandinavian
  builder network, and an Oslo schooner-builder contact), folded into
  [stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md#a-scandinavian-newcomers-scaling-question).
  A third one-off poster, "Paul" (`kayaker37`), uploaded a second,
  independent Paradox CAD model with a few flagged modeling errors,
  folded into
  [notching the stem for chine logs and sheer clamps](/construction/stem-notching.md)
  alongside Alastair's earlier CATIA gallery, with an explicit
  disambiguation note distinguishing this Paul from the existing
  [Paul V.](/people/paul-v.md) and [Paul (NZ)](/people/paul-nz.md). Checked
  the standing open items (Jeff UK/`roverticket`, botebilder/Ernie Fossel,
  below-threshold Larry, Derek Clark's promotion threshold): none resurfaced
  this batch; recorded in `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. No new person, boat,
  or design concepts created — all content extends existing files. No
  renames or prunings. Verified no new citation-number collisions in any of
  the seven files touched (`boats/aussie-paradox.md`,
  `people/andrew-graham.md`, `people/nels.md`,
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md`,
  `people/david-beard.md`, `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md`,
  `construction/stem-notching.md`). Cursor advanced to 1490.

* **Batch 148 (pages 1471–1480, msgnum 1491–1500, 1–2 December 2003) —
  centerpiece: Andrew Graham launches "Zero," the first Australian-built
  Paradox to sail.** Two threads. First, the "Chine Log shape" thread (opened
  in batch 147) closed out: [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) explained the chine log's
  puzzling extra triangular section as simply another rolling bevel — chine
  logs and sheer clamps are both cut to a constant bevel per the plans, then
  dressed off with a straight edge once glued in place to accept the flat
  bottom, exactly as the sheer clamps are separately faired to the deck
  beams; [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) added that some angle is
  unavoidable since the bottom measurement isn't vertical, but in practice
  it "doesn't matter much"; Bill Serjeant independently reasoned to the same
  conclusion from the lines drawing; the original asker, Paul, then worked
  out for himself that it's easier to glue the chine log flat to the side
  first and cut the rolling bevel into the bottom face rather than the log's
  side, which Smith confirmed ("Bingo. Same with the sheer clamp."). Folded
  into `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md` (extended "Fit at trial
  assembly rather than pre-cutting a perfect bevel" section, citations
  37-41; corrected the prior "remained open in the archive" line, now
  resolved), with cross-link citations in `people/alastair-law.md` (143). Second
  and larger, "Aussie Paradox sails": [Andrew Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md)
  reported launching his Brisbane-built Paradox on 29 November 2003, naming
  her **"Zero"** and tentatively claiming the first Australian Paradox to
  hit the water — a claim [Nels](/people/nels.md) confirmed and
  congratulated. The launch report is the archive's fullest single-message
  account of a Paradox's first days afloat: sail sewn from 6.3 oz cloth to
  Matt Layden's Mk 2 dimensions, a straight sweep and nylon rowlock
  substituted for the yuloh, 145 kg lead ballast plus trial stores, a first
  solo sail sculled from the Bribie Island ramp that held to windward
  against a foul tide and tacked reliably from the outset, an overnight
  aboard with his partner "Senorita," her first-ever sail the next day
  (tacking reliably two-up despite an alarming heel that a reef promptly
  fixed), and a full cost breakdown (~$5,500 AUD sail-away). Folded into
  `boats/aussie-paradox.md` (new section "Launch, and a name: 'Zero'",
  citations 13-17), with the boat's frontmatter title/description updated to
  reflect the launch and name. Reception drew Nels's and Alastair's
  congratulations (Alastair: "soon it will be truly said that the sun never
  sets on the Paradox"), an unanswered request for interior photos from the
  recurring below-threshold correspondent Derek Clark (`dclark52001`, first
  seen batch 145, now his fifth appearance — still no boat of his own, kept
  inline only), and word from
  [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) that Zero's photos sent him
  straight back to work on his own "[Whisper](/boats/whisper.md)." All five
  cross-linked from `people/andrew-graham.md` (citation 11),
  `people/nels.md` (citation 48), `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 144), and
  `people/david-beard.md` (citation 176); `boats/index.md` and
  `people/index.md` description lines updated. Standing Open items checked
  per HANDOVER.md: Jeff UK/roverticket, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, and
  below-threshold Larry do not appear in this batch; the Derek Clark check
  is recorded in `.okf-progress/backlog.md`. No new files, promotions, or
  prunings. Msgnum run 1491-1500 confirmed distinct and consecutive
  continuing directly from batch 147's 1490; all ten byte sizes matched
  instructions exactly on direct read, no stale-read issue. Verified no new
  citation-number collisions across all six touched files
  (`construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md`, `boats/aussie-paradox.md`,
  `people/andrew-graham.md`, `people/nels.md`, `people/alastair-law.md`,
  `people/david-beard.md`). Cursor advanced to 1480.

* **Batch 147 (pages 1461–1470, msgnum 1481–1490, 29–30 November 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Off to Philly" thread's aftermath, a small but concrete
  illustration of how raw the Don Elliott ban still was.** [Nels](/people/nels.md),
  confirming as co-moderator that he'd received Glen Maxwell's delegation
  instructions for his Philadelphia absence, added a joking aside that
  anyone getting out of line would be sent to "the other site" (Don's rival
  group); a below-threshold correspondent, Derek Clark, objected sharply
  that even joking sniping doesn't help and that Don's critics should be
  extending peace feelers instead; Nels apologized, clarifying he held no
  real grievance and that tone is easily misread in writing. Folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (new prose under "A
  moderator's absence, delegated in advance," citations 64-66) and
  `people/nels.md` (new bullet, citations 45-46). Nels separately stood in
  to welcome about a dozen recent new members, and disclosed the group
  itself was "in the progress of considering developing plans for a
  somewhat enlarged version of Paradox" — a new, group-level enlargement
  effort distinct from the individual builders' scaling proposals already
  on record. Folded into `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`
  (new prose under "Welcoming newcomers," citation 67) and
  `design/variants/stretched-paradox.md` (new section "A group-level
  enlarged Paradox under consideration," citation 51), with
  `people/nels.md` (citation 47) cross-linking. A second thread, "Foam
  sheets?": a non-builder from New Zealand, wanting to use foam in a dinghy
  rather than a Paradox, asked what foam type builders use;
  [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) answered simply — expanded polystyrene
  sheet from the builders merchant, held with hardboard panels, a distinct
  material choice from the sheet/spray-in polyurethane foam used for a
  Paradox's own flotation and insulation. Folded into
  `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` (new short section,
  citations 34-35) and `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 141). A third thread,
  "Chine Log shape": a puzzled builder's question about the chine log's
  non-uniform drawn bevel on sheet 5 got Alastair's answer that the bevel
  is correct at the log's ends but not the middle, since the logs and
  bulkheads are meant to be faired together once installed rather than
  pre-cut to a perfect bevel and simply assembled — restating, with a new
  concrete example, the design's general "assemble to what you've already
  made" build philosophy already on record elsewhere in the archive; Bill
  Serjeant confirmed the same reading by working the bevel out from sheet
  2; the original asker's follow-up, about a separate extra triangular
  section at the chine log's bottom (illustrated with a photo posted to
  the group's files), went unanswered in the archive. Folded into
  `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md` (new section "Fit at trial
  assembly rather than pre-cutting a perfect bevel," citations 33-36) and
  `people/alastair-law.md` (new bullet, citation 142). No new files, no
  promotions or prunings. All three standing Open items checked (Jeff UK,
  botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/newspipetr3): none
  resurfaced. The below-threshold Derek Clark resurfaced once more (his
  fourth appearance), still no boat or build progress, so he remains below
  the promotion threshold. Clean fully consecutive msgnum run 1481-1490,
  no gaps, continuing directly from batch 146's 1480, spanning 29-30
  November 2003; all ten byte sizes matched instructions exactly and all
  ten X-Yahoo-Msgnum headers confirmed distinct on direct read, no
  stale-read issue; verified no new citation-number collisions across the
  five touched files; cursor.txt set to 1470.**

* **Batch 146 (pages 1451–1460, msgnum 1471–1480, 25–28 November 2003) —
  centerpiece: the "Insulation and rot" thread, opened in batch 145,
  answered in full.** [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) pointed Derek
  Clark to Glen Maxwell's removable-foam idea; [David C. Smith](/people/david-c-smith.md) gave the
  full technical answer — his own choice of epoxy-glued foam board under a
  3 mm Okoume veneer versus Glen's removable-panel approach, weighing
  condensation risk, foil-facing durability, and deck-leak exposure, and
  likening the choice to painted-vs-varnished spars; Derek asked two
  follow-ups (whether thin closed-cell camping-mat foam and silicone
  sealant as an adhesive could work) and a tangent (where to stow wet
  warps); [Nels](/people/nels.md) gave a cost data point on camping-mat
  foam. Folded into `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md`
  (citation 29 updated, new citations 30-33) and `operations/ground-tackle.md`
  (new section "Stowing wet warps," citation 18), with `people/david-beard.md`
  (citation 175) and `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 86) cross-linking. A second
  thread, "Mast Construction help": Paul (`kayaker37`) worked through the
  same mast plan-view confusion documented in `design/rig.md` a year and a
  half earlier; [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) gave the fullest published
  explanation yet of the plans' three mast views and the stave-assembly
  sequence, plus a practical tip of mounting the mast step/vent trunk to
  bulkhead 2 before hull assembly (a second builder's confirmation of Glen
  Maxwell's own sequence) — folded into `design/rig.md` (new section
  "Reading the mast's plan views" extended, citations 60-62) and
  `design/mast-partners.md` (new bullet under "Build sequence"), with
  `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 139) cross-linking. A third thread, "new
  sail": Kjell Karlsson quantified his own earlier boom-mod question with a
  full geometric analysis of Matt Layden's new sailplan's effect on
  furling proportions (halyard attachment point, boom circumference fore
  and aft); Glen Maxwell replied, crediting Alastair's foam-on-boom-end
  idea and confirming the halyard position as drawn — folded into
  `design/rig.md` (new section "Quantifying the new sailplan's effect on
  boom furling geometry," citations 63-64), with `people/kjell-karlsson.md`
  (citations 11-12) and `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 140) cross-linking.
  Glen Maxwell's own "Off to Philly" announcement — a planned absence for
  his second grandchild's birth, delegating new-member requests to the
  assistant moderators and hoping to progress the Build Manual remotely —
  folded into `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (new
  section "A moderator's absence, delegated in advance," citation 63). No
  new files, no promotions or prunings. Checked all standing backlog items
  ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/
  `newspipetr3`, and the batch-145 Derek Clark below-threshold mention):
  Derek Clark resurfaced with two further messages in the now-resolved
  "Insulation and rot" thread (still no boat/build of his own — remains a
  below-threshold correspondent per the same threshold as other one-off
  posters); the other three did not resurface — see `.okf-progress/backlog.md`
  for the updated Open items. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully
  consecutive run (1471-1480) with no gaps**, continuing directly from
  batch 145's 1470, spanning 25-28 November 2003. All ten source files'
  byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly, and all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read (no
  stale-read issue). Verified no new citation-number collisions across the
  eight files that received edits
  (`construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` [30]-[33],
  `operations/ground-tackle.md` [18], `people/david-beard.md` [175],
  `people/david-c-smith.md` [86], `design/rig.md` [60]-[64],
  `design/mast-partners.md` [prose only], `people/alastair-law.md` [139]-[140],
  `people/kjell-karlsson.md` [11]-[12],
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` [63]).

* **Batch 145 (pages 1441–1450, msgnum 1461–1470, 23–25 November 2003) —
  centerpiece: Smith posted the Build Manual's draft Introduction.** Smith's
  group-written Paradox Build Manual proposal (batch 139-141) reached actual
  content: Smith posted a full draft introduction setting the manual's scope
  (guidance for builders of Matt Layden's design, following the plans as
  drawn), covering metric plan measurements, plans-copying and build-journal
  advice, ambiguous-species materials referencing, individually-compiled
  materials lists, bulk-purchase savings, and flexible task ordering — folded
  into `construction/build-effort.md` (new section "The Build Manual's
  introduction," citation 40), cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation
  84, prose extended) and `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`.
  David Beard volunteered to write the Yuloh section — folded into
  `people/david-beard.md` (citation 173) and cross-linked from
  `construction/build-effort.md` (citation 41). Smith's follow-up asking after
  David's own progress, whether "Whisper" was still being prepped for a
  planned trip, and how a new boat was coming along went unanswered in the
  archive — folded into `people/david-beard.md` (citation 174) and
  `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 85, prose extended). A second thread: William
  Longyard opened "Spray In Foam," pointing to `TekSupply.com` for
  high-volume urethane spray-in insulation/flotation foam after finding
  canned "Great Stuff" unpredictable; Smith answered with a full technical
  rundown of commercially applied two-part polyurethane spray foam as a
  standardized metal-boat insulation technique adaptable to a Paradox,
  weighing it against the sheet foam he used on his own boat — folded into
  `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` (new section
  "Spray-in foam versus sheet foam for insulation," citations 27-28), with
  `people/william-longyard.md` (citation 31, new bullet) and `people/david-c-smith.md`
  (citation 83) cross-linking. A new correspondent, Derek Clark
  (`dclark52001`), asked whether fixed insulation might contribute to and
  hide rot, weighed against its contribution to positive buoyancy —
  unanswered in the archive, folded into
  `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` (citation 29) as a
  below-threshold single-message mention. Two shorter threads closed out:
  David Beard asked Glen Maxwell to scan his own *Messing About In Boats*
  shakedown-cruise article into the group's files, having missed it in
  print; Glen tried uploading it four times without success and planned to
  strip the photographs to reduce file size — folded into `boats/zoe.md`
  (citations 28-29, extending the "Press coverage" section) and
  `people/david-beard.md` (citation 172). Glen also replied to Ron de Hon's
  further "RHXII" photo upload (batch 144) asking about a launch date —
  folded into `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation
  62). Smith gave a thin, one-line confirmation that his "rolling bevels"
  technique post would be folded into the Build Manual, apologizing for
  being longwinded — folded into `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 82). No new
  files, no promotions or prunings. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully
  consecutive run (1461-1470) with no gaps**, continuing directly from
  batch 144's 1460, spanning 23-25 November 2003. All ten source files'
  byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly, and all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read (no
  stale-read issue). Verified no new citation-number collisions across the
  seven files that received edits
  (`people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` [62],
  `boats/zoe.md` [28]-[29], `people/david-beard.md` [172]-[174],
  `construction/build-effort.md` [40]-[41], `people/david-c-smith.md` [82]-[85],
  `construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md` [27]-[29],
  `people/william-longyard.md` [31]). Checked all three standing Open
  items ("Jeff UK"/`roverticket`, "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): **none resurfaced in this
  batch.**

* **Batch 144 (pages 1431–1440, msgnum 1451–1460, 20–23 November 2003) —
  routine loose ends: Bill Serjeant's new "smallsailboats" Yahoo Group, the
  moderator-access fix for the FAQ/Build Manual folders, Kjell Karlsson's
  Swedish-sourcing follow-up on T-12 thinner, and Glen Maxwell's anchor-count
  answer.** No single centerpiece. Bill Serjeant announced a new,
  same-name **Yahoo Group companion** to his existing `smallsailboats.co.uk`
  site pairing, open to any small-sailing-craft discussion — folded into
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md` (citation 9). Answering Bill's earlier
  FAQ-folder question, Glen Maxwell confirmed he never sails without at
  least two anchors (generally three), main anchor an 11 lb Delta Quickset,
  with a float tied to the line as insurance against losing one — folded
  into `operations/ground-tackle.md` (new section "How many anchors to
  carry," citation 17). Glen fixed the FAQ folder's editing bottleneck
  directly by making **Nels and Smith assistant moderators**, giving them file
  access — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 58). Kjell
  Karlsson clarified his earlier T-12 question was about finding a Swedish
  equivalent, not ordering across the Atlantic, and reported finding via web
  search that T-12 is alcohols and ketones; Glen pointed him to Fiberglass
  Coatings' website for a full MSDS — folded into
  `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` (citations 26-27) and
  `people/kjell-karlsson.md` (citation 9, extended). Glen's own follow-up
  replies closed out three threads already captured in batch 142/143:
  pointing Nels to the "NS" (new sail) photo series on the boom-mod question,
  unable to compare fairly since the original sail was so poorly cut —
  folded into
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` (citation 18); calling
  Smith's "rolling bevels" post a great explanation and asking if it would join
  the Build Manual, and thanking Kjell for his lower-gudgeon write-up —
  both folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citations 59-60).
  David Beard uploaded a photo of "Whisper" motoring under her 2 hp Honda —
  folded into `people/david-beard.md` (citation 171). Ron de Hon, who had
  claimed the "RHXII" mystery hull in batch 137, uploaded a further round of
  photos — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 61). No new
  files, no promotions or prunings.

* **Batch 143 (pages 1421–1430, msgnum 1441–1450, 18–20 November 2003) —
  Smith's mass-and-momentum case against carrying a spare yuloh; Kjell
  Karlsson's own lower-gudgeon method and T-12 sourcing follow-up; the
  Tools list and FAQ File threads reach their conclusions.** "Larry"
  closed out the tools-list authorship question, reassuring Bill Serjeant
  that crediting Don Elliott as the source of his own compiled list needed
  no apology — folded into `construction/build-effort.md` (citation 39).
  Paul suggested the Yahoo Groups database feature as an alternative to
  Glen Maxwell's edit-only FAQ folder (citing the Bolger group's precedent),
  and Glen adopted it — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citations 55-56).
  The **batch centerpiece**: continuing the "2 HP Motors" thread into a
  yuloh discussion, Nels doubted (as a paddler) that a heavy hull like
  Paradox's would scull well and asked about carrying a spare yuloh; Alastair
  confirmed yuloh plans are included with the Paradox plans; Smith answered with
  a full mass-and-momentum argument — heavier vessels carry farther between
  strokes than light ones (schooners working up rivers, a supertanker
  coasting past a stopped Hobie 18, a loaded vs. solo-paddled canoe), while
  light hulls instead gain acceleration — and judged a spare yuloh
  unnecessary, since the yuloh is auxiliary rather than sole propulsion —
  folded into `operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md` (new section,
  citations 23-25), with `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 81), `people/nels.md`
  (citation 44), and `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 138) cross-linking
  rather than duplicating. Kjell Karlsson offered his own lower-gudgeon
  construction method (a thick fiberglass-strip lamination in a simple
  angled mold, with a Delrin bushing for wear) ahead of Glen Maxwell's
  promised build-manual write-up — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 57), with
  `people/david-c-smith.md` and `people/kjell-karlsson.md` (citation 10) cross-linking.
  Kjell separately asked Glen for T-12 thinner's chemical name; Glen didn't
  know it either but phoned Fiberglass Coatings directly for current stock
  and pricing (nine 5-gallon pails at $37.50, three 1-gallon containers at
  $7.50, a $20 HazMat surcharge per item over a gallon) — folded into
  `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` (citations 25-26) and
  `people/kjell-karlsson.md` (citation 9). "Jeff UK" forwarded a
  coatings-related letter from the Aircar website with no further detail —
  folded into `people/jeff-doryman.md` (citation 33) as a thin, one-line
  mention. No new files, no promotions or prunings. Msgnum mapping was a
  **clean, fully consecutive run (1441-1450) with no gaps**, continuing
  directly from batch 142's 1440, spanning 18-20 November 2003. All ten
  source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly, and all
  ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read (no
  stale-read issue). Checked all three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK"
  vs. `doryman_uk`, "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold
  "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced beyond the already-established
  "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`/"Jeffery Please") appearing under his normal,
  already-tracked identity. Verified no new citation-number collisions
  across the nine files that received edits
  (`construction/build-effort.md` [39],
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` [55]-[57],
  `operations/sculling-and-yuloh-technique.md` [23]-[25], `people/david-c-smith.md`
  [81], `people/nels.md` [44], `people/alastair-law.md` [138],
  `people/jeff-doryman.md` [33], `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`
  [25]-[26], `people/kjell-karlsson.md` [9]-[10]).

* **Batch 142 (pages 1411–1420, msgnum 1431–1440, 18–19 November 2003) —
  Smith and Nels close out the FAQ file's chine-runner example with a
  waterline-length correction and the Egret sharpie example; Alastair's
  foam-taper fix resolves the boom-mod thread; Smith writes a standalone
  "rolling bevels" technique post.** In the FAQ File thread, Smith corrected
  Nels's chine-runner FAQ example on one point — the runners themselves do
  not lengthen the waterline, since Paradox has little net overhang to
  submerge under heel or speed — while endorsing Nels's broader heeled-hull/
  long-keel analogy and offering his own "Scheel Keel, without the keel"
  description; Nels accepted the correction; Smith then illustrated true
  waterline-lengthening with Commodore Ralph Munroe's sharpie Egret, and
  Nels closed the exchange restating his original point (Paradox sails
  faster than her static hull shape suggests, though she is not a racing
  machine) — folded into
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` (citations 12-15). In
  the "boom mod?" thread, Alastair supplied a simple field fix for his
  sail's incomplete furl — a bit of rigid foam taped to the boom's outboard
  end — which Glen Maxwell called clever and planned to try on Zoë's new
  sail — folded into the same file (citations 16-17). Smith continued the "2 HP
  Motors" discussion into the FAQ file with a note on oars, sculling, and
  John Letcher's writing on oar-powered displacement sailboats, looking
  forward to using a proper yuloh — `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md`
  (citation 26). William Longyard closed the "Speaking up for Don" thread
  with a brief conciliatory note to Glen Maxwell — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 54) and
  `people/william-longyard.md` (citation 30). Alastair answered Smith's
  "Autumn refit" follow-up questions in full: reefing on a single tack is
  desirable since it de-powers the sail faster in gusts, a topping lift adds
  about 5 seconds to furling, and the endless halyard/reefing-line system
  works well despite minor kinking and two acknowledged risks (no instant
  sail drop, and chafe damage requires replacing the whole line) — folded
  into `boats/little-jim.md` (citation 81, and citation 80 updated from
  "unanswered" to answered). The batch centerpiece is Smith's standalone
  **"rolling bevels"** post — a clean technique writeup on cutting the
  Paradox's rolling bevels (chine runners; bulkhead #2/#3 sides), including
  his own measured bulkhead #2 angles (8° at the sheer, rolling to 13° at
  the chine) and a two-person bandsaw technique for tracking a changing
  bevel angle — folded into `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md` (new
  section, citation 32) and cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 80).
  No new files, no promotions or prunings. All three standing Open backlog
  items (Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/
  newspipetr3) checked: none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping was a clean, fully
  consecutive run (1431-1440) with no gaps, continuing directly from batch
  141's 1430, spanning 18-19 November 2003.

* **Batch 141 (pages 1401–1410, msgnum 1421–1430, 18 November 2003) — the
  FAQ file gets its first content, the "Tools list" copyright question is
  fully resolved, and Nels answers the open boom-modification question.**
  Glen Maxwell created and opened the FAQ file for editing, using Nels's
  30-question list as a starting point; discovering only the folder's
  creator could edit it, Nels suggested contributors offline answers to
  Glen or post them for group discussion (with polls for disputed
  questions), and supplied a worked example explaining chine-runner
  effectiveness via a "shallow fin keel" analogy — folded into
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` (new section, citation
  9) and `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citations
  51-52). Smith added five more candidate FAQ questions (lofting, "instant
  boat" status, build difficulty, time estimate, need for a helper) —
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 53) and
  `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 79). The "Tools list" authorship thread reached
  its full resolution: Nels argued no such list can really be copyrighted;
  Bill Serjeant then drew a sharp distinction between his own freely-posted
  tools list (compiled independently from Don Elliott's booklet) and a
  separate, possibly-copyrighted Building Materials list bundled with a
  bought plans package, on which he wanted Matt Layden's or Dave Bolduc's
  sign-off before adding it to the FAQ file — folded into
  `construction/build-effort.md` (citations 37-38) and `people/nels.md`
  (citation 40). The **batch centerpiece**: Kjell Karlsson's open "boom
  mod?" question about Matt Layden's taller new sailplan got Nels's direct
  answer — a tapered boom is plausible in theory, though the exact
  aft-diameter increase needed is unclear, and per Matt the incomplete furl
  is not a serious problem in practice given the peaked gaff — folded into
  `design/chine-runners-and-windward-performance.md` (new section,
  citations 10-11), with `design/rig.md` and `people/kjell-karlsson.md`
  updated to cross-link rather than duplicate, and `people/nels.md`
  (citation 43). Nels also closed out the "2 HP Motors" thread's physics
  with a Phil Bolger quote on why low-hp motors suit short, flat-bottomed,
  rockered hulls — `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` (citation 25)
  and `people/nels.md` (citation 42). Smith asked Alastair two unanswered
  rig-maintenance follow-ups on the "Autumn refit" (a parrel to contain the
  yard when reefed; whether the endless halyard/reefing-line arrangement
  still works) — `boats/little-jim.md` (citation 80) — and separately
  thanked Glen for volunteering to write up the lower gudgeon, floating
  three candidate construction methods of his own — `people/david-c-smith.md`
  (citation 78). A one-off newcomer, "Kellan," recommended William
  Longyard's *A Speck on the Sea* to the list unprompted —
  `people/william-longyard.md` (citation 29). No new files, no promotions
  or prunings. Msgnum run 1421-1430, clean and fully consecutive, all ten
  messages dated 18 November 2003.

* **Batch 140 (pages 1391–1400, msgnum 1411–1420, 18 November 2003) — the
  "Speaking up for Don" fallout settles, the FAQ folder is created, and a
  bibliography vote.** Glen Maxwell gave his fullest account yet of the ban's
  reasoning, answering Bill Longyard's gentle "was I too hasty?" check-in: he
  had weighed Don's photo/file/message history for any positive contribution
  and found the negative far outweighed it, had tried simply ignoring Don's
  personal attacks only to conclude that (by Don's own logic) silence would
  be read as confirming them, and cited Don's unfounded plagiarism
  accusation against Alastair as typifying the pattern. Answering Longyard's
  deferential "it's his house, he's the host" line, Glen preferred "our
  house," having no wish to be the dominant voice or cast as disciplinarian.
  Nels noted, after the ban, that Don was still cross-posting his articles
  and new site to the Bolducs' own `microcruising` group. Building on Smith's
  build-manual proposal, Nels proposed a companion **FAQ folder** with a
  30-question candidate list spanning plans sourcing through day-to-day
  living aboard; Glen created the folder the same morning. Bill Serjeant
  suggested adding anchoring/anchor-stowage to the FAQ (open question: one
