  anchor or two?) and asked, unanswered, whether the booklet's materials
  list could be reused without a copyright issue; separately, in a short
  standalone note, he clarified the group's "Tools" list was his own
  compilation of what Don had used to build his boat, not Don's own list. On
  the build-manual bibliography thread, Glen cast the first vote: *The
  Gougeon Brothers on Boat Construction*. Glen also answered Will's
  protective-clothing question with his own sweatshirt-and-hospital-scrubs
  and WoodenBoat-apron routine, with a caution about glue on Tyvek-suit
  zippers. Kjell Karlsson raised an unanswered boom-modification question
  about Matt Layden's taller new sailplan leaving an unfurlable top
  triangle. Folded into `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`
  (citations 44–50, new "The FAQ folder" section), `people/don-elliott.md`
  (citations 39–41), `people/nels.md` (citations 38–39),
  `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` (citation 24),
  `construction/build-effort.md` (citation 36), `references/paradox-uk-website.md`
  (citations 7–8), `design/rig.md` (citation 59), `people/kjell-karlsson.md`
  (citation 8), and `people/david-c-smith.md` (bibliography-thread prose update, no new
  citation). No new files, no promotions or prunings. Checked all three
  standing backlog identity items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced in this batch.
  Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1411–1420) with no
  gaps**, continuing directly from batch 139's 1410, all ten messages dated
  18 November 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed
  distinct on direct read (no stale-read issue).
* **Batch 139 (pages 1381–1390, msgnum 1401–1410, 17–18 November 2003) — the
  "2 HP Motors" thread reaches its planing verdict, and a light bibliography/
  book coda.** David Beard's field test of a 4 hp four-stroke Yamaha (6.5 kn
  max, "pushing way too much water") confirmed Nels's physics explanation for
  why: too much rocker in Paradox's stern section to climb over her own bow
  wave, since the flatter run aft that planing needs would instead drag the
  boat's tail when heeled under sail — the same trade-off familiar from
  Bolger's sharpies. The thread closed in banter — William Longyard's
  full-span-trim-tab and pressure-tunnel/hydrofoil jokes, met by "Larry"'s
  adjustable-chine-runner riposte. Folded into
  `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` (citations 20–24),
  `people/david-beard.md` (169–170), `people/nels.md` (37), and
  `people/william-longyard.md` (25, 27–28). David Beard and William Longyard
  traded thanks over Longyard's book *A Speck on the Sea* ("couldn't put it
  down") and Beard's loaned photographs, with a teased future story about
  tracking down fellow voyager Tom McNally in a rough part of Liverpool;
  Nels's Dynamite Payson bibliography recommendation drew a one-line
  appreciative reply from Longyard — folded into `people/william-longyard.md`
  (25–26) and `people/david-beard.md` (170). "Will" (`willmarsh3`) thanked
  Glen Maxwell for his epoxy-solvent-on-skin warning, switching to a Tyvek
  suit going forward — folded into `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`
  (23). "Jeff UK" seconded William Longyard's public plea to Glen Maxwell to
  reconsider the Don Elliott ban ("Bill, I will second that.") — folded into
  `people/jeff-doryman.md` (32) and `people/don-elliott.md` (38). No new
  files, no promotions or prunings. Msgnum run 1401–1410, clean and fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 138's 1400.

* **Batch 138 (pages 1371–1380, msgnum 1391–1400, 17 November 2003) — a
  dissenting voice on the Don Elliott ban, Glen Maxwell's concrete push on
  the build manual, and a quiet casualty of the week's flaming.** William
  Longyard publicly broke with the otherwise unanimous support for Glen
  Maxwell's ban of Don Elliott — praising Don's talent and generosity (Don
  had let him reproduce a sketch in *A Speck on the Sea*) and proposing a
  general truce instead. Nels answered with the ban's fullest defense on
  record: a two-year, cross-group pattern he had personally watched, of Don
  turning on a group's moderator after an initial friendly period, quoting
  at length Don's own message dismissing disagreement as "time-wasters"
  playing "mind games." Longyard held his civility position in reply but
  deferred the final call to Glen as the list's "host." Folded into
  `people/don-elliott.md` (citations 34–36), `people/nels.md` (citation 35),
  and `people/william-longyard.md` (citations 23–24), with
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`'s prior "unanimous"
  reception line corrected to "near-unanimous" and cross-linked. Separately,
  a newcomer who had joined only to discuss chine runners on planing
  daysailers publicly unsubscribed over "the flaming and long diatribes,"
  leaving quote-trimming and take-it-private etiquette advice on his way
  out — folded as a citation-level mention into `people/don-elliott.md`
  (citation 37). Smith's build manual thread advanced: Smith offered to defer the
  introduction to a builder of a completed boat; Glen Maxwell gave the
  effort its most concrete push yet, asking members to simply write up one
  specific problem they had had to puzzle through rather than worry about
  polish, and volunteered himself to document the lower gudgeon; the
  bibliography thread drew two recommendations — William Longyard named
  three out-of-print Percy Blandford titles, and Nels named Harold
  "Dynamite" Payson, whom he had met in person years earlier. Folded into
  `people/david-c-smith.md` (citations 74–77), `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`
  (new section, citation 43), `people/william-longyard.md` (citation 22),
  and `people/nels.md` (citation 36). Glen also closed out two threads from
  the prior day with brief replies — "Now this is more like it" to
  Alastair's autumn refit report, and thanks to Ron de Hon for finally
  claiming the "RHXll" boat — folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citations 41–42). No
  new files, no promotions or prunings; Steve Henshall kept inline per the
  people threshold (one message, no boat, departing). All three standing
  Open backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) checked: none resurfaced. Msgnum
  mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1391–1400) with no gaps**,
  continuing directly from batch 137's 1390, all ten messages dated 17
  November 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed
  distinct on direct read (no stale-read issue). Verified no new
  citation-number collisions across the five files that received new
  citations (`people/don-elliott.md` [34]–[37], `people/nels.md`
  [35]–[36], `people/william-longyard.md` [22]–[24], `people/david-c-smith.md`
  [74]–[77], `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`
  [41]–[43]).

* **Batch 137 (pages 1361–1370, msgnum 1381–1390, 17 November 2003) — the
  Don Elliott ban's unanimous reception, the "RHXll" mystery boat finally
  claimed, Little Jim's autumn refit, and Smith's build manual gains a
  bibliography thread.** Every member who responded to Glen Maxwell's ban of
  Don Elliott backed it: David Hahn (Delta, Utah) called it a favor to the
  group, having left the separate Smallboats group over similar fighting;
  Bert Vercauteren, "a small part of the silent majority," publicly thanked
  Glen. Nels agreed the ban was warranted but, in a post titled
  "Censorship," argued a moderator's duty to curb real abuse should still
  come with a warning and a fair hearing first — a standard he felt he had
  not been given himself when Don Elliott banned him outright from Don's own
  new rival group, despite Nels never having posted there, over an unrelated
  private reply Don took offense at. Folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citations 34–39),
  `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 33), and `people/nels.md` (citation 34).
  Separately, the "RHXll" mystery hull Glen had been asking the group to
  identify since batch 128 — previously only disclaimed by "Jeff UK" — was
  finally claimed: **Ron de Hon**, building in Amsterdam, the Netherlands,
  confirmed it as his own, promising more photos and hoping to finish the
  following spring; folded as a resolved-mystery note into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 40), kept
  inline rather than promoted to a dedicated page given how thin the message
  is (one email, no build history yet), on the same precedent as "Hugh"
  (New Zealand) in `boats/index.md`. Alastair's "Autumn refit" post covered
  two lessons from the recent Solent crossing — beach-sand and bin-dampness
  fixes, and chafe repairs to the furling-line and tack-line deck fairleads
  — folded into `boats/little-jim.md` (new section, citation 79) with a
  one-line cross-link from `people/alastair-law.md`. Smith's build-manual thread
  (running since batch 134) continued: welcoming Bill Longyard's and Andy's
  volunteering, reiterating that the manual should still point builders to
  the group archives and direct questions rather than try to capture every
  trick of the trade, and opening a bibliography-nominations thread with
  *The Gougeon Brothers on Boat Construction* as the first suggestion —
  folded into `people/david-c-smith.md` (citations 72–73). No new files, no promotions
  or prunings. Msgnum mapping was a clean, fully consecutive run
  (1381–1390) with no gaps, continuing directly from batch 136's 1380, all
  ten messages dated 17 November 2003.

* **Batch 136 (pages 1351–1360, msgnum 1371–1380, 17 November 2003) — Glen
  Maxwell bans Don Elliott from `paradoxbuilders`, the archive's only
  recorded member ban; Smith's build manual proposal draws two more volunteers;
  Jeff UK settles the planing question.** The batch centerpiece: after
  returning from a few days away to an inbox full of complaints, Glen
  Maxwell announced he was banning Don Elliott from the group — quoting at
  length a private email describing Don as "a dark spirit" whose posts
  "ruins my day," noting he had previously resisted pressure to ban Don but
  now judged a silent majority shared that view, and framing the action as
  curbing conduct rather than censoring speech (Don's wide cross-posting
  meant his views stayed accessible regardless). Folded into
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md`'s existing "A rival
  group" section (citation 33) and `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 32),
  which also now notes Don continued posting afterward, suggesting the ban
  went unenforced or was later lifted. Don separately announced his "Ballast
  Installation part 2" article and all future Paradox building articles
  would post only to the new Paradoxboatbuilders group — folded into
  `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 31) and cross-linked from
  `design/weight-and-ballast.md`'s existing ballast-pig-layout section
  (citation 64). Smith's build-manual proposal (running since batch 134) drew
  two concrete volunteers: William Longyard agreed to help "in any way I
  can," and a new tech-writer correspondent, "Andy," offered editorial
  help and suggested a bill of materials, errata section, and sailmaking
  directions; Glen Maxwell endorsed the proposal outright. Folded into
  `people/david-c-smith.md` (citations 69–71), `people/william-longyard.md` (citation
  21), and `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` (citation 175); "Andy"
  kept inline per the people threshold (one message, no boat). Separately,
  "Jeff UK" gave the archive's definitive answer on whether a Paradox can
  plane under power — no, by her hull shape alone, though she will surf;
  genuine planing requires a purpose-built power hull with a sailing rig
  added, unlike Paradox's shape — closing out batch 135's open question.
  Folded into `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` (citation 19) and
  `people/jeff-doryman.md` (citation 30). A new correspondent, Thomas
  Thomas, publicly unsubscribed citing the group's "mindless nonsense,"
  drawing a good-humored reply from Jeff UK ("arguing over how many Angels
  can dance on the head of a pin") — folded into `people/jeff-doryman.md`
  (citation 31), kept inline for both one-off correspondents. The "Square
  tubs verses round tubs" epoxy thread got one more exchange: a new
  correspondent, "Will" (`willmarsh3`), described his own cat-food-can
  mixing setup and asked why vinegar isn't used to clean brushes as well as
  skin; Glen Maxwell warned against any solvent on skin at all, epoxy
  diluted by a solvent being able to enter the skin and reach body organs,
  ketones especially settling in the liver — folded into
  `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` (citations 21–22) and
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` (citation 177); "Will" kept inline
  per the people threshold. No new files, no promotions or prunings; checked
  all three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel,
  the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) — none resurfaced (Jeff UK's
  own appearances this batch are already tracked under the existing
  disambiguation entries, not new corroboration). Msgnum mapping was a
  **clean, fully consecutive run (1371–1380) with no gaps**, continuing
  directly from batch 135's 1370, all ten messages dated 17 November 2003.
  All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly,
  and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read (no
  stale-read issue). Verified no new citation-number collisions across the
  eight files that received new citations
  (`people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` [32]-[33],
  `people/don-elliott.md` [31]-[32], `design/weight-and-ballast.md` [64],
  `people/david-c-smith.md` [69]-[71], `people/william-longyard.md` [21],
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` [175]-[177],
  `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` [19], `people/jeff-doryman.md`
  [30]-[31], `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` [21]-[22]).

* **Batch 135 (pages 1341–1350, msgnum 1361–1370, 16–17 November 2003) —
  the Don Elliott/Smith schism reaches its sharpest exchange, and Smith's build
  manual proposal recruits an author.** The "Taking my marbles and going
  away" thread continued directly from batch 134's split: Smith rebutted Don's
  "time-wasters" characterization point by point — he had posted boat
  photos while Don had posted none, and Don had "flamed" his own customers
  and the list's moderator — and needled Don's grammar with a reading list
  (Strunk & White, Plotnik, Shertzer). Don's two replies did not engage the
  substance, calling Smith's post itself proof of the pettiness described and
  dismissing him as "a sniper," closing with "I only react in kind." Folded
  into `people/don-elliott.md`'s existing "A rival group" section
  (citations 29–30) and `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 66). Separately, Smith's
  build-manual proposal (introduced batch 134) got a direct follow-up: Smith
  invited William H. Longyard — citing his authorship of *A Speck on the
  Sea* — to help keep the manual "unbiased, concise and factual"; no reply
  is recorded. Folded into `people/david-c-smith.md`'s existing build-manual bullet
  (citation 67) and `people/william-longyard.md` (citation 20). A new "2 HP
  Motors" thread (titled by Nels "Oppositional Defiant Disorder and 2 HP
  Motors," advising Smith that responding to Don only feeds the behavior) ran
  through outboard-sizing questions: Nels asked whether a 2 hp Yamaha
  four-stroke suits a Paradox; William Longyard, prompted by seeing one
  aboard Dave and Mindy Bolduc's Little Cruiser, asked separately whether
  anyone had gotten a Paradox to plane with a larger engine; Don Elliott
  noted the plans design in no motor at all, only a single oar; Smith weighed
  four-stroke-vs-two-stroke tradeoffs and Yamaha's parts/service edge,
  deferred the weight-and-practicality question to David Beard's and the
  Bolducs' own experience, described his own yuloh-plus-reserve-outboard
  plan for "Swash," and, on planing, cited the Bartender powerboats'
  aft "planing rails" (George Calkins) as reminiscent of Paradox's chine
  runners without directly answering the question. Folded as a new section
  into `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` (citations 14–18),
  cross-linked from `people/nels.md` (citation 33) and `people/david-c-smith.md`
  (citation 68). No new files, no promotions or prunings; checked all three
  standing backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) — none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping
  was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1361–1370) with no gaps**,
  continuing directly from batch 134's 1360, spanning 16–17 November 2003.
  All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly,
  and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read
  (no stale-read issue). Verified no new citation-number collisions across
  the five files that received new citations (`people/don-elliott.md`
  [29]–[30], `people/david-c-smith.md` [66]–[68], `people/william-longyard.md` [20],
  `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` [14]–[18], `people/nels.md`
  [33]).

* **Batch 134 (pages 1331–1340, msgnum 1351–1360, 16–17 November 2003) —
  Don Elliott founds a rival "Paradoxboatbuilders" splinter group, and Smith's
  build-manual proposal.** The batch centerpiece: after weeks of friction
  over tone — capped by a lurker's ("Brian"/`jerseyman999`) public plea for
  Don Elliott to "count to ten" rather than answer disagreement with
  rudeness — Don announced a second Yahoo group, "Paradoxboatbuilders,"
  explicitly excluding Nels, Smith, and "Betelgeuserdude" (Smith's own Yahoo
  handle, named as if a separate person) as unserious "time-wasters." Don
  defended his combative style to Brian at length, then, replying to Smith's
  same-day proposal for a group-written Paradox build manual, dismissed the
  idea, claimed primary credit for Paradox's public existence (his own 1996
  *Messing About In Boats* article and having built "the first Paradox
  after Matt"), and claimed co-support of the craft with Dave Bolduc "since
  its beginning." Smith's reply declined to join the rival group and corrected
  the record that Glen Maxwell — not Don — is `paradoxbuilders`' actual
  founder and moderator. Folded as a new section into `people/don-elliott.md`
  ("A rival group, and a dispute over who founded and moderates the list,"
  citations 25–28), cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (new "Proposed a
  group-written Paradox build manual" bullet, citations 64–65),
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (new "A rival group,
  and Glen's moderator role reaffirmed" section), and `people/david-beard.md`
  (168, "Great Idea Smith."). Separately, the "Rudder Foil Shape" thread closed
  out: "Paul" (`kayaker37`) corrected his own earlier arithmetic — the
  rudder foil's total 24 mm thickness is two 12 mm plies, not two 24 mm
  plies, giving a 0.06 ratio that confirms NACA 0006 as the closest
  standard section rather than NACA 0015 — and Alastair took the correction
  to his own estimate in good humor ("Silly me, still math never was my
  strong suit"). Folded into `design/rudder.md`'s existing "Foil section"
  section (citations 34–35), cross-linked from `people/alastair-law.md` (137).
  The "Square tubs verses round tubs" epoxy-mixing thread ran out its last
  banter — Don needling Nels ("You need to increase your medication") and
  a new one-off correspondent, Gene T (`tehansky`), thanking Nels for his
  round-container suggestion — recorded as citation-only entries on
  `people/don-elliott.md` (24) and `people/nels.md` (32), consistent with
  how prior thin banter has been handled. No new files, no promotions or
  prunings; Brian (`jerseyman999`) and Gene T (`tehansky`) both kept inline
  per the people threshold (single appearances). Msgnum mapping was a
  **clean, fully consecutive run (1351–1360) with no gaps**, continuing
  directly from batch 133's 1350, spanning 16–17 November 2003 (the archive
  crosses midnight partway through the batch). All ten source files' byte
  sizes matched the batch instructions exactly, and all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read (no stale-read
  issue). All three standing Open backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/
  Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) checked: none
  resurfaced.

* **Batch 133 (pages 1321–1330, msgnum 1341–1350, 16 November 2003) —
  the "Fresh Water tanks" thread's closing data points, a "Rudder Foil
  Shape" follow-up picture, and the "Square tubs verses round tubs"
  thread's last banter.** "Larry" (`LJGeib`) suggested mounting a tank's
  inspection ports on the outside rather than inside; Derek
  (`dclark52001`) closed his own question, settling on 10-litre bladders
  refillable at European house/village pumps over drinking rainwater; Don
  Elliott offered fresh-water-tank builders a middle path — open,
  oval-shaped hatch lids instead of sealed covers, so the tank space can
  double as dry stowage — calling Paradox a genuinely versatile cruiser;
  and Nels, from his own backpacking experience, cautioned that local
  water anywhere should be run through a filter before drinking. Folded
  into `operations/provisioning-and-water.md` (citations 15–18),
  cross-linked from `people/don-elliott.md` (22) and `people/nels.md`
  (30). "Paul" (`kayaker37`) followed up his own rudder-foil question with
  a posted picture comparing a NACA 0006 airfoil to the plans' rudder
  outline (closer than NACA 0008, still not exact), disclaiming real
  expertise — folded into `design/rudder.md` (citation 33). The "Square
  tubs verses round tubs" thread ran out on pure banter between Nels and
  Don Elliott (paper cups, spelling, who reads whose posts) — recorded as
  citation-only entries on `people/don-elliott.md` (23) and
  `people/nels.md` (28, 29, 31), with no new body prose. No new files, no
  promotions or prunings; all three standing backlog identity items
  checked, none resurfaced. Msgnum run 1341–1350, clean and fully
  consecutive, continuing directly from batch 132's 1340, all ten messages
  dated 16 November 2003.

* **Batch 132 (pages 1311–1320, msgnum 1331–1340, 16 November 2003) —
  the "Fresh Water tanks" thread's practical answers, the "Rudder Foil
  Shape" question answered, a Tools-List rasp footnote, and the "Square
  tubs verses round tubs" thread's closing exchange.** Derek
  (`dclark52001`) asked whether Alastair meant to do without inspection
  hatches on a self-flooding ballast-tank conversion, using a sea cock and
  pump instead; Alastair would never have an uninspectable cavity — "you
  never know what may be living in there" — and sketched a simpler
  gravity-only alternative instead: a permanently open drain hole at each
  tank's aft end, plugged as an enlarged air vent, pulled to flood at
  launch and drain on the trailer; he also mentioned still wanting a
  draining anchor well. Don Elliott gave the marine-hardware version of the
  same conversion — a bronze through-hull valve in the tank floor, with a
  diagram — but cautioned it would give up Paradox's tank being a
  deliberate rainwater-catching cruising feature, worth keeping for
  long-range trips where shore water is scarce. Folded into
  [operations/provisioning-and-water.md](/operations/provisioning-and-water.md#self-flooding-sea-water-ballast-tanks-feasible-but-risky)
  (citations 12-14), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (134) and
  [people/don-elliott.md](/people/don-elliott.md) (20). Batch 131's
  unanswered "Rudder Foil Shape" question got two direct answers: Alastair
  worked out that the blade's own dimensions (400 mm long, 2×24 mm ply
  thick before glue) make a 16.66% profile achievable, close enough to
  NACA 0015 to use as a reference shape, though he shaped his own by eye
  with an angle grinder; Nels argued the flatter section is not a
  compromise but an advantage at Paradox's typical slow speeds, since less
  trailing-edge taper means better low-speed control, less damage risk, and
  eddies that help swing the stern through a turn — folded into
  [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md#foil-section-not-a-standard-naca-airfoil)
  (citations 31-32, citation 30's "unanswered" note removed), cross-linked
  from alastair-law.md (135) and [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (27). Nels
  also closed out the Tools-List rasp-sourcing thread with a one-line
  footnote: only the coarse Nicholson rasp is worth buying, a finer second
  one being redundant next to cheaper small sanding files — folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#tools)
  (citation 35), cross-linked from nels.md (26). The "Square tubs verses
  round tubs" epoxy-mixing thread closed with a round of restated
  positions rather than new technical content: Don Elliott repeated that
  his advice is general boatbuilding guidance, not personal opinion, unlike
  (in his telling) Glen Maxwell's; David Beard sided with Don in one word,
  "Amen!!!!!!"; Alastair closed the thread with a wry, even-handed summary
  freeing everyone to form their own opinion without it becoming "another
  of Don's confrontations" — recorded as brief citation-only entries on
  [people/don-elliott.md](/people/don-elliott.md) (21),
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (167), and alastair-law.md
  (136), since the exchange added no new technique or figure beyond what
  Glen's and Don's substantive answers (already on record from batch 130)
  established. No new files, no promotions or prunings. All three standing
  backlog identity items (Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold
  Larry/`newspipetr3`) checked: none resurfaced. Msgnum run 1331-1340,
  clean and fully consecutive, continuing directly from batch 131's 1330,
  all ten messages dated 16 November 2003.

* **Batch 131 (pages 1301–1310, msgnum 1321–1330, 15–16 November 2003) —
  a Nicholson-rasp sourcing follow-up, the "outboard & paradox" thread's
  close, a new self-flooding ballast-tank idea, Glen Maxwell's belated
  epoxy-ratio answer, and a new unanswered rudder-airfoil question.** Bill
  Serjeant, unable to find a Nicholson pattern maker's rasp in the UK, drew
  international mail-order sources from "Larry" (`LJGeib`) — sculpt.com,
  Nolan Supply, Lee Valley, Garrett Wade, and a German half-round
  alternative — and found Lee Valley's own price steep, more than double
  what Larry had originally paid — folded into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#tools)
  (citations 32-34). The "outboard & paradox" thread closed out: new
  correspondent Derek (`dclark52001`) asked about the 2.3 hp four-stroke
  Honda's fuel range, indoor/rain operation, and rudder clearance; David
  Beard answered that the rudder does not clear the propeller, a non-issue
  outside tight-quarters maneuvering; Bill Serjeant added that British
  Seagull outboards once shipped with a gunwale-mount fitting as an
  alternative to the transom bracket — folded into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#operating-an-outboard-from-inside-the-cabin)
  (citations 11-13), cross-linked from `people/david-beard.md` (citation
  166); the indoor-operation and fuel-range questions remain unanswered. A
  new "Fresh Water tanks" thread had Derek ask about using the built-in
  fresh-water tanks as self-flooding sea-water ballast instead of carrying
  70 litres of fresh water; Alastair judged it feasible in principle, since
  the tanks sit below the waterline, but cautioned the inspection hatches
  would need to be genuinely watertight, and described his own 2-litre
  bottle water-marking system (doubling as fenders in an emergency) — folded
  as a new section into
  [operations/provisioning-and-water.md](/operations/provisioning-and-water.md#self-flooding-sea-water-ballast-tanks-feasible-but-risky)
  (citations 10-11), cross-linked from `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 133).
  Glen Maxwell directly answered batch 130's unanswered "Square tubs verses
  round tubs" question: lower resin:hardener ratios (such as 1:1) are indeed
  more forgiving of measurement error, at a cost in cured strength; he
  standardizes on a 5:1 mix from Fiberglass Coatings and recommends T-12
  thinner over acetone or vinegar for clean-up, kept in a shallow coffee-can
  bath that lets tools stay usable for up to a year — folded into
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#mixing-container-shape-and-stir-time)
  (citation 20), cross-linked from `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`
  (citation 174). A new, unanswered "Rudder Foil Shape" question from
  established below-threshold correspondent "Paul" (`kayaker37`) noted
  Paradox's rudder looks comparatively flat next to the commonly-referenced
  NACA 0012/0015 airfoil sections — folded as a new section into
  [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md#foil-section-not-a-standard-naca-airfoil)
  (citation 30). No new files, no promotions or prunings. Checked all three
  standing backlog identity items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping
  was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1321-1330) with no gaps**,
  continuing directly from batch 130's 1320.

* **Batch 130 (pages 1291–1300, msgnum 1311–1320, 15 November 2003) —
  the "Tools" and "Square tubs verses round tubs" threads, and a Larry
  Whited/"LJGeib" citation-error fix.** David Beard answered the batch
  129 outboard question directly: a 2 hp Honda works great on "Whisper"
  (4-5 kn at a little over half throttle), where a heavier 58 lb 4 hp
  Yamaha could not be balanced for sailing — folded into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#operating-an-outboard-from-inside-the-cabin)
  (citation 10), cross-linked from `people/david-beard.md` (citation 165);
  the inside-the-cabin control question itself remains unanswered. Bill
  Serjeant's "Tools" thread (opened batch 129) got its full answer: Alastair
  posted a complete annotated tool list built from Don Elliott's own
  recommendations, with UK-sourcing and technique notes on nearly every
  item; Don Elliott confirmed it was in fact his own "Building Paradox"
  booklet list and flagged one change since — his lead ballast pigs moved
  from the bread-pan wedge shape to square, elongated cast forms; Alastair
  countered that he still prefers the original wedge shape for easy
  one-handed lifting, painting the pigs with Hammerite while still warm; a
  correspondent signing only "Larry" (`LJGeib`/`lorenzojose2000`)
  recommended a Nicholson pattern maker's rasp for shaping complex parts
  such as chine runners, Nels pointed to Lee Valley as a cheaper source, and
  Alastair seconded the recommendation — all folded as an expansion of
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#tools)
  (citations 26-31), cross-linked from `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 132)
  and `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 18). A new "Square tubs verses round
  tubs" thread had Glen Maxwell argue, from his Gold Coast Yachts
  experience, that round mixing containers give better epoxy molecular
  linkage than square ones, tracing soft/uncured epoxy finds to
  round-cornered stir sticks; Don Elliott sharply disputed this as "utter
  nonsense," holding that measurement, temperature, and attention lapses —
  not container shape — cause the great majority of bad mixes; Nels thanked
  both, plus Alastair and Bill Serjeant, for their practical knowledge and
  asked whether 1:1-ratio epoxies are more forgiving of measurement error
  than higher-ratio mixes (unanswered) — all folded as a new section into
  [construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md#mixing-container-shape-and-stir-time)
  (citations 17-19), cross-linked from `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`
  (citation 173), `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 19), and `people/nels.md`
  (citations 24-25). While sourcing this batch's "Larry" (`LJGeib`), found
  and fixed a **pre-existing citation error**: three earlier citations
  (`boats/little-jim.md` [22], `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md`
  [10] and [12]) had mislabeled `LJGeib`/`lorenzojose2000` messages as
  "Larry Whited" — but a genuinely separate, self-identified **Larry
  Whited** (`lwhited@...`/`lwhited`) is independently confirmed elsewhere in
  the archive (e.g. `people/david-c-smith.md` citation 8, and `little-jim.md` citation
  23 itself, immediately following the miscited one). Corrected all three to
  read `LJGeib / "Larry"` and added disambiguation notes in both files; no
  facts were lost, only the false surname attribution. No new files, no
  promotions or prunings. Checked all three standing backlog identities
  ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold
  "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced in this batch. Msgnum mapping was
  a **clean, fully consecutive run (1311-1320) with no gaps**, continuing
  directly from batch 129's 1310 — all ten messages dated the same day, 15
  November 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed
  distinct on direct read. Verified no new citation-number collisions across
  the nine files touched (`operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` [10],
  `construction/build-effort.md` [26]-[31],
  `construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md` [16]-[19], `people/alastair-law.md`
  [132], `people/don-elliott.md` [18]-[19], `people/nels.md` [24]-[25],
  `people/david-beard.md` [165], `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`
  [173], `boats/little-jim.md` — body-text fix only, no new citation).

* **Batch 129 (pages 1281–1290, msgnum 1301–1310, 14–15 November 2003) —
  "The Edge" confirmed by name, Don Elliott's ballast-pig article, and
  Glen Maxwell's Everglades Challenge bilge-pumping story.** "Jeff UK"
  (`roverticket`) disclaimed ownership of the mystery "RHXll" boat ("I
  cannot tell a lie. It's not mine."), folded into
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#chasing-down-quiet-builders-for-photos-and-progress-reports)
  (citation 30) and `people/jeff-doryman.md` (citation 29). Bill Serjeant
  confirmed Glen's inferred boat name for Don Elliott's Paradox — **"The
  Edge"** — apt, he added, for how a Paradox can feel beating to windward
  in a rough sea or surfing downwind on breakers; his own boat remained
  unnamed pending his cash flow — folded into
  [boats/elliott-paradox.md](/boats/elliott-paradox.md) (citation 8,
  "probable" promoted to "confirmed" in the body) and
  `people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` (citation 31). The
  bilge-pump thread continued: Smith praised Glen's pump installation and
  explained his own choice to eliminate limber holes in favor of closed,
  compartmentalized bilge spaces with a three-tier sponge/portable-pump/
  fixed-pump response to water; Glen Maxwell then gave the batch's
  centerpiece eyewitness story — watching Matt Layden hand-pump "Paradox"'s
  flooded bilge dry after a rough beach haul-out at the start of the 2003
  Everglades Challenge, which left Glen wanting a bigger pump and floating
  an unbuilt rubber-disc tiller-seal idea, plus a Ft. Pierce inlet
  near-swamping story of his own — both folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#real-world-need-for-the-bilge-pump)
  (citations 60-61), cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 61) and, for
  the Matt Layden eyewitness material, a new section of
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md#watching-matt-pump-out-at-the-everglades-challenge-start)
  (citation 16). A new "Tools" thread had Bill Serjeant, alarmed at
  clamp-heavy deck-beam photos, ask how many clamps a build really needs and
  for help trimming Don Elliott's tool-list page down to essentials; Smith
  explained his own clamp count came from laminating thinner strips than
  necessary, gave a test-lamination method to find the thickest workable
  stock, and generalized that tool needs grow with a build's progress —
  folded as a new section into
  [construction/build-effort.md](/construction/build-effort.md#tools)
  (citations 24-25), cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 63).
  Separately, Smith and Alastair's "Introducing Swash" exchange continued with
  a "round robin" boat-swap race idea and Smith's clamp-shopping-spree detail
  (Homier and Harbor Freight), folded into `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 62). Alan
  Terry asked, in a duplicate post sent twice within the hour, whether
  Little Cruiser's outboard installation (seen in a photo) had proved
  unsatisfactory, seconding dclark52001's unanswered inside-the-cabin
  outboard-control question from the prior batch — both remain unanswered in
  the archive, folded into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#operating-an-outboard-from-inside-the-cabin)
  (citation 9). The batch centerpiece: Don Elliott posted "Paradox-Ballast
  installation part 1," the first of a planned illustrated article series
  intended for his "Building the Paradox" booklet — full ballast-pig sizing
  (70-180 kg/155-400 lb range, 18 kg/40 lb pigs), a suggested pig layout by
  the food locker and water tanks, rot-isolation and epoxy-coating detail,
  a two-layer fiberglass floor barrier with two wet-out methods, and a
  favorite epoxy-mixing-container recommendation — folded as a new section
  into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#the-ballast-pig-layout-in-full-don-elliott)
  (citation 63), cross-linked from `people/don-elliott.md` (citation 17,
  updated "Weight and ballast" bullet). No new files, no promotions or
  prunings; checked all three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK,"
  "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) —
  only "Jeff UK" resurfaced (the RHXll disclaimer, no new linking detail).
  Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1301-1310) with no
  gaps**, continuing directly from batch 128's 1300 — spanning 14-15
  November 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly; page_1288/page_1289 are confirmed genuine duplicate
  posts (same sender, same body, distinct msgnum/Message-ID/timestamp an
  hour apart), not a stale read. Verified no new citation-number collisions
  across the nine files that received new citations
  (`people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md` [30]-[31],
  `boats/elliott-paradox.md` [8], `design/weight-and-ballast.md` [60]-[63],
  `people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md` [16],
  `construction/build-effort.md` [24]-[25], `people/david-c-smith.md` [61]-[63],
  `operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md` [9], `people/don-elliott.md`
  [17], `people/jeff-doryman.md` [29]).

* **Batch 128 (pages 1271–1280, msgnum 1291–1300, 13–14 November 2003) —
  a Whale Gusher/Guzzler correction, the unresolved "RHXll" mystery boat,
  and "The Edge" as Don Elliott's probable boat name.** Glen Maxwell
  followed up his "Introducing Swash" reply by uploading photos of Zoë's
  bilge-pump installation, modeled directly on studying Matt Layden's own,
  and correcting himself — the pump is a Whale **Guzzler**, not a "Gusher"
  as he'd first said; "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`) added that the same
  product is sold in the UK as a "Gusher," folded into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#how-the-no-open-hatch-bailing-actually-works)
  (citations 53a-53b), cross-linked from `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md`
  (citation 172) and `people/jeff-doryman.md` (citation 28, appended to his
  existing "Jeff UK" disambiguation section — still no linking detail).
  Alastair separately asked whether the pump sees much real use, since his
  own knockdown took on too little water for his portable pump to get a
  purchase on — he simply sponged it out — folded as a new subsection into
  `design/weight-and-ballast.md` (citation 59), cross-linked from
  `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 130). Glen also posted an unidentified,
  well-built hull photographed under the group album's "RHXll" folder,
  asking who was building it — Bill Serjeant echoed the request but no
  builder ever claimed it in the archive — and, separately, a broader
  "Need more pictures and progress reports" call naming Andrew Graham and
  David Beard and prodding Don Elliott specifically, still referred to as
  "Elliott's Paradox" two years on; Bill Serjeant answered with a link to
  his own site's launch photos of Don's boat (URL ending `edge.htm`), from
  which Glen inferred the probable name **"The Edge"** — both threads
  folded into a new section of
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#chasing-down-quiet-builders-for-photos-and-progress-reports)
  (citations 25-29), with the name finding promoted into
  [boats/elliott-paradox.md](/boats/elliott-paradox.md#record)
  (citations 5-7) and cross-linked as a one-line addition to
  `people/don-elliott.md`. The "Introducing Swash" thread closed out with
  Glen's and Alastair's praise for Smith's photos and workmanship (Alastair
  joking Smith had "cornered the market in clamps"), folded into `people/david-c-smith.md`
  (citations 59-60), cross-linked from `people/alastair-law.md` (citation 131).
  Considered but did not promote a dedicated `boats/swash.md` — this
  batch's Swash content was thin (two short replies, no new build
  narrative beyond batch 127's naming), so the full narrative continues to
  live on `people/david-c-smith.md` per convention; revisit once Swash gets a
  substantial launch/cruise narrative of her own. No new files, no
  prunings. Msgnum mapping was a clean, fully consecutive run (1291–1300)
  with no gaps, continuing directly from batch 127's 1290.

* **Batch 127 (pages 1261–1270, msgnum 1281–1290, 11–13 November 2003) —
  the bulkhead-bevel FAQ thread's final round, and Smith names his boat
  "Swash."** The "Bulkhead questions (long)" thread (running since batch
  125) reached its natural close: Smith restated for the record why Paradox's
  bulkheads need beveling at all — unlike "instant"-boat jig practice,
  where a bevel is designed out — then walked "Paul" (`kayaker37`) through
  a detailed, sheet-by-sheet reading of dimensioned faces, bevels, and
  cleat-camber arithmetic (correcting his own "least width" cleat figure to
  "most width" along the way), folded into
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#which-side-of-the-station-line-a-bulkheads-dimensioned-face-falls-on)
  (citations 27–31), cross-linked from `people/david-c-smith.md` (citation 57). The
  batch centerpiece: Smith's "Introducing Swash" post finally named his
  in-progress Paradox "sistership" **"Swash"** — for "the sound of water,"
  "swagger," and the USGS acronym "Survey Wide Area SHorelines" — with her
  bottom on and right-side-up in the garage, a self-built rolling cradle,
  and interior foam-board prep underway; he also floated a speculative
  second vent trunk forward of bulkhead #1 (deck hole, water baffles,
  flapper-valve drain) for extra ventilation, folded into `people/david-c-smith.md`
  (citation 58, updated lead) and, for the vent-trunk idea specifically, a
  new subsection of
  [construction/transom-deck-plate.md](/construction/transom-deck-plate.md#a-second-forward-vent-trunk-speculative)
  (citation 19); Bill Serjeant replied praising the photos, folded into
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md` (citation 6). Bill Serjeant separately
  announced a third UK small-boat community resource — the `irbs.com`
  bulletin boards' new Small Sailboats Usergroup, distinct from his own
  `smallsailboats.co.uk`/`paradox-uk.co.uk` site pairing — folded into
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md` as a new section (citation 5). Updated
  `people/index.md`'s Smith summary to reflect the "Swash" naming. No new
  files, no promotions or prunings. Msgnum mapping was a clean, fully
  consecutive run (1281–1290) with no gaps, continuing directly from batch
  126's 1280.

* **Batch 126 (pages 1251–1260, msgnum 1271–1280, 8–11 November 2003) —
  bulkhead-bevel and "scab" follow-ups close out the FAQ thread, tank tops,
  offshore strength, and an unanswered outboard-from-the-cabin question.**
  The "Paradox offshore" thread-tail continued: Alastair reframed the
  offshore-vs-coastal-cruiser question around structural strength — a
  coastal cruiser arguably needs to be *stronger* than an offshore boat,
  since it must survive reefs and beach groundings a true offshore design
  avoids — pointing to Paradox's 15 mm rudder pintle as evidence, folded
  into
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#structural-strength-as-the-real-limit-not-distance-from-shore)
  (citation 20). The unanswered "discharges from Paradox" sanitation
  question got a belated, direct answer from Alastair — a sealed bucket for
  emergencies, shore facilities otherwise — folded into
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md#informal-practice-bucket-and-chuck-it-not-a-fitted-head)
  (citation 11). Batch 125's "Bulkhead questions (long)" FAQ thread ran to
  its close: "Paul" (`kayaker37`) asked Alastair to clarify the bevel/cleat
  curve relationship, drawing a rebuild-it-differently alternative (cut
  plywood to size, overlap cleats, cut bevels only at trial assembly, work
  outward from bulkhead 3); separately, Kjell Karlsson asked what a "6mm ply
  scab" is (his English-Swedish dictionary gave no translation), answered by
  Smith — a scab is simply a butt block, eliminable by scarphing the bulkhead's
  plywood together, alongside Smith's own full bandsaw-bevel-cutting method —
  both folded into
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#clarifying-the-bevel-and-cleat-relationship-and-a-rebuild-it-differently-alternative)
  and
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#what-a-scab-is-and-a-scarphed-alternative-that-eliminates-it)
  (citations 23–26), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (128),
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) (7), and
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (55). A new "Tank tops" thread had David
  Beard ask how to reinforce "Whisper"'s plans-structural hinged tank tops
  without losing their hinged storage access; Alastair doubted the extra
  stiffness is strictly necessary and described his own planned
  removable-hatch alternative if he converts to lead ballast — folded into
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md#structural-hinged-tank-tops-and-a-lightened-alternative)
  (citations 16–17), cross-linked from
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (164),
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) (72), and
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (129). A new correspondent,
  `dclark52001`, asked — citing a bad back — whether an outboard could be
  run and controlled entirely from inside the cabin with the hatch closed;
  no reply survives, folded as a new, unanswered subsection into
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md#operating-an-outboard-from-inside-the-cabin)
  (citation 8). Skipped page_1257 (Duane Hall's eBay plans-for-sale spam for
  an unrelated Glen-L design — off-topic, no Paradox content). No new files
  created, no promotions or prunings; "Paul" (`kayaker37`) and `dclark52001`
  both kept inline per the people threshold (below-threshold, no boat). All
  three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`) checked — none resurfaced. Msgnum
  mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1271–1280) with no gaps**,
  continuing directly from batch 125's 1270 — spanning 8–11 November 2003.
  All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly,
  and no stale-read issue surfaced (all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers
  confirmed distinct on direct read). Verified no new citation-number
  collisions were introduced across the nine files that received new
  citations
  ([design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md)
  [20],
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md)
  [11],
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md)
  [23]–[26],
  [construction/water-tank-lining.md](/construction/water-tank-lining.md)
  [16]–[17],
  [operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md)
  [8], [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) [126]–[129],
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) [164],
  [boats/whisper.md](/boats/whisper.md) [72],
  [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) [7],
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) [55]).

* **Batch 125 (pages 1241–1250, msgnum 1261–1270, 6–8 November 2003) — how
  bailing actually works without opening the hatch, and a full bulkhead
  construction FAQ.** A "Digest Number 381" reply thread closed out batch
  124's unanswered bailing question: Glen Maxwell answered with an ordinary
  bilge pump, favoring a Whale Gusher — the same type of pump Matt Layden
  uses, discharging out the tiller opening in the stern; Alastair gave the
  shorter version, "Paradox has a hole in the bottom to let the water out";
  Nels added the caveat that this drain path only works while the boat is
  planing; a new one-off, Ben Thomas (`k4zep`, has plans, kept inline below
  the people threshold), added a joking reply; Glen then clarified for Nels
  that the vent trunk is not a deliberate "bailer" — it drains continuously,
  at anchor as well as underway — folded as a new subsection into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#how-the-no-open-hatch-bailing-actually-works)
  (citations 53–58), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (124),
  [people/nels.md](/people/nels.md) (23), and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (169–170). Dave (Birmingham, AL) then opened a new "discharges from
  Paradox" thread asking about sanitation/potty arrangements, which went
  unanswered — folded into
  [operations/head-and-sanitation.md](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md)
  (citation 10). Separately, "Paul" (`kayaker37`, an established but
  below-threshold correspondent, distinct from "Paul V.") sent a long list
  of detailed bulkhead 1–4/transom construction questions as a first-time
  hull builder; Alastair answered each in turn — bevels, cleats, limber
  holes, the undocumented "2mm dado," deck-beam height, the tiller-opening
  shape, carlines — closing with general reassurance that epoxy-filled gaps
  only add strength, plus a tip on using spare wire nails as temporary dowel
  pins — folded as a new section into
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#bulkhead-construction-details-not-spelled-out-on-the-plans)
  (citations 21–22), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (125). A "Paradox offshore"
  thread-tail had new correspondent Freddie Cox (Annapolis, MD) compare
  Paradox against the Dix-designed Mini Transat 650 and ask about
  kevlar/carbon-fiber reinforcement; Bill Serjeant answered with the
  archive's clearest one-line racer/cruiser classification — "as different
  as chalk and cheese" — folded into
  [design/blue-water-suitability.md](/design/blue-water-suitability.md#a-racercruiser-comparison-and-bill-serjeants-verdict)
  (citations 18–19), cross-linked from
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md) (4);
  Freddie Cox kept inline per the people threshold (single appearance, no
  boat yet, just ordered plans). No new files created, no promotions or
  prunings. Checked all three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK,"
  "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`):
  none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run
  (1261–1270) with no gaps**, continuing directly from batch 124's 1260. All
  ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions exactly, and
  no stale-read issue surfaced (all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed
  distinct on direct read). Verified no new citation-number collisions were
  introduced across the eight files that received new citations
  (`design/weight-and-ballast.md` [53]–[58], `people/alastair-law.md`
  [124]–[125], `people/nels.md` [23],
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` [169]–[170],
  `operations/head-and-sanitation.md` [10],
  `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md` [21]–[22],
  `design/blue-water-suitability.md` [18]–[19],
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md` [4]).

* **Batch 124 (pages 1231–1240, msgnum 1251–1260, 28 October – 6 November
  2003) — the rudder's pivot-bolt fixing vindicated by Matt Layden's own
  10,000 miles, and a full stability/self-righting/bailing FAQ answer.** A UK
  builder's question about the rudder blade's single-bolt pivot fixing —
  what happens if the boat grounds before the blade is lifted, under an
  unfair sideways strain from wind and waves — drew Glen Maxwell's answer
  that Matt Layden has about 10,000 miles on that exact configuration with
  no reported problem, and his own several hundred miles on "Zoë" likewise
  trouble-free; folded into a new section of
  [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md#the-pivot-bolt-fixing-and-its-record-under-grounding-loads)
  (citations 28–29), cross-linked from
  [people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md](/people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md)
  (citation 15, a Matt-mileage authority point) and
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md)
  (citation 3). Separately, a newcomer's basic stability question (how
  stable, how much ballast, self-righting, and does the non-self-draining
  enclosed cockpit/cabin cause a problem) drew a full answer: Don Elliott's
  previously-published stability curve puts Paradox's range of positive
  stability at about 160°, matching the archive's existing 165° figure;
  Alastair gave the fullest firsthand account — "Little Jim" pops back up
  quickly from 90°, leaning out from the rail only heels her about 30°, his
  own ballast is 80 kg water plus 100 kg lead under the floor, and the only
  significant water ever shipped was 2-3 litres during a 90° knockdown with
  the hatch cracked, since the boat can otherwise be bailed from inside
  without opening the hatch; Bill Serjeant pointed to a stability photo on
  Alastair's own website — folded into a new section of
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#self-righting-range-and-bailing-without-opening-the-hatch)
  (citations 48–52), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citation 123). A follow-up
  question from a new Birmingham, Alabama correspondent — exactly how one
  bails a closed-up boat without opening the hatch — went unanswered.
  Skipped page_1231 (msgnum 1251, a garbled Swedish-language travel-ad spam
  post with no boat content) and two off-topic newcomer messages with no
  Paradox-relevant substance: "pibracing" (Mike, `mcerio02`) asking where to
  buy a copy of Hugo Vihlen's *The Stormy Voyage of Father's Day*, and new
  correspondent Steve Henshall asking what other small-boat sailing groups
  people belong to (building a Bolger Featherwind variant, not a Paradox).
  No new files, no promotions or prunings. Checked all three standing
  backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold
  "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping was a **clean,
  fully consecutive run (1251–1260) with no gaps**, continuing directly from
  batch 123's 1250. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and no stale-read issue surfaced (all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read). Verified no
  new citation-number collisions were introduced across the five files that
  received new citations (`design/rudder.md` [28]–[29],
  `people/glen-maxwell/matt-layden-eyewitness.md` [15],
  `references/paradox-uk-website.md` [3], `design/weight-and-ballast.md`
  [48]–[52], `people/alastair-law.md` [123]).

* **Batch 123 (pages 1221–1230, msgnum 1241–1250, 16–25 October 2003) — a
  plywood bill-of-materials FAQ, a new UK Paradox website, and the vent
  trunk's own drain.** "Pibracing" (Mike, `mcerio02`, introduced batch 122)
  ordered his plans and asked how much plywood a build needs and in what
  sizes; "Jeff UK" (`roverticket`/"Jay") answered by reading the sheet count
  straight off the plans' drawing 6 parts layout — 3 sheets 3/8 in, 4 sheets
  1/2 in, 2 sheets 3/4 in, 1 sheet 1/4 in — corroborating the archive's
  existing plywood bill of materials with a second, plans-sourced count;
  folded into
  [design/hull-scantlings.md](/design/hull-scantlings.md#plywood-bill-of-materials)
  (citations 8–9), cross-linked from
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (citation 27); Mike kept
  inline per the people threshold. UK builder Bill Serjeant (`barnacleid`)
  announced a new Paradox section on his existing Small Sailboats site
  (`smallsailboats.co.uk`) and a dedicated offshoot, `paradox-uk.co.uk`,
  under the same umbrella — a genuinely new UK community resource, promoted
  to a **new file**
  [references/paradox-uk-website.md](/references/paradox-uk-website.md)
  (citations 1–2, added to `references/index.md`), cross-linked from
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citation 53), who congratulated him. A new
  correspondent, Bill Nolen (`billnolen66`, sailing a West Wight Potter 14
  "Piglet" out of Oklahoma City), asked — quoting a line from "Little Jim"'s
  website — where the vent trunk's own drain is; Smith answered it drains
  through a thru-hull fitted directly below the trunk and pointed to photos
  of Zoë's vent trunk in the files section; Bill Nolen thanked him but
  decided to keep his Potter rather than start a Paradox — folded as a new,
  clearly-distinct section (alongside the existing transom-deck-plate and
  vent-baffle sections) into
  [construction/transom-deck-plate.md](/construction/transom-deck-plate.md#the-vent-trunks-own-drain)
  (citations 17–18), cross-linked from [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md)
  (citation 54); Bill Nolen kept inline per the people threshold (single
  appearance, explicitly not building a Paradox). Skipped page_1229 (Susan
  Walker's unsubscribe request) and page_1230 (Bill Serjeant's reply with
  Yahoo Groups' "Leave Group" instructions) — pure list-administration
  content with no boat-history substance. No new person pages, no
  promotions or prunings. Checked all three standing backlog items ("Jeff
  UK," "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`):
  none resurfaced. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run
  (1241–1250) with no gaps**, continuing directly from batch 122's 1240 —
  spanning 16–25 October 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the
  batch instructions exactly, and no stale-read issue surfaced (all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read). Verified no
  new citation-number collisions were introduced across the five files that
  received new citations (`design/hull-scantlings.md` [8]-[9],
  `people/jeff-doryman.md` [27], `people/david-c-smith.md` [53]-[54],
  `construction/transom-deck-plate.md` [17]-[18], plus the new file's own
  [1]-[2]).

* **Batch 122 (pages 1211–1220, msgnum 1231–1240, 3–6 October 2003) — the
  "ballast" thread's tail: bronze vs. steel chine-runner shoes, a
  birdshot-epoxy putty, a casting-plug correction, and a battery-ballast
  aside; plus a small bulkhead-bevel-angle FAQ.** Continuing straight from
  batch 121's plaster-mold ballast thread,
  [construction/chine-runner-construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#bronze-versus-steel-if-a-builder-insists-on-a-shoe)
  picked up: Smith cautioned against steel/wood composites over corrosion and
  bedding-compound risk, favoring bronze plate with bronze fasteners as the
  durable (if costlier) alternative, and reiterated the chine runners are
  shaped fairing blocks, not simple structural fillets; Michael (`reekree8`,
  the Bolger-sharpie builder introduced batch 121) proposed epoxy-coated
  steel bedded in 3M 5200 as a middle path, asking whether resulting
  corrosion would be disastrous once the coating chipped; Paul V. proposed a
  lead-birdshot-and-silica-epoxy putty worked onto the runners (or under the
  hull-bottom glass) by hand, estimating 100–200 lb achievable this way; Smith
  separately corrected his own batch-121 casting-mold proposal — a finished,
  beveled runner section cannot serve as the mold plug, which must be built
  flat on the bench instead — folded into
  [construction/chine-runner-construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md)
  (citations 20–24), cross-linked from
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citations 51–52),
  [people/paul-v.md](/people/paul-v.md) (citation 49), and
  [people/william-longyard.md](/people/william-longyard.md) (citation 19, a
  US Hammerite-sourcing aside seconding Alastair's paint choice from batch
  121). A new one-off, Jim Goeckermann (`jgoeckermann`), opened a parallel
  "Paradox Ballast/Runners" thread from outside the design — musing about
  golf-cart-battery ballast and a "warped canoe"-style offset-patterning
  method for adapting chine runners to other hard-chined hulls (a
  Featherwind derivative, his own West Wight Potter and Hobie 16) — kept
  inline per the people threshold (two messages, no Paradox of his own,
  general commentary) and folded into the same chine-runner-construction.md
  section. Separately, a newcomer, "pibracing" (Mike, `mcerio02`), asked
  whether bulkhead-side stick angles are shown on the plans or must be
  faired with a batten; Glen Maxwell answered that angles hold at any
  scale and can be lifted straight off the drawing with a bevel square;
  Alastair added a looser after-assembly-fairing alternative from his own
  experience getting some angles wrong; Mike thanked them and said he would
  order plans — folded as a new section into
  [construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md#finding-bulkhead-member-angles-from-the-drawings)
  (citations 17–20), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citation 122) and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (citation 168); Mike kept inline per the people threshold (below-threshold
  newcomer, no boat yet). No new files created, no promotions or prunings.
  Checked all three standing backlog items ("Jeff UK," "botebilder"/Ernie
  Fossel, the below-threshold "Larry"/`newspipetr3`): none resurfaced.
  Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1231–1240) with no
  gaps**, continuing directly from batch 121's 1230 — the archive's
  twenty-ninth-plus gap-free ten-page batch in recent memory — spanning 3–6
  October 2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and no stale-read issue surfaced (all ten
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers confirmed distinct on direct read). Verified no
  new citation-number collisions were introduced across the seven files
  that received new citations
  (`construction/chine-runner-construction.md` [20]-[24],
  `people/william-longyard.md` [19], `people/david-c-smith.md` [51]-[52],
  `people/paul-v.md` [49], `construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md`
  [17]-[20], `people/alastair-law.md` [122],
  `people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md` [168]).

* **Batch 121 (pages 1201–1210, msgnum 1221–1230, 24 September – 3 October
  2003) — Alastair's first Solent crossing and knockdown, a two-year-old
  "Jeff Please" thread finally answered, and a second round on
  lead-in-chine-runner ballast.** The centerpiece: Alastair's "A new world
  record" — the first (and only) recorded Paradox crossing of the Solent,
  Poole to Cowes and back, surviving an estimated 80–90 degree knockdown
  from a rogue wave near Hurst Narrows without damage — folded as a full
  new section into
  [boats/little-jim.md](/boats/little-jim.md#a-first-crossing-of-the-solent-poole-to-cowes-september-2003)
  (citations 76–78), cross-linked from
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citations 120–121), with
  one-line replies from Glen Maxwell
  ([people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md),
  citation 167) and Smith ([people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md), citation 48, who
  also asked how a heavier sailor's weight would affect the righting-moment
  graph). Separately, Jeff Forrest (`paddler_jeff`) repeated, nearly two
  years on, his original check-in on Jeff (UK, `doryman_uk`)'s stretched
  16 ft "Paramour" redesign under the recurring subject "Jeff Please" —
  this time drawing an answer from the separate, still-unconfirmed "Jeff
  UK" (`roverticket`/"Jay"): still lurking, still meaning to build, and a
  report of meeting Alastair in person at Cowes; his 16 ft drawings were by
  then described more modestly as an 8 in stretch of Paradox rather than
  the original named "Paramour" specification — folded into
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (citations 23–24),
  [people/jeff-forrest.md](/people/jeff-forrest.md) (citation 11), and
  [design/variants/paramour-design.md](/design/variants/paramour-design.md)
  (citation 6, "Fate unknown" retitled to reflect the update). A new
  "ballast" thread had Smith reopen the lead-in-chine-runner-mold idea first
  raised in batch 112, this time with full plaster-mold casting detail;
  Jeff UK reprised the movable-vs-fixed-ballast objection and proposed a
  flush-runner alternative; a new one-off, Michael (`reekree8`, building
  his own Bolger-derived sharpie), proposed steel-plate chine runners in
  place of lead; Alastair closed the thread with the archive's fullest
  practical lead-casting account (bread-tin-cast 25 lb bricks, 250 lb total
  ballast, movable to trim) — folded into
  [construction/chine-runner-construction.md](/chine-runners/construction.md#casting-lead-ballast-into-the-runners)
  (citations 15–19), cross-linked from
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (citations 25–26),
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citations 49–50), and
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citation 121); Michael kept
  inline below the people threshold. No new files, no promotions or
  prunings. Msgnum mapping was a clean, fully consecutive run
  (1221–1230) with no gaps, continuing directly from batch 120's 1220.

* **Batch 120 (pages 1191–1200, msgnum 1208–1220, 17–23 September 2003) —
  a light thread-tail batch: a Minisail speed comparison, a mast-head
  photo request, and a Trojan Horse warning.** Dave Bolduc added a
  one-line postscript to his "New Paradox pictures" post pointing to a
  Tongue of the Ocean cruising photo on microcruising.com's "poem" page —
  folded into [people/dave-bolduc.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (citation
  74). Alastair opened a new "Paradox stability" thread with lake
  photographs of "Little Jim" illustrating weight-distribution
  sensitivity, reporting he could keep pace with a two-up Minisail dinghy
  in light air; a new one-off correspondent, Paul (`kayaker37`), asked how
  the boat performs lightly loaded versus fully cruising-loaded; Alastair
  answered that he has never sailed without the water ballast and
  believes he carries the full 1,400 lb design displacement, concluding a
  Paradox must be sailed "as a yacht, not a dinghy"; Glen Maxwell added a
  corroborating data point — sailing "Zoë" with empty water tanks made her
  a little more tender but did not much affect stability, handling 16 kt
  reefed without trouble — folded as a new dated continuation into
  [design/weight-and-ballast.md](/design/weight-and-ballast.md#how-a-paradox-feels-underloaded)
  (citations 45–47), with a full new section on
  [boats/little-jim.md](/boats/little-jim.md#a-lake-outing-and-a-minisail-speed-comparison-september-2003)
  (citation 75) and cross-linked summaries in
  [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citations 118–119) and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (citation 166); Paul kept inline below the people threshold. A thin
  "Mast Head" thread — Bjorn Harbo asking for a close-up masthead photo,
  Glen Maxwell first offering to take one, then redirecting to existing
  close-ups already posted in the group's files section under "Topping
  Lift," and Bjorn's thanks — folded into
  [design/rig.md](/components/rig/index.md#masthead-reference-photographs) (citations
  55–58). Smith's "Re: newest network critical patch" flagged a forwarded
  fake-Microsoft email carrying a malicious attachment as a Trojan Horse —
  list-hygiene content with no boat relevance, folded minimally (one line
  plus citation, matching the tree's existing JS/Kak virus-incident
  precedent) into [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citation 47) and
  [people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md](/people/glen-maxwell/list-founder-and-moderator.md#the-tripod-link-spam-saga)
  (citation 24). No new files, no promotions or prunings. Msgnum mapping
  had one confirmed real archive gap between 1216 and 1220 (both
  surrounding messages independently confirmed distinct on direct read),
  otherwise fully consecutive (1208–1216), continuing directly from batch
  119's 1207. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch
  instructions exactly, and no stale-read issue surfaced. Verified no new
  citation-number collisions across the eight touched files.

* **Batch 119 (pages 1181–1190, msgnum 1198–1207, 11–17 September 2003) —
  the "Robust construction" thread's final week, closed out by Matt
  Layden's own scantlings post.** Smith and [Jeff (UK,
  "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (posting as "Jeff UK"/`roverticket`/
  "Jay") traded a further round: Smith's maritime/aircraft-museum analogy for
  building to spec (judging Paradox's 12mm sides "not the least bit
  troublesome," 9mm adequate, and the hull buildable in aluminum, but
  drawn for plywood) and his Folkboat analogy answering Kjell Karlsson
  (a faithful fiberglass replica is still "not a true Folkboat" — "you
  can't improve [a design], you can only change it"); Jeff UK's countering
  defense of a builder's right to tinker (an early-automobile red-flag-law
  analogy), while crediting Matt Layden as "a far sighted tinker." The
  thread then drew a rare direct post from **Matt Layden himself**,
  explaining the plywood specification: hem-fir chosen for cost and
  renewability, its low density letting a thicker panel go on
  weight-free, 3-ply 3/8 in hem-fir the worst grade (hence 1/2 in sides);
  builders may shave 1/8 in for "better" tropical hardwood ply without
  hurting his feelings, though thinner ply saves only ~20 lb and no
  construction time while needing more temporary bracing; the original
  Paradox's 10-year, minimal-maintenance service record as evidence; and
  his view that alternative materials can all work if intelligently
  engineered but none beats plywood's overall combination of virtues. All
  folded into
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md#matt-laydens-own-account-of-the-scantling-choice)
  (citations 22–27), a new cross-link pointer added to
  [design/hull-scantlings.md](/design/hull-scantlings.md), and cross-linked
  from [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citations 44–45) and
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (citations 21–22,
  updated disambiguation note). Separately: Smith shared an off-topic
  'Lectronic Latitude sailing-video link, folded into a new section of
  references/off-topic-cross-posts.md
  with a cross-linked summary in [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md); [Andrew
  Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md) posted a further "Aussie Paradox"
  photo round (sheathed hull/deck, cabin framing, foredeck anchor
  storage, a flat-cabin-front/back plans deviation), folded into
  [boats/aussie-paradox.md](/boats/aussie-paradox.md#further-photos-sheathed-hull-cabin-framing-and-a-foredeck-anchor-stow)
  with a cross-linked summary in
  [people/andrew-graham.md](/people/andrew-graham.md) (citation 10); and
  [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) announced two new digitized photo
  pages at microcruising.com (`paradox3.htm`, `paradox4.htm`), folded into
  [people/dave-bolduc.md](/people/bolduc/dave.md) (citation 73). No new
  files, no promotions or prunings; all three standing backlog identity
  items ("Jeff UK" vs. `doryman_uk`, "botebilder"/Ernie Fossel, the
  below-threshold UK "Larry") checked — none resurfaced beyond "Jeff UK"'s
  already-tracked, still-unconfirmed reappearance. Msgnum mapping was a
  **clean, fully consecutive run (1198–1207) with no gaps**, continuing
  directly from batch 118's 1197 — spanning 11–17 September 2003.

* **Batch 118 (pages 1171–1180, msgnum 1188–1197, 9–11 September 2003) —
  the full "Robust construction" thread, continuing directly from batch
  117's opening posts.** Smith's design-fidelity strength argument (batch 117)
  and David Beard's "Whisper" sides revelation drew a wide round of replies
  spanning both the archive's philosophical case for building to spec and a
  new, separate rudder-ballast sub-thread. Smith answered David directly (added
  strength helps an owner building up an already-underbuilt secondhand boat,
  not new construction) and extended his designer-fidelity logic to material
  substitution — a Paradox built in aluminum could only still bear the name
  if Matt Layden himself modified the design for it, otherwise it is a
  small-block Chevy in a Jaguar. [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md)
  reappeared under his disambiguated "Jeff UK"/`roverticket`/"Jay" identity,
  citing Shane Acton's "SHRIMPY" — the same precedent his established
  namesake used over a year earlier — to argue Paradox's sides are already
  more than adequate. [Kjell Karlsson](/people/kjell-karlsson.md) closed the
  philosophical side with a reflection on why builders are drawn to Paradox
  at all (a "deeper"/"wider" interest in design, not just wanting to sail),
  framing Matt Layden and Sven Yrvind as free-thinking, non-proprietary
  "amateurs." [Andrew Graham](/people/andrew-graham.md) gave the fullest
  builder's-choice statement (accept the consequences of your own material
  choices) and a genuine build update (dynel/epoxy deck sheathing, a
  self-draining sand-anchor well, a PVC hawse pipe). [Glen
  Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) illustrated "how strong is too strong"
  with a pre-Paradox racing story — crewing Roger Hatfield's trimaran
  "Hatter" in the Around St. Croix Race, going airborne off a wave and
  landing hard, Hatfield's own verdict "I built her too heavy." Smith closed the
  thread noting that added hull weight also strains the rig and deck
  hardware above the sheer line. All folded into
  [construction/hull-planking.md](/construction/hull-planking.md#the-case-for-a-second-layer-of-cloth)
  (citations 16–21), cross-linked from
  [people/david-c-smith.md](/people/david-c-smith.md) (citations 42–43),
  [people/jeff-doryman.md](/people/jeff-doryman.md) (citation 20, updated
  disambiguation note), [people/kjell-karlsson.md](/people/kjell-karlsson.md)
  (citation 6), [people/andrew-graham.md](/people/andrew-graham.md)
  (citations 8–9), and
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (citation 164). A separate sub-thread — Kjell wondering whether the
  rudder's lead ballast could be replaced with a bungee-cord downhaul — drew
  Glen Maxwell's own lead-shot-in-epoxy rudder-ballast technique (built for a
  Little Cruiser rudder, easier to fair than a hot-lead casting) and
  Alastair's caution that a bungee downhaul would need a built-in forward
  stop, since the stock rudder has nothing else to prevent it swinging
  forward to the transom; folded into a new
  [design/rudder.md](/components/rudder.md#rudder-ballast-without-a-lead-casting)
  section (citations 25–27), cross-linked from
  [people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md](/people/glen-maxwell/build-and-zoe.md)
  (citation 165) and [people/alastair-law.md](/people/alastair-law.md) (citation
  117). Andrew Graham's dynel/sand-anchor-well/hawse-pipe build update was
  also folded into [boats/aussie-paradox.md](/boats/aussie-paradox.md) (new
  section, citation 12). David Beard's two brief replies ("Point well
  taken," "Thanks to all") were added to
  [people/david-beard.md](/people/david-beard.md) (citations 162–163). No
  new files, no promotions or prunings. Checked all three standing backlog
  items (Jeff UK, botebilder/Ernie Fossel, below-threshold Larry/
  `newspipetr3`): only Jeff UK resurfaced (see above); the other two did
  not. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1188–1197) with
  no gaps**, continuing directly from batch 117's 1187 — the thirty-second
  gap-free ten-page batch of the last thirty-three, spanning 9–11 September
  2003. All ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions
  exactly, and all ten `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` headers were confirmed distinct on
  direct read — no stale-read issue. Verified no new citation-number
  collisions across the eight touched files.

* **Batch 117 (pages 1161–1170, msgnum 1178–1187, 7–9 September 2003) —
  resolves the standing-open "Glass" second-layer-cloth question via two
  converging threads: a Sven Yrvind flexible-sandwich explanation, and Smith's
  "Robust construction" case answered by David Beard revealing Whisper's
  true 1/4 in ply sides.** Read all ten source e-mails; every
  `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` header was distinct (1178–1187), confirming no stale-read
  issue, and all ten source files' byte sizes matched the batch instructions
  exactly. Msgnum mapping was a **clean, fully consecutive run (1178–1187)
  with no gaps**, continuing directly from batch 116's 1177 — the
  thirty-first gap-free ten-page batch of the last thirty-two. William
  Longyard answered David Beard's "Glass" question (is a second 6 oz cloth
  layer over the sides/top worth it?) with the mechanical case for glassing
  the *inside* as well (an I-beam effect) versus a plain second outside
  layer (abrasion resistance only), then gave the fullest technical account
  yet of why Sven Yrvind's "Bris" boats sandwich Divinycell foam, glass, and
  a flexible resin to let the hull rebound rather than crack when struck —
  the same logic as a flexing airliner wing, and, per Yrvind himself, useful
  for surviving a strike on a sleeping whale. Paul (`kayaker37`, an
  already-established below-threshold correspondent from the 2002 "Design"
  thread) quoted Sven Yrvind's own article "BRIS LIFE.doc" on Dacron's ~17%
  elongation-before-breaking advantage over glass fiber (4%), Kevlar
  (2.5%), and carbon fiber (1.5%, which also stiffens and grows brittle).
