Continued the previous batch's "Bottom Paint" thread by extending [Bottom painting over epoxy](/construction/bottom-painting.md) with a new "finding the waterline" sub-thread: David Beard, unable to locate a transom waterline on the plans, asked how to determine where to stop his bottom paint; Glen Maxwell answered with his own reference points from painting "Zoë" (bottom of the transom; ~4 1/4 in up from the chine at the bow) and a masking-tape/level-the-floor-boards technique for eyeballing a fair line despite hull curvature; new one-off poster **Frank McClure** (below the people threshold, inline citation only) offered a simpler water-level (clear-tubing-and-hose) alternative, then clarified his own description the same day. **New reference concept** [Epoxy and fiberglass materials sourcing (raka.com) and Kevlar cost debate](/references/materials-sourcing-links.md): David LeBlanc pointed to raka.com for epoxy/cloth and used its prices to argue against Kevlar on cost grounds (roughly 4x fiberglass cloth by the yard, with biaxial-weave Kevlar higher still, though the total premium for a Paradox-sized ~5 yd bottom is modest in absolute terms); [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) replied that his own Kevlar-bottom plan (see [hull planking](/construction/hull-planking.md)) always meant scrounging cheap offcuts from a professional race-boat yard rather than buying retail, illustrated with a mould-shop-scrap-bin anecdote — filed as a cost-driven footnote to the existing stitch-and-glue/Kevlar debate rather than a new technical thread. Folded in, as a minor same-day, content-free aside, David LeBlanc's bare "Boat Finishes" Amazon.com link (no commentary beyond "FWIW"). Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md), and [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts (Frank McClure is a one-off, single-topic poster per the people-threshold guidance); no renames; no pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted bottom-painting/hull-planking threads or is a minor, clearly-scoped reference leaf, consistent with the thought-tree principle of consolidating recurring themes rather than fragmenting them. Updated the construction/references/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_381–page_390)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 379–388, 25–26 February 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body — no duplicates. Continued the prior batch's "Design" (blue-water) thread in [Blue-water suitability and comparison to Sven Yrvind's boats](/design/blue-water-suitability.md): [Paul V. (cruisenewsnet)](/people/paul-v.md) — previously a one-off Swamp Thing questioner, now promoted with a second substantive contribution — revealed he is designing his own trailerable Great Lakes boat, considered Paradox for it but worried its chine-keel hull form could trip the boat in steep breaking waves, and prioritized self-righting and full flotation over Paradox's approach, choosing a dory hull instead and asking about Sven Yrvind's "Bris" hull shape; separately, **Luke S** (a recurring one-off, previously seen only on water tanks) publicly changed his mind mid-thread — "this post alone has convinced me to buy plans for Paradox" — having always assumed it was a day sailer only, drawing Glen Maxwell's reply that Matt Layden "has sailed thousands of miles in her" and, days later, Glen's identification of a Bahamas Paradox website as Dave and Mindy Bolduc's (who "have cruised the Bahamas extensively with Matt"); Luke also recalled, unreconciled with Glen's claims, that Matt had once endorsed Paradox as a coastal cruiser only. Extended [Bottom painting over epoxy](/construction/bottom-painting.md) with a new, distinct "Bottom Paint" thread on paint **selection** (as opposed to the concept's existing bonding-technique and refit-prep material): David Beard asked for a bottom-paint recommendation for his frequently-trailered Paradox; David LeBlanc gave a full breakdown of the factors governing antifouling choice (water temperature, hull material, fresh/tidal/marine water, haul-out frequency), recommending a copolymer ablative paint and warning against DIY cuprous-oxide shortcuts; Glen Maxwell thanked him; Susan Davis ("futabachan") half-joked about needing to repaint twice a year splitting her season between Lake Ontario and the Bahamas, answered by David LeBlanc's simple "just buy tropical-strength paint" advice. Extended [Paul V.](/people/paul-v.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), and [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts (Luke S and Susan Davis remain below the people threshold — recurring but without a boat/build credential of their own beyond what's already recorded); no renames; no pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material extends two already-promoted threads (blue-water suitability, bottom painting), consistent with the thought-tree principle of consolidating recurring themes rather than fragmenting them into new leaves. Updated the design/construction/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_371–page_380)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 369–378, spanning the tail of the "refit" thread dated 22 Feb 2002, a one-off "Building a sharpie article" pointer, and a new "Design" thread dated 24 Feb 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body — one message (page_379, Glen Maxwell, msgnum 377) carries the misleading subject "Re: Design" but its body is actually an off-batch reply about installing removable foam, evidently a threading/quoting error rather than genuine "Design"-thread content; recorded as a citation curiosity in [removable foam flotation panels](/construction/removable-foam-panels.md) rather than new technical material. Extended [Checking a used Paradox for rot](/construction/rot-inspection-ice-pick.md) with the refit thread's resolution: [Susan Davis](/components/yuloh.md) ("futabachan") clarified that *she*, not David Beard, is the one planning to Kevlar-glass her bottom (also correcting David LeBlanc's assumption she was male), reasoned Kevlar's notorious difficulty is a side effect of its abrasion resistance and worth the effort given how often a Paradox bumps bottom in shallow water, and planned a proof-of-concept test layup on a Bolger Tortoise dinghy first. Created new reference leaf [West System Epoxy resources](/references/west-system-resources.md) for David LeBlanc's two West System pointers (general moisture-barrier/penetrating-epoxy information, and a specific "Building a Wood/Epoxy Sharpie" Epoxyworks article) — minor external-resource color, consistent with existing thin reference leaves. **New concept** [Blue-water suitability and comparison to Sven Yrvind's boats](/design/blue-water-suitability.md): David Beard opened a "Design" thread asking why Paradox is classed a coastal cruiser while Swedish designer Sven Yrvind's "Bris"/Sofia boats are classed blue-water, drawing a structural comparison from William H. Longyard (hydrodynamically similar hulls, but Yrvind's high-tech composite construction can absorb impact and "bounce back," plus a differing multi-sail-component rig strategy) and a strong personal endorsement from Glen Maxwell (36 years' sailing experience, 30,000+ blue-water miles, feels safer in "Zoë" than in bigger boats "because I'm sitting in her and not on her") — left with two open questions (what would make Paradox blue-water-capable; whether Glen would have built "Zoë" differently for an Atlantic crossing) and a one-off reference request from "kayaker37" ("Paul," distinct from the earlier, unrelated "Paul (NZ)"). **New person concept** [William H. Longyard](/people/william-longyard.md): promoted from a below-threshold inline mention (a single prior outboard-conversion answer) to a full person concept, now recurring across three substantive messages in two batches — cross-linked from [auxiliary outboard power](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md) and [interior bins and hinged floor](/components/interior.md), both updated accordingly. Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with citations for the "Design" thread. No renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — the blue-water topic is a single, not-yet-recurring thread with open questions, consistent with the thought-tree principle of keeping thin, single-thread material at leaf level pending further evidence. Updated the design/construction/references/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_361–page_370)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 359–368, spanning two threads: "Stitch and Glue Paradox" replies dated 20 Feb 2002 — two of which, Jeff Forrest's page_363/page_365, carry internal `Date:` headers of 31 Dec 2001, evidently delayed/misdated relative to their delivery order — and a new "refit" thread dated 21–22 Feb 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body. Substantially extended [Hull planking](/construction/hull-planking.md) with the archive's fullest debate yet on whether stitch-and-glue is actually worth adopting for Paradox: futabachan (Susan Davis, inline-only) argued it buys nothing given the need for real chine logs and the boat's ballast requirement working against any weight savings; Jeff Forrest rebutted that it would simplify bulkhead construction regardless, citing Sam Devlin's stitch-and-glue designs; [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) revealed his own concrete plan (3/8 in ply sides, two-layer 3/8 in Kevlar-glassed bottom, citing Shane Acton's circumnavigating "SHRIMPY" as thin-ply precedent) and asked about epoxy fillet-radius sizing, answered by Jeff Forrest quoting Devlin's published rule (depth = thickest ply; width = 1.5–2× that) — no consensus reached. Extended [Chine runner construction technique](/chine-runners/construction.md) with Pete Hodges's speculative idea that the chine runners plus a heavy inside fillet could substitute for an internal chine log (a common rot-start point, mitigated on Paradox by the enclosed cabin) — unanswered. Created a new "refit" sequence, extending [Bottom painting over epoxy](/construction/bottom-painting.md) and [Checking a used Paradox for rot](/construction/rot-inspection-ice-pick.md): continuing the previous batch's "Bottom Woven Roving" prep, David Beard found a second damp-but-not-rotten spot at bulkhead #3 (the same location as his January ice-pick find) while sanding the bottom's paint off, traced afterward to a leaking hatch rather than a preexisting problem; three members answered with drying/diagnosis advice — David LeBlanc (heat lamp, up to several weeks to dry, void-detection by tapping/dew, moisture-barrier-coating question, and a Kevlar-difficulty caution after asking if David was the one Kevlar-ing his bottom), Glen Maxwell (heat gun or light bulb as alternatives), and Pete Hodges (a pointer to Dave Carnell's rot article, already cited in January, plus a new anti-freeze-soak suggestion). Extended [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md), [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md), [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md), [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md), and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts (futabachan remains a one-off, inline-only per the people threshold); no renames; no pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted stitch-and-glue, chine-runner, bottom-painting, and rot-inspection threads, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_351–page_360)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 351–358, all dated 19 Feb 2002, all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body). This batch is a dense cluster of same-day replies, mostly from Glen Maxwell, resolving several previously-open threads and adding one new design leaf. **New concept**: [Yuloh and stowed-rig retaining loops](/components/yuloh.md) — Susan Davis ("futabachan") asked how the Paradox's two brass tube deck loops actually retain the yuloh (starboard) and the lowered rig (port); Glen Maxwell answered that the forward furling-drum tack fitting retains the rig's front end, and the yuloh is simply jammed snug against the coach roof and against a cleat — a one-off poster (Davis) recorded by inline citation only, below the people threshold. **Resolved open questions**: extended [Weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) with Glen Maxwell's answer to Alastair's previously-unanswered center-of-gravity question (CG sits at the "C/A" mark on plan page one) — Alastair's immediate follow-up asking what "C/A" stands for remains unanswered; also added Glen's general worked lever-arm/moment-arm method for rebalancing ballast against an added outboard's weight, answering (in general form, not with a specific figure) David Beard's still-open ballast-rebalancing question. Extended [Bottom painting over epoxy](/construction/bottom-painting.md) with a new refit sub-thread: David Beard asked whether to strip an existing bottom to bare glass or just remove paint before laying new roving; Dave Bolduc favored the more cautious full inspection, Glen favored paint-only — the original question (msgnum 349) is referenced via Glen's reply since it falls outside this batch's files. Extended (h)yelp! — Golant Gaffer cross-post, [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md), and [Glen Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with Glen's belated but direct answer to David's original forum-etiquette question (no problem sharing his non-Paradox build here). Extended [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) and [Hull planking](/construction/hull-planking.md) with a new, unanswered "Stitch and Glue Paradox" question: Jeff asked whether anyone has worked out stitch-and-glue bottom-panel dimensions to match the plans' already-supplied side-panel dimensions. No new person concepts beyond the inline-only Susan Davis mention; no renames; no pruning — all new material extends already-promoted ballast/furling/(h)yelp threads or is a small, clearly-scoped new design leaf, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/construction/references indexes.
* **Update (batch page_341–page_350)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 339–348, 13–16 Feb 2002). Note on source integrity: page_349 and page_350 are byte-identical duplicate files on disk of page_347 and page_348 respectively (confirmed via `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`, Message-ID, and full-body diff — not a Read-tool caching artifact this time, genuinely duplicated source files), so this batch covers eight distinct messages; duplication noted in citations rather than treated as new content. Substantially extended (h)yelp! — Golant Gaffer cross-post, [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md), and [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) with the thread's continuation: Jeff tracked down two Classic Boat magazine citations for David's requested Golant Gaffer/Europe-to-Turkey voyage article (Sept 2001 #159; a July 1998 #121 Golant Gaffer review), and David added a Classic Boat-vs-Watercraft magazine preference aside plus his own fading memory of once owning the July 1998 issue. Substantially extended [Paramour](/design/variants/paramour-design.md), [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md), and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with a second, independent buyer inquiry: David Beard asked to purchase a set of Paramour plans, and Jeff replied he wanted to check the drawings for errors first, giving a progress update (two drawings ready but blocked by a non-working Mustek 1200 scanner under Windows XP, current work at 3 in = 1 ft scale, a planned TURBOCAD sail-plan sketch) and a one-off aside about an unrelated personal project (a 16 ft Chamberlain Dory Skiff, PLYBOATS-designed, four years to finish) — filed as inline color, not a new concept, per the thought-tree pruning guidance. Extended [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md) and [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md) with a new "Keeping the dream alive" section: David LeBlanc's build-motivation tips for winter-stalled builders (log-keeping, skill-building, reward gear purchases, destination research, and the closing discipline of building at least weekly) — filed as a section of the existing build-effort concept rather than a new leaf, since it directly complements the existing time/cost/difficulty data there. Created new reference leaf [General boatbuilding resource links](/references/general-boatbuilding-links.md) for [Mat ("mat_man22")](/people/mat-man22.md)'s three non-Paradox-specific boatbuilding-community pointers (a cedar-strip site, rec.boats.building, The WoodenBoat Forum) given to David LeBlanc — tangential external links, consistent with the precedent of other minor reference leaves ("The Aegre," Dave Carnell's articles). No new person concepts (all posters this batch already have concepts); no renames; no pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted Golant-Gaffer/Paramour/build-effort threads or is a minor reference leaf, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the references/construction indexes.
* **Update (batch page_331–page_340)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 329–338, 12–13 Feb 2002 plus one Dec-2001-dated message delivered/quoted in this window), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/body diff. Resolved the previously-open "14 ft rule" question in [Enlarging Paradox for a tall/heavy sailor](/design/variants/enlarging-for-a-large-sailor.md): Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk") answered Dave LeBlanc — a US craft under 14 ft, not engine-powered, needs no hull registration numbers — with Dave LeBlanc noting state registration rules vary regardless (Washington would "register a bathtub"); the rule remains stated but not formally cited. Extended [Paramour](/design/variants/paramour-design.md) and [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) with the archive's first recorded buyer interest in Jeff (UK)'s 16 ft redesign — Jeff Forrest asked when it would be finished and whether he could buy the completed boat; unanswered in this batch. Extended [Weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md), [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with two replies to David Beard's still-open ballast/outboard question: Larry (LJGeib)'s "weathervaning" (too-far-forward weight) diagnosis, which does not actually match David's reported aft-shifted ballast, and Alastair's separate, also-unanswered question asking where the plans specify the boat's center of gravity. Created new reference leaf (h)yelp! — Golant Gaffer cross-post for a tangential, off-topic thread: David LeBlanc introduced his newly ordered non-Paradox "Golant Gaffer" plans and asked whether off-topic discussion was welcome, drawing replies from one-off poster Stuart Crawford (New Zealand, below the people threshold, inline only) and Jeff (UK); folded in a same-day, uncommented stitch-and-glue-vs-stringers construction link from the same poster. **New person concept** [David LeBlanc](/people/david-leblanc.md): promoted from recurring one-off citations (Aegre cross-post, Bahamas charter cross-post, head/sanitation, water tanks) to a full person concept, since he now recurs across six-plus distinct threads spanning three batches — though the Golant Gaffer reveal confirms he has never built a Paradox himself, consistent with his consistent role as an engaged outside commentator/cross-poster rather than a builder; cross-linked from the concepts he touches. No renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level (a resolved side-question, a buyer-interest footnote, two unanswered ballast replies, and an off-topic thread), consistent with the thought-tree principle, aside from the single new person-concept promotion. Updated the design/people/references indexes.
* **Update (batch page_321–page_330)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 319–328, 6–13 Feb 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/body diff. Closed out the "Mast rake" thread in [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) with Alastair's one-line thanks (no new fact). Extended [Group cruise planning and firsthand trip reports](/operations/group-cruise-reports.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), and [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md) with the follow-through on the previously-proposed Apalachicola Bay cruise: rather than waiting for a joint date, Glen went ahead with a solo scouting trip aboard "Zoë," inviting the list and specifically David on short notice (David: "might be able to pull it off"); no report of the trip itself or of whether David joined is recorded. Created two new design concepts for a distinct "Paradox size" thread — fitting a large sailor's body, as opposed to the earlier speed/headroom-driven stretch discussions: [Enlarging Paradox for a tall/heavy sailor](/design/variants/enlarging-for-a-large-sailor.md) (newcomer Dan Fickett, 6'6"/200 lb, asks 10% vs. 15%; Larry/LJGeib suggests scaling length only so mass grows linearly rather than as the cube of the dimensions — left unreconciled with Glen Maxwell's standing "scale everything uniformly" guidance; Pete Hodges reconfirms his own 10% ceiling, citing an extra side-panel scarf and an over-width bottom panel beyond that; Dave LeBlanc's still-unanswered "what is the 14 ft rule?" question) and [Paramour](/design/variants/paramour-design.md) (Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")'s fuller reveal of his from-scratch 16 ft stitch-and-glue redesign — full PLYBOATS-computed dimensions, displacement 1,507 lb vs. Paradox's 1,410 lb, prismatic coefficient .566 vs. .580 — superseding his earlier, thinner UK-stretch proposal). Extended [stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md), [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md), and [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) with cross-links and citations for the above. Extended [Weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md), [Auxiliary outboard power](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md), and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with a new "Ballast" thread: David, about to fit a heavier 4 hp 4-stroke outboard (65 lb), gave the archive's most granular ballast-placement detail yet (400 lb in 25 lb blocks between bulkheads #2 and #3, mostly shifted aft) and asked how to rebalance for the added weight, tying a poor-windward/won't-tack complaint to ballast placement for the first time in the archive — unanswered in this batch. No new person concepts this batch — Dan Fickett is a one-off questioner (inline mention only, per the people threshold) and Larry/LJGeib remains a recurring one-off below the threshold; no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level (a closed-out thread, a cruise follow-through, a sizing/redesign side-thread, and a ballast question), consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/operations/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_311–page_320)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 309–318, 31 Jan–5 Feb 2002). Note on source integrity: an initial Read pass served stale/shifted content for page_317–page_320 (repeating page_315/316's content); re-verified and corrected every file via direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body diff (using raw file reads to bypass the caching quirk) before writing anything — the true content is the "Don's articles" thanks (page_317, Harald Lillevang) and a new "Mast rake" thread (page_318–320: Alastair, Pete Hodges, Glen Maxwell). Closed out the "Need advise" rot-check thread: extended [Checking a used Paradox for rot](/construction/rot-inspection-ice-pick.md) with David Beard's own ice-pick survey of his secondhand boat (one half-dollar-sized, discolored-but-solid spot aft of bulkhead #3, no true rot) and Glen Maxwell's reassurance plus a pointer to a second Dave Carnell article on rot-fungus treatment — extended [Dave Carnell's boat-building articles](/references/dave-carnell-articles.md) accordingly. Created new operations concept [Group cruise planning and firsthand trip reports](/operations/group-cruise-reports.md): Glen Maxwell's proposal of the archive's first group cruise (Apalachicola Bay), his firsthand two-up weekend with wife Nancy at Sebastian, FL (first time sailing "Zoë" with two aboard, sizes given, camped ashore but cooked aboard — folded into [crew size and cabin fit](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md)), and David Beard's solo winter river cruise, including a lee-shore tacking failure and grounding — folded into [sailing characteristics and rig balance](/operations/sailing-characteristics.md) as the archive's first firsthand tacking/handling-limit report. Substantially extended ["Building the Paradox" articles](/references/building-the-paradox-articles.md) and [Don Elliott](/people/don-elliott.md) with a concrete price quote from one-off poster russa1111 ("Russ," below the people threshold — inline mention only): $8.00 plus $2.00 (US)/$4.00 (overseas) shipping for the ~50-page, 16-article set, same-day shipping on order; Harald Lillevang's brief thanks closed the thread. Extended [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) with a new "Mast rake" section — Alastair's question about where the plans define the mast's visible rearward rake, Pete Hodges's hedged best-guess answer (parallel to bulkhead #2, offsets on plan page 6), and Glen Maxwell's fuller construction answer (straight back edge, forward face raked by curving the side plates so the face plate follows their curve) — the archive's only recorded detail on how the mast's rake is actually built. Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md), and [Harald Lillevang](/people/harald-lillevang.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts this batch (russa1111 is a one-off poster, below the people threshold); no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — the new material is practical leaf-level detail (a rot-check outcome, a cruise-planning/trip-report thread, a price data point, and a mast-construction clarification) consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/operations/references indexes.
* **Update (batch page_301–page_310)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 298–307, 29–31 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/body diff. Resolved the previously-open question in [Boom-end tack line holes and drilled-hole rot protection](/construction/boom-end-tack-line-holes.md): Pete Hodges's practical objection (the tack-line holes are too narrow/deep to epoxy-coat without filling them solid) drew two independent same-day fixes — Glen Maxwell's pipe-cleaner-dipped-in-epoxy-then-redrill method, and one-off AOL poster Don Peters's ("Thundershield") drill-oversize/fill-with-thickened-epoxy/redrill alternative. Created new construction leaf [Checking a used Paradox for rot (ice-pick probe technique)](/construction/rot-inspection-ice-pick.md): David Beard, increasingly drawn to fix up his secondhand Archibald-built Paradox to "Zoë" standard, asked Glen Maxwell whether the hull was sound enough to be worth the effort; Glen judged it well worth fixing up and gave a simple ice-pick-probe rot survey technique (sound wood resists, rotten wood admits the pick like a dry sponge) — folded into [secondhand Paradox market](/operations/secondhand-market.md). Substantially extended ["Building the Paradox" articles](/references/building-the-paradox-articles.md) with a "Don's articles" thread prompted by new prospective builder [Harald Lillevang](/people/harald-lillevang.md) (Denmark — the archive's first recorded Scandinavian builder, new person concept, found the design online in mid-2001 and decided in Jan 2002 to build): kayaker37 pointed to the *Messing About In Boats* index and editor Bob Hicks; David LeBlanc posted the full five-part MAIB bibliographic citation (vol. 14, issues 9-13); roger chan confirmed buying a set directly from Don and gave his mailing address (Tomah, WI) — also folded into [Don Elliott](/people/don-elliott.md). Created new reference leaf "Bahamas beach-cruising charter cross-post" for a thin, tangential David LeBlanc/roger chan cross-post (a paid Sea Pearl Bahamas charter vs. Little Cruiser ownership cost, plus a folding-kayak tour alternative) — no Paradox-specific content, filed as a minor leaf per the existing "Aegre" precedent. Extended [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with citations for all of the above. One new person concept (Harald Lillevang — a genuine prospective builder, crossing the people threshold); no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level construction/reference detail or the close of an already-promoted thread, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/references/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_291–page_300)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 288–297, 26–29 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/body diff. Closed out the "Glass bottom Paradox?" thread in [Glass-bottom Paradox (speculative)](/design/glass-bottom-idea.md) with two further replies giving real precedent: Dave Bolduc cited two boats he knew personally with thick-Lexan bottom windows ("Hog Fish Lips," a 32 ft cruising sharpie, and the power sharpie "Tropicstar"); LJGeib (recurring one-off, already known from cabin-heating) pointed to a Chesapeake Light Craft "Glass Bottomed Mill Creek" how-to article and gave concrete build advice from his own teenage skiff-window experience (keep it under 1 sq ft, 3/8-1/2 in polycarbonate, rub strips, sand out scratches). Added a minor lumber/plywood sourcing link (`hardwoodint.com`, Glen Maxwell) to [hull scantlings](/design/hull-scantlings.md). Created new construction leaf [Bulkhead #3 side cleats (unanswered question)](/construction/bulkhead-3-cleats-question.md) for a new one-off model-builder poster ("Phillip"/`pmorawa`, below the people threshold — no boat, single message) who asked why bulkhead #3 alone lacks side cleats in the plans; left unanswered. Created new construction concept [Boom-end tack line holes and drilled-hole rot protection](/construction/boom-end-tack-line-holes.md) for a "Tack Line Holes"/"Bottom Bevel Left Standing" thread: Pete Hodges asked about the boom-end pulley's two tack-line holes (through-and-back to the tack grommet for tightening, per Glen Maxwell) and got Glen's reconfirmation — a month after it was first documented — of the bulkhead/floor-timber bevel-together-after-gluing convention; Pete's rot-prevention follow-up drew Glen's epoxy-and-Cabosil hole-armoring technique (straight epoxy to saturate end grain, then a thickened "armor coating"); Pete's final practical objection — that the plans' ~1/4 in, several-inches-deep holes seem too narrow/deep to coat without filling them solid — was left unanswered in this batch. Cross-linked the new concept from [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md), [furling system](/components/rig/furling.md) (as hardware distinct from the tack strop/downhaul), [hull assembly sequence](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md), and [fastening and gluing](/construction/fastening-and-gluing.md) (Cabosil reuse). Extended [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts — "Phillip"/`pmorawa` and LJGeib (recurring but no boat) remain below the people threshold; no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level construction/hardware detail or the close of an existing speculative thread, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/design/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_281–page_290)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 278–287, 23–26 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages — an initial Read pass returned page_285–287's content for page_288–290 as well (the same shifted/stale-serving quirk flagged in earlier batches), re-verified and corrected via direct `X-Yahoo-Msgnum`/Message-ID/body diff against the source files before writing anything. Closed out the "Forward compartment" thread in [interior bins and hinged floor](/components/interior.md): Glen Maxwell confirmed bulkhead #2 is designed to be watertight with the access opening optional (though the opening adds real structural strength), and one-off poster William H. Longyard — distinct from, but the same person as, the Longyard who answered an outboard-conversion question in the prior batch, still below the people threshold — independently proposed sealing and foam-filling the forward compartment as a bow "crumple zone" against collision. Extended [Glass-bottom Paradox (speculative)](/design/glass-bottom-idea.md) with its only two replies: Pete Hodges's 1 sq ft Lexan-window suggestion (doubling as interior lighting) and Mat ("mat_man22")'s pointer to a Google Groups archive search, neither engaging Sue's structural/obstruction follow-up questions. Substantially extended [Head and sanitation arrangements](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md) with a new "Head options" thread: "futabachan" ("Sue") laid out a detailed brainstorm of MSD placement options (helm-seat variants vs. forward-compartment variants, each with real cabin-layout tradeoffs); Pete Hodges recommended the helm-seat option inside a proper watertight box; Glen Maxwell gave a practical, lived-experience answer (wade ashore where possible; carries a small K-Mart portable potty in his VW camper) rather than endorsing a fitted design; David LeBlanc then forwarded a cross-posted "Talking trash" exchange from the companion Micro Cruising list describing a commercial X-stand toilet/bag product and relaying Dave Bolduc's (`mbolduc`) continuous-tube/diaper-genie-style waste-bagging alternative to a fitted head. Reconfirmed, in a new "Completion time?" thread, Glen Maxwell's build-time answer to a fresh questioner (Sue): ~1,000 hours over about two years to build "Zoë" — the same figure he gave days earlier in the prior batch, now given twice, establishing it (not the original ~500-hour estimate) as his settled, latest-known answer; updated the trunk [Paradox](/paradox.md), [build effort](/construction/build-effort.md), and [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md) to state this superseding figure per the temporal-framing guidance rather than continuing to lead with the stale ~500-hour figure. Extended [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts this batch — "futabachan"/"Sue" (recurring but still without a boat of her own), David LeBlanc, William H. Longyard, and Mat (already a known person) all fall below or are already above the people threshold as applicable; no renames or pruning; no new trunk promotion beyond the build-time correction, which fixes a stale fact rather than promoting new material — the new head-options and glass-bottom-reply content are useful practical leaves, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/operations/boats indexes.
* **Update (batch page_271–page_280)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 268–277, 20–23 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct header/msgnum/body diff. Resolved the previously-open [auxiliary outboard power](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md) question: William H. Longyard (one-off, no boat, kept as inline citation) answered David Beard's Evinrude-conversion question with a generic 12V-electric-fuel-pump-and-regulator method. Created new operations concept [Head and sanitation arrangements](/operations/head-and-sanitation.md) — the archive's first discussion of toilet arrangements: "futabachan" ("Sue," one-off) asked where a portable head fits on the Paradox; Dave Bolduc (posting as `boldav38`) reported (secondhand) that Matt Layden himself uses a bucket ("bucket and chuck it") while he and Mindy prefer shore facilities, reasoning a proper holding-tank head is impractical on a boat this size; David LeBlanc (one-off) confirmed via an external link that "bucket and chuck it" is legal under U.S. federal law; Pete Hodges added that enforcement varies sharply by jurisdiction (Florida lax, California strict, Turkey's citizen-informant fine-sharing). Extended [Interior bin panels and hinged floor](/components/interior.md) with a new "Forward compartment access" section: Pete Hodges worried the bulkhead-#2 access hole (drawing #7) was too small to use without getting stuck; Glen Maxwell confirmed it's tight but workable, describing how he reaches bulkhead #1 lying on his back/stomach; Pete's follow-up floated foam-filling the space entirely or cutting a much larger opening, left unanswered. Created new design leaf [Glass-bottom Paradox (speculative)](/design/glass-bottom-idea.md) for "futabachan"'s unanswered idea of a transparent hull bottom for viewing shallow-water marine life. Created new reference leaf "The Aegre" (Caledonia Yawl website) for David LeBlanc's cross-posted external link to a 1973 engineless 21 ft Shetland-foureen voyage account (Scotland to South Pacific) — tangential microcruising-history color, not Paradox-specific. Extended [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) and [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts this batch — "futabachan"/"Sue," David LeBlanc, and William H. Longyard are single-batch posters with no boat of their own recorded, kept as inline mentions per the people-threshold guidance (David LeBlanc posted twice this batch on unrelated topics but still below the recurrence bar); no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — the new sanitation and forward-compartment-access material are useful practical leaves but not yet defining, recurring themes, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/operations/references indexes.
* **Update (batch page_261–page_270)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 258–267, 19–20 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct header/msgnum/body diff (an initial batch of Read results was served in a shifted, stale order — re-verified each file's `X-Yahoo-Msgnum` and body against page number before writing anything). Created new construction concept [Water tank construction and lining options](/construction/water-tank-lining.md) for a new "Water Tanks" thread — distinct from the existing [transom deck plate](/construction/transom-deck-plate.md) concept, which covers tank-port hardware rather than tank lining: [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) asked what to coat his secondhand Paradox's built-in tank tops with, recalling Don Elliott's articles reporting West System advised against their own epoxy for water tanks while System Three did not object; the thread drew four competing, unreconciled answers — Glen Maxwell's 9-years-trouble-free experience with a cheaper Fiberglass Coatings 5-part epoxy; David LeBlanc's detailed case against amateur epoxy linings (cleaning difficulty, volatile off-gassing, osmosis/hydrolizing risk, single-point-of-failure water supply) favoring jugs/jerry cans instead; Barend Migchelsen's and Luke S's tarred-wood tradition (the latter from tarring galvanized-iron tanks, a metal-tank precedent); Larry (LJGeib)'s Vulkem polyurethane coating recommendation from koi-pond/fish-tank experience, with a same-day toxicity/ventilation caution; Alastair's report that three UK epoxy suppliers called their products unsuitable (plasticiser leaching), leaning him toward using his tanks as ballast only; and dr01allen's flexible-bladder/rigid-insert alternative — with no consensus reached and David Beard's own choice unrecorded. Created new operations leaf [Auxiliary outboard power](/operations/auxiliary-outboard-power.md) for the archive's first recorded mention of engine power on a Paradox: David Beard's unanswered question about converting an old Evinrude 2 hp outboard to run on an external fuel tank. Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) with their respective contributions and citations. No new person concepts — David LeBlanc, Barend Migchelsen (already a known one-off elsewhere in the archive), Luke S, and Larry (LJGeib) are single-thread posters with no boat of their own recorded, kept as inline citations per the people-threshold guidance; no renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — both new concepts are single-thread, unresolved leaves consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/operations indexes.
* **Update (batch page_251–page_260)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 248–257, 14–19 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct header/body diff — a single continuous exchange spanning "Building Question," "Deck and Cabin Beams," the tail of "Thanks everyone...", "questions," "Chine runner construction," and the tail of "hello members !". Created new construction concept [Chine runner construction technique](/chine-runners/construction.md) — the first detailed record of how builders actually shape and fit the chine runners, as distinct from the existing design-level [chine runners and windward performance](/chine-runners/windward-performance.md): Pete Hodges's question about Don Elliott's hull-curve pattern method (unanswered directly); Glen Maxwell's own method (small pre-cut solid fir 2x4 blocks, ~10 in each, cut on a band saw — rated the single most valuable boatbuilding power tool — glued along the overhang and heavily glassed, since the runners change shape in three dimensions aft); Dave Bolduc's confirmation that chine runners and the rudder are the first parts to ground in shallow water; and Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")'s alternative kerf-and-bend method (cut to the drawing, saw kerfs 4-6 in apart leaving ~12 mm of wood, deeper aft for a tighter curve, fill with epoxy) plus his caution against fitting metal edges without real metalworking skill, since the runners' shape governs windward ability — foreshadowing his later bilge-keel argument. Created new construction concept [Deck and cabin beam lamination](/construction/deck-and-cabin-beams.md): Glen Maxwell's method of laminating 1/4 in timber to thickness on a mold (a separate mold for the deck vs. the cabin top, since their crowns differ), cut to length after lamination, one beam a day early in the build. Revised the build-time figure for "Zoë" in [build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md) and [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md): Glen's fuller January 2002 account gives **over a thousand hours** (two years on-and-off, an intense final three months, ~6 months full-time equivalent, the vent trunk singled out as especially time-consuming) — a higher, more complete figure than his original ~500-hour estimate from six months into the build, recorded as an updated/superseding snapshot per the temporal-framing guidance rather than reconciled. Extended [is Paradox a suitable first boat?](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md), [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), and [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) with the thread's coda: David's apology to Dave Bolduc for answering out of order, his point that studying his already-built secondhand Paradox answered many of his own construction questions, and Dave's warm "no problem" reply describing his own slow, iterative, mistake-and-redo building style as normal encouragement for a first-timer. Extended [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) with citations for all of the above. No new person concepts, renames, or pruning this batch — all posters already have concepts; no trunk promotion — the new chine-runner-construction and beam-lamination material is filed as construction-technique leaves, cross-linked from the existing design-level chine-runner and stretched-Paradox concepts, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction index.
* **Update (batch page_241–page_250)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 238–247, 10–14 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct header/body diff. Resolved the previously-open "renewed checking question" in [plywood grade and foam flotation debate](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md): Glen Maxwell answered Mike Vacanti with a hardened stance — not overly concerned about "Zoë"'s interior checking since she's stored inside and well-vented, but he would probably not use even **Marine-grade fir plywood** again, recommending **gaboon or mahogany** (genuinely check-resistant) for hull skin and MDO/HDO for interior bulkheads, with AC fir plywood reserved for a deliberately disposable "proof of concept" boat pretreated per Dave Carnell's method; Mike corroborated with a fir-plywood Searunner 31 already checking/splitting after only ~4 years. Extended [stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md), [Mat ("mat_man22")](/people/mat-man22.md), and [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md) with a chine-runner engineering exchange: Mat explained the runners are curved to follow the hull (a straight or better-angled runner would have less drag), and Jeff proposed a head-to-head Paradox-vs-Little-Cruiser reach test to isolate the chine runners' contribution (unanswered). Created new operations concept [Is Paradox a suitable first boat?](/operations/first-boat-suitability.md) for a newcomer's ("Guy Lichtenstern," one-off, no boat — inline mention only) buildability/cost question, answered with genuinely conflicting advice: Dave Bolduc cautioned against it without prior woodworking experience (also supplying a shallow-water Paradox-vs-Little-Cruiser windward-advantage data point and, in a follow-up, build-time figures reconciling around Matt Layden's ~3.5-month full-time build, a 3-4x part-time multiplier, and Don Elliott's ~1-year part-time estimate, rating Paradox "medium difficulty"); David Beard and Alastair countered that Paradox was in fact their own first boat, with Alastair crediting the design's dry-fit-check-then-bond build sequence as particularly novice-friendly; Kevin (already known) offered a middle path of building confidence on simpler boats first. Extended [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md), [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) accordingly. No new person concepts, renames, or pruning this batch — Guy Lichtenstern is a one-off newcomer per the people-threshold guidance; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted plywood-grade/stretch threads or is a clearly-scoped new leaf (first-boat suitability), consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction/design/operations indexes.
* **Update (batch page_231–page_240)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 228–237, 8–10 Jan 2002), all genuinely distinct messages confirmed by direct header/body diff. Extended [furling system](/components/rig/furling.md) and [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) with a firsthand comparison: Dave reported the stock furling gear gave him little trouble (~30 sec to reef Little Cruiser), but that **Matt Layden reefs and raises Paradox's sail noticeably faster**, including reducing sail downwind in 20-25 kt where Dave and Mindy must head up — evidence the furling system's difficulty is partly operator skill, not just design; Dave also confirmed Matt remains "a very private person" who won't join the forum, and that Paradox #1 was in the water. Substantially extended [stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md) with a "Paradox 1.1" side-thread on **metal chine-runner shoe materials** (lead, ferrocement, steel, and copper-nickel plate, with cost/strength figures and 5200-adhesive fastening advice), prompting a new person concept [Mat ("mat_man22")](/people/mat-man22.md) — a recurring, substantive technical contributor distinct from designer Matt Layden per the identity-caution guidance — and follow-up questions from [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md); and with a rebuttal from [Jeff (UK, "doryman_uk")](/people/jeff-doryman.md), now promoted beyond one-off status, arguing against fitting bilge keels to Paradox (drag, leeway, poor trailering, wrong hull shape) and proposing a from-scratch UK 16 ft stretch (same beam/draft/displacement/rig, no US 14 ft trailering-rule constraint) — echoing Charles Wilson's original 16 ft question from an earlier batch. Extended [plywood grade and foam flotation debate](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md) and [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md) with a renewed, unanswered checking question from new one-off member Mike Vacanti, who asked Glen Maxwell directly whether any plywood/coating combination fully resists checking and whether he was concerned about checking on fir-plywood "Zoë," citing his own badly-checking Bolger "Junebug" (Superply, enamel only, no glass/epoxy) — below the people threshold, recorded only via inline citation. No renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted furling/stretch/plywood-grade threads consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/construction/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_221–page_230)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 217, 219–227, 5–7 Jan 2002) — all genuinely distinct messages, confirmed by direct header/msgnum diff (msgnum 218 is not in this batch). Resolved two previously-open threads. In [furling system](/components/rig/furling.md) and [Bert Vercauteren](/people/bert-vercauteren.md): Glen Maxwell field-tested Bert's speculative tack-strop fix and reported it **does not work** (the furling-drum line has nothing to work against once the strop is released), plus a build lesson (cut the tack-strop pipe with ~2 in of trim allowance); Bert accepted the result gracefully and separately warned the list about a suspicious, virus-flagged, content-free offlist e-mail from a one-off, unverified contact "Ken La Rose" (recorded only inline — well below the people threshold). In [transom deck plate](/construction/transom-deck-plate.md), David Beard's watertightness question was answered twice: [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) reported his transom port drains safely via the tiller-vent baffle and doubles as a ventilation opening, while [Glen Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) raised the more consequential water-tank-port case (a leak there mixes ballast/drinking water into the cabin), disclosed enlarging his own tank ports from 4 in to 6 in, described siphoning tanks dry via the galley pump, and shared a dowel-rod fix for a tank-port leak on his own boat. Created new operations leaf [Trailering — tow weight and vehicle capacity](/operations/trailering.md) for a newcomer's ("tech_necro," one-off, no boat — inline mention only) question about towing a Paradox behind a VW Golf TDI: Glen Maxwell's answer of **~410 kg fully loaded**, a figure notably lower than, and left unreconciled with, other loaded-weight figures already in [weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md); also reconfirmed the $35 US plans price and David Bolduc as distributor, cross-linked into [building plans](/references/building-plans.md). Extended [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with all of the above. No new person concepts (tech_necro and "Ken La Rose" are one-off, unverified, or content-free posters); no renames or pruning; no trunk promotion — all new material closes out or extends existing leaf-level threads consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/construction/operations/references/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_211–page_220)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 207–216, 29 Dec 2001–5 Jan 2002) — all genuinely distinct messages, confirmed by direct header/subject diff. Extended [furling system](/components/rig/furling.md) with Glen Maxwell's follow-up: furl the sail on the side of the boom away from the mast for a fair lead, his modified nylon bulls-eye fair lead in place of the plans' thimble, and his revised diagnosis that the **topping lift**, not the thimble, was the real friction culprit; plus [Bert Vercauteren](/people/bert-vercauteren.md)'s speculative (untested) fix — cleating the tack strop like the reef line — offered with explicit deference to Matt Layden's design authority. Extended [Stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md) and [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) with the thread's first practical construction step: Pete began building a 10%-upsized "Paradox 1.1," asking how to lay out the now-oversized bottom panel (scarf-edges vs. two-piece scarf) and floating an unanswered steel-shoe ballast/chine-bracing idea; Glen Maxwell answered favoring a butt-block-type edge addition over a true scarf, with a temporary 1/4 in ply strip to hold the joint flat during assembly. Created new construction leaf [Transom deck plate](/construction/transom-deck-plate.md) for David Beard's unanswered watertightness/security question about the transom's 6 in inspection plate. Extended [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md) and [Walden](/boats/walden.md) with a New Year status update (all panels now cut, three weeks away on military exercise) and his fullest personal-geography detail yet (Brisbane, Queensland; seasonal temperatures; distance from the contemporaneous Wagga Wagga bushfires), prompted by a exchange with [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md). Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with the tail of the JS/Kak virus saga (still recurring, Windows 95) and the new deck-plate question. Extended [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md) with a belated compliment from one-off poster "ipygmalion" ("Fred") — no new person concept, below the people threshold. No renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material extends already-promoted furling/stretch/Walden threads or is a small, clearly-scoped new leaf (deck plate), consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/construction/boats/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_201–page_210)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 197–206, 21–28 Dec 2001) — all genuinely distinct messages, confirmed by direct header/body diff. Closed the "Stretch Paradox"/"Wearing the Boat" thread with [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md)'s decision to "give a 1.1 version a shot," recorded in [Stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md) and his own person page (no build report follows). Opened and substantially developed a new "Little Cruiser's sail" thread, extending [Little Cruiser](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md) with a new "Sail" section: Dave Bolduc's Little Cruiser (15'1", ~120 sq ft sail) offered as a real-world comparison point for a stretched Paradox's sail area, full sail-plan dimensions in mm, Pete Hodges's question about flat vs. curved panels, and Dave's answer that Little Cruiser's sail is far more complex than Paradox's simple, flat-cut sail (broadseamed panels, segmented/tapered leech, battens, still evolving toward 5 oz cloth). Resolved the previously-open primer question in [topside and hull painting choices](/construction/topside-painting.md): Glen Maxwell's belated reply recommending West Marine Sea Gloss over a sanded surface and advising against latex, extended into [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md). Created new design concept [Furling system (reefing and stowing the rolling-boom sail)](/components/rig/furling.md) for a new "Furling system" thread: David Beard's reefing-thimble friction problem (plus his single-glass-layer bottom and a warm note about sailing solo with his 3-year-old son), Pete Hodges's remaining reservations about the furling system's uniqueness and unanswered heavy-blow question, and Glen Maxwell's detailed topping-lift fix (standing topping lift via trucker's hitch, a second lift for the boom's front, and a planned running-topping-lift upgrade) — cross-linked from [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) and extended into David Beard's, Pete Hodges's, and Glen Maxwell's person pages. No new person concepts, renames, or pruning this batch — all posters (Pete Hodges, Dave Bolduc, Glen Maxwell, David Beard) already have concepts; no trunk promotion, as this batch's material extends already-promoted rig/stretch themes and a sibling-design comparison rather than establishing new defining facts. Updated the design/matt-layden indexes.
* **Update (batch page_191–page_200)**: Ten archived pages (Yahoo Msgnum 187–196, all 21 Dec 2001) — genuinely distinct messages despite superficially similar page numbers to earlier batches, confirmed by direct diff of message headers/bodies. Closed out the "Bulkhead question" thread: extended [hull assembly sequence](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with Glen's brief confirming reply (floor members need beveling too, or should be cut short to avoid it). Substantially extended two existing concepts with the rest of the "Wearing the Boat" thread's continuation: [Crew size and cabin fit](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md) gained a detailed proposal from [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) (6'3", measuring his own headroom shortfall against a wall mock-up) for gaining headroom without a full stretch — lowering the sole and substituting steel-strap ballast (~3/16 in × 5/8 in) along the keel exterior, raising the cabin roof 1–2 in, and a slight mast-height increase, projected to yield 3–4 in more headroom; and [Stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md) gained the thread's pivot to a simpler answer — Glen Maxwell's concrete worked dimensions for a uniform 10% stretch (15' 2 5/8" LOA, 4' 4 13/16" beam, 9 7/8" draft, 40" headroom), Pete's technical pushback (angled parts need lofting/trig, not flat scaling; linear scaling over-grows sail area; the stock bottom panel already equals a full plywood sheet's width), [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md)'s independent confirmation of the plywood-width constraint at 110% scale, and Glen's worked square-root method for correctly scaling sail area (100→110 sq ft example with resulting Foot/Luff/Head/Leech dimensions) — with a one-off pointer to the SailCut sail-design software from Barend Migchelsen (previously seen once before for an unrelated shipping-cost data point; still below the people threshold, recorded only via inline citation). Extended [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) into a fuller person concept (was a single-citation stub) and [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with their citations accordingly. No new person concepts, renames, or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level technical detail (a scaling worked example, a headroom-modification proposal) consistent with the thought-tree principle, filed as extensions of already-promoted concepts rather than new branches. Updated the design/operations/people indexes.
* **Update (batch page_181–page_190)**: Ten archived pages completing the December 2001 "Paint," "It's a Start!," and "Wearing the Boat" threads, plus a new "Bulkhead question" thread. Promoted a recurring theme into a new operations concept, [Crew size and cabin fit ("wearing the boat")](/operations/crew-size-and-fit.md): the phrase's origin (kingfm, then Dave Bolduc comparing the enclosed cabin to a sea kayak / Little Cruiser to a car), Chris Becker's cardboard cabin mockup confirming a comfortable fit at 6 ft/210 lb (headroom under the closed hatch the only tight margin), kingfm's safety concern about sailing alone at a similar size, and new list member "javelin3155" concluding at 6'2"/240 lb that the stock cabin was too small to maneuver in, stating personal minimums of 48 in sitting headroom and 72 in beam — cross-linked to the existing [newcomer FAQ](/operations/newcomer-faq-comfort-and-safety.md). Substantially extended [topside and hull painting choices](/construction/topside-painting.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) with Glen's fuller roll-and-tip mechanics (halved foam roller, 15 in vertical sections, 3 in tipping brush stroked wet-to-dry, wet-edge and spare-brush technique, water-immersion tool storage between coats) and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md)'s follow-up primer question (unanswered). Extended [hull assembly sequence](/construction/hull-assembly-sequence.md) with a bulkhead/floor-member bevel Q&A: [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) asked whether floor members need the same bevels as the bulkheads they attach to; David Beard answered with his own variant (bevel after gluing/nailing, all parts including floor members cut oversize), naming Don Elliott's articles as essential to his first-time build. Extended [weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) and [interior bins and hinged floor](/components/interior.md) with a second, distinct open ballast-location question from Chris Becker (fore-and-aft: does the space between bulkheads #2 and #3 hold the lead pigs and/or a smaller battery per side?) alongside the already-recorded lateral outboard-vs-centered question. Added a closing, inconclusive coda to the [plywood grade and foam flotation debate](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md)'s wood-identification thread (Glen still couldn't identify Stuart Crawford's ply species). No new person concepts for one-off posters LJGeib (Portland, OR, prospective builder) or "javelin3155" (single-exchange, no boat) per the people-threshold guidance — both recorded as inline citations. No renames or pruning this batch; the crew-size/fit theme was promoted to its own concept because it is now evidenced across five independent messages from four different posters, consistent with the thought-tree principle of promoting recurring, well-sourced themes. Updated the operations index.
* **Update (batch page_171–page_180)**: Ten archived pages continuing December 2001 threads: the tail of "It's a Start!" (Chris Becker resuming "Walden"), a new "Laminating Paradox Bottom" thread, and a late reply to the "Paint" thread. Created new construction concept [Epoxy pre-coating technique](/construction/epoxy-coating-technique.md): Glen Maxwell's method for pre-coating plywood with epoxy before cutting (squeegee application, heavier first coat), a screw-mounted "paint/glue board" for coating small cut pieces without sticking, cut-down foam-roller sections for tipping to a glossy finish, and post-cure sanding prep (washing off amine blush with soapy water before sanding). Extended [panel layout and nesting](/construction/panel-layout.md) with a second, independent builder (Chris Becker) hitting the same bulkhead-#2 fit problem documented months earlier — resolved by moving the bulkhead 40 mm and relocating the transom to a spare sheet — plus a list of parts the plans' layout diagram omits (vent trunk pieces, breasthook, butt blocks). Extended [hull scantlings](/design/hull-scantlings.md) with a new Q&A: [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) asked whether a laminated 1/2 in + 1/4 in bottom could substitute for the plans' single 3/4 in sheet; Glen Maxwell confirmed it would be stronger, recommending the bad faces be glued together. Extended [plywood grade and foam flotation debate](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md) with a plywood-identification exchange: Glen's "Paraply" (South America, fine for furniture but fails a boil test, not for boats) versus 1/4 in exterior AC ply, and new list member Stuart Crawford (New Zealand) describing his own ply as sold-as-marine but stamped AC exterior, speculating its veneers might be Kauri (*Agathis australis*) — a practical warning that "marine ply" labeling can be unreliable. Extended [topside and hull painting choices](/construction/topside-painting.md) and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) with his follow-up asking what "roll and tip" means (a painting novice) and reporting bad weather had stalled his own roller-furling experiments. Extended [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md) and [Walden](/boats/walden.md) with the "It's a Start!" panel-marking-out update, and added a one-off aside from list member "kingfm" (already known from cabin-heating) asking about Seattle-area builders. No new standalone person concept for Stuart Crawford (a single-thread technical exchange, no boat of his own recorded) — kept as an inline mention per the people-threshold guidance. No renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material is construction-technique and materials-identification detail consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction index.
* **Update (batch page_161–page_170)**: Ten archived pages completing the "A Query" census thread and a new December 2001 "Paint" thread. Extended [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md)'s census with the answers: [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) reported only **four Paradoxes known to be under sail** (Matt's own boat, Don Elliott's, David Beard's, and "Zoë"), naming David Beard, Alastair, Chris Becker, and Jeff Forrest as then-building, and extended an open sailing invitation from his home near Jacksonville, FL; [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) confirmed he had **one Paradox under construction and one in the driveway** (Tupelo, MS); and [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) reported **beginning construction** in Southern Oregon using Dave Bolduc's plans, Don Elliott's articles, and already-purchased marine ply. Created new construction concept [Topside and hull painting choices](/construction/topside-painting.md) — David Beard's question about cheaply painting his secondhand Paradox with ordinary house paint, answered by Glen Maxwell's own house-paint/West-Marine-Sea-Gloss experience, his "Zoë" Awlgrip data point (only 1 qt used), and his roll-and-tip (3 thin coats) technique, plus James Fuller's pointer to an external article arguing water-based paint is superior — filed as a sibling to, and distinguished from, the existing [bottom painting over epoxy](/construction/bottom-painting.md) (which addresses antifouling bonding, not finish-paint choice). Created new reference [Dave Carnell's boat-building articles](/references/dave-carnell-articles.md) for that external site. Extended [self-steering](/operations/self-steering.md) with a minor, unresolved external pointer (two Wharram-catamaran-website links posted by one-off contributor "pererieravicens," suggesting a possibly adaptable vane/rudder design — no follow-up recorded). No new person concepts for one-off posters James Fuller, Ron Eike (recorded in a prior batch), or "pererieravicens," per the people-threshold guidance. No renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material is leaf-level detail (a census snapshot, a paint-economy thread, a minor external link) consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the people/construction/references indexes.
* **Update (batch page_151–page_160)**: Ten archived pages, mostly continuing December 2001 threads. Created new operations leaf [Newcomer FAQ — cabin comfort, capacity, and perceived safety](/operations/newcomer-faq-comfort-and-safety.md) for a distinct cluster of first-impression questions from non-builder **Kevin** (`kevin1b`) — cabin comfort with the hatch shut, two-person capacity, forward-hatch crawl safety, general seaworthiness, on-deck reefing, and on-deck safety given the boat is "only 4' wide" — left unanswered in this batch and flagged for later reconciliation. Substantially extended [Cabin heating for cold-weather sailing](/operations/cabin-heating.md) with three new data points: a vented-exterior-heat-source link (Zodi "Hot Vent," kingfm), Matt Layden's own anecdote of deliberately freezing his boat into a Cape Cod saltwater pond and relying on body heat plus solar gain (relayed by Dave Bolduc), and Glen Maxwell's inverted-clay-flower-pot heat-retention trick for the cabin stove (with a repeated no-sleeping-with-the-stove-lit warning). Extended [Ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md), [Little Cruiser](/matt-layden/little-cruiser.md), and [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md) with a reconfirmation that Matt's stern-throw/walk-forward anchoring method is used identically and successfully on Little Cruiser, plus the historical detail that Little Cruiser's Bruce anchor originally just hung off the stern. Extended [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) and [Secondhand Paradox market](/operations/secondhand-market.md) with his December 2001 "My Paradox" reply — sailing the ex-Archibald boat ("no Zoë, but strong") while resuming his own build, rejecting Chris Becker's icebox idea for lack of room, and deciding to leave bulkhead #1 solid/watertight — met by [Dave Bolduc](/people/bolduc/dave.md)'s caution to keep bulkhead-#1 access for future inspection. Extended [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md), [Walden](/boats/walden.md), and [Build effort and workspace](/construction/build-effort.md) with his "A Query" census thread (naming Alastair, Glen Maxwell, and Don Elliott as known builders/owners, with Ron Eike — one-off, no new person concept — asking to see a boat in person) and a new plywood-cost data point ($AUS790 for all ten sheets of Pacific Maple marine ply). Extended [Wind Vane Self-Steering (book)](/references/wind-vane-self-steering-book.md) with an availability follow-up (out of print by Dec 2001; purchasable via armchairsailor.com for $36.95). No renames, pruning, or trunk promotion this batch — all new material is leaf-level detail or reconfirmation of existing themes, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the operations/people/construction/references/boats indexes.
* **Update (batch page_141–page_150)**: Resolved the previously-open [self-steering](/operations/self-steering.md) question: [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) reported that in light air the Paradox "sails herself" via tiller-rope friction (moved around inside and made a sandwich on an overnighter without the course changing), while ["guylita"](/people/guylita.md) — now recorded across four messages in one day — qualified this by stressing that hands-free tiller capability matters more in stronger winds (to reef, and because an unattended tiller can jibe the boat fast under weather helm). A later message (judecarm2) added a candidate purpose-built solution, creating new reference [Wind Vane Self-Steering (book)](/references/wind-vane-self-steering-book.md) (Bill Belcher's plywood wind-vane plans). Extended [rig and sail](/components/rig/index.md) and the trunk [Paradox](/paradox.md) with a defining, recurring clarification from Glen: **all sail handling including reefing is worked from the helm station, with no need to go forward** — promoted to the trunk as a well-supported theme. Created two new leaf concepts for guylita's further unanswered questions: [Sailing characteristics and rig balance](/operations/sailing-characteristics.md) (weather helm to windward, sail-trim counteraction, heeling) and a new "Ballast placement: outboard vs. centered" section in [weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) (torque/oscillation concern) — both left open per the archive. Created new operations concept [Cabin heating for cold-weather sailing](/operations/cabin-heating.md): David Beard's question met with a joking Coleman-gas-light suggestion (king/kingfm) and a serious carbon-monoxide safety rebuttal (LJGeib, citing deaths from unvented heaters in enclosed spaces; noting expensive marine coal/pellet heaters exist) — no solution adopted. Extended [Chris Becker](/people/chris-becker.md) and [Walden](/boats/walden.md) with a December 2001 status update: the build had stalled for months (work/health) and Chris resumed, ordering the rest of the ply and epoxy, floating an icebox and detachable-sink idea. No renames or pruning this batch. Updated the operations/people/references indexes and the trunk.
* **Update (batch page_131–page_140)**: Closed out the November 2001 "Stretch Paradox" thread with two more independent questioners — [Pete Hodges](/people/pete-hodges.md) (new person; asked whether mast height/sail area must scale with hull length) and [Jeff Forrest](/people/jeff-forrest.md) (new person; introduced himself as an incoming builder, asked whether beam scales too, then asked whether anyone had actually built a stretched Paradox — left unanswered in this batch) — both answered by Glen Maxwell with the same "scale everything uniformly" rule; extended [Stretched Paradox](/design/variants/stretched-paradox.md) and [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) accordingly. Extended the "Anchoring" thread from the previous batch: [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) proposed a permanent snap-hook bow line doubling as a safety-harness jackline (worrying it would leave the boat stern-to-wind when weighing anchor), and Glen resolved that by advising builders to raise sail before hauling in the anchor — folded into [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md). Created new construction concept [Removable foam flotation panels (door-skin technique)](/construction/removable-foam-panels.md): Glen's method (worked out months after leaving foam out of "Zoë" over moisture-inspection and cosmetic concerns) for mounting foam on removable, paintable 1/8 in door-skin panels rather than gluing it to the hull — cross-linked as a practical follow-up to the earlier [plywood grade and foam flotation debate](/construction/plywood-grades-and-foam-flotation.md); Alastair endorsed it and noted it also makes expanded polystyrene a viable, protected flotation material. Created new operations concept [Self-steering](/operations/self-steering.md) and new person stub ["guylita"](/people/guylita.md) (screen name only) for a single unanswered question — whether Paradox can self-steer or needs a wind vane — flagged as an open question for later batches to resolve, per the identity-caution and thought-tree conventions (kept as a lean leaf, not promoted, since it is a single unanswered one-liner). No renames or pruning this batch; no trunk promotion — all new material is single-exchange, leaf-level detail (rig-scaling clarifications, an anchoring refinement, a construction technique, and an open handling question) consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the people/construction/operations indexes.
* **Update (batch page_121–page_130)**: Six of the ten archived pages in this batch (page_121–123) are the tail of the JS/Kak virus saga — David Beard's 22 Nov 2001 apology and two "all clear" replies (Ron Eike, and a previously unrecorded list member "Bob") — folded into [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md)'s citations with no new person concept for "Bob" (one-off, per the thought-tree principle). The remaining pages continue two genuine threads: (1) the "Mast construction"/"Interior photos"/"More photos posted" exchange (page_124–128) in which [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) asked [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) for a "Zoë" photo with a person aboard for scale, and Glen followed up with scale photos plus four from **"Zoë"'s first overnighter** (full stores and water tanks) — reporting she still needed roughly **100 lb more ballast** to feel stiff enough for confident forward movement on deck; and (2) a new "Anchoring" thread (page_129–130) where [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) flagged the real hazard of having to go forward to the bow to anchor on an enclosed boat, and Glen answered with a **cockpit-worked inhaul-line technique** (flake rode aft to the stern cleat by 7× depth, deploy/set without going forward, retrieve via a separate ring/carabiner inhaul line) that lets the whole anchoring evolution be worked from the cockpit/cabin. Extended [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md) with the photo/overnighter narrative; extended [weight, ballast, and displacement](/design/weight-and-ballast.md) with the real-world under-ballasted data point; substantially extended [ground tackle and anchoring](/operations/ground-tackle.md) with the new inhaul-line technique (a good complement to the plans' below-deck-stowage rationale already recorded there) and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) accordingly. No renames, pruning, or trunk promotion this batch — the anchoring technique and ballast data point are useful practical leaves, not yet a defining structural fact, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the design/operations index descriptions.
* **Update (batch page_111–page_120)**: Eight of the ten archived pages in this batch (page_111–114, 116–118) continue the same November 2001 JS/Kak (KakWorm) virus outbreak documented in the previous batch — David Beard's outgoing mail kept carrying the virus despite Norton scans finding nothing on his end, reported repeatedly by David Beede, James Fuller, Glen Maxwell, and Ron Eike — recorded only as citations on [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md), with no new one-off person concepts (James Fuller, David Beede) per the thought-tree principle. The two substantive threads: (1) confirmation that David Beard successfully collected the secondhand Paradox from Bob Archibald in Florida and went sailing (closing the loop from the previous batch's purchase saga), folded into [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md); and (2) a "Mast construction" thread (page_119–120) where [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), having finished all bulkheads and gone into winter semi-hibernation, asked whether to epoxy-coat the mast interior, and [Glen Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md) answered yes and disclosed that "Zoë"'s finished mast turned out 1.5 in too long to stow with the inspection port closed because the plans didn't account for the anchor light's height — a cautionary build-fit lesson. Created new construction concept [Mast interior sealing and anchor-light fit](/construction/mast-sealing-and-fit.md). Extended [Zoë](/boats/zoe.md) with Glen's trailer-modification detail (lengthened axle atop the spring, welded spindle, low bunk boards enabling launch in under 2 ft of water) posted alongside new interior/trailer photos requested by David Beard. Extended [Alastair](/people/alastair-law.md), [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), and [David Beard](/people/david-beard.md) accordingly. No renames, pruning, or trunk promotion this batch — the mast-sealing/fit lesson is a useful but single-exchange construction leaf, consistent with the thought-tree principle. Updated the construction index.
* **Update (batch page_101–page_110)**: Nine of the ten archived pages in this batch (page_101, 102, 104, 105, 107, 108, 110) are pure list-administration noise — a chain of November 2001 messages (David Beard, Les Lampman, Ron Eike, Glen Maxwell) about a JS/Kak e-mail worm circulating via Outlook Express replies, with zero boat content; recorded only as a brief citation aside on [Glen C. Maxwell](/people/glen-maxwell.md), with no new person concepts created for one-off participants (Les Lampman, Ron Eike) per the thought-tree principle.
